In her letter, she wrote: "Upon receiving your precious letter, I deeply appreciate your kind words. However, your messenger brought only empty words, and my father, involved in suspicion and doubt, abruptly severed ties. How could you not know this, Yun? From now on, the ponds and pavilions of Ji and Yun will only be heard in the echoes of cranes; the towers and terraces of Wang and Xie will have nowhere to rest. This day, the once vibrant life of the household has faded, and my youthful beauty is now gone. This is indeed my fate. I suppose you must share this sorrow and anger. But I am no longer whole, how could I serve two masters? At night, when she thinks, tears stream down her face; in the morning, when she feels something, her fingers scratch wildly."

    40

    嗟，嗟，失此于归，终成堕落。如不弃粪土，敬订逾垣，面诉离忱，以表永诀耳。临风呜咽，援笔酸辛。云郎采览。”云得书，心乱于麻，泪零如雨。细问其母，犹含糊答应，知事之决无济也。俟蟾明之候，踏梯逾墙，女已于檐头接入。相持对泣，泪继以血。女曰：“妾生不逢辰，之死靡他。既不能续前缘，当以魂依左右耳。惟愿郎君新不忘故，时以杯羹呼名而奠，则九原如在矣。今邀郎来，知我死所。”遂指其床，己红丝结扣，悬于顶格。云曰：“生不同衾，死当同城。奚忍舍我！”抢先入扣，女往牵救，则抱女同登，双双毕命。

    Explanation

    40

    Ah, ah, to have missed this opportunity to return home, I have finally fallen into ruin. If you do not abandon me like filth, I respectfully agree to climb over the wall, and we can face each other to express our heartfelt feelings of separation, to signify a final farewell. Weeping while facing the wind, I pick up the pen with bitterness and sorrow. Cloud Lord, please read and appreciate this. When Cloud received the letter, his heart was as chaotic as hemp, and tears fell like rain. He carefully asked her mother, who still answered vaguely, and he knew that the matter would not be of any help. Waiting for the time of the moon's brightness, he stepped on a ladder to climb over the wall, and the woman had already met him at the eaves. They held each other while weeping, and tears followed by blood. She said, "I was born at an inauspicious time, and until death, nothing else. Since I cannot continue our previous bond, I will rely on my spirit to be by your side. I only wish that my lord does not forget the past, and to call my name while offering a bowl of porridge as an offering, as if I were still in the Nine Graves. Now inviting you to come, so you know my place of death." Then pointing to her bed, she tied a red thread knot and hung it at the top. Cloud said, "Not sharing the same bed in life, but dying in the same city. How could I bear to leave you!" Rushing to tie the knot, the woman went to pull and save, and then holding the woman and climbing together, both ended their lives.

    41

    次日，婢媪唤女不应，掇户而入，瞥睹双悬，惊呼翁至，抚之俱僵，且两尸互抱不解。唤白翁来，共鸣诸官，判曰：“审看得白云娥与任宜男者，居本比邻，幼而同学。盈盈弱女，僭称冠带之雄；渺渺丈夫，反袭裙钗之饰。阴阳颠倒，堪嗟两老之朦胧；天地絪缊，宜有双星之缱绻。继而琼田大去，碧海无归，借斑管以描愁，托瑶笺而请命。既以参媒氏妁，好逑称意之花；允宜凤舞鸾歌，竟唱定情之曲。而乃不容坦腹，徒悔噬脐。密简传心，愁甚衡阳之雁；捧书泣血，凄逾巴峡之猿。缟袂趍风，匹夫之志难夺；红颜赴义，匹妇之谅可悲。遽尔双璧同组，立绞鸳鸯之颈；循环合体，牢牵蛤蚧之身。虽事不可风，而节犹足取。律设大法，例顺人情，用是殓以巨棺，俾作同工之茧；葬诸大陆，将生连理之枝。从此地下长眠，不羡人间短景。本县特以表圭璋之坚志，非徒艳花月之新闻，此狱。”断令合葬西湖之麓。

    Explanation

    41

    The next day, the female attendants called out to the girl but received no response. They pushed open the door and entered, only to glimpse two bodies hanging. In alarm, they summoned the old man, who touched them and found them both stiff and lifeless, their corpses tightly embracing one another, unable to be separated. They called for Master Bai, and the officials gathered together. The judge declared:

    "Upon examination, it is found that Bai Yun'e and Ren Yinan were originally neighbors, and as children they studied together. A delicate and frail girl, she inappropriately claimed the status of a man in official robes; a slight and insignificant man, he donned the attire of a woman. Heaven and Earth were inverted; how pitiable are the confusion of the two elders. The heavens and earth are intertwined, and it is natural for two stars to be entwined in affection. Later, the jade fields vanished, and the blue sea had no return. She used a brush to describe her sorrow, and entrusted a jade letter to plead for her fate. Having consulted the matchmakers, they became a union pleasing to both. It was fitting for phoenixes to dance and cranes to sing, and they sang the song of confirmed love. Yet they were not allowed to lie open and honest, only to regret in vain. Secret letters were sent to convey their hearts, their sorrow greater than that of the geese of Hengyang; they held the letter and wept blood, their sorrow exceeding that of the apes of Baxia. In white robes, rushing toward the wind, the will of a common man was hard to change; a beautiful woman who went to fulfill her duty, the understanding of a woman was pitiable. Suddenly, the two pearls were tied together, strangling each other’s necks like mandarin ducks; their bodies merged in a cycle, their souls bound like the toad and the chameleon. Though the matter cannot be praised, their virtue is still commendable. The law sets the great principle, the example follows human sentiment. Therefore, they were buried in a large coffin, to become a cocoon of shared labor; they were interred on the mainland, to grow into entwined branches. From now on, they will sleep eternally beneath the earth, not envying the fleeting scenes of the world. This county specially records their firm will, not merely for the sake of a romantic news story. This is the verdict."

    The judge ordered that they be buried together at the foot of West Lake.

    42

    风流花判，传诵一时。故士有往吊之者。乡愚不知，谓其有所祈祷，尤而效之，有求必应，趋之如市。竟于坟前立庙，香火大盛，至今庙貌狁新，其为发情止义之报耶?
    　　
    　　呆官
    　　
    　　进士某，铨得某县令，戚友虑其呆也，荐与一干仆谙习仪文者，官欣纳之。至省谒上官，势将直入中门，其仆挽之由左。继又拜首邑，令亦由左扉入，其仆推之入中门。官回馆舍，怒其仆曰：“若何颠倒摇弄，我岂木偶耶?”仆知其意，对曰：“非为中门左门之故耶，抚司道府上宪也，礼应偏门入；首邑同寅也，礼应中门入。此所以辨等级，非敢播弄也。”官大悟曰：“是汝不愧为纪纲之仆，将重托汝。”仆乘机求司阍，官大笑曰：“为大令亲随，何等亲密。反求管门，是下走之职。汝舍近就远，辞尊居卑，岂非呆耶?”仆曰：“小人欲之。”官曰：“非我薄汝，莫悔莫悔。”遂命司阍。

    Explanation

    42

    The Charismatic Judge
    The story of the charming and romantic judge was widely circulated and popular at the time. Thus, scholars and literati went to pay their respects to him. The uneducated villagers, however, did not understand the true nature of the matter, believing that he had been praying or performing rituals, and they imitated him, thinking that any request would be granted. They flocked to him like people going to a market. Eventually, they built a temple at his grave, and the temple became very prosperous with offerings and worship. To this day, the temple still looks new. Could this be the retribution for indulging in passion and abandoning righteousness?

    The Dull Official

    A certain jinshi (imperial examination graduate) was appointed magistrate of a county. His relatives and friends, concerned that he was dull or slow-witted, recommended a servant who was well-versed in official etiquette and procedures. The magistrate was pleased and accepted the recommendation. When he went to the provincial capital to pay respects to his superiors, he was about to enter directly through the central gate, but his servant pulled him to the left side. Later, when he went to pay respects to the county magistrate, the county official also entered through the left gate, and the servant pushed him through the central gate. When the magistrate returned to his lodging, he angrily scolded his servant, saying, “Why are you messing things up? Am I a puppet?”

    The servant understood his intent and replied, “Is it not because of the central and side gates? The higher officials—such as the governor, provincial administrator, and other superiors—require one to enter through the side gate as a sign of respect. The county magistrate, however, is a fellow official, and etiquette requires entering through the central gate. This is how hierarchy is maintained, not that I am trying to be disrespectful.”

    The magistrate then realized and said, “You are not unworthy of being a servant of discipline and order; I will entrust you with important duties.”

    The servant seized the opportunity and asked to be made the gatekeeper. The magistrate laughed and said, “As my personal attendant, how close we are. Yet you are asking to be a gatekeeper, which is the position of a low-ranking servant. You are choosing a distant and lower position instead of a closer and higher one—aren’t you being foolish?”

    The servant replied, “I, a lowly servant, desire it.”

    The magistrate said, “It is not that I look down on you, but do not regret it.” And so, he appointed the servant as the gatekeeper.

    43

    往莅任所，书役盛服跪迎，官下舆答礼。至署，吏送下马，饭甚丰腆，官大乐，命仆速备名柬谢之。仆曰：“此皆属下人所应为者，受之而已，无答礼。”官以礼无不答之言反复辩论，仆以仪注告之，始悟官体应尊严也。次日谒庙行香，师生鞠恭迎之，入门三揖，傲慢不答。师生怒，咸讥诮之，抱惭而归。怒詈其仆所云仪注之非，仆曰：“入圣人礼法之门，接师生清高之士，谦让未遑，方合仪注。今自背之，非小人言谬也。”官摇首曰：“世故变幻，较之八股时文难做多矣。”

    Explanation

    43

    Going to the place of office, the clerk in formal attire knelt to greet the official, who stepped down from the carriage to reciprocate the greeting. Upon arriving at the office, the officials escorted the official off the horse, and the meal was very abundant, greatly pleasing the official, who ordered his servant to quickly prepare a formal note to thank the hosts. The servant said, "This is what subordinates should do; one should accept it and not reciprocate with a formal reply." The official argued that there is no situation where a formal reply is not appropriate, and the servant informed him about the proper etiquette, leading the official to realize that one's official demeanor should be dignified. The next day, the official visited the temple to burn incense, and the teachers and students bowed respectfully to welcome him. Upon entering, the official performed three bows, but the teachers and students were arrogant and did not respond. The teachers and students became angry and mocked the official, who returned home feeling ashamed. The official angrily scolded his servant for saying that the etiquette was wrong. The servant replied, "Entering the gate of the sage's ritual and legal system, meeting teachers and students who are pure and noble, one does not have time for humility and yielding, which is in accordance with the proper etiquette. Now, by acting contrary to this, you are not mistaken in what I say." The official shook his head and said, "The complexities of social life are ever-changing, and compared to writing the eight-part essay and regulated prose, they are much more difficult."

    44

    又次日，点卯至东关地保，官大怒拍案，一筒之签尽撒于地，叱役选大杖扑之。地保不知所以，惶悚受杖讫，匍伏哀鸣曰：“小人应卯无误，从何开罪?求明谕以便改过。”官余怒未息，喝曰：“我未达时，偶欠钱粮者，借贷未遂也，尔逼我逃遁，辱及妻孥，此仇铭刻于心，剥尔肤削尔骨方消我恨。一挞尔尚何冤?”地保日；“爷府居原籍之东关耶?”曰：“然。”曰：“离此数千里矣，与小人何干?”官方知误，遽叱曰：“无论远近，我知东关地保决非好人。敢与官哗辩，应加责尔口。”地保抱头鼠窜而逸。

    Explanation

    44

    On the following day, when he arrived at the morning roll call at the East Gate village headman, the official became extremely angry and slammed the table, scattering all the bamboo slips on the ground. He ordered the servant to select a large stick and beat the village headman. The village headman, not knowing the reason, was terrified and received the beating, crawling on the ground and crying out, "I have attended the roll call without error. What have I done wrong? Please give me a clear instruction so I can correct my mistake." The official's anger was still not appeased and he shouted, "When I was not yet successful, I happened to owe some money and grain, and you forced me to flee, disgracing my wife and children. This grudge is deeply engraved in my heart. Only by peeling your skin and cutting your bones can I erase my hatred. One beating is not enough for you to feel wronged?" The village headman asked, "Is your residence in the East Gate of your ancestral home?" The official replied, "Yes." The village headman then said, "It is thousands of miles away from here. What does it have to do with me?" The official realized his mistake and immediately shouted, "Regardless of distance, I know that the East Gate village headman is definitely not a good person. Dare to argue with an official, you should be punished for your mouth." The village headman clutched his head and fled like a rat.

    45

    又次日放告，有老人面禀其子忤逆者。官讯之，对曰，“小人世业纫工，今眼目昏花，藉子奉养。祭小人之子游手好闲，弃人小不顾，将为饿殍矣。求教训之。”官怒曰：“不孝重情，汝退，我即唤汝子责之。”时为纷纷递呈者所嬲，官心烦而退。忽忆及忤逆事，命仆唤纫工，仆误谓其欲成衣也，唤平日伺应之纫工至。官盛怒升堂，喝命大杖，责讫，纫工泣诉曰：“小人何罪?”官曰：“弃养汝父，尚称无罪，真不知薄罚之恩者。”纫工曰：“小人幼失怙恃，何父之有?”官益怒，曰：“父尚不认，悖逆之徒岂可轻恕!”吏役佥跪告曰：“此人自幼在署应差，小人等俱知底细，其父母实于某年月日病故，众所共知，惟祈照察。”官骇曰：“才口诉之老人其鬼耶?”众曰：“此某纫工之父，非此人父也。”官知又误，强叱曰：“为人子者，不能显亲扬名，仅作贱业，是为辱亲不孝莫甚，再敢辩者，立予重罪。”纫工茹痛而去。

    Explanation

    45

    On the following day, the court announced cases, and an old man personally reported that his son was disrespectful and disobedient. The official questioned him, and he replied, "I am a hereditary tailor by profession, but now that my eyesight has dimmed, I rely on my son for support. My son is idle and lazy, neglecting me, and will soon become a starving corpse. I beg you to admonish him." The official became angry and said, "This is a serious case of filial impiety. Go back, and I will immediately summon your son to punish him." At that moment, he was annoyed by the crowd of people presenting documents and left in frustration. Suddenly, he remembered the case of the disrespectful son and ordered his servant to call the tailor. The servant mistakenly thought the official wanted a new outfit made and summoned the tailor who usually served him. The official, in a rage, ascended the dais and ordered a severe beating. After the punishment was completed, the tailor wept and said, "What crime have I committed?" The official replied, "To abandon your father, and still claim innocence, truly shows a lack of appreciation for leniency in punishment." The tailor said, "I lost my parents at a young age. How can I have a father?" The official became even angrier and said, "If you do not recognize your father, how can you be lightly forgiven for such rebellion?" The officials and clerks knelt and reported, "This person has served in the office since childhood, and we all know the details. His parents actually died on a certain date, as is common knowledge. We only ask for your consideration." The official was startled and said, "Is the old man who just spoke a ghost?" They replied, "This is the father of a certain tailor, not this person's father." The official realized he had made another mistake and sternly ordered, "As a son, if you cannot bring honor to your parents and only engage in menial work, this is the greatest disgrace and filial impiety. If you dare to argue again, you will be severely punished." The tailor endured the pain and left.

    46

    官饮量甚洪，日必沽酒数觔。怡然独酌，突有喊冤者，正醺醺时也，阻其雅兴，怀怒升堂，拍案喝打，并不掷签。役跪请曰：“打若干?”官伸指曰：“再打二觔。”吏役笑不可遏，竟至哄堂。官惭而退。
    　　
    　　各役领工食，动需数百金，官怒曰：“我安得如许闲钱养如许闲人耶?”欲批饬不准。仆曰：“此非出自己资，系钱粮项下留支之款，国家定例，不能不准。”官犹豫曰：“然第若辈居心狡狯，闻其正名之外有所谓混名者，一混则无所不混，恐其今日以正名领，异日复以混名领，则被冒支无穷矣。我意欲令其正混各名俱注明领状，当堂验发，庶免混冒。”仆以此无碍于事，饬吏照办。官升堂，点给至轿夫，怒谓曰：“我仅见二人抬轿，如何领工食者四名，岂非冒混耶?

    Explanation

    46

    The official drinks a lot, buying several jin of wine every day. He enjoys drinking alone in a pleasant manner, but suddenly, someone cries out in distress, just when he is drunk. This interrupts his elegant mood, and he ascends the hall with anger, slapping the table and shouting to beat, without throwing the sign. The official kneels and asks, "How many times should I beat?" The official extends his finger and says, "Beat two more jin." The officials and servants laugh uncontrollably, until the whole hall is in uproar. The official leaves feeling ashamed.

    Each official receives their salary, which amounts to several hundred gold coins. The official angrily says, "How can I afford to have so much idle money to support so many idle people?" He wants to order but is not allowed. The servant says, "This is not from my own funds; it is the remaining funds from the treasury, by national regulations, and cannot be denied." The official hesitates and says, "However, these people have cunning hearts. I hear that there are also so-called aliases. Once they start mixing, they will mix everything. I fear they will claim with their official names today and their aliases tomorrow, and thus be exploited endlessly. I intend to have them clearly state their official and alias names on the receipt, verify and distribute on the spot, to avoid confusion and fraud." The servant thinks this does not hinder the matter and orders the officials to follow through. The official ascends the hall and distributes down to the sedan bearers, angrily saying, "I only see two people carrying the sedan, how can four people receive their salaries? Is this not fraud?"

    47

    ”轿夫曰：“轿后尚有二人。”官微哂曰：“据汝言，亦仅有二人。”对曰：“配以轿前之二人，非四耶?”官无词以诘，方按其名。其一曰某，又名洋洋得意；其二曰某，又名不敢放屁；其三曰某，又名昏天黑地；其四曰某，又名拖来扯去。官诧曰：“世安得有如是异名?且停放。”退问幕客，客笑曰：“若辈讥诮阁下不浅矣。”官曰：“何为?”客曰：“阁下后拥前呼，则洋洋得意；谒见上司，则不敢放屁；问案对词，则昏天黑地；何以结讼，则拖来扯去而已。”后此官决囚，因斩绞错误，被参罢去。

    Explanation

    47

    The sedan chair attendant said, "There are still two people behind the sedan chair." The official smiled slightly and said, "According to your words, there are only two." The attendant replied, "Counting the two in front of the sedan chair, aren't they four?" The official had no words to question and began to check their names. The first one was named [name], also known as "洋洋得意" (self-satisfied). The second one was named [name], also known as "不敢放屁" (afraid to let out gas). The third one was named [name], also known as "昏天黑地" (confused and disoriented). The fourth one was named [name], also known as "拖来扯去" (dragging and pulling). The official was astonished and said, "How can there be such unusual names in the world?" He decided to hold off on this for now. Later, he asked the advisors in private. The advisor laughed and said, "You are being mocked by these people quite severely." The official asked, "Why?" The advisor explained, "When you are surrounded by attendants in front and behind, you are '洋洋得意' (self-satisfied); when you meet your superiors, you are '不敢放屁' (afraid to let out gas); when you question witnesses or defendants, you are '昏天黑地' (confused and disoriented); and how do you settle disputes, you are '拖来扯去' (dragging and pulling)." Because of errors in capital punishment, he was impeached and dismissed from office.

    48

    或问曰，“天下竟有是人耶?天下遂无是人耶?”芗厈正衿对曰：“民饥而曰何不吃肉麋?”古来有此帝王，世间宁无此官长?且此官之呆，胜于自以为精明强干者多矣。即其礼节颠倒，尚知与仆反复辩论，其虚心胜于自满者。其责东关地保，尚能实告以故，其朴直胜于迁怒者。其用刑错谬，尚自觉有误，其知过胜于偏执者。至刻扣工食，今已视为当然，此官尚能当堂给发，其廉洁胜子贪婪者。嗟乎！世之亲仆用事，贿赂公行，奔竟上官，即登荐牍，挪移亏欠，旋入咨追，较此官未知其谁呆也。要之幕客一论，实为中下者定评，故轿役混名至今，人人知之。”

    Explanation

    48

    Someone asked, "Could there really be such a person in the world? Or could there really not be such a person in the world?" Xiang Yan, with a straightened collar, replied, "It's like asking a starving person why they don't eat meat." Since ancient times, there have been such emperors; could there really not be such officials in the world? Moreover, the foolishness of this official surpasses that of those who think they are smart and capable. Even though his etiquette is reversed, he still knows to debate with his servants, showing more humility than arrogance. When he holds the Eastern Pass officials accountable, he can still truthfully explain the reasons, demonstrating more straightforwardness than those who take out their anger on the wrong person. When he makes mistakes in his use of punishment, he still realizes his errors, showing more awareness of his faults than those who are stubborn. As for withholding wages, it is now seen as a matter of course, yet this official still manages to distribute them on the spot, showing more integrity than those who are greedy. Alas! The world is full of officials who rely on their servants, engage in public bribery, and rush to please their superiors, only to be recommended for promotion, shifting responsibility for debts and losses, and then being summoned for investigation. Compared to this official, who knows who is more foolish? Ultimately, the opinion of the official's subordinates is the definitive evaluation of such mediocre officials, which is why the nickname for such officials has persisted until today, known by everyone.

    49

    〇曾历一邑，见绅民公檄讨官者，其文仿骆宾王讨武曌檄，附录一证。
    　　
    　　为某县某令者，人本龌龊，家起寒微。昔逢糊目试官，曾以芜词中式。洎乎入仕，大玷官箴。潜萌弄法之奸，阴图受赇之实。入门见贿，鲸吞不肯让人；掩袖进私，狐媚偏能惑宪。移某县之毒手，剥某邑之脂膏。加以鬼蜮居心，豺狼成性，近狎邪僻，深恶贤良。罔上欺君，虐民酷吏，人神之所共嫉，天地之所不容。犹复包藏祸心，窥窃巨室，邑之富户，绳之以小愆；妾之私亲，委之以重任。呜呼！包龙图之不作，海忠介之已亡。国赋横征，任呼嚎而莫应；关节周到，反保举以扬名。某令恃有护符，益逞贪墨，论案情之巨细，索价值之重轻。

    Explanation

    49

    Once governed a county, I saw gentry and common people publicly issue a proclamation to oppose an official, the text of which imitated the style of Luo Binwang's proclamation against Empress Wu Zetian, and I append it as evidence.

    The official in charge of a certain county was originally despicable, coming from a humble background. In the past, he passed an exam through corruption, once passing with a poorly written essay. When he entered office, he greatly tarnished the official's integrity. He secretly harbored the cunning to manipulate the law and plotted to accept bribes. Upon seeing bribes, he greedily swallowed everything without giving way; secretly offering favors, he used cunning to deceive the law. He transferred his cruel methods to another county, stripping it of its resources. Furthermore, with the heart of a demon and the nature of a wolf, favoring the wicked and despising the virtuous, deceiving the emperor and oppressing the people as a cruel official, hated by both humans and gods, and intolerable by heaven and earth. Still harboring evil intentions, secretly plotting to seize wealth, he punished the wealthy of the county for minor faults; entrusted the relatives of his concubines with important tasks. Alas! There are no officials like Bao Zheng or Hai Rui. Taxes were levied arbitrarily, and people's cries went unanswered; through connections, he was recommended and gained fame. The official relied on his protection and grew even more corrupt, regardless of the seriousness of cases, demanding bribes of varying amounts.

    50

    激胥隶之狂狺，良有以也；致绅衿之讦告，岂徒然哉。是用气愤风云，志安闾里，因四乡之失望，顺万姓之同心。爰舒义词，以儆孽宦。南连邻境，北尽本乡，冤黎成群，哀鸿遍野。米麦柴炭，折价之计靡穷；盐当牙行，剥削之谋何尽。嗟声动而悲风起，怨气冲而明斗昏。暗鸣则贫富恐惶，叱咤则男妇觳觫。以此谋利，何利不兴?以此害人，何人不害?某等或居要地，或到公堂，或膺摧折于詈言，或受扑责于非理，事犹在迩，孰肯忘情?三尺之法未亡，一纸之书可托。倘能转祸为福，改过自新，共矢精白之心，立洗腥膻之秽，誓以皦日，安镇此邦。若其眷恋铜山，徘徊阿堵，坐昧先几之兆，必遭盈满之诛。请看今日之檄文，岂是寻常之泛话!

    Explanation

    50

    To provoke the rabid barking of胥隶, there is indeed good reason; to cause the gentry and scholars to accuse, is it not for a purpose? Therefore, filled with righteous indignation as if the clouds and winds are stirred, we aim to ensure the safety of the local community, driven by the disappointment of the four surrounding regions and the united will of the common people. We express righteous words to warn corrupt officials. From the south to neighboring regions, and from the north to the local area, a multitude of oppressed people suffer, and crying birds fill the fields. The calculation of reduced prices for rice, wheat, firewood, and charcoal is endless; the schemes for exploiting salt dealers are endless. The lamentations stir the winds, and the resentment darkens the stars. When they whisper, the rich and poor are filled with fear; when they shout, men and women tremble. With this, what profit cannot be made? With this, who cannot be harmed? We either hold important positions or appear in court, or are subjected to verbal abuse and unjust punishment. The events are still recent, who could forget them? The law of the three feet has not been abolished, and a single sheet of paper can be relied upon. If they can turn misfortune into fortune, repent and reform, together vow a pure heart, and immediately cleanse the filth, swear by the bright sun to stabilize this region. If they cling to their wealth and hesitate, they will miss the early signs and inevitably face the punishment of overabundance. Please see today's proclamation, is it an ordinary idle talk?

    51

    萧希贤
    　　
    　　维扬萧姓，世业鹾商。翁以好士，挥洒数万金，家因中落，郁郁而亡。其长子希圣，自幼得与诸名士游，熏陶成学，由甲科入馆选，转部郎而膺外任。初为云南大理太守，奉太夫人并携其幼弟希贤赴任。希贤者，太夫人中年所生，溺爱过甚，已弱冠而不事诗书。太守以母故，亦不甚督责，然亦无大过。其在衙斋，好与幕客谈黄白之术。客因说之以滇地为产矿之区，山中银苗盛衰，视其草木即辨，每有以数十金置一峦而发家千倍者，故外来游民半以此营生也。希贤艳羡之，因是日偕客遍历群山，以求奇遇。搜索年余无获。

    Explanation

    51

    Xiao Xiyan

    The Xiao family of Yangzhou has long been engaged in the salt trade. The father, known for his generosity, spent lavishly on scholars, leading to the family's decline and his eventual death in sorrow. His eldest son, Xiao Xisheng, was raised in the company of prominent scholars, who nurtured his learning. He excelled in the imperial examinations, entered the Hanlin Academy, and later served as a minister before being appointed to an external post. Initially, he was the governor of Dali in Yunnan, where he accompanied his mother and brought along his younger brother, Xiao Xiyan, to take up the post. Xiao Xiyan was born to the mother during her middle years, and she doted on him excessively. By the time he reached the age of twenty, he had not pursued the study of poetry and books. The governor, out of respect for his mother, did not strictly discipline him, though he also did not cause significant trouble. In the governor's residence, Xiao Xiyan enjoyed discussing alchemy with his guests. The guests told him that Yunnan was a region rich in minerals, and the presence and quality of silver ore could be discerned by observing the vegetation. They noted that some outsiders had made fortunes by investing a few dozen gold pieces in a mountain range, thus many outsiders made a living through mining. Xiao Xiyan was envious of this and, as a result, daily accompanied his guests to traverse the mountains in search of a rare opportunity. He searched for over a year without any significant success.

    52

    客妄指一峰，谓此中产矿甚旺，不过为脱卸计耳。希贤深信不疑，即与山主议价，以百金得之。骤欲集夫开采，太守力阻不从，以娇赖太夫人，使乃兄属下贡夫以助。咸趋奉贵介弟，无不乐从其事者。于是丁男涌集，合力兴工，锯木凿山，穿石穴土。希贤往来监工无倦色，如是者五六年，虚掷亿万工，一无所得。太守以俸满入觐，须携眷去，劝其弟舍是同行。希贤泣下曰：“弟一生事业在此，工既将成，本亦不少，若半途而废，死不甘心。”太夫人不忍拂其志，倾乃兄之宦囊，剖其半与之曰：“儿姑以此相抟，若一二载不得，则剩数百金作腰缠而归，切勿迷恋沉溺于此，以贻母兄戚也。”遂泣别。

    Explanation

    52

    A guest recklessly pointed to a peak, claiming that it was rich in minerals and merely a plan for evasion. Xi Xian, deeply convinced, immediately negotiated with the mountain owner and bought it for a hundred gold pieces. He suddenly wanted to gather workers for mining, but the governor strongly opposed and was not heeded. Xi Xian pleaded with his respected mother, asking his elder brother to contribute workers. Everyone was delighted to serve the esteemed younger brother, and all willingly participated in the endeavor. Thus, men from all over gathered, working together to start the project, cutting wood, carving into the mountains, drilling through rocks, and digging into the earth. Xi Xian went back and forth supervising the work without showing any signs of fatigue. For five or six years, millions of man-hours were wasted, and nothing was achieved. The governor, upon completing his term, entered the court and had to take his family with him, urging his younger brother to abandon the project and join him. Xi Xian wept and said, "My entire life's work is here. The project is almost complete, and the investment is substantial. If I give up halfway, I will not rest in peace." His respected mother, unwilling to thwart his ambition, emptied her elder brother's official resources, dividing half of it to give to Xi Xian, saying, "Son, use this to try. If you do not succeed within one or two years, the remaining several hundred gold pieces can be used for personal expenses and you can return. Do not become addicted or obsessed with this, to bring sorrow to your mother and elder brother." They then wept and bid farewell.

    53

    太守去后，属下之夫皆星散，希贤自募役夫，昕夕从事者又数载。是岁中秋，计短夫价若干缯，工头已言之屡矣，而囊中仅剩十余金。度无以应，乃嘱其仆尽以余金市酒肉，号召众夫，劳之曰：“予夜梦神人谓予，大矿将见，须协力往东南开，则望日可得。敢告诸君，尽今日之力，予将倍偿工价。”众皆踊跃欢呼，饱餐而去。希贤前往峰头巡察，则丁丁之声，众力齐奋。喟然叹曰：“予诓此役夫，心力交瘁，而前欠难偿，必遭其辱，止则无以为生，逃则足力不济。不如死休。”于是解带悬树，正遥拜母兄，哀泣追悔之际，忽见其仆偕头目飞奔而来，料不及投缳矣，迎问其故，佥喘息叩首，道贺曰：“神人之言不谬，已得大矿，请往拜祭。”希贤喜出望外，趋视之见，大穴已辟，其内黑色拳块，一望皆是。众夫佥来叩首请赏，欢声雷动，曰：“此墨绿矿也，最难得者。其源远，其色高，非大福人乌能致此！”

    Explanation

    53

    After the magistrate departed, his subordinates scattered like stars, and Xi Xian personally recruited laborers, working day and night for several more years. On the Mid-Autumn of that year, he calculated the wages owed to the laborers in terms of several bolts of silk. The foreman had already brought this matter up many times, yet in his purse there remained only more than ten taels of silver. Judging that he had no means to pay, he instructed his servant to spend all the remaining silver on wine and meat, and then called upon the workers, addressing them thus: "Last night I dreamed of a divine being who told me that a great mine is about to be revealed. We must work together to open it to the southeast, and on the full moon day it will be found. I dare to inform you all: if you give your best efforts today, I shall double your wages." The workers all leapt with joy and cheered, then ate heartily and departed. Xi Xian went to the peak to inspect the site, and there was the sound of axes ringing out, with the workers' efforts unified. He sighed and said: "I have deceived these laborers, and I am now exhausted in both mind and body. My previous debts cannot be repaid, and I will surely be disgraced. If I stop, I cannot live; if I flee, my strength will not carry me. It would be better to die." With these words, he untied his sash and hung it on the tree, then bowed from a distance to his mother and brothers. In the midst of weeping and regret, he suddenly saw his servant and the foreman running toward him. He had not yet time to throw the rope around his neck. He greeted them and asked the reason, and they all, gasping and bowing, said in celebration: "The words of the divine being were not false; we have already found the great mine. Please come to worship it." Xi Xian was overjoyed beyond expectation and hurried to look, and saw that the great cave had already been opened, and inside were black, solid blocks, all visible at once. The workers all came to bow and request a reward, and the joyous voices were like thunder, saying: "This is the black-green ore, the rarest of all. Its source is far-reaching, its color is of great quality. Only one with great fortune could attain this!"

    54

    希贤乐极，不知所云。其仆为之邀集旧友，或司载籍，或司会计，或司监督，或司宾客，量能授任。群谋报官设厂，内有三分皇税。是以帅遣弁兵，安营环守，文自中丞以下，咸来纳交，声势一时喧赫。希贤乃于穴口设板屋，置大权，持筹握算，凡百觔一载，俾夫递运，厂内匠人收之。百炉并开，以鼓以铸，皆熔为方锭。每方五百两，以防小窃。自近达远，环山之厂皆盈，而穴中尚未尽也。希贤酬应烦劳，志愿已满，惟思载运回籍，以与母兄享此终身。所虑归途遥远，盗贼堪虞。时值有郡守萧姓者，以侍御出守。其为人有心计魄力，能任大事。与希贤联谱，交甚欢，谋以巨资通枢密，超迁大巡，奉旨查盘七省。此明季御吏之最有权力者。所过之处，文武员弁慎重迎送。希贤藉其威势，保护而归。

    Explanation

    54

    Xixian reached the peak of joy, not knowing what to say. His servant gathered his old friends, some responsible for records, others for accounting, supervision, or entertaining guests, and assigned them roles based on their abilities. The group collectively reported to the authorities to set up a factory, with a portion of the profits going to the imperial tax. As a result, the commander dispatched military personnel to camp and guard the site. Officials from the level of Zhongcheng downwards all came to interact with Xixian, and his fame and influence reached a peak. Xixian set up a temporary structure at the entrance of the cave, took charge of the operations, and managed the transport of goods, with each load weighing one hundred pounds. The factory workers collected the ingots. A hundred furnaces were opened simultaneously, and the metal was melted into square ingots. Each ingot weighed five hundred taels to prevent theft. The factory expanded from near to far, and all the factories around the mountains were full, but the cave still had resources left. Xixian was busy with his duties, and his aspirations were fulfilled. He only thought about transporting the wealth back to his hometown to share it with his mother and brothers for life. He was concerned about the long journey back and the potential risks of bandits. At that time, there was a county magistrate named Xiao who was appointed to govern a region. Xiao was a high-ranking official with strategic thinking and the ability to handle major affairs. He was connected by family ties with Xixian, and they had a close relationship. Xiao planned to use a large sum to influence the central government, was promoted to a higher position, and was commissioned by imperial decree to inspect seven provinces. This was one of the most powerful officials in the late Ming dynasty. Wherever he went, civil and military officials received him with great respect. Xixian relied on Xiao's authority and influence to protect himself and return safely.

    55

    抵家，则门庭悬白，老仆披麻，希贤迫切惶恐，直入中堂，则嫂氏携其幼子衰绖以迎。泣告曰：“夫自朝京后观察东河，前年疾卒，太夫人思长忆幼，悲痛而亡。茕茕母子，贫无所依。叔来幸甚。”希贤大恸曰：“何不讣予？”嫂曰：“邮筒已递百十矣，想因中州盗起，路途梗塞，故不达耳。”希贤曰：“嫂无虑困穷，予有金不知其数。所可虑者，老屋实不能容，仍配盐业为商，以其余分设质库，广置田宅，虽百世亦不能尽我所有也。”于是亲友闻之，造宅环请，奔走效劳者，争出新奇以媚之。知希贤尚未娶妻，或构美婢，或觅艳妾，或为谋婚大族。时则有林下尚书郑姓者，羡其富豪，以女嫁之。希贤日则公冗私繁，夜则左抱右拥，劳瘁过甚，病瘵而亡。年未三十，尚无子嗣。族中以长房仅有一子不准过继，遂将现物争夺一空。幸郑尚书为之控诉当道，俾亲侄兼祧，仅得承其盐业而已。

    Explanation

    55

    Upon arriving home, he found the gate hung with white, an old servant in mourning attire, and Hsi-hsien in a state of urgency and fear. He entered directly into the main hall, where his sister-in-law met him with her young son, both in mourning clothes. She wept and said, "My husband, after going to the capital, was sent to observe the eastern river region, and died of illness last year. The grandmother-in-law, thinking of her long-lost son and grandson, grieved and died. We are poor and without support. Uncle's arrival is very fortunate." Hsi-hsien was deeply moved and said, "Why didn't you send me a notice of death?" His sister-in-law replied, "The official post has been sent many times. Perhaps because of bandits in the central region, the roads were blocked, so it did not reach." Hsi-hsien said, "Sister-in-law, don't worry about poverty; I have gold that I don't know the amount of. What I am concerned about is that the old house is really not spacious enough. I will combine the salt business as a merchant, use the rest to set up pawnshops, buy large amounts of land and houses. Even after a hundred generations, I cannot exhaust my wealth." Friends and relatives heard about it, came to the house to request, and those who rushed to serve competed to show new and strange things to flatter him. They knew Hsi-hsien had not yet married, so some arranged for beautiful maidservants, some sought beautiful concubines, and some planned to marry into wealthy families. At that time, there was a retired official named Zheng, envious of his wealth, who gave his daughter in marriage to him. During the day, Hsi-hsien was busy with official duties and private affairs, and at night, he was surrounded by women on both sides. Overworked to the point of exhaustion, he died of consumption. Before thirty years old, he still had no children. The clan did not allow the eldest branch to adopt the only son, so they fought over and emptied all the current possessions. Fortunately, the retired official Zheng lodged a complaint with the authorities, allowing his nephew to inherit jointly. He only inherited the salt business.

    56

    芗厈曰：“多藏厚亡，多财丧身，古人言之屡矣。观萧希贤之以富促其寿，反不如食贫之为安也。”或曰：“诚如子言，则富不当在五福之列矣。”对曰：“富而攸好德，则可冀长远；否则富而纵欲，六极之凶，短折立至。朝暮不保，何福之有？”客唯而退。
    　　
    　　李寡妇

    Explanation

    56

    Xiang Yan said, "Those who hoard wealth will suffer great loss, and wealth can lead to one's downfall. The ancients have often warned against this. Observe how Xiao Xixian's wealth hastened his death, making it worse than living in poverty, which is safer." Someone replied, "If what you say is true, then wealth should not be included among the Five Blessings." He responded, "If one is wealthy and also values virtue, one can hope for a long life; otherwise, if one is wealthy and indulges in desires, the Six Evils will strike, leading to a short and sudden death. One cannot even be sure of surviving from morning to evening—what good fortune is there?" The guest nodded in agreement and then withdrew.

    李寡妇

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    57

    李氏者，明慧人也。虽为农家妇，夫亡守志，其翁姑百计不能夺。于是分受房屋，以针黹自食其力。妇有姊嫁王鬼头，生女及笄，貌甚风月。鬼头欲以居奇，故未字。邻村有张傻子者，母耄而惛，积有制钱百贯，欲为其子觅一好妇。与鬼头素识，频频嘱托。鬼头艳其钱，欲设法取之。因思其姨李寡妇尚少艾，诓而与傻子结亲，则钱可得矣。
    　　
    　　一日，牵驴过寡妇家，饰为惶迫之状，泣而言曰：“汝姊病革，待汝一言瞑目，使我相迎，幸勿他却。”妇见其意诚，且昭昭白日，必无他虑，遂乘驴而行。抵傻子家，呼妇下曰：“此张巫家，我欲邀其媪去为汝姊医治，曷为我入告。”妇诺下骑。鬼头先入，耳语张母曰：“新妇来矣，尔相之，如合意，使尔子务亟成亲，我后日来取身价。”母喜诺而出，鬼头由后户潜出，牵驴而道。妇入见张母，致其姊相邀治病之说，母笑曰：“新妇勿支吾，令亲已为吾子媒，今夜良辰，送来完其姻好，何治病之有？”

    Explanation

    57

    The widow Li was a wise and intelligent woman. Although she was a farmer's wife, after her husband's death, she remained steadfast in her resolve, and her in-laws tried every means but could not persuade her. She supported herself through needlework. Her sister, who had married Wang the Ghost, had a daughter who reached the age of marriageability. The ghost wanted to keep her as a valuable asset and had not yet arranged her marriage. In the neighboring village, there was a fool named Zhang. His mother, who was old and senile, had accumulated a large sum of money and wanted to find a good wife for her son. She knew the ghost well and frequently entrusted him with the task. The ghost coveted the money and devised a plan to obtain it. He thought of the young widow Li, who was still beautiful, and decided to deceive her into marrying the fool, thereby securing the dowry.

    One day, the ghost led a donkey to the widow's house, pretending to be in a state of panic and urgency, and cried, "Your sister is gravely ill and needs your help to pass away. Please let me come and fetch her. Please do not refuse." The widow, seeing his sincerity and knowing it was broad daylight, believed him and rode the donkey to the fool's house. Upon arrival, the ghost called out to the widow and said, "This is the Zhang family. I want to invite your mother-in-law to come and treat your sister. Please go inside and inform her." The widow agreed and dismounted from the donkey. The ghost entered first and whispered to Zhang's mother, "The new bride has arrived. Please look at her. If you approve, make your son hurry to get married. I will come later to take the dowry." The mother was pleased and agreed, then the ghost secretly left through the back door, leading the donkey along the road. The widow entered and saw Zhang's mother, who said, "New bride, do not stammer. Your brother-in-law has already arranged the marriage for my son. Tonight is a good time, so he will send her to complete the marriage. What is there to treat?"

    58

    妇方知为鬼头所卖，始而怒，继而思，忽然大笑谓母曰：“误矣，我齿己长，想汝子年必不大，老少岂可为婚？我系王鬼头之姨，我有嗣女甚佳，亦嘱其为媒，渠送我来相新郎，如可匹偶，则择吉亲迎可也。”母笑曰：“然则亲家耶？勿讶我惛耄搪突。”急呼其子出拜丈母。妇目之，年与甥女正相若，神气稍憨耳。笑谓之曰：“好为我嗣女婿矣，改日驱车从我迎娶去。”母子皆喜诺，留妇信宿，敬之如大宾。越三日，妇曰：“今日黄道，勿误佳期，可觅大车一辆，偕往亲迎。”傻子与族人假车，整其衣履，为御而往。

    Explanation

    58

    The woman only now realized she had been deceived by Ghost Head. At first angry, then thoughtful, she suddenly burst into laughter and said to her mother, "You were wrong; my teeth have already grown. I suppose your son must not be very old. How could an old person marry a young one? I am Auntie of Ghost Head. I have a very good daughter-in-law, and I also asked her to be a matchmaker. She sent me here to meet the groom. If he is a suitable match, then we can choose a good day for the wedding." Her mother laughed and said, "So you are my in-laws? Don't be surprised if I seem a bit senile and impolite." She quickly called her son out to greet the mother-in-law. The woman looked at him and saw that his age matched that of her niece, though his demeanor was slightly naive. She laughed and told him, "You are a good son-in-law for me. Tomorrow I will drive a carriage to come and fetch her." Both the mother and son happily agreed, and they kept the woman for two nights, treating her with the respect due to a distinguished guest. After three days, the woman said, "Today is a favorable day. Don't miss the good time. Can find a large carriage. Put on his clothes and shoes, and to drive and go." The fool borrowed a carriage from his clan, got dressed, and set off to go.

    59

    鬼头遥见潜避，妇入，怒其姊曰：“汝夫既好意为我作媒，何不相谋？诓我与张氏子成亲，今已三朝，姊亦不来一看，何忍心耶？”其姊曰：“我未知之也，妹来何为？”妇曰：“我为新妇寂寞过甚，欲同甥女去作伴数日。”回视大姑曰：“汝愿否？”大姑曰：“姨母见招，敢不惟命？”妇即携其手，登车而去，抵张氏宅，母见之大悦。妇悄谓傻子曰：“新妇年轻害羞，我慰其早睡，潜出易汝，事成则明日拜堂可也。”傻子唯唯。于是夕飧毕，大姑相依姨母入新房安宿。妇婉劝其解衣同衾卧，俟其睡酣，潜起易傻子入，突成其事。大姑声喊，妇隔门谕之曰：“此汝父之命，我为主婚，安之勿讶，明早当接汝父母来观合卺耳。”大姑始明其意，然此身已失，听之而己。

    Explanation

    59

    A ghostly figure saw someone hiding from afar. The woman entered and angrily scolded her sister, saying, "Your husband had good intentions in acting as a matchmaker for me, so why didn't you consult with me? He tricked me into marrying Zhang's son, and it has already been three days since the wedding. You didn't even come to see me once, how could you be so heartless?" Her sister replied, "I was unaware of this, why did you come?" The woman said, "I am here because the new bride is too lonely, and I want to go with her niece to keep her company for a few days." She then turned to the eldest daughter-in-law and asked, "Do you wish to go?" The eldest daughter-in-law replied, "My aunt has invited me, how dare I not follow your command?" The woman then took her hand, got into the carriage, and they left for Zhang's house. The mother was very pleased to see them. The woman quietly told the simpleton, "The new bride is young and shy. I will comfort her and tell her to go to bed early, then I will sneak out and switch places with you. If everything goes well, we can hold the wedding ceremony tomorrow." The simpleton nodded. That evening, after the meal, the eldest daughter-in-law accompanied her aunt into the new bridal chamber to settle in. The woman gently persuaded her to take off her clothes and sleep in the same bed. When she fell into a deep sleep, the woman sneaked up and switched places with the simpleton, suddenly accomplishing the deed. The eldest daughter-in-law shouted, and the woman called out through the door, saying, "This is your father's command, I am the matchmaker, accept it and do not be surprised. Early tomorrow morning, I will bring your parents to come and see the wedding ceremony." The eldest daughter-in-law finally understood her meaning, but by then her innocence had already been lost, and she could only accept it.

    60

    是日，鬼头虑其姨至家必不干休，故寄宿在外，翌辰归，方知携其女去，怒其妻不偕往，其妻曰：“既在亲串间，今日去不迟。”于是夫驭其妻往，则张氏之堂邻族亲朋满座，其女方与傻子交拜也，鬼头急喊，为鼓吹声所混，无闻之者。及男女相将入，寡妇出堂，当众直诉其事。众皆不直鬼头，共吐骂之，且有义忿者，欲以鸣官。鬼头羞畏而遁，其姊哀祈始息，张氏母子德之无已，集绅衿公旌李氏门，而以百贯酬其义。

    Explanation

    60

    On that day, the ghost head, fearing that his aunt coming to the house would not let things rest, stayed overnight outside. The next morning when he returned, he only then realized that his daughter had been taken away. He was angry with his wife for not going together, but she said, "Since it was among relatives, it was not too late to go today." So the husband drove his wife there, and at the Zhang family's house, relatives and friends were all present. His daughter was just about to be married to a man called "Stupid One." The ghost head shouted urgently, but his voice was drowned out by the drum and flute music, and no one heard him. When the couple entered, the widow came out of the hall and complained about the matter publicly. Everyone condemned the ghost head, and all cursed him. Some, filled with righteous anger, wanted to report to the authorities. The ghost head, shamed and fearful, fled. Only after pleading did it stop. The mother and son of the Zhang family were forever grateful, and they gathered the scholars and officials to publicly commend the Li family, rewarding his righteousness with a hundred strings of cash.

    61

    芗厈曰：即以其人之道，还治其人之身，智者妇也。当日公旌李氏之门者不知何语，若问题于我，当额之曰：“冰雪聪明。”
    　　
    　　鸾仙五则
    　　
    　　扶鸾之术，南方为盛，真伪不一，传闻者夥矣。录数则以助一粲。
    　　
    　　有狂生不信鸾仙者，适友家，见骈集多士开坛请仙，其诚肃之容，如对严师，如临大祀，咸端庄拱立，无敢出声者。狂生大笑曰：“清平世界，敢以妖言惑众，我将治之。”其友曰：“慎毋哗，真仙在此，汝若不信，可作文字，固封以叩之，能直言其隐。岂我辈所妄托耶？”生曰：“果尔，请尝试之，然公等所请者何仙？”友曰：“麻姑耳。”生又大笑，至密室，潜书一封掷坛上，曰：“请判！”鸾少息，生曰：“其技穷矣。”忽大书曰：“调寄《耍孩儿》，其词曰：立似沙弥合掌，坐如莲瓣微开。无知小子休弄乖，是你出身所在。”狂生失色而遁，众开其封，大书一“屄”字也。（《正字通》曰：布非切，女子阴。）

    Explanation

    61

    Xiang Yan said: "To use someone's own methods against them, the wise woman knows this. The person who honored the Li family's door that day did not say what, but if asked by me, I would say: 'As clear and bright as ice and snow.'"

    Five Tales of the Divine Medium

    The practice of the divine medium, known as "Fuluan," is most prevalent in the south. Its authenticity varies, and there are many rumors about it. I record several cases to add to the amusement.

    There was a wild scholar who did not believe in the divine medium. He happened to visit a friend's house and saw many scholars gathered to invoke the spirits. Their solemn and respectful demeanor was like facing a strict teacher or attending a grand ritual, all standing upright and silent. The wild scholar laughed and said, "In a peaceful world, to dare to use false words to mislead the people, I will deal with you!" His friend said, "Be careful not to make noise. The true immortal is here. If you do not believe, you can write a letter, seal it, and ask for a response. It will speak the truth. Could we be falsely claiming this?" The scholar said, "If so, please let me try it. But what immortal are you invoking?" The friend said, "Lady Magu." The scholar laughed again and went to a secret room, wrote a letter, and threw it onto the altar, saying, "Please judge!" The medium paused briefly, and the scholar said, "Its skills are exhausted." Suddenly, it wrote: "Set to the tune of 'Playing with the Child,' the lyrics say: Stand like a novice monk with hands joined in prayer, sit like a slightly opened lotus flower. Foolish boy, stop being clever, this is where you come from." The wild scholar turned pale and fled. The others opened the sealed letter and found a large "vagina" character written on it. (According to Zhengzitong: "Bùfēi" cut, the female organ.)

    62

    其年童子试，诸生群集请仙。鸾书曰；“今日上真皆赴元玄会，不暇降坛，命吾土地权摄，诸生何问？”众曰：“明日小试，请问试官所命何题？”鸾曰：“题目在吾堂内，尔等自往寻之。”于是众皆执香入土地祠堂，跪拜讫，遍览一周，并无只字。再祷之，鸾不动矣。皆腹诽土神谬。翌日赴试，题纸下“土地”二字，此神可谓现身说法。

    Explanation

    62

    In that year, during the preliminary examination for young scholars, the scholars gathered in a group to invite a deity. The divine medium wrote: "Today, all the high-ranking deities have gone to the Daoist gathering of immortals, and have no time to descend to the altar. I am ordered by my local deity to temporarily take charge. What are the scholars asking?" The scholars replied: "Tomorrow is the minor examination. Please tell us what question the examiner will set." The divine medium wrote: "The question is in my hall. You all go and look for it yourselves." Thus, all the scholars held incense and entered the land deity's shrine, bowed and prayed, then looked all around once, but found no single word. They prayed again, but the divine medium did not move. All of them privately thought the land deity was wrong. The next day, when they took the exam, the question paper had the two characters 'land deity' written on it. This deity can truly be said to have taught by personal example.

    63

    大比之年，有父子诸生，共叩鸾仙问得失。鸾书曰：“速往南行，路遇疯僧，问之不已，可决前程。”父子大奔而去，其子年少足捷，果追及一僧。问之不应，牵袖苦缠不休。僧瞪目大骂曰：“入你娘的中！”生怒欲殴，经众劝释，是科其父高捷，始悟其言。
    　　
    　　有诸生群集鸾坛问功名者，鸾书曰：“赵酒鬼到。”众皆詈曰：“我等请吕仙，野鬼何敢干预？行将请天师剑斩汝矣。”鸾乃止，而复作曰：“洞宾道人过此，请生何问？”众皆肃容载拜，叩问科名，鸾书曰：“多研墨。”于是各分砚研之，顷刻盈碗，跪请所用。鸾曰：“诸生分饮之，听查判断。”众乃分饮讫，鸾大书曰：“平时不读书，临时吃墨水，吾非吕祖师，依然赵酒鬼。”诸生大惭而毁其坛。

    Explanation

    63

    In the year of the imperial examination, a father and son, both scholars, went together to consult a spirit for advice on their prospects. The spirit wrote, "Hurry to the south, on the way you will meet a mad monk. Keep asking him until you can decide your future." The father and son hurried away, and the son, being young and quick-footed, indeed caught up with a monk. When he asked the monk, the monk did not respond, but the son persisted, holding onto the monk's sleeve and not letting go. The monk glared at him and shouted, "Get lost!" The son was furious and wanted to attack the monk, but they were persuaded by others to release him. In that year, the father achieved high success in the examination, and the son finally understood the spirit's message.

    There were also scholars who gathered at a spirit altar to ask about their prospects. The spirit wrote, "Zhaogui (Saojiu) has arrived." Everyone cursed, "We have asked for Lüxian, how dare a lowly spirit interfere? We will summon the Master's sword to cut you down!" The spirit stopped writing, but then wrote again, "Lübinqi (Dongbin) Daoist passes by here. What do you want to ask?" Everyone bowed respectfully and asked about their prospects. The spirit wrote, "Prepare a lot of ink." So they each ground ink, and in no time, the ink filled their bowls. They knelt and asked for the ink to be used. The spirit said, "Share the ink and listen to the judgment." They drank the ink together, and the spirit wrote, "Those who don't read books all the time end up drinking ink at the last minute. I am not Lüxian, I am still Zhaogui (Saojiu)." The scholars were deeply ashamed and destroyed the altar.

    64

    有幕客于衙斋请仙，鸾大书；“浙绍星槎先生来此，汝曹有何领教？”众讶其倨傲，问曰：“先生何仙？请示降坛诗。”鸾曰：“吾乃汝曹之大老前辈，昔在此署总理刑名钱谷大幕也。诗云：情淡封轻仅五钱，旁花随柳过前川。时人不识子心苦，将谓偷闲乐少年。”众皆笑曰：“此《千家诗》耳，人所共知，先生袭之，又连作别字，某等不解尊意。”鸾曰：“此正吾辈名幕套法巧夺古人处，汝曹学浅难解，吾为注之。敝居停进士公也，酸刻而好嬉戏，每传班演剧，与优伶杂采仅朱提五星。

    Explanation

    64

    A retainer invited a spirit in the office, and the spirit wrote large: "Mr. Xingcha from Zhejiang and Shaoxing has arrived here. What do you have to teach us?" The people were surprised by the spirit's arrogance and asked, "What kind of spirit are you? Please show us a poem written during your descent." The spirit replied, "I am your senior predecessor. I once served here as the chief of the office handling criminal cases, finance, and grain. The poem reads: 'The matter is light and insignificant, worth only five coins. Passing by flowers and willows, crossing the river. The people of the time do not understand the bitterness of your heart. They will think you are just enjoying your youth.'" Everyone laughed and said, "This is just a poem from the 'Thousand Family Poems,' common knowledge. You have copied it and made mistakes in characters. We do not understand your meaning." The spirit replied, "This is exactly where our clever methods surpass the ancients. You are too inexperienced to understand. I will explain it for you. My employer is a scholar-official, strict and fond of play, often staging plays, and mixing with actors, selecting only five of the best."

    65

    时有花柳二小优与吾甚昵，恒随带过前川堂，众皆目逆而送之，曰“先生乐甚”。殊不知去其一月修金，正难与细君销算之苦也，故诗云云。且吾辈办案，无不套叙一切，留心套熟，则不犯驳饬。何也？上游幕客皆吾辈套中人耳。此等秘妙法诀，夜台无用，以赠诸公，无负老夫一片婆心也。”是后开鸾必至，所言皆卑鄙琐陋，呶呶不休，皆厌恶之，而无可如何。一日，仆偶以稿案误置坛上，忽大书曰：“此案难办难办。”而寂，众视案卷，令君粘签其上，应禀复宪司者，无熟套可循，故鬼畏而遁。或曰：“此鬼在生，因作禀不妥而窘死者。”
    　　
    　　芗厈曰：前四则可谓夫人必自侮，然后鬼侮之。后一则套字误尽天下苍生，然宪司牧令相延，若辈惟命是从，此吏治之所以日坏也。安所得豪杰之士跳脱圈套，与贤有司共挽颓风哉。滔滔者不过步此庸鬼之后尘，以求生活也。噫！

    Explanation

    65

    There were two young performers, Huayu and Liu, who were very close to me. They always accompanied me to the Qianchuan Hall, and everyone would look back and send them off, saying, "Master is very happy." Unbeknownst to others, I lose a month's salary for them, which is exactly the difficulty in reconciling with my wife over the financial burden. Hence the poem says so. And when we handle cases, we always follow established procedures. If one pays attention to these routines, one won't make mistakes. Why is that? The officials in higher positions are all familiar with our routines. These secret and wonderful methods are useless in the afterlife, so I give them to you all to show my genuine care. After that, the spirit would always appear, and what it says is all base and trivial, talking incessantly. We dislike it, but there's nothing we can do. One day, the servant accidentally placed the draft on the altar, and suddenly wrote in large characters, "This case is difficult to handle." Then it became silent. Everyone looked at the case file, and the magistrate attached a note to it. What should be reported to the higher authorities, there is no established procedure to follow, so the ghost feared and fled. Some said, "This ghost was alive when he died because he made an improper report."

    Xiangyan said: The first four points can be said that a person must first humiliate themselves, then ghosts will humiliate them. The last point is that the word 'routine' has misled all the people. However, the higher officials and local magistrates follow these routines, and these people follow orders blindly, which is the reason why governance is deteriorating day by day. How can we find heroic men who can break free from these traps, to work together with virtuous officials to reverse the decline? The masses are no more than following the footsteps of this mediocre ghost, to seek a living. Alas!

    66

    卷二
    　　
    　　第二卷
    　　
    　　王梦蛟
    　　
    　　长乐马某，操布业。妻许氏，中年无子，遂娶王姬。姬身具鳞甲文，其母梦长蛟缠体而生者，名之曰梦蛟，记其瑞也。归马年仅十六，未几生一子，名铎。许氏阳为喜悦，而阴实妒忌，思有以中伤之。王识其心，故防卫惟谨，母子不片刻离也。一日，许谕浣衣，王怀子持衣登楼，当窗以晾。许潜蹑其踪，自后推其母子坠楼，而作惊讶状。马闻之趋救，王头面虽伤，其子则端坐无恙。
    　　
    　　马察知其妻不能相容而畏之，遂成悸疾。其夥李某自远方贸布回，生平相与之至笃者，泣告以故，出妾与子属之。李曰：“知己之托，敢当重任，但某无家室，何以安如夫人也？”马曰：“予筹之审矣，请以王姬侍足下，以存吾孤。”李推之不得，厚嫁之，带其子铎往。逾年生一子，名之曰马，盖不忘其发之赠妾生子，以志其恩义也。

    Explanation

    66

    Volume Two
    Chapter Two
    Wang Mengjiao

    A man from Changle, surnamed Ma, was engaged in the cloth-making or trading business. His wife, surnamed Xu, had no son in middle age, so he married a woman from the Wang family. The Wang woman had scales or patterns resembling armor on her body, and her mother had dreamed of a long dragon entwining her body before her birth. She was named Mengjiao, a name that commemorated this auspicious dream. When she returned to the Ma family at the age of sixteen, she soon gave birth to a son named Diao. Xu pretended to be happy but secretly harbored jealousy, thinking of ways to harm her. Wang recognized her intentions and guarded them carefully, never leaving her and her son apart for a moment. One day, Xu instructed her to wash clothes. Wang carried her son up the stairs and hung the clothes by the window. Xu secretly followed her and pushed her and the child off the stairs, then pretended to be surprised. Ma heard and rushed to save them. Wang’s face and head were injured, but her son remained unharmed.

    Ma realized his wife could not tolerate him and feared her, which led to a heart condition. His partner, Li, returned from a distant place with cloth, a close friend from his past. Li told him the story and gave his concubine and son to him. Li said, “A trusted friend’s request, I dare not refuse the heavy responsibility, but I have no family, how can I provide for her?” Ma replied, “I have thought it over carefully, please take Wang’s daughter-in-law to serve you, to take care of my orphaned child.” Li could not refuse and arranged a generous marriage for her, taking her and her son Diao with him. The following year, she gave birth to a son named Ma, to remember the gift of a concubine and son, to commemorate the kindness and righteousness.

    67

    未几马某卒，而许氏亦颠沛死。李以马赠嫁之资经营起家，富甲一邑，重聘延名师以训二子，恩勤兼挚，马铎得中永乐壬辰状元，其子李马亦发解。李夫妇大悦，分马铎以家资之半，俾归其宗。铎泣辞曰：“若非继父，何有今兹？愿以空身守先人庐墓。”李强与之，铎以财产为弟游扬名誉，且与改名曰骐，以避嫌疑。戊戌，李骐亦状元及第，未几李夫妇以寿考终，铎欲黜其嫡母许氏，以王姬归葬父所。骐不愿曰，“若依兄命，则弟为无母之儿，于礼不顺。”不得已，陈情于朝，帝命礼部议曰：“王氏改嫁，义已绝于前夫；教子成名，理应隆以异数。况李骐不能无母，而马某本自有妻。论妇道之有终，应从后葬；嘉英才之连育，请锡荣封。事出创闻，后不为例。”议上，封以长乐县君，谕祭葬。

    Explanation

    67

    Shortly after, M passed away, and X also died in adversity. R used the dowry given by M's family to start a business and became the richest person in the county. He hired highly respected teachers with generous payment to educate his two sons, showing both kindness and diligence. M passed the imperial examination and became the top scholar in the year Renchen of Yongle era, and his son L also passed the provincial examination. R and his wife were very pleased and decided to give M half of the family wealth to return to his ancestral home. M cried and refused, saying, "If it were not for my stepfather, how could I have achieved this today? I wish to guard the ancestral tomb without accepting the inheritance." R insisted on giving it to him, and M used the inheritance to support his younger brother and changed his name to Qi to avoid suspicion. In the year Wu Xu, Qi also passed the imperial examination and became the top scholar. Shortly after, R and his wife passed away at an old age. M wanted to demote his stepmother X and bury his father's wife W in his father's burial place. Qi did not agree, saying, "If I follow my elder brother's command, then I would be a child without a mother, which is not in accordance with ritual propriety." M had no choice but to present a petition to the court. The emperor ordered the Ministry of Rites to deliberate, saying, "Wang remarried, and the bond with the former husband has been severed. To educate the son to achieve fame, it is appropriate to bestow exceptional honors. Moreover, Qi cannot be without a mother, and M already had a wife. To discuss the virtue of a woman's loyalty, she should be buried in her current husband's burial place. To praise the continuous cultivation of talented individuals, please bestow honor and titles. This is a new and unprecedented matter, and it will not be repeated in the future." The Ministry of Rites submitted the recommendation, and the emperor bestowed the title of Lady of Changle County on W and issued an edict for a memorial service and burial.

    68

    假和尚
    　　
    　　金生者，浙右人也。幼患秃疮，头无毫发，然聪慧异常，经史百家，过目成诵。临摹法帖，逼肖名家。真未易才也。惜好为巧诈，不务正业，年十六，入黉门，试优等食饩。父母为完娶后，相继而殁。生益无拘束，日与浮浪子为伍，凡狎邪之事，无所不为。未几家业倾尽，则播弄其亲族朋友，以博升斗，如是者十余年。人人畏而避之，无可行其欺诈矣。蓝缕如丐，室人交谪。生奋然曰：“大丈夫博功名富贵犹反手耳，奈吾乡人目小如豆，不识贤豪，无能助吾入青云者。吾将遨游四海以图之。”妻孥皆破涕为笑曰：“博场妓馆，足下之青云耳。累及乡党，不为少矣。不自成立，惟觊人有，狁肆口讥谈，能无汗颜？所可取者，不肯玷辱先人。轻去其乡而为流丐，尚知羞耻，是则足下之大志也。”

    Explanation

    68

    Fake Monk

    　　Jin Sheng was a man from the right bank of the Zhejiang River. As a child, he suffered from a skin condition that left him bald, but he was exceptionally intelligent. He could memorize after a single reading of the classics and histories, and his calligraphy closely resembled that of famous masters. Truly, he was a rare talent. Unfortunately, he enjoyed being cunning and deceitful, neglecting his proper duties. At the age of sixteen, he entered a school and passed with top grades, receiving a stipend. After his parents arranged a complete marriage for him, they died one after another. With no restraints, he daily associated with idle and dissolute people, engaging in all sorts of immoral activities. Before long, his family assets were depleted, and he began manipulating relatives and friends to gain a little. For over a decade, everyone feared and avoided him, unable to carry out his deceit. Worn out like a beggar, his wife and children scolded him. With determination, he said, "A true man can achieve fame and wealth as easily as turning one's hand, but my fellow villagers have eyes as small as beans, do not recognize talented and noble people, and no one can help me rise to high positions. I will travel the world to achieve it." His wife and children burst into laughter and said, "Gambling halls and brothels are your own path to success. They involve the village and are not few in number. You do not establish yourself, hoping others have, and freely criticize and talk, how can one not feel ashamed? What is commendable is that you do not want to disgrace your ancestors. Leaving your hometown and becoming a wandering beggar, you still know shame, this is your great ambition."

    69

    生忿忿而出，遍求亲友，告以远游之意，以妻子为托曰：“以十载为期，若不得上达，誓不空归。”众皆曰：“十年则君之子亦长矣，似有跨灶之才，君无后虑。”生曰：“吾子不过贵公子之貌耳，焉能清于老凤？”众笑曰：“但愿如是，我等十年之担，庶可息肩。”生叩首谢，众曰：“毋匆促，当为君筹画旅资，以壮行色。”生曰：“负累己多，何可再扰？即此行矣。”急奔而去。

    Explanation

    69

    Angry and determined, he left, seeking out his friends and relatives, informing them of his intention to travel far, and entrusting his wife and children, saying, "I set ten years as my deadline. If I do not achieve a high rank, I swear not to return empty-handed." They all said, "After ten years, your son will also have grown up, and he seems to have the talent to surpass his father. You have no further worries." He replied, "My son is no more than the appearance of a noble son; how can he be clearer than an old phoenix?" They laughed and said, "We only hope that is true. Then our burden of ten years can finally rest." He bowed deeply in thanks. They said, "Do not be in a rush. We will arrange your travel funds to make your departure more impressive." He said, "I am already burdened with many responsibilities. How could I trouble you again? I will go now." And he hurried away.

    70

    途遇一僧，醉卧于道，身畔有担，生触机而叹曰：“噫，是可为也。”随窃其衣钵，并有度牒，名曰“悟真”。因是周流于丛林间，但可驻足，无以发迹。转辗入粤东，有古大寺者，雄据一方，为通都大邑之胜境也。时不戒于火，琳宫璇室焚毁其半，其主持僧募缘修葺，尚无人应。生周阅之曰，“噫，是可为也。”遂谒主僧，愿留为役。问客何能，对曰：“吾乃粗莽和尚，未能识字诵经，不过任洒扫执爨之事耳。”主僧留之，令其入市买物，则哀祈阛阓中之能者为之，书单必详列某物价若干，共用钱若干，交单时物既便宜，单亦明晰。主僧甚宠遇之。

    Explanation

    70

    On the road, he encountered a monk who was drunkenly sleeping by the path, with a pole by his side. The man touched the pole and sighed, saying, "Ah, this can be done." He then stole the monk's robe and bowl, along with a permit, which was named "Wuzhen." Therefore, he wandered among the monasteries, only able to stay temporarily and unable to make a name for himself. Eventually, he entered Guangdong, where there was an ancient large temple that dominated one area, a famous place in a major city. A fire broke out unexpectedly, destroying half of the jade palaces and crystal chambers. The abbot raised funds to repair it, but no one had responded. The man inspected it and said, "Ah, this can be done." Then he approached the abbot, willing to stay as a laborer. The abbot asked what the guest could do, and he replied, "I am a rough and unrefined monk, unable to read or recite scriptures, but I can only handle cleaning and cooking." The abbot kept him, ordering him to go to the market to buy things, begging the skilled people in the market to do it, with the list detailing the price of each item, and the total amount of money. When handing over the list, the items were cheap, and the list was clear. The abbot highly favored him.

    71

    如是者半载，人咸知悟真和尚之真无能也，莫不怜其朴诚，惜其愚蠢。生故作憨戆之状，以取信于人。于是潜置紫金衣钵，以策藏于毁余之佛座下。一日辰兴，冠毗罗服紫衣，据大殿之基，趺跏而坐。众僧见之，走报主僧曰：“悟真疯矣。”笑述其状，主僧往观，生徐起曰：“佛旨在身，不敢行礼。”主僧诘之，对曰：“弟子于夜半梦释伽牟尼降，嘱曰：‘是庙之兴，惟汝能为。其勉力募化，以结善缘。’弟子以愚昧辞，我佛微笑以手摩顶，授以五色珠，使吞之，曰：‘服此舍利子，自能领悟一切法。吾座下有正传衣钵，亦以付汝，是可取信于人也。

    Explanation

    71

    For half a year, everyone knew that the real Master Wuzhen was truly incapable. No one did not pity his simplicity and honesty, and regret his stupidity. The person deliberately acted foolishly to gain trust from others. He secretly placed the purple golden robe and bowl, to store the plan under the Buddha's seat that was partially destroyed. One morning, he put on the crown of Vairocana and wore the purple robe, sat cross-legged on the foundation of the main hall. The monks saw him and ran to report to the head monk, saying, "Master Wuzhen has gone mad." He smiled and described his appearance. The head monk went to see him, and the person slowly stood up and said, "The Buddha's will is in me, I dare not perform the ritual." The head monk questioned him, and he replied, "The disciple dreamed of the descent of Shakyamuni Buddha at midnight, who said, 'The revival of this temple can only be done by you. You should strive to raise funds to build good karma.' The disciple declined due to his ignorance. The Buddha smiled and placed his hand on the disciple's head, gave him a five-colored pearl to swallow, and said, 'By swallowing this relic, you will naturally understand all the Dharma. There is a true transmission robe and bowl under my seat, which I will also give to you. This can be used to gain trust from others.'

    72

    ’弟子觉而寻之，果于莲花座下得此，敢不敬谨奉持，以彰佛道？请吾师号召施主，以观弟子撰文书榜，以募善缘。”众僧闻之，喧传遐迩，于是男妇聚观者以数万计。生乃布硬黄纸，对大众书疏，其文如《圣教序》之清丽，其字仿《多宝塔》之端劲。士大夫佥顶礼佩服，大众无不涕泣赞叹，哄呼活佛，施舍恐后。弥月间朱提堆积矣。乃延善士以董其事，佥曰：“创建之资虽敷，梁栋之材未备，何从得此大木也？”生曰：“吾慧照四方，惟蜀山有巨林可采，第不难往买而难于递运，须广大神通以摄之，似亦可至也。”众皆曰：“运大神力，非活佛不能。”生伪辞再三，众请益力，生曰：“姑以二十万金易轻便之物，俾予独往独来，以成此善果。”众皆欣诺，为之置珠宝以行。

    Explanation

    72

    The disciple woke up and searched for it, and indeed found it under the lotus seat. How dare I not respectfully hold it to glorify the Buddhist path? I request our master to call upon the donors to see the disciple write the notice to solicit good karma. The monks heard this and spread the news far and wide, and thus men and women gathered to watch in the tens of thousands. The disciple then laid out hard yellow paper and wrote a letter to the crowd, the text as clear and beautiful as the Sacred Teaching Sequence, the characters as upright and strong as those in the Jewel Pagoda. The scholars and officials all bowed in respect and admired, and the crowd all cried and praised, shouting "living Buddha," giving alms fearing to be late. Within a month, the gold piled up. Then they invited virtuous men to oversee the matter, and all said, "The funds for construction are sufficient, but the timber for the beams and pillars is not ready, how can we get such large timber?" The disciple said, "My wisdom shines in all directions, only in the mountains of Shu are there vast forests that can be cut, it is not difficult to go and buy, but difficult to transport, requires great supernatural power to bring it, it seems possible to achieve." All said, "To move with great supernatural power, only a living Buddha can do it." The disciple refused repeatedly, but the crowd pleaded even more strongly, and the disciple said, "Temporarily exchange twenty million coins for something light and easy to carry, to allow me to go and return alone, to complete this good deed." All were delighted and agreed, arranging for jewelry to be carried.

    73

    生出粤，弃其缁素，兼程入都，货其珠宝，丰获赢余。值大捐例开，生以原名纳资，得太守，入觐奏对，称旨交部，即铨选得闽郡。过其乡里，仆从舆马炫耀，一时亲友争趋奉之，生皆厚报，乃携妻孥之任。缘历尽艰难，深知民间疾苦，以清勤自持，故称贤太守也。
    　　
    　　公大将军延师
    　　
    　　公大将军某，权臣也。威震内廷，势倾中外，庶司百寮，莫不拱手听命。适其幼子需师教读，与公卿言及，众皆唯唯。时有江南沈孝廉，因不第流寓都中，觅馆糊口，已托其座师某持郎允为推荐。适冬至朝贺之期，百官咸集，大将军复理前言，某侍郎即举沈生以应。大将军曰：“为童子师，以品行为要，俾幼而习见师范，则长可观矣。此生端方否？”侍郎对以有儒者气象，大将军欣然许可。侍郎归，召沈生，嘱令小心谨慎，不仅安砚有方，若得大将军青目，则功名未可限量也。沈感谢而退。

    Explanation

    73

    A Man from Guangdong

    Born in Guangdong, he renounced his scholarly attire and hastened to the capital, where he sold his jewels and made a substantial profit. When a major fundraising campaign was launched, he paid a sum of money under his original name and became a magistrate. He attended court, presented his report, pleased the emperor, and was sent to the Ministry. He was then appointed to govern Min Prefecture. Passing through his hometown, he displayed his servants and vehicles, and his friends and relatives rushed to flatter him. He repaid them generously and then took his family to his new post. Having experienced hardship, he deeply understood the hardships of the common people. Maintaining himself with integrity and diligence, he was thus called a virtuous magistrate.

    The Grand General Hires a Teacher

    The Grand General, a powerful minister, wielded influence in the inner court and his power extended throughout the empire. All officials bowed and obeyed him. When his young son needed a teacher, he mentioned it to the nobles, and everyone responded affirmatively. At that time, there was Shen, a scholar-official from Jiangnan, who had failed the imperial exams and lived in the capital, seeking a teaching position to make a living. He had entrusted his mentor, a certain official, to recommend him. During the winter solstice court ceremony, when all officials gathered, the Grand General revisited the previous topic, and a certain official recommended Shen. The Grand General said, "To teach children, morality is the most important. To make them familiar with the teacher's example, so they can grow up well. Is this man upright and proper?" The official replied that he had a scholar's demeanor. The Grand General approved. The official returned, summoned Shen, and instructed him to be careful and cautious. Not only should he excel in his studies, but if he gained the Grand General's favor, his prospects for success were limitless. Shen expressed his gratitude and withdrew.

    74

    新正，大将军遣从官以名柬邀，沈生至第，大将军躬迎于堂，专席南向，命公子盛服拜师。毕，大开筵宴，声伎杂奏，宾主尽欢而止。送先生入西园，书室三楹，峰峦周匝，林木青苍，室内书籍充栋，陈设精雅。公子年甫六龄，不过指教数字而已。其供奔走者，苍头四人，俊童八人。晚入卧室，牙床绛帐，绣樨锦衾，并皆佳妙。次日，苍头带长髯者至，约视沈生体段，趋出未几，进貂裘全袭服之，长短适宜。每日辰兴，则八童齐侍。其一首顶银盆，跪而请盥，一执漱具，一执巾帕，一执镜奁，一执香皂，余皆撩衣摄裳，环伺左右。

    Explanation

    74

    On the first day of the lunar New Year, the Grand General sent an official attendant with an invitation to Mr. Shen. Upon his arrival at the General's residence, the Grand General personally welcomed him in the hall, seating him in the honored position facing south, and instructed his young master to wear formal attire and pay respects as a student. After the ceremony, a grand feast was held, with various performances of music and dance, and both the host and guest enjoyed themselves thoroughly. The General then escorted Mr. Shen to the West Garden, where he found a study with three rooms filled with books, surrounded by mountains and hills, and lush green trees. Inside, the shelves were lined with numerous books, and the furnishings were elegant and tasteful. The young master, just six years old, was only taught a few characters. The attendants included four experienced servants and eight young boys. In the evening, Mr. Shen entered the bedroom, where he found a luxurious bed with a red canopy and finely embroidered bedding, all of which were exquisite. The next day, an experienced servant with long gray hair arrived to measure Mr. Shen's body and fitted him with a fur coat that was just the right size. Every morning, the eight young boys would attend to him, one holding a silver basin for washing, another with a toothbrush or mouthwash, another with a towel or handkerchief, another with a mirror case, and another with soap. The rest would adjust their clothing and stand around on both sides, ready to serve.

    75

    沈生平所未经者，意甚不安，谕之曰：“其以盥盆置架上，恐沾尔等衣也。”童曰：“某等受大将军命，日事师如事予。大将军盥沐皆如是也，敢不勉效执事，以速重愆。”沈曰：“我所命，与尔无碍。”童不敢违，以梓楠雕架承之。沈正沐，大将军至，见童不顶盆，怒目视之，向随带护卫一颠首，护卫喻意，带诸童出。未几，献首阶前曰：“某童不敬先生，已斩之矣。”沈大惊骇，视大将军色转和，心始安。大将军喜古玩，每得禹鼎汤盘之属，与沈共赏鉴，沈若赞叹，则曰：“先生欲之，留此为玩。”虽千金所得者，未尝顾惜。

    Explanation

    75

    Se, who had never experienced this before, felt very uneasy. He told them, "Put the washbasin on the shelf to prevent it from getting on your clothes." The children replied, "We have been ordered by the great general to treat the teacher as we treat you. The great general always does it this way; how dare we not strive to serve properly to avoid serious mistakes?" Se said, "What I command does not harm you." The children dared not disobey and supported it with a carved shelf made of zini and nanmu. Se was in the middle of washing when the great general arrived and saw that the children were not holding the washbasin. He looked at them angrily, gave a nod to the guard he brought along, and the guard understood the meaning and brought the children out. Shortly after, they presented their heads at the steps and said, "That child was disrespectful to the teacher; he has already been executed." Se was greatly shocked, but seeing the great general's expression become more gentle, he began to feel at ease. The great general liked antiques and would share items like the Yu Ding or Shangpan with Se for appreciation. If Se praised them, he would say, "If the teacher wants it, keep it as a plaything." Even if it cost a thousand gold pieces, he had never been stingy.

    76

    一日，大将军来与沈共膳。饭中有完谷，沈出之。大将军见，回首示护卫意，护卫去，未几献首阶前，曰：“庖人拣米不净，已斩之矣。”沈不胜惊愕，视大将军，谈笑自若，不得不勉强承欢。逾月，沈忆家中所需，欲支修金。与苍头商之，苍头曰：“需若干，请作书呈大将军。”沈请数十金，连家书送去。苍头回日。“已如命奇江南矣。”未几，大将军奉旨征西夷，来辞沈曰：“先生之品学，予所敬佩。今予承命西征，未稔何年葳事，敬以幼子属先生，有不率教者笃责之，勿弃之而去。予旋师之日，当有以报先生也。”沈唯唯。大将军去后，沈欲出访友，阍者拒曰，“府中出入，皆有载籍，按月录报。

    Explanation

    76

    One day, the General came to dine with Shen. There was a whole grain in the rice, and Shen took it out. The General saw this and turned his head to indicate to the guards. The guards left, and soon brought a head to the front of the steps, saying, "The cook did not sift the rice properly, and has already been beheaded." Shen could not help but be shocked and surprised, but the General talked and laughed as usual, forcing himself to be cheerful. After a month, Shen remembered the needs of his family and wanted to request a stipend. He discussed it with the servant, who said, "Need a certain amount, please write a letter to present to the General." Shen requested several dozen gold pieces and sent along with a family letter. The servant returned and said, "Has already been ordered to go to Jiangnan." Soon after, the General received an imperial order to campaign against the Western barbarians and bade farewell to Shen, saying, "Your character and learning, I greatly admire. Now I have received the command to campaign to the west, and I do not know when I will return. I respectfully entrust my young son to you. If he does not follow instructions, strictly discipline him, do not abandon him. I will have something to repay you." Shen nodded in agreement. After the General left, Shen wanted to visit a friend, but the gatekeeper refused, saying, "All entries and exits in the mansion are recorded in a register, and reported monthly."

    77

    大将军在时，先生足不出户，今忽有是，恐贻奴辈性命之忧。可已则已，乞哀怜之。”沈知大将军家法严，遂不果出。次年应会试，亦为阍者哀祈而止。沈愤欲告归，又不敢拂大将军命。所幸四季衣服屡为更新，而小衣十日一新。至肴馔，则翻新出奇，总无恒品，不第丰腆而已。若闷时，苍头望见颜色，即传府中男女名优赴园演剧，为先生排遣，则又乐而安之。荏苒三年，屡有信致家中，未得一回书，深以父母妻子为念。委婉致意大将军，得还书，意甚款洽，言亦谦退，惟云予报捷在迩，请先生姑俟，晤商可也。又半载，大将军献俘还阙，圣驾郊迎。公事毕，入视先生，欢然道故，并谢勤劳之意，沈未敢骤辞。

    Explanation

    77

    During the time when the Grand General was still in power, the Master remained sequestered in his home. Now, however, something unexpected had occurred, and he feared that it might bring danger to the lives of the servants. If it could be stopped, he should stop it; he begged for their mercy. Knowing that the Grand General's household rules were strict, he ultimately did not venture out. The following year, when he was due to take the imperial examination, he was also dissuaded by the gatekeeper's plea. Feeling frustrated, he wished to resign and return home, but he dared not defy the Grand General's command. Fortunately, his seasonal clothing was frequently updated, and his undergarments were replaced every ten days. As for the food, it was always innovative and varied, never staying the same, and it was not just abundant but also exquisite. When he felt bored, the servants, noticing his mood, would invite performers from the household to entertain him in the garden, alleviating his boredom, and he found joy and contentment in this. Over the course of three years, he sent many letters home but received no replies, deeply worrying about his parents, wife, and children. He tactfully conveyed his concerns to the Grand General, who replied in a very cordial manner, speaking humbly and saying only that victory was near at hand, and he asked the Master to wait for a while, suggesting they discuss it further when they met. After another half-year, the Grand General returned victorious with captives to the capital, and the emperor's procession greeted him in the suburbs. Once official duties were completed, the Grand General visited the Master, joyfully recounting old stories and expressing his gratitude for the Master's hard work. The Master did not dare to decline immediately.

    78

    一日，登假山闲步，忽闻哀痛之声，问童曰：“此声何来？”童曰：“将军凯旋后，在厅事考功过定赏罚耳。”沈曰：“得窥探否？”童不敢违，引之往，潜伏厅后窃视。见大将军高坐，庭下甲士森列，所执刀斧之光耀目。两旁司官按籍论功过，其功多者，立易以应升之品服，酌酒赐坐；其过多者，大将军面数之，曰某战汝失机宜，某事承办不力，应斩。任其哀吁不顾也。护卫即洗剥其衣，推出门外，砍首以献。惟是大将军严刻，罚多赏少，悲嚎不绝。沈观之惨甚，不觉首触门屏而倒。

    Explanation

    78

    One day, Shen was strolling leisurely up a man-made hill when he suddenly heard a sound of sorrow and pain. He asked the child, "Where does this sound come from?" The child replied, "After the general returned victorious from battle, they are in the hall evaluating merits and demerits to decide rewards and punishments." Shen said, "May I observe?" The child, fearing to disobey, led him there, and Shen hid behind the hall and secretly watched. He saw the great general sitting high up, with soldiers in armor standing in dense formation, the light of the swords and axes they held dazzling his eyes. Officials on both sides were evaluating merits and demerits according to the records. Those who performed well were immediately changed into the appropriate rank and uniform for promotion, and wine was poured and a seat granted. Those who made too many mistakes were pointed out to their face by the general, who said, "In a certain battle, you missed the right opportunity," and "In a certain matter, you failed to carry out your duties effectively," and should be beheaded. Ignoring their cries of sorrow, the guards stripped off their clothes, pushed them out the door, and decapitated them. Only the great general was strict and harsh, with more punishments and fewer rewards, and the cries of sorrow never ceased. Shen observed it and found it extremely cruel, and unconsciously hit his head against the door panel and fell down.

    79

    大将军微闻之，事毕退视厅后，见先生倒卧于地，数童扶之不起。大将军亲挠之入室，慰令安卧，唤仆速取安神丸，以参汤服之。从容问曰：“谁告先生，俾惊怯也？”沈思大将军威严，不敢实告，因徐曰：“闻公子之言。”以其爱子之心，则无所责罚。大将军唯而去。忽有仆妇怆惶入室，跪告曰：“公子干犯先生，大将军裸而鞭之将毙矣。奉夫人命，求先生速救之。”沈愕然曰：“我不能入内室，其奈之何？”妇曰：“只须先生命苍头往唤公子，则大将军不能不释。”沈如教，苍头负公子来，恹恹欲绝。沈抚之遍体皆伤，泣曰：“我冤汝矣。”命苍头携公子卧具来。与之同榻，虑大将军怒犹未息也。

    Explanation

    79

    The general heard vaguely that something had happened. After the matter was finished, he retreated to view the hall behind. He saw the master lying face down on the ground, several children unable to lift him up. The general personally comforted him into the room, instructed him to rest peacefully, and called a servant to quickly fetch the calming pill, to be taken with ginseng decoction. Calmly, he asked, "Who told the master to make him fearful and timid?" Chen thought about the general's authority and dared not tell the truth, so he slowly replied, "I heard the young master's words." Because of his love for his child, the general would not punish or reprimand him. Suddenly, a servant entered the room in a state of distress, knelt and reported, "The young master has wronged the master, and the general stripped him and was about to beat him to death. On the order of the lady, I request the master to rescue him quickly." Chen was surprised and said, "I cannot enter the inner chamber, what can I do?" The servant said, "Just have the master command the attendant to go and call the young master, then the general cannot fail to release him." Chen followed the advice, and the attendant carried the young master here, weak and on the verge of death. Chen touched his body, which was all over injuries, and cried, "I am wronging you." He ordered the attendant to bring the young master's bedding. He shared a bed with him, worrying that the general's anger still had not subsided.

    80

    逾月，乘大将军欢悦，沈复以归宁为请。大将军曰：“先生孝思，予曷敢阻？”择日盛饯，大将军欲亲送出关，沈力辞，犹依依不舍，命公子代送。登程之际，后车数十乘，从者数十人，卿相咸设饯，行则卫士前驱，止则馆舍盛备，所历之都邑，自节使监司以下，迎送惟谨。至河干，则巨艘十余。沈意谓一肩行李，何用如许舟车，始问苍头，则以单纸进。默数之，凡几年所备之衣笥及书室中所陈之物，无论书籍古玩，无不载来。

    Explanation

    80

    After a month, taking advantage of the general's good mood, Shen Fu requested to return home to visit his parents. The general said, "Your filial thoughts, how dare I obstruct?" He chose a day for a grand farewell feast. The general wanted to see him off personally, but Shen strongly declined, and the general, reluctantly letting go, ordered the young master to escort him instead. At the moment of departure, dozens of vehicles followed behind, accompanied by dozens of attendants.
