There were two chairs, with the head monk and Wang facing each other, while more than ten monks in plain hemp robes stood around, each holding a weapon. The group called out, “Come, come, come.” Wang said to the head monk, “May the disciples fight among themselves, and the masters fight among themselves. May I have my disciples go first? Is that acceptable?” The head monk, whose eyes were sharp and whose appearance was gaunt, replied indifferently, “Why not? Please let your most talented disciple go first. Be mindful of any injury or offense, and do not feel ashamed.”

    123

    ”驾长亦已释服，曰：“秃有贼形，恐窃吾衣，必谨藏之。”乃蹲身抱大殿之后柱，起尺余，檐椽震动，砖瓦齐鸣，以左足扫础倒，置衣其下，以右足扶直之，仍安柱。转身呼斗，声若巨雷。于是首座及寮僧咸股栗膜拜曰：“我教中韦驮天尊，旋乾转坤之力不过如是。僧辈肉体凡胎，何敢相角？若尊客一挥肱，则俱成麋粉，情甘降服，不敢再言技勇矣。”王与驾长皆大笑，群僧屏气肃候，延至方丈侍茶毕，王告退，首座与寮僧尽易法衣，执幡幢以送。王返舟，谓驾长曰：“壮士之力，天下无敌，盍不入营为伍，则显职立至。请以资助，聊报大德。”驾长曰：“嘻，吾若不为显职，亦可小康，何至操贱业？客尚欲吾博显职耶？”王叩其旧职及里居姓氏，驾长不答。王凛然报以百金，亦不受。及置药回，逢人述且感云。

    Explanation

    123

    The driver had already taken off his ceremonial attire and said, "The bald one has a thief's appearance. I fear he might steal my clothes, so they must be carefully hidden." He then crouched and hugged the back pillar of the great hall, rising more than a foot, causing the rafters to vibrate and the tiles to ring out. With his left foot, he swept the base over, placing the clothes underneath, and then with his right foot he straightened it and reinstalled the pillar. Turning around, he shouted in combat, his voice like a thunderclap. The chief monk and the other monks all trembled and prostrated themselves, saying, "Our teachings' guardian, the Vajrapani Bodhisattva, has the power to turn heaven and earth, but not to this extent. The monks, mere mortal beings, dare not challenge. If you, esteemed guest, were to swing your arm once, we would all turn to dust. We gladly submit and dare not speak of our skills again." The king and the driver both laughed, and the monks held their breath and waited respectfully. They escorted him to the abbot's quarters and served tea. The king left, and the chief monk and the other monks changed into their ritual robes and held banners to see him off. When the king returned to his boat, he said to the driver, "The strength of a valiant man is unmatched in the world. Why not join the army and become a soldier? Then you would quickly achieve a distinguished position. Please accept this support, to repay your great virtue." The driver replied, "Ha! If I don't achieve a distinguished position, I can still live comfortably, why should I engage in a lowly profession? Do you still want me to pursue a distinguished position?" The king asked about his previous position and hometown and surname, but the driver did not answer. The king respectfully offered a hundred gold pieces, but the driver also refused. When he returned with the medicine, he told people about it and expressed his gratitude.

    124

    张慧仙寄外诗记
    　　
    　　蕙仙，张氏女，名妊，保阳郡人。其父纫工也，母氏胡，困乏嗣，祷于子母宫，觊太君侧垂髫侍女娟好，戏谓妯娌曰：“得女如是，纵不得男亦足矣。”妯娌争以赤绳系土偶臂而祝曰：“而母欲之，盍随以归乎？”是夕，胡母感而孕，弥月妊生，眉目清秀，聪慧过常。妯娌皆贺曰：“仙女来矣，后日不知谁幸而作刘郎也。”有滇南冯进士者，作宰畿南，耄而致仕，偶至纫肆中，见女，不觉潸潸泣下。其父讶曰：“何以触使君怒？”冯曰：“不，不，老夫有女某，其丰材与而女埒，为老夫所钟爱。不幸夭逝，隐痛于心。

    Explanation

    124

    The Poems of Zhang Huixian Sent to Her Husband**

    Hui Xian, a daughter of the Zhang family, named Ren, was from Bao Yang County. Her father was a sewing worker, and her mother, surnamed Hu, was childless. They prayed at the Temple of Fertility, hoping that a young girl serving by the respected lady would be beautiful. Playfully, they told their sisters-in-law, "If we get a daughter like this, even if we don't have a son, it is still enough." The sisters-in-law competed to tie a red thread to the arm of a clay figure and prayed, "Your mother wants it, why not follow and return?" That night, Hu's mother was moved and became pregnant. After a full month, Ren was born with clear and beautiful features and intelligence beyond the norm. The sisters-in-law all congratulated, saying, "An immortal fairy has come, but it is not known who will be fortunate enough to become Liu Lang in the future." There was a Jinshi named Feng from the south of Dian, who served as an official in the south of the capital and retired due to old age. Once, he happened to visit a sewing shop and saw the girl, and tears streamed down his face. Her father was surprised and asked, "Why did you offend the official?" Feng replied, "No, no, I have a daughter, whose excellent qualities are equal to your daughter, and she is deeply cherished by me. Unfortunately, she died young, and I still carry hidden pain in my heart."

    125

    今睹而女，得不感恸耶？”问女年几何，曰：“十二岁矣。”问近习何事，曰：“学刺绣耳。”问识字不，曰：“愿学而无师资。”琅然对答，声若洞萧。冯叟大悦，问愿为老夫女不？嫣然不答，以目视父。冯叟曰：“老夫年届八旬，顾不足为尔父耶？”其父曰：“吾侪小人，何幸而与显者亲？恐若母不忍舍耳。”冯叟笑曰：“吾岂夺尔女，只谓老夫父，老夫当日至而肆教之读，以成其志，而慰老夫之寥寞耳。”父女喜诺，真是冯叟日至，拳拳训导，女慧甚，过目成诵。不二年，而通韵语，书习赵文敏《闲邪公家传》，居然挺秀。冯叟字之曰慧仙。

    Explanation

    125

    Seeing the girl, how could one not be moved to tears?" The elder asked the girl her age, and she replied, "Twelve years old." He then inquired what she had been learning recently, and she said, "I have been studying embroidery." When asked if she knew how to read, she answered, "I wish to learn but have no teacher." She responded clearly and distinctly, her voice as pure as a jade flute. The elder, Feng, was greatly pleased and asked if she would like to be his daughter. With a smile, she did not answer but looked at her father. Feng said, "I am nearly eighty years old, but am I not sufficient to be your father?" Her father replied, "We are common folk, how fortunate to be related to a distinguished person? I fear your mother may not be willing to let you go." Feng laughed and said, "I am not taking your daughter away; I merely consider myself as a father to her. I will come daily to teach her to read and help her achieve her aspirations, and thus alleviate my loneliness." The father and daughter happily agreed. Indeed, Feng came every day, earnestly instructing her. The girl was very intelligent and could recite after a single reading. Within two years, she mastered poetry and studied the Jian Xie Gong Jia Zhuan by Zhao Wenshen, becoming surprisingly elegant. Feng named her Hui Xian.

    126

    越三载，女年十五，秀若幽兰，婉同春柳，直可侍莲台舞掌上矣。问字者踵相接，其父母皆无所许可。媒者曰：“富商大贾能掣带两老，一生衣食不尽者尚不之许，直欲归王孙公子耶？宁不思门第悬殊，难相匹偶，恐物以类骤，仍作纫工妇，悔之晚矣。”母曰：“咄，吾女纵无夫人相，必为才士妻，岂可配痴呆汉，使失所天耶？”此声一播，于是游士薄宦皆图之。时有北河刺史天水明府，以重资购媵，小人图中饱者引二宦瞷之，皆相见恨晚。刺史先以千金，使强有力者要其父，父心动，成有日矣，冯叟闻乞，怒谓其父曰，“若欲售而女耶？何为以弱息俾老奴，使茹恨终身？老夫且不忍，亲生者忍诸？直非人类矣。”即以告女，窥其志向。

    Explanation

    126

    After three years, the girl was fifteen years old, graceful as a hidden orchid, tender as a spring willow, and so elegant that she could dance on the lotus platform held in the palm of one's hand. Those seeking instruction came one after another, but her parents refused all proposals. A matchmaker said, "Even wealthy merchants who could support her parents for life would not be acceptable, let alone a prince or a nobleman? Do you not consider the vast difference in social status, making it difficult to match them? Fear that if things happen too quickly, she might end up as a seamstress's wife, and it will be too late to regret it." Her mother replied, "Hurry, my daughter may not have the appearance of a lady, but she will surely be the wife of a talented scholar. How can she be matched with a dullard, causing her to lose her rightful place?" Once this news spread, all wandering scholars and minor officials sought her. At that time, the governor of Beihé, Tian Shui, offered a large sum of money to purchase her as a concubine. Those who sought to profit from this introduced two officials to her, and they all regretted not meeting her sooner. The governor first offered a thousand gold pieces and had a strong man persuade her father. Her father was moved, and the arrangement was nearly complete. Old Feng heard and angrily told her father, "Do you want to sell your daughter? Why do you give her to an old servant, causing her to bear resentment for life? Even I cannot bear it, how can you, her parent, bear it? You are truly not human." He immediately told her, observing her aspirations.

    127

    女闻之则大啼不食数日，作绝命词曰：“兰有芳而被折兮，象有齿而焚身。予小草之不若兮，何见厄于紫宸？”父母大恐，面誓而谢绝之，使女自为计。遂深藏斗室，雾鬓云鬟，人皆不得轻见矣。
    　　
    　　先是有延陵生者，衿怀倜傥，学究天人，兼悉康熙朝掌故。游幕燕南，因觅纫时，至女父肆，遇冯叟，纵谈子史百家，旁及天文星数之学，娓娓千万言，听者忘倦。冯叟心倾，每啧啧称叹曰：“不世才也，终当奋翮云霄，岂池中物哉？”时女年十三四，支颐听讲，至会心处笑靥频开，凝眸属目，生虽好之，无容心焉。及宦室既不许婚，游士虽欲委禽，父母又不敢主，悉与女谋。女皆却之。延陵生之戚，虑生壮年无子，私与女父谋。

    Explanation

    127
    Source Text Translation Notes
    女闻之则大啼不食数日，作绝命词曰：“兰有芳而被折兮，象有齿而焚身。予小草之不若兮，何见厄于紫宸？”父母大恐，面誓而谢绝之，使女自为计。遂深藏斗室，雾鬓云鬟，人皆不得轻见矣。 Upon hearing the news, she wept greatly and did not eat for several days. She wrote a final poem saying, "Orchids have fragrance but are cut down, elephants have tusks but are burned. I am less than a blade of grass, why am I persecuted by the imperial court?" Her parents were greatly alarmed, made a face-to-face vow, and rejected the proposal, leaving her to make her own decision. She then secluded herself in a small room, her hair like mist and clouds, and no one could see her lightly. The translation aims to capture the emotional depth and the poetic nature of the original text. The phrase "面誓而谢绝之" is rendered as "made a face-to-face vow and rejected the proposal" to convey the formal and public nature of the rejection.
    先是有延陵生者，衿怀倜傥，学究天人，兼悉康熙朝掌故。游幕燕南，因觅纫时，至女父肆，遇冯叟，纵谈子史百家，旁及天文星数之学，娓娓千万言，听者忘倦。冯叟心倾，每啧啧称叹曰：“不世才也，终当奋翮云霄，岂池中物哉？”时女年十三四，支颐听讲，至会心处笑靥频开，凝眸属目，生虽好之，无容心焉。及宦室既不许婚，游士虽欲委禽，父母又不敢主，悉与女谋。女皆却之。延陵生之戚，虑生壮年无子，私与女父谋。 There was once a man from Yanling, who had a broad and unrestrained mind, extensive knowledge that penetrated heaven and man, and a deep understanding of the historical anecdotes of the Kangxi era. He traveled to the south of Yan in search of a wife, and came to the shop of the girl's father, where he met an old man named Feng. They engaged in a lengthy discussion of various historical and literary topics, as well as the study of astronomy and divination, speaking for countless words, and the listeners were never tired. Old Man Feng was deeply impressed, often exclaiming, "A talent of a different kind! He will surely soar to the heavens, not a mere pond-dwelling creature!" At that time, the girl was around thirteen or fourteen years old. She leaned her chin on her hand while listening, and at the point of understanding, her smile often appeared. She fixed her gaze on him, but although he liked her, he did not have any romantic thoughts. When the official family refused the marriage, the traveling scholar wanted to propose, but the girl's parents were also afraid to take the initiative. They all consulted with the girl, and she rejected them all. A relative of the Yanling man, concerned that he would be childless in his old age, secretly plotted with the girl's father. The translation aims to maintain the narrative flow and the detailed descriptions of the characters and their interactions. The phrase "面誓而谢绝之" is rendered as "made a face-to-face vow and rejected the proposal" to convey the formal and public nature of the rejection. The poetic and metaphorical expressions are preserved to maintain the original tone and style.
    128

    父知生才，有允意，恐女未必谐，归而佯述之，女首肯者再。父喜出意外，以告戚。戚以语生，生搔首曰：“予无金屋，奚以藏娇？”戚曰：“是宦室名门，富商大贾，求之不得者。今女幕先生才，已首肯矣，奈何却之，致负知己？”生曰：“果尔，试以翡翠双条脱为聘，其父母愿则从之，不则置之。予无长物，亦不忍贷之也。”戚复往，既而持庚柬报允，一无所求。生跃然喜曰：“予自谓穷措大，不足人齿数，竟为彼美垂青，咄咄奇遇，即登金马玉堂不是过矣。”时虽纳聘，而无资以娶，会颖川使君约生幕游韩魏间，与女音问间隔。

    Explanation

    128

    The father, recognizing the son's talent, had a favorable opinion of the marriage proposal. However, he feared that the daughter might not agree, so he returned home and pretended to recount the proposal to her. The daughter nodded in agreement twice. The father was pleasantly surprised and informed his relatives. The relatives then told the son, who scratched his head and said, "I have no golden house, what can I use to shelter my beauty?" The relatives replied, "This is a prestigious official family, a household of wealthy merchants and traders, those who cannot be obtained. Now that the daughter admires your talent and has already agreed, why reject it, to disappoint a good friend?" The son said, "If so, let me try using a pair of jade bracelets as a dowry. If her parents agree, then follow it; if not, then leave it. I have no valuable possessions, and I also cannot borrow them." The relatives went again and, afterward, held the birth date and name card to report acceptance, without any demands. The son jumped with joy and said, "I consider myself a poor scholar, not worthy to be counted among people. Unexpectedly, the beautiful woman favors me, a surprising and wonderful encounter, to enter the imperial court is not too much to ask." Although the dowry was accepted, he lacked the money to marry. Coincidentally, the governor of Yingchuan invited the son to join his entourage and travel between Han and Wei, which interrupted his communication with the woman.

    129

    而嫉忌者，欲夺女志，贿其党扬言于其父母曰：“延陵生者，年逾强仕，家有悍妻劣子，是以浪迹江河，携而女归，必为鱼肉，且遗侧室之羞，不如某某者，貌潘安而富石崇，为贵游上客，真快婿焉。奈何不与众议而与儿女子谋，贻误岂浅鲜哉！今幸聘而未娶，尚可改图。”其父母惑，将悔之。女微闻，复饮泣不食，病几殆。父母惧，委婉探之，女曰：“儿已受聘，可再议耶？使谣言实，儿之命也，不实，亦儿之命也。莫受他人诬。必欲悔之，儿誓终身不嫁。”父母知女志不可摇，遂息异议。

    Explanation

    129

    Envious individuals, wishing to undermine the girl's resolve, bribed her associates to spread rumors to her parents, saying, "Yanling is over forty years old, has a domineering wife and unruly children, and therefore roams the rivers and streams. If he takes her daughter with him, she will be at the mercy of others and will bring shame to a secondary wife. He is not as good as someone named [name], who looks like Pan An and is as wealthy as Shi Chong, a guest of the elite and influential, truly a suitable son-in-law. Why not consult with others instead of just the children? The mistake is not trivial! Now that he has been betrothed but not yet married, it is still possible to change the plan." Her parents were confused and were about to regret their decision. The girl, hearing this, cried again and refused to eat, becoming almost ill. Her parents, fearing for her, gently inquired. The girl said, "I have already been betrothed. Can we reconsider? If the rumors are true, it is my fate. If they are not true, it is still my fate. Do not let others slander me. If you must regret it, I swear to never marry again." The parents knew that the girl's resolve was unshakable and therefore stopped any objections.

    130

    逾年，生在乐邑，密迩保阳，遣媒纳征，彩舆继至，冠盖郊迎，供帐纷华，陈设眩耀。其父母大悦，谓女曰：“婿固不负吾家，儿之目力远出我二人万万矣。”女赧然曰：“此不过婿之小遇，尚不足以展其大才也。快心处请俟异日。”定情之夕，女谓生曰：“闻郎有妻子，果不？”生曰：“妻则有之，子犹未也。”女曰：“然则迎以比来，妾愿以嫡礼侍之。郎若以妾故而舍，是妾负心焉。”生泣然曰：“予妻身抱沉疴，不能生育。为姒续计，故娶卿耳。岂负妻哉？”请即发使，比至而正妻已逝。生哭之恸，女百计慰解，自是情好弥笃，所至必偕。南历恒山，东游孤竹，徜徉千里间已四年矣。

    Explanation

    130

    After a year, the protagonist was in Le County, close to Baoyang. The groom's family sent a matchmaker to propose, and a decorated carriage arrived, followed by a formal reception in the suburbs with elaborate and luxurious arrangements, displayed in dazzling splendor. The parents were very pleased and said to the daughter, "Your husband certainly does not辜负我们的家，你的视力远远超过我们两人。”女儿害羞地说：“这不过是丈夫的一点小经历，还不足以展示他的大才能。最令人满意的地方请留待将来。”在定情的那天晚上，女儿对他说：“听说你有妻子，真的吗？”他说：“有妻子，但还没有孩子。”她说：“那么，让我们一起把她带过来，我愿意以正式的礼仪侍奉她。如果你因为我的缘故而舍弃她，那就是我负心了。”他说：“我的妻子身患重病，不能生育。为了延续她的家族，所以我娶了你。难道我背叛了我的妻子吗？”他请求立即派遣使者，等使者到达时，正妻已经去世。他痛哭流涕，女儿百般安慰。从此，他们的情感更加深厚，无论去哪里都形影不离。他们南下经过恒山，东游孤竹，四处游历，已经四年了。

    131

    会生有口北之行，关山险阻，不能同车。女作闺词七章寄之，其词曰：“一、闻郎指日出边关，强作欢颜泪暗弹。叮嘱再三无别语，频缄锦字报平安。二、日送兰轺过短亭，妾心共遂马蹄征。揣摹飞渡关山处，环绕深闺得得行。三、遣愁女伴画相过，长夜漫漫可奈何。欲使斜晖永不昧，倩谁借得鲁阳戈？四、吹罢银缸赴黑甜，只期飞梦到郎边。恼人偏是中天月，半榻清辉映独眠。五、闻说风高塞外天，绵衣制就又添绵。阿娘莫笑缡褷甚，郎识痴心总爱穿。六、家书一纸达妆台，喜极翻将清泪催。只当与郎同絮语，沉吟反复百千回。七、忽报郎君返故乡，忙开镜匣理新妆。岂知小婢传言误，依旧和衣卧绣床。”

    Explanation

    131

    A Man Named Hui Sheng Was Sent on a Mission to the Northern Frontier, Where the Passes Were Dangerous and the Journey Difficult, and They Could Not Travel Together. His Wife Wrote Seven Chapters of a Gūcí (闺词) to Send to Him, and the Poem Went as Follows:

    1. I heard that my husband would soon leave the border.
      Forcing a smile, I silently shed tears.
      After repeatedly giving him instructions, I had nothing more to say.
      I frequently wrote letters to report his safety.

    2. Every day, I sent him off in a carriage, passing by the short pavilion.
      My heart went along with the journey of his horse.
      I imagined him flying over the passes and mountains,
      Walking slowly around my deep inner chamber.

    3. I asked my female companion to draw a picture of me to relieve my sorrow.
      The long, endless night, what can be done?
      I wished that the slanting light would never fade,
      Who could borrow the legendary spear of Luyang to stop time?

    4. After blowing out the silver lamp, I went into deep sleep.
      Only hoping that my flying dream would take me to my husband's side.
      Annoyingly, it was the full moon in the sky,
      Half of my bed was bathed in clear light, shining on my lonely sleep.

    5. I heard that the sky beyond the frontier was high with strong winds.
      I made warm woolen clothes for him and added more wool.
      My mother, do not laugh at my carefulness,
      My husband knows my foolish heart and will always love to wear them.

    6. A family letter arrived at my dressing table.
      Overjoyed, I shed tears.
      I thought it was as if I was chatting with my husband,
      Pondering and repeating a thousand times.

    7. Suddenly, I heard that my husband was returning to his hometown.
      I quickly opened the mirror box and prepared new makeup.
      How could I know that the young maid had misreported the message?
      I still lay on the embroidered bed with clothes on.

    132

    白安人
    　　
    　　钟俊，浙人。幼业儒，父母早故，孑然一身，教读以糊口，亲戚故旧皆远之。年二十余，获一芹，戚友稍礼之，然无与婚媾者。鳏居下帷，刻志勤学，未几登贤书，捷南宫，入庶常，乡党中争欲联姻。俊谢曰：“予贫犹昔，宦亦未成，何以家为？”时有山右富室官侍卫者白姓，有女及笄，因乏嗣，欲赘清华之士为婿。彼乡人以其武职，虑女有赳赳风，莫愿结婚者。俊于同年席同遇白，谈相洽，询及未婚故，白即嘱同年为媒。俊知其丰于财，欣然许诺，遂赘也。女貌婉丽，夫妇相得甚欢。旋散馆，俊铨得南都宰，将至任，白因爱女远离，盛备奁具，媵以婢仆百余，雇群艘由水路行。运奁之日，自京至通四十余里，络绎不绝于道者，翌日始毕。白送婿女至河干，珍重而别。于是宵小觊觎，群谋肆劫，但知其多侍卫家丁，恐有能者，不敢遽发。以小舟追随而下。及官舫晚泊处，俱在通都大邑，无可下手。然一路知风，羽党日伙矣。

    Explanation

    132

    White Madam

    Zong Jun was a native of Zhejiang. Young and engaged in Confucian studies, his parents died early, leaving him alone. He earned a living by teaching and was shunned by relatives and old acquaintances. In his twenties, he achieved a minor success, and his relatives and friends began to treat him with a bit more respect, but he had no prospects for marriage. Living alone and closing the curtain, he was diligent and studious, and soon he was listed in the register of scholars, excelled in the Southern Palace exams, and entered the ranks of the imperial scholars. In his hometown, people vied to form alliances through marriage with him. Zong Jun declined, saying, "I am still as poor as before, and my official career is not yet established. What need is there for a family?" At that time, there was a wealthy family in Shanxi whose head was a military officer named Bai. They had a daughter who had reached the age of marriage, but due to a lack of heirs, they wanted to marry a scholar of high repute. The locals, fearing that the daughter would have a strong and bold demeanor due to her father's military background, were unwilling to arrange the marriage. Zong Jun met Bai at the same examination session, and they had a harmonious conversation. When Bai inquired about Zong Jun's lack of marriage, Zong Jun explained. Bai then asked his fellow examinee to act as a matchmaker. Zong Jun, knowing that the Bai family was rich, gladly agreed and became a son-in-law. The daughter was graceful and beautiful, and the couple got along very well. Shortly after, Zong Jun was assigned to a government post and was appointed as the governor of the southern capital. As he was about to take up his post, Bai, because his beloved daughter was leaving, prepared a lavish dowry and accompanied it with over a hundred servants and maids, hiring a fleet of boats to travel by water. On the day of the dowry's journey, from the capital to Tong, over forty miles, there was a continuous stream of people, and it took until the next day to complete. Bai saw his son-in-law and daughter off at the riverbank, parting with respect and care. Thus, the small people coveted, and they planned and carried out a robbery, but knowing there were many guards from the military officer's household, they were afraid of encountering someone capable and dared not act immediately. They followed with small boats. By the time the official boat docked in the evening, it was in a large and prosperous city, and they had no place to act. However, along the way, they learned of the situation, and the number of accomplices increased day by day.

    133

    夏初，舟抵维扬。是夜，月朗风清，江波恬静，俊与白安人舟中对弈，思乘月色启行，饬仆叱舟子连樯而进。盗闻之大喜，投其魁伪镇江王者，挥百余艇，胡哨而来。舟子闻啃声，咸呼曰：“巨寇至矣，奈何？”诸仆骇告主人，俊不胜战栗，泣曰：“举家休矣。”安人微哂曰：“小丑何敢跳梁，婢子足以退之。郎何惧为？”遂命仆令舟子停帆，将群艘一贯锁连，官舫在中，灯火俱息，男子均伏匿不动。安人呼婢十余人来前，皆已易短装，黑衣黑裤，望之如墨。各与棋子一握，密嘱数语曰，“速退小偷，毋俾登舟，若惊官人，惟汝等罪。”婢领命而去。安人遂自起，易乌缎袄裤，以青绫蒙首，挂铁丸囊，俊视之，目立眉扬，英武之概，另具风流，非复平时娇弱矣。俊曰，“夫人将何之？”安人曰：“御盗耳，郎如不畏，偕往观之。”俊见指挥自如，胆稍壮，遂携手伏舱门内，静无喘息。

    Explanation

    133

    At the beginning of summer, the boat arrived at Yangzhou. That night, the moon was bright, the wind was clear, and the river waves were calm. Jun and Lady Bai played chess on the boat, thinking to take advantage of the moonlight to set sail. They ordered the servants to urge the boatmen to move the boats forward in a line. The bandits heard and were greatly pleased, sending their leader, pretending to be the Duke of Zhenjiang. They waved over a hundred boats and came shouting. The boatmen heard the noise and all cried out, "Great bandits are coming, what shall we do?" The servants were terrified and reported to the master. Jun could not bear the trembling and wept, saying, "Our whole family is finished." Lady Bai smiled slightly and said, "How dare these small rascals be so bold? My maids are enough to drive them away. Why should you be afraid?" She then ordered the servants to tell the boatmen to stop the sails, to chain all the boats together in a line, to have the official boat in the middle, to extinguish all the lights, and for the men to hide and remain still. She called ten or more maids forward, all of whom had changed into short clothes, black dresses, and black pants, looking like ink. She gave each maid a handful of chess pieces and whispered a few words, saying, "Quickly drive away the thieves, do not let them board the boat, if you frighten the master, it will be your fault." The maids took their orders and left. Lady Bai then got up herself, changed into a black satin jacket and pants, covered her head with green silk, and hung an iron ball bag. Jun looked at her, his eyes and eyebrows raised, admiring her heroic bearing, which was different from her usual gentle demeanor. Jun said, "Where is Lady Bai going?" Lady Bai said, "To fight the bandits, if you are not afraid, come with me to see." Jun saw her command with ease, his courage slightly strengthened, and they held hands and crouched inside the cabin door, silent and breathless.

    134

    未几，哨声逼近，盗舟合围焉。彼见巨艘联络，若索战然，而无一人在外，怀疑未决。相持既久，觉无他异，乃命酋长挖钩持刀跃上，未及登舷，皆被飞子中要害，堕江而毙，已数十人。贼帅怒，自携大盾挟白刃飞登中舟，觉盾上炮子雨下，始知击者在上，于是蒙首俯身，才欲入舱，安人手发铁丸，中盗顶，扑跌入江。众呼曰：“大王殆矣！”皆溷水抢护而遁。群婢咸集，安人命举火厚赏之，叱舟子放舟前进，俊大悦，揖而请曰：“夫人是何神术，治大盗若小儿然？诚乃将门之豪杰也。”安人曰：“无他，妾父好马射，家有闲圃一顷，翼以长垣，中具马道箭厅，恒以妾置厅事观艺。妾时尚幼，戏以石子击射鹄，父笑曰：‘儿欲为此，吾以银为的，能下者予之。’妾乐此不疲，与群婢日击，至二三年，百发百中。

    Explanation

    134

    Not long after, the sound of scouts grew closer, and the bandit boats encircled them. He saw the large ships connected together, as if eager for battle, yet no one was visible on deck, and he hesitated in doubt. After a prolonged standoff, he realized there was nothing unusual, and so he ordered the chieftain to grab grappling hooks, wield a blade, and leap aboard. Before they could reach the ship's side, all were struck in vital areas by flying projectiles, fell into the river, and died—already dozens had perished. The bandit leader, furious, carried a large shield and a white blade, and leapt aboard the central boat. When he felt cannonballs raining down upon his shield, he finally realized the attackers were above. Thus, he covered his head and crouched low, just as he was about to enter the cabin, when Lady An shot iron balls from her hand, striking the bandit in the head. He crashed and fell into the river. The crowd cried out, "The great king is in peril!" All rushed in muddy water to protect him and fled in disorder. The maids gathered together, and Lady An ordered the fire to be lit and gave them generous rewards. She commanded the boatman to set sail forward. Jun was greatly pleased, bowed, and asked, "What divine skill is this, my lady, that you treat great bandits like children? Indeed, you are a heroine of a general's family." Lady An replied, "There is no other reason—my father loved horse-riding and archery. At home, there was a leisure garden of one qing, enclosed by high walls, with horse paths and archery halls inside. They always placed me in the hall to watch the art. When I was still young, I played by throwing stones to hit the target. My father laughed and said, 'If you wish to do this, I will make silver targets; whoever hits them will receive them.' I found this never-ending pleasure, and with the maids, I practiced daily. After two or three years, I hit the target every time."

    135

    父又以纸作人，画周身穴道，命击某穴，亦无不中。继又以牛革为人，画穴于背，命以铁丸穿之，习四五年，则所发皆洞。父笑曰：‘女将军天下无敌矣，’故侍婢皆知款窍。如是而已，无他长也。”俊曰：“棋子微物，何能杀人？”安人曰：“熟是法者，粒米可以杀人，况棋子乎？彼所击者，非穿太阳穴则取二目，子已入脑，能不立毙乎？”俊曰：“既闻命矣，然婢伏何所，而贼不觉？”安人曰：“婢猱踞桅巅，俯视明而用力远，故无不中其要害。且黑衣与木色等，夜色朦胧，故盗不觉也。”俊曰：“夫人伏舱内，何预知贼首之必登中舟也？”安人曰：“中舟较群舟大而有仪仗，群盗四面受亏，必料中舟为主，贼首必欲破之。既登，即知击者在上，不意妾在下也，故幸中之。”俊不胜叹服曰：“夫人真娘子军之巨帅也，智勇无双。仆何福而遇此？”

    Explanation

    135

    The father then made paper figures and drew acupoints all over their bodies, instructing to strike specific points, and every strike was accurate. Next, he used cowhide to create figures and marked acupoints on their backs, instructing to pierce them with iron pellets. After four or five years of training, every shot was precise. The father smiled and said, "You are invincible, a female general!" Thus, the maidservants all knew the techniques. It was just like that, with no other notable skills. Jun asked, "Chess pieces are small objects; how can they kill someone?" The lady replied, "Those who master this technique can kill with a grain of rice, let alone a chess piece? The strikes target either the solar plexus or the eyes; once the piece enters the brain, the target cannot survive." Jun asked, "Having heard your instructions, where did the maidservant hide, and why were the bandits unaware?" The lady said, "The maidservant clung to the mast like a monkey, looking down from a high vantage point, using her strength from a distance, so every strike hit the vital points. Moreover, her black clothing blended with the wood color, and in the dim night, the bandits were unaware." Jun asked, "How did the lady know that the bandit leader would definitely board the middle boat?" The lady said, "The middle boat was larger than the others and had an entourage; the bandits suffered losses from all sides, so they must have thought the middle boat was the leader's. Once they boarded, they knew the attacker was above, but they did not expect me to be below, so I succeeded in my plan." Jun could not help but admire and said, "The lady is truly the supreme commander of the female army, unmatched in wisdom and courage. What fortune I have to meet such a person!"

    136

    及抵官，六载任满，调首邑。时有军功参戎许杰来镇南都，与俊投合，约为昆季，时相过从。盛夏，解衣而不释便帽，俊窃疑之，及俊得保荐将入觐，杰具席饯行，饮已酣，俊问曰：“大兄当暑不去冠，何也？”杰曰：“既为昆季，事可无讳。予大江巨盗也，因劫官舫，被铁丸击顶去片骨，几殆。幸寇中有儿脑丹治痊，然不可以风，故不释冠。”俊曰：“以兄男略冠三军，何见伤于官舫？”杰笑曰：“此予之恩人也，曾访舫中，能者系少妇，予喟然自失，去盗不为，投边营入伍，屡上首功，五六载进膺是取，皆由一击玉成之。此妇非予之恩人耶？”俊曰：“知恩人何在？”杰曰：“闺阁中人，何从物识？”俊笑曰：“兄匆罪，是弟妇也。”杰骇然起，具衣冠请谒。俊命仆传请安人出。杰见其弱质盈盈，不觉再拜稽首曰：“此天人也，天命诲予矣。”

    Explanation

    136

    Upon arriving at his post, Jun completed six years of service and was transferred to the capital county. At that time, Xu Jie, a military commander with notable military achievements, came to govern the southern capital. He and Jun got along well, and they made a pact to be as brothers, frequently visiting each other. During the height of summer, Xu removed his robe but did not take off his practical hat, which caused Jun to secretly wonder. When Jun was recommended for an imperial audience and prepared to depart, Xu prepared a banquet to see him off. After the drinking had reached its peak, Jun asked, “Brother, why do you not remove your hat even in the heat of summer?” Xu replied, “Since we are like brothers, I may speak without reserve. I was once a great bandit on the Yangtze River. I attacked an official boat and was struck in the head by an iron pellet, which shattered part of my bone, nearly killing me. Fortunately, a boy in the band had a remedy called naodan that cured me, but now I cannot be exposed to the wind, so I do not remove my hat.” Jun said, “Given that you have surpassed the entire army, how could you be injured on an official boat?” Xu laughed and said, “This woman is my benefactor. I once visited the boat and saw a beautiful young woman, and I suddenly felt ashamed of myself. I gave up banditry and joined the frontier garrison, where I frequently claimed major victories. After five or six years, I was promoted and received these honors—all made possible by that one strike. Is this woman not my benefactor?” Jun asked, “Do you know where your benefactor is?” Xu replied, “She is a woman of the inner quarters—how could I recognize her?” Jun smiled and said, “Brother, please do not blame me—it is my wife.” Xu was startled and immediately stood up, donning his formal attire to pay respects. Jun ordered his servant to summon his wife. When Xu saw her delicate and graceful form, he could not help but bow twice in deep reverence and said, “This is a celestial being—Heaven itself has instructed me.”

    137

    凛然而退。
    　　
    　　齐叫化
    　　
    　　齐丐，不知何许人。江浙谓丐为叫化，因以为名。其为人似颠狂而实好义。恒乞食，有余，则与同丐中之病不能行者，故群丐皆推尊之。日者齐食于丧家，果腹而回，酣睡邮亭，悠悠自得。忽来一丐，蹴之起曰：“汝非齐叫化乎？”齐张目熟视，索不识者，曰：“呼我何为？”来丐曰：“吾向知汝好义，今有一技传汝，愿学乎？”齐曰：“愿。”来丐出红丸曰：“食此，能知人病之所在。”齐立吞之，又授之针，长尺许，曰：“既知病源，以此刺之，无不立愈。”齐恍然大悟，踊跃狂笑，而来丐已逝。遂游行乡市，遇跛者，针之即平。遇瞽者，针之即明。遇因风而哑者，针之即能言。于是齐叫化之名噪传一时，所到之处，病人环伺之。然齐必择人而施，其贫者不必求而治之，其富者必婪索满意而后投针。日得数金，呼同丐遍散之，不藏分毫。

    Explanation

    137

    He withdrew abruptly.

    Qi Jiaohua

    Qi the Beggar was a man of unknown origin. In Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces, beggars are called "Jiaohua," hence his nickname. He appeared mad but was actually very righteous. He constantly begged for food, and when he had extra, he shared it with other beggars who were ill and unable to walk, earning him the respect of all the beggars. One day, Qi ate at a mourning household, returned full, and slept soundly in a post station, feeling content and at ease. Suddenly, another beggar kicked him awake and said, "Are you not Qi Jiaohua?" Qi opened his eyes and looked at him carefully, not recognizing him, and asked, "Why are you calling me?" The other beggar said, "I have known of your righteousness. Now I have a skill to pass on to you. Would you like to learn it?" Qi replied, "I would." The other beggar took out a red pill and said, "Eat this, and you will be able to diagnose the source of a person's illness." Qi immediately swallowed it, and the beggar then gave him a needle about a foot long, saying, "Once you know the source of the illness, use this to treat them, and they will be cured instantly." Qi suddenly understood and jumped up laughing, but by then, the other beggar had vanished. Qi then wandered through the villages and markets, treating lame people with the needle, who were immediately healed. He treated blind people, who regained their sight, and mute people who had lost their voice due to illness, who could speak again. Thus, the name Qi Jiaohua became widely known for a time, and wherever he went, sick people gathered around him. However, Qi was selective in his treatment; he did not treat poor people who did not ask for help, but he would only treat wealthy people after they satisfied his demands. He earned several coins a day, which he called out to the other beggars and distributed among them, not keeping a single coin for himself.

    138

    有典商某，因风致疾，右臂已废，百药莫效。闻齐名，使人邀至，示之臂，齐曰：“易耳，必予我百金则立能复旧。不则半握而已。”商不得已，与之。齐针其肩成巨孔，曰：“不可封穴，俾风从此出。”拔针而臂即运动。自此逢节举发，则风从穴出，飒飒有声，而终身不复病矣。齐得金，济生葬死，数日立尽。又与群丐游于村落，见殡者四人，辇一白木棺，血涔涔下。齐熟视而呼曰：“若奈何杀活人于棺中耶？既露我等目，当为复仇。”挥丐群围之，棺不得行。辇者曰：“毋得卤莽，死者为予妇，因难产，胎上攻心，亡已周日矣。”齐曰：“必启棺予我观之。”辇者怒，正喧争间，有识者曰：“此齐叫化，良医也。汝盍启棺，伊必有说。”辇者大悦，以棺回家，出其尸致室中，齐以针刺心下，唤众出曰：“速命妇女伺之，将产矣。”众退媪入，哇然一声，子产而妇醒。齐大笑，不索一钱，掉臂竟去。

    Explanation

    138

    A merchant named Ding, due to exposure to wind, contracted an illness that rendered his right arm useless, and no medicine could cure it. Hearing of Qi's reputation, he sent someone to invite him, showing him his arm. Qi said, "It's easy, but if you give me a hundred gold pieces, I can immediately restore it to its original state. Otherwise, only half a grasp will be possible." The merchant, having no choice, gave him the money. Qi made a large hole in his shoulder with a needle and said, "Do not seal the wound, let the wind come out from here." When he pulled out the needle, the arm immediately moved. From then on, every time a joint was flexed, the wind came out from the wound, making a rustling sound, and he was never sick again. Qi received the gold and used it to help the living and bury the dead, spending it all in a few days. He then went around villages with a group of beggars, where he saw four mourners carrying a white wooden coffin, with blood dripping down. Qi looked closely and shouted, "Why would you kill a living person in the coffin? Since you have exposed us, we will take revenge." He waved the beggars to surround them, and the coffin could not move. The carrier said, "Do not be rash, the dead woman was my wife, who died due to difficult childbirth, with the fetus pressing on her heart, and she died a week ago." Qi said, "You must open the coffin for me to see." The carrier was angry, and they were arguing. A knowledgeable person said, "This is Qi the beggar, a good doctor. Why don't you open the coffin? He will certainly have a reason." The carrier was very pleased and took the coffin home, taking out the body and placing it in the room. Qi inserted a needle into the chest and called everyone out, saying, "Quickly send women to watch over her, she is about to give birth." Everyone stepped back, and the old woman entered. A loud cry, the child was born, and the woman woke up. Qi laughed loudly, did not ask for a single coin, and left with a wave.

    139

    有金宦者，家佣少妇晕死于室，不知所以。家人佥曰：“齐叫化必能活之。”于是厚给丐者寻之至，视少妇，笑不可遏，曰：“伊夫何在？速呼来，尚可治。”宦家即唤其夫至，齐曰：“我活之，必重酬我。”宦者以巨金置几上曰：“若能立活，即持去。”齐又大笑，针少妇之腹，谓其夫曰：“我拔铁针出，汝以肉针入，即无恙，否则虽醒不久也。”其夫忸怩应之。遂去针攫金，推宦者出，反闭其夫曰：“莫管莫管我。”问，“宦何不谨于房事，而令少妇潜睹，致成欲闭耶？”宦者愧谢之，齐大笑去。或病家为父母故，衣冠设席，延为上宾，齐来大怒，不顾而去。问其党，佥曰：“欲得其欢心，须以矮几席地，丰以酒肉，俾独据而食之，则高歌喜笑矣。”试之果然。

    Explanation

    139

    A eunuch of the Jin family had a household servant who was a young woman who fainted in the room, and no one knew the cause. The family members all said, "Qí Jiàohuà must be able to revive her." So they generously provided for the beggar and sent him to see the young woman. He examined her and laughed uncontrollably, saying, "Where is her husband? Call him quickly, and she can still be treated." The eunuch immediately called her husband, and Qí said, "I will revive her, but I must be well rewarded." The eunuch placed a large sum of money on the table and said, "If you can revive her immediately, take the money and leave." Qí laughed again and inserted a needle into the young woman's abdomen, telling her husband, "I will remove the iron needle, and you insert a flesh needle; she will then be fine. Otherwise, she will not live long even if she wakes up." The husband, embarrassed, agreed. Qí then removed the needle, took the money, pushed the eunuch out, and locked the husband inside, saying, "Don't worry about me." He asked, "Why didn't the eunuch be cautious in his private affairs, allowing the young woman to witness it, leading to this state of desire and closure?" The eunuch felt ashamed and thanked him. Qí laughed and left. Sometimes, a sick person's family, out of filial piety, would dress formally, set up a table, and invite him as a guest of honor. When Qí came, he became furious and left without regard. When asked about it, his associates said, "To please him, you must use a low table on the ground, provide abundant wine and meat, allow him to sit alone and eat, and he will sing and laugh joyfully." They tried it, and it worked as expected.

    140

    时有与叫化同姓者，少年业儒，娶妇淫甚，瞥遇所欢，忍之不可，去之不能，徘徊门外，闷极而倒。齐适过之，为针其胸，吐痰升余而苏，对齐痛哭，齐扣其故，悉告之。齐大笑曰：“易耳。汝呼妇出，我为治之。”少年唤妇，齐突前以针刺小腹，妇大惊，反奔入室，即病。病已，人道遂闭，但能溺而已。少年感甚，舍家觅齐，得之，拜谒于地，愿投膝下，以从其业。齐狂笑曰：“我无妻，那得有子？然我无子，继我业者，即我子也。汝能叫化乎？”少年曰：“子何敢改父之道？”齐曰：“我无家，容我身者，即我家也。

    Explanation

    140

    At that time, there was a man with the same surname as the beggar. A young scholar, he had married a very promiscuous wife. One day, he accidentally encountered his beloved, unable to bear it and unable to leave, he lingered outside the door, became extremely depressed, and fainted. Qí happened to pass by, pierced his chest, and he vomited phlegm and a little more, then revived. He cried bitterly to Qí, who probed the reason. The young man told him everything. Qí laughed loudly and said, "It's easy. You call your wife out, and I will treat her." The young man called his wife, and Qí suddenly stepped forward and pierced her lower abdomen. The wife was greatly shocked and rushed back into the room, immediately falling ill. After she recovered, her sexual desire was closed off, and she could only urinate. The young man was deeply moved and left home to find Qí. When he found him, he knelt and paid respects, wishing to become his disciple and follow his profession. Qí laughed wildly and said, "I have no wife, how could I have a child? But since I have no child, whoever carries on my profession is my son. Can you beg?" The young man said, "How dare I change my father's way?" Qí said, "I have no home, wherever I am welcomed, that is my home."

    141

    汝能露宿乎？”少年曰：“儿因家累，何用家为？”齐大笑曰：“好，好。”以杖与筐授之，曰：“从我游，悔则杀汝。”少年敬诺，遂飘流三江两浙间，勤为服事，居然父子。阅数载，得其按穴针刺之法，齐曰：“儿业成矣，虽不若乃翁，已超于凡辈。可归而家，致富不难。”以针授曰：“报汝辛勤，箕裘勿替矣。我将有远行，儿柔弱不能追随，盍去诸。”少年不忍，涕泣请从。齐怒曰：“违我教，大不孝。”挥群丐驱之，齐狂笑而行，走如奔马，瞬息已杳，不知所终。少年归，其妇尚在室，已改行为良，家业不堕。见夫归，泣告之悔，少年寻其原穴反针之，人道复通，遂为夫妇如初。既而有子，因世业针医。
    　　
    　　芗厈曰：按古律有幽闭之法，以治荡妇。盖女人阴内有羞秘骨，下之如闸，即不通人道。齐丐所行，或其法也。但能闭不能启，若得齐丐之道，启闭自如，则游子咸得封志其妇，天下省无数因奸酿命案矣。

    Explanation

    141

    "Can you sleep outdoors?" the beggar asked. The youth replied, "Because of family burdens, what use is there for a family?" The beggar laughed heartily and said, "Good, good." He handed the youth a staff and a basket and said, "Follow me on my travels. If you regret it, I will kill you." The youth respectfully agreed, and they drifted through the Three Rivers and Two Zhe regions. He diligently served the beggar, and they became like father and son. After several years, he learned the art of acupuncture and pressure point therapy. The beggar said, "Your skill is complete. Though you may not match your father, you have surpassed ordinary people. You can return home and make wealth easily." He handed the youth the needles and said, "In return for your hard work, continue the family tradition. I am about to embark on a distant journey. You are too weak to follow, so why don't you go?" The youth was unwilling to part and begged to accompany him. The beggar became angry and said, "To disobey my teachings is great disrespect." He waved his fellow beggars to drive the youth away. The beggar laughed wildly and ran like a galloping horse, disappearing instantly, and no one knew where he went.

    The youth returned home. His wife was still in the house, and she had reformed her behavior, returning to being a virtuous woman. Their family business remained intact. When she saw her husband return, she wept and confessed her regret. The youth found her original pressure points and reversed the treatment, restoring her to normal. They became husband and wife once more. Soon after, they had a son, continuing the family tradition of acupuncture and medicine.

    The Xiang Yan said: According to ancient law, there was a practice called "youbi" (幽闭) to deal with unfaithful women. It is said that women have a "shoumi gu" (羞秘骨) in their bodies, which closes like a gate, blocking the path of intimacy. The beggar's method may be related to this practice. However, it can only close but not open. If one could master the beggar's technique, opening and closing at will, then all wanderers could ensure the fidelity of their wives, and countless cases of adultery leading to violence could be prevented.

    142

    八松墓
    　　
    　　岁在庚辰，余自沧州言旋保阳，道出河间。惊砂夕起，宿乌归林，将投旅舍，遥望荒烟古墓中，八松环绕，郁乎苍苍，宛若张盖，亦似游龙。余心羡之，喟然而叹，仆夫告余曰：此有明义士某公夫妇之冢也。当义士在生之日，开张旅舍，东西南北之人，投止者不知凡几。一日客去，扫榻于尘埃中，得布缚一囊，内盛白金五百。义士奉之入室，谨藏箱箧中。其妻亦莫之知。越一载，有客莅止，入室痛哭。义士骇而问之，客曰：“我某省之布商伙也，前岁将暮，仓卒之间，拥三千金驰回乡里，比及点交主人，则失其五百。商主怒，我无以对，破产偿之不足，质其妻孥与主，情愿效力五载，以补其号。主知我素不为非，怜而允之。当是时，茫然不知银之失于何所。今入此舍，不觉心动，感触而悲，非有他也。”义士曰：“客之囊有字记乎？”对曰：“鼎盛字号。

    Explanation

    142

    Eight Pines Tomb

    In the year of the Fire Dragon (庚辰年), I returned from Cangzhou to Baoyang, passing through Hejian. Sandstorms arose in the evening, and nocturnal birds returned to the forest. As I was about to seek lodging, I gazed afar at an ancient tomb shrouded in desolate smoke, surrounded by eight pines. Their dense, lush appearance was as if they formed a canopy, or like a wandering dragon. I admired them deeply and sighed, when my servant informed me, "This is the tomb of a righteous man and his wife from the Ming dynasty." During the man's lifetime, he operated an inn, welcoming travelers from all directions. One day, after a guest departed, he swept the bed in the dust and found a cloth-tied bag containing five hundred taels of silver. The man carefully stored it in his chest, and his wife was unaware of it. After one year, another guest arrived and entered the room, weeping bitterly. The man was startled and asked him why. The guest replied, "I am a partner in a cloth merchant's business. Last year, in a hurry, I carried three thousand taels of silver back to my hometown. When it came time to hand over to the master, I lost five hundred. The merchant master was angry, and I had no way to respond. Even bankruptcy would not be enough to repay, so I pledged my wife and children to the master and volunteered to serve for five years to make up for the loss. The master knew I had never done wrong, pitied me, and agreed. At that time, I was completely unaware of where the silver was lost. Now entering this inn, I unconsciously felt moved, and felt sad, for no other reason." The man asked, "Did your bag have any markings?" The guest replied, "The mark of the prosperous business."

    143

    ”曰：“银有数目乎？”对曰：“整宝十枚。”曰：“然则客毋悲也，原物在，请自验收。”乃以囊还客。客见囊金如故，不觉欢欣载拜，愿以半酬之。义士不许。感激涕零而去。未几偕其主来，愿识义士。曰，“我阅人多矣，从未有寒俭士而五百金不足以动其心者。我愿以兄事之，请言所欲。”义士笑曰：“老夫年已周甲，膝下无嗣，多金何为？纵彼苍怜而富，我且愿辞之，岂阁下能动我所欲乎？”商主曰：“我知之矣。”再拜而退，乃觅女子之温厚端庄者，以重价购得，载以赠义士曰：“不孝有三，无后为大。兄必从我言，纳此以绵善人之后，非敢言报也。”义士之妻亦谆谆劝留，不得已从之，连举丈夫子，七旬而有八男，且俱敏于诵读，先后登进士。翁年九十外，夫妇齐眉，犹及见八子之齐登科甲。及其终也，布客之子适过之，欲以表意，乃购异松植其墓，越三百余岁而益茂，且有神物护之，采樵者不敢近云。

    Explanation

    143

    The gentleman asked, "Is there a specific amount of silver?" The other replied, "Ten whole treasures." The gentleman then said, "Then there is no need for you to grieve; the original property is still here. Please inspect it yourself." He returned the bag to the guest. The guest saw the gold in the bag as before, and without realizing it, he was overjoyed and bowed repeatedly, wishing to repay half of it. The righteous man refused. The guest was so moved as to shed tears and left. Soon after, accompanied by his master, he returned, wishing to meet the righteous man. He said, "I have seen many people, and never have I encountered a poor and frugal man for whom five hundred taels of gold would not move his heart. I wish to treat you as a brother and ask what you desire." The righteous man smiled and said, "I am already sixty years old, with no children to carry on my family name. What use would more gold be to me? Even if Heaven were to pity me and make me wealthy, I would still wish to decline it. How could you possibly move what I desire?" The merchant said, "I understand." He bowed twice and left. Then he sought out a warm-hearted and dignified woman, bought her at a high price, and presented her to the righteous man, saying, "There are three forms of filial impiety, and the greatest is not to have descendants. You must follow my advice and accept her to continue the lineage of virtuous people, though I dare not speak of repayment." The wife of the righteous man also earnestly urged him to accept, and he could not refuse. As a result, he had eight sons, all of whom were quick to learn and read. They all passed the imperial examinations in succession. When the old man was over ninety, he and his wife lived in harmony, still witnessing their eight sons all achieving success in the imperial examinations. Upon his death, the son of the merchant happened to pass by and wished to express his feelings, so he bought a unique pine tree to plant at the righteous man's grave. After more than three hundred years, the tree grew even more luxuriant, and it was said to be guarded by a divine entity, so woodcutters dared not approach it.

    144

    义丐
    　　
    　　丐某，燕人也。孑然一身，游食市闾。饱则出其垔堵，在康衢古木之阴诩诩而睡。一日，有策马而驰者，颠播囊裂，落宝银二枚于道，丐呼之不觉，狂奔而去。丐乃拾之，自忖曰：“吾其以此易钱乎，彼肆主必疑吾为盗，何以自白？且缉捕者见之必掇去。即不然，同侪见吾多金，有不思杀而夺之者乎？然则此祸基也，不如献诸官，以脱吾身，非旷然自得之道乎？”遂投献。邑宰奇之曰：“得遗失物者给之半，此律之明条。汝其受诸。”丐叩首曰，“小人无罪，怀宝其罪。筹之审矣，非所愿也。”宰益奇之。适金主驰归呈诉，宰语之，故还其宝物。金主再拜曰：“小人何幸而值此义士！渠之所虑者，无宅以庇身耳。小人愿助之置宅。”宰曰，“能如是乎，余亦给之资本，以旌其善。”乃呼里长为之谋宅于市廛，置货立业也。且表之以额曰：“拾金不昧。”

    Explanation

    144

    The Righteous Beggar

    A certain beggar, a man from Yan, lived alone and wandered through the market streets, begging for food. When he was full, he would sit by the ancient trees in the shade of the broad road and sleep carelessly. One day, a horseman galloped past, tripped, and broke his bag, dropping two pieces of silver onto the road. The beggar called out but the rider did not hear and rode off wildly. The beggar then picked up the silver and thought to himself, "Should I exchange this for money? The shop owner will surely suspect me of theft. How can I prove my innocence? Moreover, if the police see me with so much money, won't they think of killing me and taking it? Then this is the root of disaster. It would be better to hand it over to the authorities to free myself from trouble. Is this not the way of a free and contented person?" So he handed it over. The county magistrate was surprised by this and said, "According to the law, the finder gets half of the lost item. You should accept it." The beggar bowed and said, "I am not guilty, but keeping treasure is a crime. I have considered this carefully, and it is not what I desire." The magistrate was even more surprised. The gold owner happened to ride back and report the incident. The magistrate returned the treasure to him. The gold owner bowed twice and said, "What luck I have to meet such a righteous person! His only concern is having no place to shelter himself. I wish to help him buy a house." The magistrate said, "If you can do this, I will also provide him with capital to commend his goodness." So he called the village headman to arrange a house in the market area for him to set up a business and establish a career. He also inscribed a plaque that said, "Return Lost Property Without Seeking Reward."

    145

    芗厈曰：义者，开国成家之至宝也。昔孟尝市义而安其身，曹瞒假义以创其业，彼市且假者，天尚许之，况堂堂义气之出于天性者乎？一以裕后，一以发身，冥冥相之，信无疑义。
    　　
    　　卷四
    　　
    　　书安邑狱
    　　
    　　林宝光，山右安邑巨室，五旬余始得一子，名继业。至六旬外，家计益饶，而精神衰颓，不胜劳乏，延中表之子张歧指者，其左手有赘指，故名。其为人少年佻达而有心计，俾总理家业，持筹握算，井井有条。宝光倚赖之。是岁，继业年十五，宝光为之聘同邑富室杨氏女，毕姻有日，张戏谓继业曰：“弟年来冠，焉知房中事？盍倩我代，勿贻新妇笑也。”继业怒之以目。晋俗，嫁女者亲串与役人随送三日而还，其迎娶之家戚友毕集，两姻家上下混杂，往往不能辨认。是时继业亲迎回，哄随者数百人，有小偷程三儿者，右手亦有歧指，溷入厮役队内。奔走出入，欲乘间肆窃。

    Explanation

    145

    Xiang Yan said: Righteousness is the most precious treasure for founding a nation and establishing a family. In ancient times, Shen Yuan bought righteousness to secure his position, and Cao Cao pretended to be righteous to establish his career. Even those who buy and pretend to be righteous are still approved by Heaven; how much more so for the great righteousness that comes from one's nature? One to benefit future generations, one to elevate one's own status, in the darkness, they guide you, there is no doubt or hesitation.

    Volume Four

    Book of Anyi Prison

    Lin Baoguang, a wealthy family in Anyi, Shanxi Province, finally had a son after fifty years, named Jiye. By the age of sixty, his family's wealth increased, but his spirit and energy declined, and he could not bear the fatigue. He invited his cousin Zhang Qizhi, whose left hand had an extra finger, to manage the family affairs. Zhang was young, bold, and had a good mind for business. He managed the accounts and calculations meticulously and systematically. Baoguang relied on him. That year, Jiye was fifteen years old. Baoguang arranged for him to marry a wealthy woman from the same town. The wedding was scheduled for a certain day. Zhang joked with Jiye and said, "Brother, you have come of age. How do you know about the bedroom affairs? Why don't you let me do it for you, so as not to make the new wife laugh?" Jiye glared at him with anger. In Shanxi custom, when a woman is married, relatives and servants accompany her for three days and then return. All relatives and friends of the groom's family gather, and the families of both the bride and groom are mixed together, often making it difficult to distinguish between them. At that time, Jiye returned after the wedding, and hundreds of people followed him. There was a thief named Cheng San'er, whose right hand also had an extra finger. He mixed into the servant group, ran in and out, and wanted to take advantage of the opportunity to steal.

    146

    是晚内外宴客毕，其客中之年少者，群拥新郎入密室，纵酒行令，必欲新郎醉而后已。时张亦在座，至三鼓，忽患腹疾，告继业而回。适三儿潜入客舍，盗袭客之衣冠带履，居然上宾，欣欣得计。闯入新妇房。时已夜深，女眷各散，仆妇使女皆倦卧外室，鼻息雷鸣。三儿不知衣饰所在，举烛照之，见新妇美而艳，三儿心动，亟阖户释衣推新妇卧。新妇误为其夫，难与抗拒，听其解衣宽带。时觉有歧指相触，狂荡甫毕，急起着衣而遁。新妇斯睡未几，诸客皆醉倒，继业归房，不见新妇，亦举烛照之，新妇见非前人，即起诘曰：“汝何人，擅敢遥视？”继业笑曰：“予而夫也。”新妇谔然曰：“何以为信？”继业曰：“何为不信？”新妇曰：“果尔，汝以手与我验之。”继业出两手与观，新妇大啼曰：“有歧指者，已冒作新郎，去犹未久也。”继业触张歧指谑语，盛怒拔剑而去。

    Explanation

    146

    That evening, the host entertained guests inside and out. Among the guests, the young ones gathered to take the groom into a private room, where they drank freely and played drinking games, insisting on getting the groom drunk before stopping. At the time, Zhang was also present. By the third drumbeat, Zhang suddenly suffered from stomach pain and reported to his father before returning home. Just then, a mischievous boy sneaked into the guesthouse and stole the guest's clothing, hat, belt, and shoes. He presented himself as a distinguished guest and felt very pleased and clever. He burst into the bride's room. It was already deep into the night, and the women had dispersed. The maids and servants were all tired and sleeping in the outer room, their snoring like thunder. The boy did not know where the clothing was, so he lit a candle to look around. He saw the bride was beautiful and charming, and the boy was moved. Quickly closing the door, he undressed and pushed the bride to lie down. The bride mistook him for her husband and found it difficult to resist, so she let him undress her. She felt something unusual touching her, and after the act was over, he quickly dressed and fled. The bride had just fallen asleep when all the guests were drunk and passed out. The father returned to his room and did not see the bride. He also lit a candle and looked around. The bride saw that it was not her husband and immediately stood up and asked, "Who are you? How dare you look at me?" The father laughed and said, "I am your husband." The bride said in surprise, "What proof do you have?" The father asked, "Why should I not believe you?" The bride said, "If so, show me your hands to verify." The father showed his hands, and the bride cried out, "Someone with a split finger had already pretended to be the groom and left not long ago." The father was reminded of a joke about split fingers and, in great anger, drew his sword and left.

    147

    张父闻叩门声甚厉，急起拔关，继业突入噪曰：“恶奴张歧指何在？索与俱死！”张父方骇愕间，家人皆闻声出劝，甫夺其剑。歧指亦捧腹出，睹继业颜色凶暴，问何怒为。继业见其仇，突前结其胸襟曰：“鸣冤去！”众皆问故，则愧忿难言。张父知不可解，群拥至县廨，官乃燕人方尹，唤两造入问之，继业诉其谑语与冒奸事，张始觉，极口呼冤曰：“小人腹疾归家，今犹未愈，安有是事？”继业曰：“其腹疾即脱身诈冒计，况新妇称歧指冒新郎，即为确据。

    Explanation

    147

    Zhang's father heard a loud and forceful knocking at the door, quickly pulled it open, and Ji Ye rushed in and shouted, "Where is the wicked slave Zhang Qizhi? I demand to die alongside him!" Zhang's father was in the midst of shock and astonishment, and the family members, hearing the commotion, came out to intervene and just managed to wrest his sword from him. Qizhi, laughing heartily, also came out, saw Ji Ye's fierce and violent expression, and asked why he was angry. Ji Ye, seeing his enemy, rushed forward and grabbed his chest, shouting, "To cry out injustice and leave!" Everyone asked what had happened, but he was unable to express his shame and anger. Zhang's father knew that the situation was beyond resolution and that the crowd escorted them to the county office, where the official, Yanren Fang Yin, called both parties in to question them. Ji Ye accused Qizhi of mocking words and false accusations. Qizhi, upon realizing this, vehemently protested, saying, "I was suffering from a stomach ailment and went home. It is not healed yet; how could there be such a thing?" Ji Ye retorted, "His stomach ailment was a ruse to escape and falsely accuse, and the fact that the bride claimed Qizhi falsely accused the groom is clear evidence."

    148

    ”方尹正思唤新妇质对，忽闻喧哗声，二老者相结至。一老曰：“予为杨宦，以女与林宝光之子为妇，缢死房中，请究其故。”一老曰：“新妇因何雉经，而子亦不知何往，求为伸雪。”尹指继业曰：“是非而子耶？”宝光大骇曰：“何以先至官也？”始问悉其故，尹命二老各书亲朋仆役姓名，咸按验之，无歧指者。问失物否，客惧为讼累，佥讳曰：“无之。”尹复访，闻张歧指好为桑间濮上之游，始信冒奸之情确。严刑之，张不胜楚，竟自诬服。律拟大辟，已决讫矣。

    Explanation

    148

    The magistrate was just contemplating calling the new wife to question her when he suddenly heard a commotion and two elderly men arrived together. One of the elderly men said, "I am Yang, a servant, and I gave my daughter in marriage to Lin Baoguang's son, but she hanged herself in her room. Please investigate the reason behind this." The other elderly man said, "Why did the new wife commit suicide, and why does her son not know where he is? I ask for justice to be done." The magistrate pointed to Jiye and asked, "Is this your son?" Lin Baoguang was greatly startled and asked, "How did this matter come to the attention of the authorities?" The magistrate began to investigate and learned the full story. He ordered the two elderly men to write down the names of their relatives, friends, and servants, and all were examined, with no discrepancies found. He asked if any property was lost, but the guests feared legal repercussions and all denied it. The magistrate then further investigated and learned that Zhang had a history of immoral behavior, leading him to believe that Zhang was involved in deceit. Zhang was subjected to severe punishment and, unable to endure the torture, confessed falsely. According to the law, he was sentenced to death, and the judgment was finalized.

    149

    方尹以丁忧去未几，邻县获盗即程三儿也，略一研鞠，遽将冒奸事自认不讳，且指所窃之衣履为证。邻县以赃物移知安邑，继任者悉召林宝光之客，皆出认被窃故物，且诉当时不言之故，始诛三儿而拟方尹以遣。呜呼！听讼者可不慎哉。
    　　
    　　和阗玉鼠
    　　
    　　吾浙执兴贩古玩业者，有挟巨资列肆于通都大邑，谓之行家。有以些小本，终日游行陋巷僻乡，贱价收微物，觅蝇头以糊口，谓之骨董鬼。闻得珍物，因之起家，亦不乏人，故业是者众。嘉禾有张骨董者，日持数百钱，追随货糖之人至委巷间，皆巨室后户。忽有辟者，一婢持灰石鼠易糖而入，张向货糖者以百钱得之。至晚，群骨董鬼毕集茶肆，各出所得物，互相品评。张亦出鼠，识者曰：“此灰玉也，值一串。”张唯唯而退。

    Explanation

    149

    The Magistrate Fang, having left for mourning duties not long ago, was soon succeeded by a neighboring county that captured a thief named Cheng San'er. After a brief interrogation, Cheng quickly confessed to the crime of impersonation without hesitation and pointed to the stolen clothes and shoes as evidence. The neighboring county, with the stolen goods, transferred the case to Anyi. The successor then summoned all the guests of Lin Baoguang, who all recognized the stolen items and explained the reason for not mentioning them at the time. The case began by punishing Cheng San'er and sentencing Fang to be sent away. Alas! How can those who hear cases be careless!

    The Hetian Jade Mouse

    Among the people in our province who engage in the business of antiques, there are those with large capital who set up shops in major cities and towns, called experts. There are also those with small capital who wander all day in narrow alleys and remote villages, collecting small items at low prices, seeking small profits to make a living, called "antique ghosts." Those who hear of treasures and rise to wealth through them are not few, hence many are involved in this business. In Jiaxing, there was a man named Zhang who dealt in antiques. He daily carried a few hundred coins, following people selling sugar to the back alleys of large households. Suddenly, someone opened the door, and a maid carried a gray stone mouse, exchanged it for sugar, and entered. Zhang approached the sugar seller with a hundred coins and obtained it. By evening, all the antique ghosts gathered together in a teahouse, each bringing out their acquired items, mutually evaluating them. Zhang also brought out the mouse, and the knowledgeable said, "This is gray jade, worth a string of coins." Zhang nodded and withdrew.

    150

    至家，以灰水煮之，作布囊盛米皮磨擦，不数日间，其鼠玉色洁白，二目正红，光华闪烁，出自天成，非嵌入者。张大喜，觅巧匠以紫檀镂细座，香楠为椟，修饰精致，以示行家。许以五十金，张不允，询及是玉出处，何因目赤，行家不能答，曰：“欲知究竟，非吴下大贾不能也。”张即赴吴以示行家，虽啧啧称叹，亦莫究其来历，仅曰：“玉色虽佳，为物甚微，不堪入贡，不过为贵公子案头赏玩耳。不出百金，若欲多得价，姑寓吾肆中，俟赏鉴家物色之可也。”张许诺，乃交行家，日则列于多宝厨，夜则什袭藏之。如是半载，虽有问者，许价数十金而止。

    Explanation

    150

    Upon returning home, he boiled it in ash water and rubbed it with a cloth bag filled with rice husks. Within a few days, the object turned white as jade, with two bright red eyes that gleamed with natural brilliance, not artificially inserted. Zhang was greatly pleased and sought a skilled craftsman to carve a delicate base from zitan (rosewood) and fashion a case from xiannan (fragrant camphorwood), embellishing it with great care and presenting it to connoisseurs. He offered fifty taels of silver, but Zhang refused. When asked about the origin of the jade and why its eyes were red, the connoisseurs could not answer, saying, "To know the truth, one must consult a great merchant of Wu." Zhang then traveled to Wu to show it to the connoisseurs there. Though they praised it greatly, they could not trace its origins, merely stating, "Although the jade color is fine, the object is too small to be worthy of tribute. It is only fit for a nobleman’s desk for appreciation. It will not be worth more than a hundred taels. If you wish to obtain a better price, stay in my shop for a while and wait for a connoisseur to find it." Zhang agreed and handed the object over to the connoisseur. By day, it was displayed in the treasure cabinet; by night, it was carefully wrapped and stored. In this way, for half a year, although there were those who inquired, the offers they made did not exceed tens of taels of silver.

    151

    时有相国守制回籍，将起，复广觅贡物，道经行家，瞥见玉鼠，停舆而入，索玩久之，询物从何来，需价若干。行家对以嘉禾客寄售者，价昂甚。相曰：“吾将试之，若系真者，价不嫌昂。即不真，亦值百金，可命客来府候给值。”行家诺，告张曰：“中堂贵客，汝往听命。若云物真，必索五百金，予我行规五十。若云不真，即百金货之，不可不售，恐后无识者矣。”张欣诺。登相府，已谕阍人留客宿。

    Explanation

    151

    During the reign of a chancellor who had returned to his home province after observing mourning rites and was about to resume his office, he again widely sought tribute items. On his way, passing by a shop, he caught sight of a jade mouse and stopped his carriage to enter. He spent a long time admiring it and asked where the item came from and how much it was worth. The shopkeeper replied that it was a guest from Jiahe who had entrusted it for sale, and the price was very high. The chancellor said, "I will test it. If it is genuine, the price is not too high. Even if it is not genuine, it is still worth a hundred gold coins. You can tell the guest to come to my mansion and wait for payment." The shopkeeper agreed and told Zhang, "There is an esteemed guest in the hall. You should go and obey his orders. If he says it is genuine, he will certainly demand five hundred gold coins and give me fifty gold coins as a commission. If he says it is not genuine, then sell it for a hundred gold coins. You cannot fail to sell it, for fear that later there will be no one who can recognize it." Zhang happily agreed. He went to the chancellor's mansion, where the chancellor had already instructed the gatekeeper to keep the guest for the night.

    152

    相命开内宴，诏妻妾子弟咸来贺宝，于是少长毕集，传观玉鼠，皆奖赞二目之异，而腹诽称宝之谬也。入夜，命东西分列四筵，中设黑光明漆几，高供玉鼠，堂中悬五彩琉璃灯，画烛齐辉，又命女仆娈童较准洋表时钟，守报时刻。东筵夫人率诸女眷就列，西筵子弟告坐。相则卧胡床，以矮几列精馔数品，随意饮啖。且命眷属各举新令，以尽雅兴，毋以老夫拘泥也。于是猜枚传筹，欢声盈耳。未几，童仆报亥时末刻，相命毋哗，灯烛尽息，使众目注视玉鼠，若有异，则报吾知。四座寂然，黑无所睹，莫不窃笑者。未几时钟十一响，众见鼠目透红光一线，渐引渐长，高与屋等。众皆咤报，相曰：“未尽所长也。”未几钟鸣十二，光华忽散，通室大明，如坐月光中，须眉毕见。众皆感悦，男女成列，奉觞上寿，相捻须大笑，受爵称庆毕，光亦渐敛。罢宴收宝，人各就寝。

    Explanation

    152

    The host ordered an inner banquet and summoned his wives, concubines, sons, and daughters to celebrate the treasure. Thus, both young and old gathered, passing around the jade mouse for viewing. All praised the unusual eyes of the jade mouse, but they privately criticized the treasure as a mistake. At night, he ordered the seating arranged east and west in four tables. In the center, a black lacquer table was set up, with the jade mouse placed high on it. In the hall, colorful glass lamps and painted candles shone together. He also ordered the female servants and young boys to check the foreign clocks and report the time. At the eastern table, the host's wife led the female relatives to their seats. At the western table, the sons and daughters took their seats. The host lay on a trundle bed, with a low table set out with several delicacies, eating and drinking at will. He also ordered the family members to propose new rules to fully enjoy the refined pleasure, and not to be bound by the old man's rigidity. Thus, they played guessing games and passed tokens, with laughter filling the air. Soon, the servant reported the end of the hour of the pig, and the host ordered silence, extinguishing the lamps and candles, asking everyone to watch the jade mouse. If anything unusual happened, they should report it to him. The four seats were silent, and in the darkness, nothing could be seen. No one could help but secretly laugh. Soon, the clock struck eleven, and everyone saw a red light emerging from the mouse's eyes, growing longer and taller, reaching the height of the house. Everyone exclaimed, and the host said, "It has not yet shown its full length." Soon, the clock struck twelve, and the light suddenly dispersed, illuminating the entire room as if they were sitting in moonlight, with every hair and eyebrow clearly visible. Everyone was moved and delighted, men and women arranged themselves, offering cups to wish the host a long life. The host smiled and laughed, accepting the cups and celebrating, and the light gradually faded. After the banquet, the treasure was collected, and everyone went to bed.

    153

    翌辰，相出见张，命之坐曰：“宝物真矣，汝从何处得来？”张诡对曰：“小人之祖薄宦山右得之市肆，以为传家之宝，本不忍舍，今小人为阻饥所迫，姑以割爱，实无价也。”相曰：“得之山右信然，姑陈尔价。”张虽领行家先入之言，嗫嚅不能出口。相命仆以珠盘给之，谕其自度。张本欲拨五百珠，心慌目乱，错拨五万珠。仆以呈相，相大笑曰：“五万金不为多，但毋后悔。”即唤行家至，相曰：“客已货乃宝白金五万，吾与尔百金作行规，速为立券。”行家欣然书之，授张署押，囊金同归。
    　　
    　　行家叩张物之所宝处，奚不明告我等，今已售矣，请述其异。张无可对，直告以不知之故。行家贿相府阍人而探之，阍人曰：“席中内言不出，外言不入，无从深悉。无已，有某词林者，主之门人也，吾等转告以得宝事，请词林入贺，可以问而知之。”爰启词林登堂申贺，乃见曰：“闻师相所得至宝，请以广门生之闻见。

    Explanation

    153

    The next day, the chancellor emerged to see Zhang and invited him to sit, saying, "The treasure is genuine, where did you obtain it from?" Zhang replied evasively, "My ancestor, a minor official in Shanxi, acquired it from a market in the province. He considered it a family heirloom and was reluctant to part with it. Now, I am compelled by hunger to sell it, though I must part with it reluctantly, it is truly priceless." The chancellor said, "It is indeed from Shanxi, please state your price." Zhang, influenced by the advice of the experts, stammered and could not speak. The chancellor ordered his servant to give Zhang a tray of pearls and instructed him to estimate the value. Zhang intended to count five hundred pearls but, in a state of panic, mistakenly counted five million. The servant presented the count to the chancellor, who laughed and said, "Five million gold pieces is not a lot, but do not regret it." He then called the experts and said, "The guest has sold his treasure for five million gold pieces. I will give you a hundred gold pieces as a fee, quickly draw up the contract." The experts happily wrote the contract, handed it to Zhang to sign, and returned with the money together.

    The experts then inquired about the place where Zhang had kept the treasure, wondering why he had not informed them. Zhang had already sold it and could not respond. He admitted that he did not know the reason. The experts bribed the gatekeeper of the chancellor's mansion to inquire. The gatekeeper said, "What is said inside the hall does not come out, and what is said outside does not go in, so we cannot know the details. If you must, there is a scholar who is a disciple of the chancellor. We will inform him of the matter and ask him to come and congratulate. You can then ask and learn about it." They then invited the scholar to ascend the hall to offer congratulations. The scholar said, "I have heard that the chancellor has obtained a priceless treasure, please allow me to broaden my disciple's knowledge and understanding."

    154

    ”相出玉鼠示之，词林曰：“二目之赤光异则异矣，然《博古图》、《集古录》考证诸书所未载，何称宝为？”相曰：“此大内物也。儒生焉得见之而注于图录乎？”词林曰：“然则师相何以知之？”相曰：“此物唐天宝间和阗所贡，相传以夜光玉琢成，其两目之异，遇子时能放光华，以辟恶物。是以先朝藏于书林，则蠹鱼不生，而古集完好。因兵燹之后，贼携之山右，遂失所在。而天府所藏珍器，册档内注载甚明，自失此物后，往往书籍蠢损。当今每命山右巨卿密谕访觅，久无下落，今为老夫所得，以应上命，必喜出望外，真百万黄金无以觅此至宝也。”词林再拜而退。
    　　
    　　张闻是言，拥厚资而回，访诸失物之家，果国初为山右中丞属下，以赠公子作盘中玩物。初不知目光之异，不甚宝贵，今家已中落，玉鼠在尘土中掩埋失色，小婢偶拾之，以易糖食，竟为张骨董起家之瑞。其命也夫，其命也夫！

    Explanation

    154

    The chancellor produced the jade mouse and showed it to the scholar. The scholar remarked, "The red light from its two eyes is indeed unusual, but it is not recorded in the Illustrated Records of Antiquity or the Collected Records of Antiquity and other考证 books. Why is it called a treasure?" The chancellor replied, "This is an item from the imperial palace. How could a scholar have seen it and recorded it in a catalog?" The scholar asked, "Then how does the chancellor know about it?" The chancellor explained, "This object was presented during the Tianbao period of the Tang dynasty from Hotan. It is said to be carved from moonlight jade, and its two eyes can emit a radiant light at the hour of the Rat, warding off evil things. Therefore, the previous dynasty kept it in the library, where it prevented bookworms from damaging the ancient collections. After the war and destruction, bandits took it to Shanyou, and it was lost. The treasures kept in the imperial treasury are clearly recorded in the archives. After losing this object, the books often suffered from insect damage. Nowadays, the emperor often orders the high-ranking officials in Shanyou to secretly search for it, but it has been missing for a long time. Now it has been obtained by me to fulfill the imperial command. He will surely be overjoyed. It is truly worth more than a million gold pieces to find this treasure." The scholar bowed twice and then left.

    Zhang heard these words, returned with a large sum of money, and visited the family that had lost the object. Indeed, it was originally owned by a subordinate of the governor of Shanyou during the early Qing dynasty, who gave it to his son as a decorative item on a tray. Originally, they did not know about the unusual light from its eyes and did not value it much. Now the family has fallen into decline, and the jade mouse was buried in dust and lost its luster. A young servant girl accidentally picked it up and exchanged it for candy. And it became the auspicious sign that allowed Zhang to build his fortune. It is fate, indeed, it is fate!

    155

    孙壮姑
    　　
    　　乙巳之岁，山左大饥，盗贼蜂起，胶东为甚。小康之家俱不自保。昌邑有标客孙良，技勇绝伦，有女壮姑，悉传其术。时因道路梗塞，闲居授徒。大姓之虞暴客者，争以重金为聘，良悉纳之，乃分其徒为十余部，各遣一队，以护大姓。而良周巡不息，盗贼不得肆志，咸憾之。昌邑钱尹，吴人也，捕得巨盗，诬指孙良为魁，械之至，良极口呼冤曰：“小人御盗，非为盗者。”尹曰：“盗何仇而指汝？”良曰：“邑中之巨室，彼窥伺已久，得小人捍卫，至今不得逞志。彼欲冤死小人，以遂其吞噬也。”尹察之信，竟诛盗而释良。良感甚，愿献女为妾。尹笑曰：“解释诬枉，令尹之职，何足言恩？且法不得妾部民女，汝休矣。”良涕泣而去。

    Explanation

    155

    In the year of the Rabbit (1875), there was a great famine in the region east of Mount Tai (Shandong Province), and bandits rose up like bees, with the Jiaodong region being particularly severe. Even moderately well-off families could not protect themselves. In Changyi, there was a martial arts master named Sun Liang, whose skills were unparalleled. He had a daughter named Zhuanggu, who had inherited all of his skills. At the time, due to the blocked roads, he was idle at home, teaching his students. Wealthy families who feared ruffians competed to offer him heavy gold as payment. Sun Liang accepted all of them and divided his students into more than ten groups, each sending a team to protect the wealthy families. While Sun Liang patrolled ceaselessly, the bandits could not carry out their intentions, and they all resented him. The magistrate of Changyi, a person from Wu, captured a major bandit and falsely accused Sun Liang of being the leader. He fettered him and brought him to the magistrate. Sun Liang shouted loudly, pleading his innocence: "I defended against bandits, not being a bandit myself." The magistrate said, "Why do the bandits accuse you?" Sun Liang replied, "The wealthy households in the town have been watching and waiting for a long time. With my defense, they have not been able to achieve their intentions up to now. They want to wrongfully kill me to satisfy their greed." The magistrate found his words to be true, and ultimately executed the bandit while releasing Sun Liang. Sun Liang was greatly moved and offered to give his daughter as a concubine. The magistrate smiled and said, "Clearing the wrongful accusation is my duty as magistrate; it does not deserve mention as an act of kindness. Moreover, the law does not allow the taking of a subject's daughter as a concubine. You may go now." Sun Liang wept and left.

    156

    未几，钱尹因公被劾，将回吴下，宦囊甚充，宵小私议窃发。良知之，谓尹曰：“凶年之后，道路难行，小人老矣，不能随护，民女虽陋，智勇具足，请侍左右，以备非常。”尹鉴其诚，纳之。其女年未二十而貌甚英武，遂与南行，车仗数十，仆从如云，小伙不敢举事。盗法，探有充实可劫者，或众寡不敌，则知风下程，并伙而谋，获财均分。散发益迟则盗益众。是时钱已去五六百里，至鲁界之朗月镇，觅宿地，得旅店，后屋三楹，墙垣高峻，周匝仅容一门出入，尹喜其完固，必欲居之。壮姑知非善地，然已卸装矣，勉从之。谓钱尹夫妇曰：“妾观此宅，似为谋禁客商之所。夜或有异，主君与夫人请卧观之，幸毋高声，妾有以处若辈。”尹虽唯唯，然未知其能，甚战粟也。于是安尹夫妇于东室，呼二婢伏西室，曰：“唤汝则出。”取夷灯之脐凸碧琉璃者，置窗隙，院中明似月光。乃易短袄皮裤，鞋尖置铁，腰掖利刃，灭烛跃登中门之颠，踞框以俟。

    Explanation

    156

    Not long after, Qian Yin was accused in office and was about to return to Wu. His official purse was very full, and small people privately plotted. Liangzhi knew this and said to Yin, "After a bad year, the roads are difficult to travel. I am old and cannot protect you. Although I am a common woman, I have both wisdom and courage. Please let me serve you to prepare for any emergencies." Yin appreciated her sincerity and accepted her offer. Her daughter, who was not yet twenty and appeared very strong and brave, accompanied them south. They traveled with dozens of vehicles and numerous servants, and the young bandits dared not act. According to bandit law, if they investigated wealthy targets that could be robbed, or if the numbers were unequal, they would follow the wind and move forward, planning together and dividing the loot equally. The longer they delayed in dispersing, the more bandits would gather. By then, Qian had already traveled several hundred miles south and reached the town of Langyue on the border of Lu. They found an inn with a back room having three bays, high and steep walls, and only one entrance and exit. Yin was pleased with its completeness and firmness and insisted on staying there. The strong maiden knew it was not a good place, but she had already unloaded and reluctantly agreed. She said to Qian Yin and his wife, "I see this house seems to be a place for trapping merchants. There might be something unusual at night. Please lie down and observe, and do not make a loud noise. I have a way to deal with them." Yin nodded in agreement but did not know her ability and was very fearful. He arranged for Yin and his wife to stay in the east room and called two maids to hide in the west room, telling them, "If I call you, come out." He took a lamp with a convex green glass wick, placed it in a window crack, and the courtyard was lit like moonlight. Then she changed into a short jacket and leather pants, wore iron-tipped shoes, and tucked a sharp knife in her belt. She extinguished the candle, jumped onto the top of the middle door, and sat on the frame waiting.

    157

    漏三下，内外俱寂，寓主马铁头，盗中之巨擘也，密集群寇，择其能者，皆操白刃，自后垣登屋，余盗伏于四隅，以防逸出。先命一人下探之，久而不回，马曰：“内多妇女，谅入安乐窝矣。”继命二三人下，亦如是。马曰：“真不了事，弱息数辈，尚烦乃公自往。若遇大敌，行见尔曹雌伏矣。”遂跃入院，四无人声，月光中视屋门已闭，甫拔关欲入，额颅中伤甚重，如泰山压顶然，仰跌丈余。旋飞一人坐胸前，马举刀欲砍，被掣两肩窝，而两臂软，刀自掷去。

    Explanation

    157

    At three drum strikes, both inside and outside were quiet. Ma Tietou, the giant in the world of thieves, secretly gathered a group of bandits, selecting the capable ones, all armed with sharp weapons, climbed over the back wall to the roof. The remaining bandits hid in the four corners to prevent escape. He first sent one person to investigate, but the person did not return for a long time. Ma said, "There are many women inside, surely they have entered a place of comfort." He then sent two or three more people down, and it was the same. Ma said, "Truly incompetent, a few weak and helpless ones, still trouble you to go yourself. If you encounter a great enemy, you will see your kind cowering." He then leaped into the courtyard, where there was no one making a sound. In the moonlight, he saw the house door closed and just unlocked it to enter. His forehead was severely injured, as if Mount Tai were pressing down on his head, and he fell backward several feet. Then someone flew up and sat on his chest. Ma raised his knife to cut, but his shoulders were pulled, and his arms became weak; the knife fell away.

    158

    又被掣两胯，而两腿废，身不能转动，始闻娇声唤婢，两女举烛至，视之一幼妇耳。哀祈之，壮姑微哂曰：“我见来势猛，知是能手，果恶奴也。汝为寓主，谅害行旅不少，本欲杀却，如此庸奴，徒污我刀，且留汝为作恶者戒。”遂命一婢取药来，壮姑以刀割铁头脸上肉，缕绫成条，以药揉之，血立止。时天已曙矣，仆从叩门请。壮姑以足踢马臀，拔关而叱曰：“速去领尔徒尸，在东墙下积薪内也。”从容启尹夫妇登车而行。马被踢，则手足已复旧，抱惭而窜。自此脸上皮条终不复合，丝丝悬挂，若世俗所画狮子然。

    Explanation

    158

    Once again, the legs were pulled apart, rendering them useless, and the body could not move. Suddenly, a soft, feminine voice called for a maid, and two women brought a candle. Looking at it, it was just a young woman. She pleaded in sorrow. The older woman gave a slight smile and said, "I saw that you came with great force, knowing you were a skilled one, indeed a wicked slave. You are the host, surely you have harmed many travelers. I originally wanted to kill you, but such an ordinary slave would only dirty my knife. I will leave you as a warning to those who do evil." She then ordered a maid to bring medicine. The older woman cut the face of the "iron-headed" woman, threaded silk into strips, applied medicine, and the bleeding stopped immediately. The time had already become dawn. The servants knocked on the door to ask. The older woman kicked the horse's rump, pulled the gate open, and shouted, "Go quickly and take your companions' bodies, they are in the pile of firewood under the eastern wall." She calmly helped the couple of Mr. Yin get into the carriage and they went. The horse, after being kicked, had its limbs return to normal, and it fled in shame. From then on, the strips of skin on her face never reattached, hanging thread by thread, like the lion depicted in the world.

    159

    沈竹楼
    　　
    　　沈竹楼者，浙右诸生。其父为县吏，有二子，竹楼居长，次子随父业，日有进益，以助饔飧。惟竹楼入泮后，教读自给。妻李氏，亦吏家女，纺织以佐之。既乏精馔以供高堂，而大比之年，反取给于乃父厥弟。是以父母日恹薄之，詈以书痴而虐使其妻。是岁，竹楼病，生徒皆散，益不能支。病痊，谓李氏曰：“予忝为丈夫，而不能仰事俯育，诚自愧恧，然死守蠹简，乌能奋发？戚某幕游楚南，为贵介所尊奉，予拟投之，改习刑名家言，或可致富，卿其勿辞辛苦，相随翁姑，守我三年，若无音耗，是予业勿成，誓死九原，任卿自便。”妇泣诺之。竹楼告贷亲故，得数金，飘然入楚。

    Explanation

    159

    Se Zhubao

    　　Se Zhubao was a scholar from the right side of Zhejiang. His father was a county official, and they had two sons, with Se Zhubao being the elder. The younger son followed his father's profession and made daily contributions to help with meals. Se Zhubao, after entering the school, supported himself by teaching. His wife, Li, was also the daughter of an official, and she helped by weaving to assist him. They lacked the luxury of fine food to provide for their parents, and during the year of the provincial examination, they were actually supported by his younger brother. Therefore, his parents grew increasingly distant and harsh towards him, calling him a bookworm and mistreating his wife. That year, Se Zhubao fell ill, and his students all left, making it even harder for him to cope. After recovering, he told Li, "I am ashamed to be a man, unable to support my family above and below. I truly feel ashamed, but by clinging to ancient texts, how can I achieve anything? Qi, who travels as a retainer in the south of Chu, is respected by the nobles. I plan to seek his patronage, change my studies to legal and criminal law, and perhaps become wealthy. You must not hesitate to work hard, follow your in-laws, and stay with me for three years. If I do not hear from him, it means my career will not succeed, and I swear to die in a grave, allowing you to do as you wish." The wife wept and agreed. Se Zhubao borrowed money from relatives and friends, gathered some funds, and set off for the south of Chu.

    160

    访其戚，适于月前病殁，竹楼无所依倚，行李俱尽，进退两难，决意自裁，遂潜至方城外之邮亭，解带缢。时有千户张弁，巡檄至此，瞥见之，叱兵往探，体尚温，与千户共解而撅救之。竹楼始苏，询得其故，千户曰：“若既诸生，必能书，请为我记室，我能衣食之，徒死何为？”竹楼从之，为张千户勤司笔札，甚相得也。不数年，张官至淮帅，淮上鹾商与帅往还者，必晤沈先生，乐其为人和厚，咸赞仰之。帅亦极力推许，而嗟其无业。商体帅意，群请伺公堂，岁奉数百金，竹楼绰有余裕矣。又数年，张帅卒，竹楼为之治丧，送其属归楚，复至淮上。商人益信其笃诚，谋为立鹾业。竹楼不辞劳瘁，会计维精，而上下亲睦，业浸大，分配帑金百万，俨然巨商矣。手下司事者以百计，或劝其广纳妻妾，竹楼不忍负其妇，喟然涕泣，将治装以迎之。

    Explanation

    160

    Zhu Lou visited his relatives, only to find that his close relative had passed away just a month ago. With no one to rely on and all his belongings gone, he was in a dilemma, unable to decide what to do. Determined to end his life, he secretly went to a post station outside Fangcheng and hanged himself. At that time, a hundred-man commander named Zhang Bian was patrolling the area and caught a glimpse of him. He shouted at the soldiers to go and investigate, and upon finding Zhu Lou, they discovered that his body was still warm. Together with the hundred-man commander, they untied him and revived him. Zhu Lou finally came to, and when asked about the reason, the hundred-man commander said, "If you are already a scholar, you must be able to write. Please serve as my secretary, and I can provide you with clothing and food. What is the point of dying in vain?" Zhu Lou agreed, serving as Zhang the hundred-man commander's diligent secretary, and they got along very well. In a few years, Zhang rose to the position of commander of Huai. The salt merchants of Huai, who frequently interacted with the commander, would always meet Mr. Shen, enjoying his gentle and kind nature, and all praised and admired him. The commander also strongly recommended him but lamented that he had no profession. Understanding the commander's intentions, the merchants all requested to serve in the public hall, receiving hundreds of gold pieces annually, which left Zhu Lou with ample surplus.
