    　　北平民郝连大之妻於氏，天性贤慧，其为人也，抑已尊人，让利趋义，故姑姊妯娌间，莫不亲爱而师事之，群尊之曰“大娘”，以示不敢尔汝也。其家山居，夫以樵为业。大娘生一子，甫周岁，归宁父母，住有日矣，忆及家事欲回，时值农忙之际，其弟侄皆在田间，无送之者。有邻人子，年十四五，其母倩令送女，大娘偕之行。绕豁越岭，人迹罕到处有群狼来扑，邻子倒地，大娘急呼曰：“此子不可食，是邻家倩来者。请以我子易之。”遂投其孩童于地，而与狼力争邻子。狼竟舍之，扶邻子踉跄而归。其夫见大娘颜色惨变，询得其故，携枪往捕，至其处，见群狼环伺之，其子端坐于中，挖地搏土为戏。狼见人来，跳跃而去，乃抱其子归。夫妇互庆，明日送邻于归，述之，通邑称异。未几，邻子赴野拾莱，竟为狼食，村人益神大娘，死而庙祀之，凡有遇虎狼者，大呼“郝连大娘”，则必有旋风护之，至今香烟犹盛。

    Explanation

    245

    郝连大娘

    　　北平民郝连大之妻于氏，天性贤慧，其为人也，抑已尊人，让利趋义，故姑姊妯娌间，莫不亲爱而师事之，群尊之曰“大娘”，以示不敢尔汝也。其家山居，夫以樵为业。大娘生一子，甫周岁，归宁父母，住有日矣，忆及家事欲回，时值农忙之际，其弟侄皆在田间，无送之者。有邻人子，年十四五，其母倩令送女，大娘偕之行。绕豁越岭，人迹罕到处有群狼来扑，邻子倒地，大娘急呼曰：“此子不可食，是邻家倩来者。请以我子易之。”遂投其孩童于地，而与狼力争邻子。狼竟舍之，扶邻子踉跄而归。其夫见大娘颜色惨变，询得其故，携枪往捕，至其处，见群狼环伺之，其子端坐于中，挖地搏土为戏。狼见人来，跳跃而去，乃抱其子归。夫妇互庆，明日送邻于归，述之，通邑称异。未几，邻子赴野拾莱，竟为狼食，村人益神大娘，死而庙祀之，凡有遇虎狼者，大呼“郝连大娘”，则必有旋风护之，至今香烟犹盛。

    246

    周姬
    　　
    　　燕人胡某，士而兼农，且耕且读，虽不得科第，而温饱自如。亦里间中之安乐人也。年逾强仕无子，妻牛氏，悍而妬。家无婢女，胡曾买妾，为牛氏苦挞逃去，胡不敢追。有好友钱生，闻其事深抱不平，欲觅悍过于牛氏者，纳诸胡以树之敌。适有河间周女，随父卖械至其地，能于首竖二百觔幡杆，以足蹬一百二十觔大瓮者。值其父老病死，弟幼弱，贫不能殓，愿卖身为父置榇。钱生以百贯为之治丧，俾其弟扶柩回籍，告以牛氏之悍，欲仗其力惩治之，为胡友延后，送与为姬之意。周女首肯而去。以小星礼谒见胡夫妇，胡感钱生之情，夜使值宿，然心甚惴惴。

    Explanation

    246

    Zhou Ji

    A man from Yan named Hu, who was both a scholar and a farmer, tilled the land and studied books. Although he did not attain official rank, he lived comfortably and contentedly. He was also one of the happy and content people in his community. He was past forty and had no children. His wife, Niú, was fierce and jealous. There were no maids in his household. Hu once bought a concubine, who was severely beaten by Niú and fled away. Hu dared not chase her. He had a good friend named Qian Sheng, who deeply felt injustice upon hearing the matter. Qian Sheng wanted to find someone fiercer than Niú and give her to Hu to set up an enemy. There happened to be a woman named Zhou from Hejian, who followed her father to sell weapons to that place. She could hold a two-hundred-catty banner pole upright with her head and push a one-hundred-twenty-catty large jar with her foot. Her father died when he was old and sick, and her brother was young and weak. They were too poor to bury him, so the woman wished to sell herself to prepare a coffin for her father. Qian Sheng used one hundred strings of cash to handle her father's funeral and made her brother carry the coffin back to his hometown. Qian Sheng told her about Niú's fierceness and wanted to rely on her strength to punish Niú, to continue Hu's friendship, and to give her to Hu as a concubine. The woman agreed and left. She visited Hu and his wife with a minor ritual. Hu felt Qian Sheng's feelings and made her stay overnight, but he was very anxious.

    247

    次日早起出门，牛氏已操杖伏于寝门之侧。夫出突入，姬犹未起，牛卷其衾，以杖指之曰：“汝何大胆，敢入我家，知我家法乎？凡为新妇者，须试杖一百。”姬不答，牛氏之杖乱下，自背至股挞百余。姬曰：“杖数盈矣，妾应起身，勿伤贵手。”牛氏无言而退，姬乃为之洒扫执爨，暇则浣衣绣履，凡妇工之所应为，皆代牛氏，殷勤为之。牛亦不悦，恒以横逆相加。姬裸衣伏地受挞不知凡几矣。偶与胡寝，胡怜其无辜，爱其婉娩，涕泣抚之，姬笑曰：“郎毋以妾为念也，夫人之力，能有几何？妾所顺受，有同儿戏，郎视妾身有微伤乎？妾所忧者，未知何时有妊，倘得一子，庶不负钱先生之恩耳。”

    Explanation

    247

    The next morning, as she got up to leave, Lady Niu was already holding a stick and lying in wait beside the door to the sleeping chamber. When her husband suddenly entered, Lady Ji was still not awake. Lady Niu rolled up her quilt and pointed the stick at her, saying, "How dare you enter my home! Do you know our family rules? All new brides must be beaten a hundred times." Lady Ji did not reply, and Lady Niu's stick rained down, striking her back and thighs more than a hundred times. Lady Ji said, "The number of beatings is complete; I should get up. Do not hurt your hand." Lady Niu said nothing and retreated. Lady Ji then cleaned and cooked, and in her spare time, she washed clothes and sewed shoes. She performed all the tasks a wife should do, diligently doing them for Lady Niu. Lady Niu was also displeased and constantly treated her harshly. Lady Ji, naked, lay on the ground and was beaten many times. Once, she slept with Hu, who pitied her innocence and loved her gentle and compliant nature. He cried and comforted her. Lady Ji smiled and said, "Do not worry about me, dear. My strength is limited; what I endure is like playing. Do you see any small injuries on my body? What I worry about is not knowing when I will become pregnant. If I can have a child, perhaps I will not辜负钱先生的恩情."

    248

    半载后，果怀孕。牛氏觉之，俟夫出，闭姬于房，操大杖临之曰：“我欲审汝。”姬以旧规，自去衣裳，伏地听命。牛氏微哂曰：“我知汝有孕，真乎？伪乎？”姬曰：“有之，才月余耳。”牛曰：“闻汝以贱妓出身，所得何人之种，敢乱我胡氏宗乎？汝须以腹受杖，我为挞而去之，再得者我始放心。汝尚敢以臀塞责乎？”姬大笑而起，摔牛氏于地，以一足按其背，手接其杖先挞数十，调之曰：“嫡庶之礼代操井臼，以伺应郎与夫人，若有过受杖，是我分也。今我来汝家半载余矣，腹中宜乎有孕，犹以外心疑之，不过借词欲毁我胎耳，是甘绝胡氏之宗嗣，斩祖先之血食，大罪人也，人人得而诛之。我今日为胡氏之前人代伸家法，汝能革面洗心，尚可愿恕，否则裂肤拆骨勿悔也。”牛氏始犹叫骂，继而不胜其楚，哀鸣求恕。姬姑释之。牛氏披发狂奔，诉其母家。其兄弟叔侄皆乡里之蠢人耳，闻其言，纠合三十余人，各持械器，至胡宅寻斗。

    Explanation

    248

    Half a year later, she indeed became pregnant. Mrs. Niu realized this, waited for her husband to leave, locked Lady Ji in the room, and confronted her with a large stick, saying, "I want to examine you." Lady Ji followed the old rules, took off her clothes herself, and lay on the ground, obeying. Mrs. Niu smiled slightly and said, "I know you are pregnant, is it true? Or false?" Lady Ji replied, "Yes, it has only been about a month." Mrs. Niu said, "I heard that you came from a lowly courtesan background. Whose seed did you receive? Dare you disrupt the lineage of the Hu family? You must receive the stick on your belly. I will beat it away, and if you get pregnant again, then I will be at ease. Do you still dare to cover up with your buttocks?" Lady Ji laughed and stood up, threw Mrs. Niu to the ground, pressed her back with one foot, took the stick in her hand, and first beat her dozens of times, saying, "The rites of the legitimate and illegitimate, I take over the tasks of drawing water and grinding grain, to serve the young master and the lady. If there is a fault, receive the stick; it is my duty. Now I have been in your family for more than half a year. There should be a pregnancy in my belly, but you still suspect from an external heart, just using words to destroy my fetus. This is to willingly cut off the lineage of the Hu family, to cut off the blood sacrifices to the ancestors, a great criminal, everyone can kill him/her. I am today representing the ancestors of the Hu family to enforce family law. Can you change your face and cleanse your heart? Still willing to forgive? Otherwise, don't regret being torn apart skin and bones." Mrs. Niu initially still shouted and cursed, then could not bear the pain, cried pitifully and begged for forgiveness. Lady Ji temporarily released her. Mrs. Niu ran wildly with her hair loose, complained to her mother's family. Her brothers, uncles, and cousins are all foolish people in the village. Hearing her words, they gathered more than thirty people, each holding weapons, went to the Hu residence to fight.

    249

    姬闻人声嘈杂，理发整衣，以布束腹，拔关而出，问曰：“汝等皆夫人之党乎？有明理者，请论曲直；若不言礼，欲以力胜，亦请展施技艺。”众怒甚，蜂涌而前，姬跃出门，夺白挺旋舞以斗，当之者莫不中伤扑跌，皆弃械抱首窜去。姬大笑而入，夫亦归矣。知其事曰：“目前称快，后将奈何？”姬曰：“郎其勿惧，请约诸生以备讼耳。乘此一举，未必非长治久安之道也。”牛氏之众败归，果投讼师，以逞凶夺嫡鸣官。官为唤讯，姬乃投案陈情，诸生为证。官直其言，判曰：“牛氏无子而妒，七出已犯其二，凭恃愚蠢之党，灭妾欺夫，罪无可逭。

    Explanation

    249

    The woman heard a commotion and quickly tidied her hair and clothes, tying a cloth around her waist. She forcefully opened the door and rushed out, asking, "Are you all supporters of my wife? If there is someone reasonable, please discuss right and wrong; if you intend to win by force, then let us see your skills." The crowd was furious and rushed forward like bees. The woman leaped out the door, seized a long staff, and spun it in a dance-like manner to fight. Anyone who encountered her was injured and fell down, and all abandoned their weapons, covered their heads, and fled. The woman laughed and returned home, and her husband also came back. She knew what had happened and said, "I am glad for the moment, but what will happen afterward?" The woman replied, "Do not be afraid, I will arrange for the students to be witnesses in case of a lawsuit. This one-time victory might be a way to achieve long-term stability and peace." The supporters of the wife returned defeated and indeed sought a lawyer to act violently to seize the position of the legitimate heir and report to the official. The official summoned for questioning, and the woman reported herself and presented her case, with the students serving as witnesses. The official found her words true and judged, "The wife has no children and is jealous, having already violated two of the seven grounds for divorce. Relying on a foolish group, she suppresses the wife and insults the husband; her crime cannot be avoided."

    250

    照律出之。其助恶之人，各杖八十，为大不应者戒。”遍挞诸牛，命胡生黜牛氏，而以周姬为继妻。诸牛归，因官私受责，亦不直牛氏，众弃之。牛氏无所依，行乞自活。次年，值周姬生子弥月，贺客盈门，牛氏操杖持筐以索食。见周姬，长跪叩首，姬掖之登堂，当众问其情，牛氏大哭痛悔，姬曰：“妾为夫人权摄正位者，料有今日耳。夫人既诚心悔过，请以礼让。”牛氏固不敢允，而胡生亦大不悦。姬已为之易衣，众皆贤之，钱生曰：“请全周姬之志。”牵生与牛氏入室，欢饮而散。然而牛氏自怨自恨，竟不干预家政，别洁一室，长斋奉佛以终。
    　　
    　　芗厈曰：骥不称其力，称其德也。其周姬之谓乎？悍妇不难以武胜，而难以德胜，乃能化之使悔，其姬姜、太妊之流亚欤？合观郝连大娘事，何贤良之妇，皆在田间？礼失而求诸野，其斯之谓欤？

    Explanation

    250

    According to the law, they were released. Those who assisted in wrongdoing were each beaten eighty strokes, serving as a warning to those who do not comply with the law. All the oxen were beaten, and Hu was ordered to divorce Niu and take Zhou as his new wife. Niu returned home, and due to official and private reprimands, she was also not supported by Niu, and was abandoned by the community. Niu had no one to rely on and lived by begging. The following year, when Zhou gave birth to a child who was one month old, guests filled the house with congratulations. Niu held a stick and a basket to beg for food. Seeing Zhou, she prostrated herself in reverence, and Zhou helped her up to the hall. In front of everyone, Zhou asked about the situation, and Niu wept bitterly and deeply regretted her actions. Zhou said, "I am acting as the principal wife on behalf of the lady, and I foresaw this outcome. Since you sincerely repent, please allow me to yield." Niu firmly did not agree, and Hu was also very displeased. Zhou had already changed her clothes, and everyone admired her. Qian said, "Please respect Zhou's wishes." He took Hu and Niu into the room, and they drank merrily and then dispersed. However, Niu blamed herself and harbored resentment, and she never interfered in household affairs. She kept a separate room clean and observed a long fast in devotion to Buddhism until the end.

    Xiang Yan said: The virtue of a fine horse is not in its strength, but in its character. Is this not what Zhou exemplified? A fierce woman is not difficult to defeat with force, but it is difficult to win with virtue. She was able to transform her and make her repent, akin to the virtues of Lady Jiang and Lady Tai Ren. Considering the case of Hao Lian's daughter, how many virtuous women are found in the countryside? When ritual and propriety are lost, they can be sought in the wild, as this saying implies.

    251

    沈太守
    　　
    　　浙人沈某，少为酒家佣。亏其主人钱十五贯，年终无以报命，归谓妻曰：“我已亏负，主人纵不我索，奈伙伴皆目我为负心人，其能相容乎？是此间无立足地，惟有外出营生。汝其纺织勤劳，以奉我母，勿致冻馁，纫感无既。”妻曰：“此我分内事也，毋庸多嘱。但愿贱出而荣归，不虚我望足矣。”沈曰：“汝言诚是也。”去至淮上作漕艘纤夫，相随入都，携筐卖饼饵。自给之外，日有余资，以置冠服，代人投刺，权充仆从。一日，有词林欲谒大丞相，其仆病，倩沈代。沈生平未见相府规模，贸贸然持柬直冲，值阍人刘姓将出，飞檐华毂十数骑。前引后从，盖刘已得四品头衔，其仪从直与京卿等。前徒见沈冒昧，交鞭挞之，沈横卧于地，哭喊曰：“恃尔主为宰相耶？即皇帝亦当论理。

    Explanation

    251

    Shen the Magistrate

    A man named Shen from Zhejiang, when young, worked as a servant in a tavern. He owed his master fifteen strings of coins, and at the end of the year, he had no way to repay his debt. Returning home, he told his wife, "I have already failed my master, and even if he does not demand repayment, my fellow workers all look at me as a disloyal person. How can they tolerate me? There is no place for me to stand here, so I must go out and make a living. You must work hard at weaving and spinning to support my mother, and do not let her suffer from cold and hunger. My gratitude is endless." His wife replied, "This is my duty, and there is no need for you to give me many instructions. I only hope that you leave humbly and return in glory, and that my expectations are not in vain." Shen said, "Your words are truly correct."

    He then traveled to the Huai River area, where he worked as a river porter, and later followed the boats to the capital, carrying baskets to sell baked goods. Beyond his own sustenance, he earned extra money each day, which he used to buy clothes and even acted as a messenger for others. One day, a scholar wanted to pay a visit to the Grand Chancellor, but his servant was ill, so he asked Shen to deliver the letter. Shen had never seen the grandeur of the chancellor's residence, and boldly held the letter and rushed forward. He encountered a gatekeeper surnamed Liu, who was about to leave, accompanied by several dozen horses with flying eaves and ornate wheels. The entourage was led by Liu, who had already obtained a fourth-rank official title, and his retinue was on par with that of high-ranking officials in the capital. The people in front saw Shen's boldness and beat him with whips. Shen lay flat on the ground, crying and shouting, "Do you rely on your master being the Chancellor? Even the Emperor should be treated with reason."

    252

    我何罪而辱之？”刘甫登车，闻其语操南音，命人唤至车前，详察其貌，即饬送入后院，毋得纵逸，俟我归来问话。应声雷诺，牵沈径去。词林见其滋事，已驱车远飏矣。刘回院，问客何在，众人拥沈至阶前叱跪，刘细审其籍贯戚里，沈惶悚以对。刘尽退从人，扶沈入室，纳诸上坐，曰：“弟识刘某乎？舅父母无恙耶？”沈始敢仔细端详，嗫嚅而对曰：“非我三姑母之大表兄乎？久出不通音信，自幼未闻读书应试，何富贵至是？”刘笑曰：“我非中堂，是其仆也。

    Explanation

    252

    "What crime have I committed to deserve this disgrace?" Liu Fú climbed into the carriage and, upon hearing the man speak with a Southern accent, ordered him to be brought to the front of the carriage. He examined his appearance carefully and then instructed that he be taken to the back courtyard, warning that he must not be allowed to escape, and that Liu would return to question him. There was a sudden, loud response, and Shen was taken away. The literary elite, witnessing the commotion, drove their carriages away quickly. Liu returned to the courtyard and asked where the guest was. The others brought Shen to the front of the steps and ordered him to kneel. Liu carefully questioned his place of origin and family ties, to which Shen replied in a trembling voice. Liu dismissed all the attendants, took Shen into the room, and seated him in the place of honor, saying, "Do you know me, Mr. Liu? Are your parents-in-law well?" Shen, now daring to look more closely, hesitantly replied, "Are you not the distant cousin of my aunt? You've been out for a long time without any news, and since childhood, I've never heard that you studied or took exams. How have you become so wealthy and noble?" Liu smiled and said, "I am not the Prime Minister; I am his servant."

    253

    ”沈咤曰：“均是仆也，兄何其荣，弟何其辱？愿留而受业于门。”刘曰：“弟当勉力上进，勿以区区为事。”沈曰：“弟目不识丁，力难胜甲。是文武俱无进途矣。”刘曰：“有志者事竟成，乘此青年，尚可为学，但求识字作书，谅无难者。兄之大儿现就师傅，弟伴读有人，便可研究矣。”乃为沈薰沐更衣，颐养月余，送之入塾。其子之师系名进士，体主人意，不过欲沈略知文墨，故日教以十余字，及临摩仿版而已。

    Explanation

    253

    Se exclaimed, "We are both servants, yet you are so honored while I am so disgraced. I wish to stay and study under your guidance." Liu replied, "You should strive to make progress and not be discouraged by small matters." Se said, "I am illiterate and physically unable to serve in the military. Thus, both the literary and martial paths are closed to me." Liu said, "Where there’s a will, there’s a way. Take advantage of your youth to pursue learning. Just aim to learn to read and write, which should not be too difficult. My eldest son is already under a teacher, and you will have a companion for studying, so you can begin your studies." Liu then had Se bathe and change clothes, nourishing him for over a month before sending him to school. The teacher of Liu’s son was a renowned scholar who understood the master’s intention and aimed only to give Se a basic knowledge of literature and writing, so he taught him a dozen or so characters each day and had him practice copying.

    254

    攻苦年余，居然腹中有二三千字，腕下亦笔画分明，遽夸于刘曰：“弟之学虽不能拾青紫，以此应酬，似不欲多让人矣。”刘质诸师，师曰：“令表弟非科甲中人。以就异途，远胜于没字碑矣。”刘大悦，谓沈曰：“本欲为弟纳一官，但官场中规矩礼节，弟尚未谙。兄于中堂处求书，弟携往畿南制军处，图一席地，以便观习仪文，留心从事，勿怠荒也。”遂具衣服车马送之保阳，以书于制军。制军见系中堂所嘱，鞠躬迎之，问知来意，即进首郡太守而告以故。太守以适需记室为请，制军悦，推荐与之，太守往拜，以岁修三百金，延沈入幕。其幕中人争先媚之，通谱结盟，无不曲尽其道。数日后，司记室之仆以禀启书函请其登复，沈阅之不解，作色曰：“是岂予所为哉？”怒掷置之。仆诺诺而退，告于太守，太守曰：“汝等不自小心，触先生怒矣。此等寻常答复，何足以烦大手笔？如有疑难之事，我自恳之。”从此仆不敢以书札进矣。

    Explanation

    254

    After enduring hardship for more than a year, he suddenly found himself with two or three thousand words in his mind and his hand could also write clearly. He immediately boasted to Liu, saying, "My learning is not enough to attain high office, but with this, I can handle matters without being outdone by others." Liu consulted his teacher, who said, "Your cousin is not a scholar-official. By taking an alternative path, he is far better than an empty stone tablet." Liu was greatly pleased and told Shen, "I originally wanted to help you get a position, but you are not yet familiar with the rules and etiquette of the bureaucracy. I will write a letter to the Minister, and you will take it to the Governor of Ji South, to secure a position where you can observe and learn the procedures, and be diligent in your work." He then provided clothes, carriage, and horses to send him to Baoyang, and wrote a letter to the Governor. The Governor, seeing that it was at the request of the Minister, bowed and welcomed him. After understanding his purpose, he immediately went to the Chief Prefect of the province and informed him of the matter. The Prefect, seeing that he needed a secretary, requested the Governor's recommendation, who was pleased and recommended him. The Prefect then went to invite him, offering an annual salary of three hundred gold pieces to bring him into the entourage. The people in his entourage competed to flatter him, exchanged genealogies, and formed alliances, all going to great lengths to please him. A few days later, the servant of the secretary brought a request for a reply, and when he read it, he did not understand and angrily said, "Is this something I would do?" He angrily threw it aside. The servant nodded and retreated, reporting to the Prefect, who said, "You did not take care, and angered the Master. Such ordinary replies are not worth the trouble of a great writer. If there are difficult matters, I will handle them myself." From then on, the servant dared not bring any letters to him.

    255

    沈终日闲暇，惟潜窥太守延客，退与幕中人议论仪注而已。同事者入相学，觉其腹内空空，然以丞相私人，皆欲得其欢心，曰：“兄如有事参商，弟辈当力图报效，勿以外人目之，则幸甚。”沈知众心之向己也，故居停所嘱之事，皆倩人主稿。如是者半载，心切不安，自计仪注已习，可以归矣。遂告太守曰：“昨有都中人来，奉中堂谕，唤予入都，未审其何事也。然不敢逗留，请辞。

    Explanation

    255

    Shen spends his entire day in leisure, only secretly observing the governor entertaining guests, and later discussing ceremonial protocols with the people in the inner office. His colleagues enter to learn from him, but they realize that his mind is empty. However, as the private person of the chancellor, all wish to gain his favor, saying, "If there is anything you need assistance with, we, your juniors, will strive to repay your kindness, do not regard us as outsiders, then we will be very fortunate." Shen realizes that the hearts of the people are inclined toward him, so he entrusts the matters he is staying to handle, and has others draft the main documents. Like this for half a year, his heart is restless and uneasy. He calculates that the ceremonial protocols have been learned, and it is time to return. He then tells the governor, "Yesterday, someone from the capital came, carrying the instruction from the central hall, calling me to enter the capital, I do not know what it is for. However, I dare not delay, I ask to leave."

    256

    ”太守曰：“方仗鸿才以匡不逮，岂可遣别？如有不适之处，何妨明以告我，自当谨领大教。”沈曰：“主人忠且敬矣，予又何辞，第中堂之命，不敢不应也。”太守知不可留，以告制军。制军曰：“客若不得已而去，我何以对中堂？无已，姑设法以缓其行，我等共筹一款，以壮行色。”太守唯唯，乃与宪司及幕中人递为设饯，日以优觞款之，嘱首邑长布告各牧令，佥使致赆，集五千余金，以宪司命馈之。沈大悦，满载而归，听命于刘。刘为谋捌，以参军职入军功，加等优叙，铨得县令，绕道反故乡，挈母妻至任。

    Explanation

    256

    The governor said, "Relying on your great talent to assist me in my shortcomings, how can I send you away? If there are any discomforts, feel free to tell me openly, and I will certainly follow your guidance with respect." Shen replied, "The master of the house is both loyal and respectful; what more can I say? I dare not disobey the order of the master of the house." Knowing that Shen could not be retained, the governor informed the general. The general said, "If the guest is forced to leave, how can I face the master of the house? If there is no other way, let us devise a plan to delay his departure. We will all contribute to a gift to make his departure more grand." The governor nodded in agreement and, together with the censor and the officials in the governor's office, they in turn set up farewell feasts, daily offering fine wine to honor him. He instructed the chief magistrate of the prefectural capital to inform all local officials, and they all sent gifts, collecting more than five thousand gold pieces. According to the order of the censor, they sent it to Shen. Shen was very pleased and returned with a full load, obeying Liu. Liu devised a plan, and Shen entered military service as a military advisor, and his merits were recorded with special commendation. He was appointed as a county magistrate, took a detour to return to his hometown, and brought his mother and wife to his post.

    257

    缘自起于寒微，知民间之疾苦，故其理事也勤而恕。对牍公庭，必使无情者向人服礼而已，曰：“予为朝廷执法，一挞汝容何伤？但汝子孙或有荣显之日，知之者讥议其祖父为刑伤过犯，没齿之恨，将为世仇。且讼不可终，每见胜者，亦倾家破产，矧败者乎？何如因予言以宽解之，将见日后之不可思议矣。”悟其意者感且泣。邑以大治，以是荐升太守。值丞相败，刘亦得重罪，适闻母讣音，致仕归。方其在任时，生二子，谓其妻曰：“予年将半百，有子已足。况再索耶？予意送汝归故里，以教予子。

    Explanation

    257

    From humble beginnings, he understood the hardships of the common people, and thus administered affairs diligently and with leniency. In the public court, he always made those who were indifferent or unfeeling submit to propriety, saying, "I am enforcing the law on behalf of the court. A single lash will not harm you. But if your descendants have days of honor and prominence, those who know will criticize your ancestors for being injured by punishment, harboring a lifelong grudge, which will become an eternal enemy. Moreover, lawsuits cannot be resolved; often, even the winners go bankrupt, how much more so the losers? How about using my words to offer a lenient resolution? You will see the unimaginable results in the future." Those who understood his meaning were moved to tears. The county was greatly governed, and he was therefore recommended and promoted to the position of magistrate. When the chancellor was defeated, Liu also received a severe punishment. Just hearing the news of his mother's death, he retired and returned home. While he was in office, he had two sons. He told his wife, "I am approaching fifty years old. Having children is enough. How much more so, to seek more? My intention is to send you back to your hometown to educate my children."

    258

    是方成童，尚不知习俗，假令长在署中，其安分者不过无能，其不安分者则竟败类矣。盖衙门之所尚者，骄惰奢侈，娼酒赌博，无所不为。此则知识未定之人之所大忌。天下之不为习俗所移出类拔萃者，能有几人乎？予以何等起家，祖功宗德，尽于此矣。安敢望后世有豪杰之士耶？汝以二子归，先训之读二三年，可知其志，倘能读则善，否则农工商贾各予一业，决不至饿殍者。若曰少君而已矣，此不但饿殍，皆俳优之流亚也。”其母妻皆性喜俭朴，亦恶坐享，欣然同归。教二子读，不成，置良田百亩，以一子督耕，一子学贾。及沈归来，就其子之才，在乡增田，在市立肆，各守其业。而沈则布衣草履，往来纠察，忘其曾为中大夫者。优游林下二十余年以终。

    Explanation

    258

    The young man is now in his late teens, still unaware of the customs and ways of the world. If he were to grow up in a government office, those who are content with their lot will be no more than mediocre, while those who are not content will ultimately become corrupt. For government offices are places where arrogance, laziness, and extravagance prevail, and where immoral activities such as prostitution, drinking, and gambling are common. This is a great danger for those whose knowledge is not yet firmly established. How many people in the world can resist the corrupting influence of society and stand out as exceptional? What have I done to establish my family and honor the achievements of my ancestors? How dare I hope for future generations to produce heroes? You take your two sons home, and first educate them to read for two or three years. If they show aptitude, then that is good; otherwise, they should be given a profession in farming, commerce, or industry, so that they will not end up as starving corpses. If you say they are just young lords, this is not only a fate worse than starvation, but also one that places them among the lowest of performers. His mother and wife both prefer frugality and dislike idleness, and they happily returned home together. They educated the two sons to read, and when they failed, they provided them with a hundred acres of good land. One son was put in charge of farming, and the other was sent to learn trade. When the speaker returned, he arranged for his sons based on their talents: one increased the land in the countryside, and the other established a shop in the city, each following their respective professions. The speaker himself lived a simple life, wearing plain clothes and walking barefoot, going back and forth to oversee matters, forgetting that he had once been a high-ranking official. He lived a leisurely life in seclusion for over twenty years until his death.

    259

    芗厈曰：贤哉，沈太守也。吾见纳资之宦伙矣，惟知酒色是图，骄倨自好，畴肯顾及民事乎？其子孙则效乃祖乃父之尤而过之，畴肯各安事业乎？恃其赫赫扬扬，自以为百年永享耳，曾不转瞬而冰清瓦解者不知凡几。乃为民计，为子计，勤勤恳恳，惟求得当者，竟有一于此。吾故曰：贤哉，沈太守也。
    　　
    　　第七卷
    　　
    　　先觉僧
    　　
    　　朔平陆太守，浙人也。其恭人好佛，得一玉大士像，至诚供奉，持诵大悲咒甚虔。中年举一子，自幼茹素，三四岁随母礼拜，诵咒则喜，使之入塾则瞑目枯坐。父师以其幼也，未之责备。八岁时，太守强其食肉，大呕尽吐，即心厌尘凡矣。窃母白金八十，望五台而逸。相距不远，竟日而至。向僧礼拜，求为剃度。僧不知来历，无敢收之，乃遍拜坐禅者。内有游方和尚，同乡人也，悯其幼小无依，愿为带回故里。

    Explanation

    259

    Xiang'an said: How virtuous is Governor Shen! I have seen many officials who gained their positions through financial contributions; they only sought wine and women, were proud and arrogant, and cared little for the affairs of the people. Their descendants followed in their footsteps and even surpassed them, showing no inclination to find contentment in their own pursuits. They relied on their prominence and arrogance, believing they would enjoy their wealth for a hundred years, only to see it all collapse in an instant. Yet, there are those who, in caring for the people and their children, are diligent and earnest, seeking what is appropriate—how many such individuals are there? Thus, I say: How virtuous is Governor Shen!

    Chapter 7

    The Enlightened Monk

    The governor of Shuoping County in Lu province was a native of Zhejiang. His wife, a respected lady, was devoted to Buddhism and obtained a jade statue of a Bodhisattva, which she venerated with utmost sincerity, reciting the Great Compassion Mantra with great devotion. In her middle years, she bore a son who, from a young age, adhered to a vegetarian diet. At the age of three or four, he followed his mother in worship and found joy in reciting the mantra. When he was sent to school, he would sit with closed eyes, motionless. His father and teacher, considering his young age, did not reprimand him. At eight years old, the governor forced him to eat meat, causing him to vomit violently, and from that moment, he loathed the mundane world. He secretly took eighty taels of silver from his mother and fled toward Wutai Mountain. The distance was not far, and he arrived the same day. He bowed to the monks and asked to be ordained. The monks, unaware of his background, dared not take him in, so he went to pay respects to those sitting in meditation. Among them was a wandering monk from his hometown, who pitied him for being young and without support, and wished to take him back to his hometown.

    260

    苦求为僧，相携至天台，投善知识，为之摹顶授记，赐予法名曰“先觉”。师问之曰：“浮屠之学，不出教、禅二端。所谓教者，唯识宗旨，即三藏佛经九千五百卷，以持诵而证果者也。所谓禅者，东来初祖达摩大师，宏教外别传之旨，不立文字而见性成佛。其后派虽分五宗，不出静定而已。汝愿何学？”先觉曰：“弟子不识字，何必受文字之障？愿归禅门。”乃授以秘密法旨，守之无失，惟精惟勤至十八岁，能入定数十日，历受三道戒，得衣钵度牒，别师外出云游。

    Explanation

    260

    He earnestly sought to become a monk and accompanied his companion to TianTai Mountain, where he sought out a wise and virtuous teacher. The teacher bestowed upon him a blessing and a record of his spiritual progress, granting him the Buddhist name "Xianjue." The teacher asked him, "The teachings of Buddhism are not beyond the two main branches: the study of scriptures (教) and meditation (禅). The study of scriptures refers to the teachings of the Yogacara school, which involve reciting the vast collection of Buddhist scriptures, traditionally said to number 9,500 volumes, in order to achieve enlightenment. Meditation refers to the teachings of the first patriarch of Chinese Buddhism, Bodhidharma, who spread the idea that true Buddhist teachings are transmitted outside the conventional scriptures, emphasizing direct experience and insight without relying on written texts. Although the teachings of meditation are divided into five schools, they all emphasize stillness and tranquility. What do you wish to learn?" Xianjue replied, "I cannot read or write, so why should I be burdened by the constraints of written texts? I wish to join the Chan (Zen) school." The teacher then bestowed upon him secret teachings, instructing him to guard them without loss and to practice with utmost diligence and effort. By the age of eighteen, he was able to enter meditation for dozens of days. He received the three types of precepts, obtained the robe, bowl, and official document that certify a monk's ordination, and parted from his teacher to wander and travel.

    261

    至武林之灵隐寺，遇异僧溷迹于火工者，与人不言不笑，见先觉，邀之入室，自言系大唐时人，历今千余岁矣。立愿食亿万僧而后圆正果，是以在香积厨作食。且曰：“吾与汝有前缘，故告汝此非栖息之地，以北去为善。”先觉求其指示，则曰：“吾除执爨外无法可传。”乃别去。先觉遵其教欲去，突遇旧仆，再拜曰：“公子何往？先太尊与太夫人自失公子后悲痛甚，四处招寻，逾五六载，相继而逝。大公子扶榇旋里，缘先太尊在日，曾为公子聘冯御史女，今已及笄，催完姻者屡矣。大公子遣奴辈多人相觅，小人幸遇，必请公子同归。

    Explanation

    261

    To the Lingyin Temple in West Lake, Xianjue encountered a strange monk who was blending in among the kitchen workers. The monk neither spoke nor smiled. Seeing Xianjue, he invited him into the room and said, "I am a person from the Tang Dynasty, having lived for over a thousand years. I have vowed to eat millions of monks before achieving enlightenment, which is why I work in the kitchen of the Fragrant Accumulation. I have a predestined connection with you, so I tell you that it would be good for you to go north." Xianjue asked him to show the way, but the monk said, "I have no other method to teach besides cooking." Then he left. Xianjue followed his advice and wanted to leave. Suddenly, he met an old servant who bowed and said, "Young master, where are you going? After losing you, the late father and mother were very sad. They searched everywhere for over five or six years and died one after another. The eldest son brought the coffin back home. While the late father was alive, he had already arranged for the young master to marry the daughter of an imperial censor, who is now of marriageable age. There have been many attempts to finalize the marriage. The eldest son sent many servants to look for you. I am fortunate to have met you, and I will definitely ask the young master to return home."

    262

    ”先觉知父母已故，大恸几绝，随家人回见，乃兄劝之易服毕姻，谓新妇曰：“我与汝了此一段因缘，数乃前定，但我已证罗汉果，不通人道，留此无益。我从此行矣，去留由汝。”新妇亦不挽留，曰：“我知之已久，亦愿归依佛法。”先觉授之以禅。乃携母遗之玉大士像，不别而遁。北走至燕山之西域寺，爱其岩洞幽寂，扶藤而下，面溪背壁，趺坐于中，日有寺内人来给食。坐逾两载，功益精进，忽闻车轰雷吼之声，山谷震动，见大蟒探首欲相食，先觉疑神静摄，听之而已。顷刻无蟒。次日复然。自忖此地缘满，故妖魔起。遂入都经历各寺，未遇高人，终日静定而已。其眉目间光华灿烂，迥别凡流。诸王与公卿大臣见而异之，共保举入万寿寺方丈，坐年余，获布施万余金，曰：“此势利地，非修真所也。不可堕落。”乃封其金于库而夜遁。爰朝五台，出嘉峪关历西域，寻访佛迹。到人所罕到之区，无所遇而还。

    Explanation

    262

    An Enlightened One learned that his parents had passed away and was overcome with great grief, nearly losing consciousness. He followed his family back home, where his elder brother advised him to change his mourning attire and complete the wedding. Speaking to his new wife, he said, "I and you conclude this segment of karmic connection, which was predestined. However, I have attained Arhatship and cannot interact with humans; staying here is of no benefit. I will leave from here, and whether to leave or stay is up to you." The new wife also did not try to keep him, saying, "I have known this for a long time and also wish to follow Buddhism." The Enlightened One taught her meditation. He then carried his mother's jade statue of the Great Master and left without saying goodbye. Walking north to the Western Temple of Yan Mountains, he loved the secluded and quiet cave, descended by climbing vines, and sat cross-legged in the middle, facing the stream and with his back to the cliff. Every day, people from the temple came to provide food. He sat for more than two years, progressing increasingly in his practice. Suddenly, he heard the sound of a car like thunder, and the mountains and valleys shook. He saw a large python sticking its head out to eat him. The Enlightened One doubtfully but calmly focused and just listened to it. In a moment, there was no python. The next day, it happened again. He thought that the karma here was complete, so demons arose. He then went to the capital and visited various temples, but did not meet any great masters. He spent the whole day in a state of calmness. His eyebrows and eyes were radiant with light, completely different from ordinary people. Kings, princes, and high officials were amazed by him and jointly recommended him to become the abbot of Wanshou Temple. He served as abbot for over a year and received donations worth tens of thousands of gold pieces. He said, "This place is full of worldly power, not a place for true cultivation. One must not fall into corruption." He sealed the gold in the treasury and fled at night. Then he went to Mount Wutai, left through the Jiayu Pass, and traveled to the Western Regions, seeking out the traces of the Buddha. He reached areas rarely visited by people but found nothing and returned.

    263

    游终南山，遍拜诸寺，佥在山之阳。其山阴相隔黄流，望之树木阴森，无敢入者。即有好事之人，去亦不反，相传为栖真之所。先觉欣然愿往，或阻之，不听。携带干粮，渡河而入，见古木万株，参天覆地，高皆数寻，枝叶相接，其下朦朦胧胧，微可辨路而已。先觉由之，日则攀葛结藤，夜则调息入定，约行七八日，始出树林，则现奇峰叠嶂，千态万状，或茏葱以高，或崭崱而削，或崇岺以幽，或崎岖而险。径路盘曲，似有人行者。复数日出山坳，则势更嵣莽，莫穷莫尽，然皆秀色可餐，无虎狼恶兽患。遥望山腰，时见石龛隐隐，有人趺坐。

    Explanation

    263

    He journeyed to Mount Zhongnan, visiting all the temples there, which were all located on the southern side of the mountain. The northern side of the mountain was separated by the Yellow River; looking at it, the trees appeared gloomy and ominous, and no one dared to enter. Even if there were those with a liking for adventure, they would not return, and it was said to be a place where true sages dwell. The one who had awakened was eager to go, though others tried to dissuade him, but he would not listen. Carrying dry provisions, he crossed the river and entered, where he saw countless ancient trees towering into the sky, their branches and leaves interwoven, casting a dim and hazy light beneath them, through which the path could barely be discerned. The awakened one followed the path, climbing vines and grasping lianas by day, and regulating his breath and entering meditation by night. After traveling for about seven or eight days, he finally emerged from the forest, where he beheld strange peaks and layered mountains, each with a thousand forms and ten thousand shapes—some lush and towering, some sharp and sheer, some lofty and secluded, some winding and perilous. The path was winding, as if someone had walked it before. After several more days, emerging from the mountain pass, the landscape became even more vast and boundless, with no end in sight, yet all was breathtaking in beauty, with no danger from tigers, wolves, or fierce beasts. Looking afar at the mountain’s waist, one could sometimes see faint stone niches, with someone sitting in meditation.

    264

    第四面峭壁，无路可通，不知从何处登也。先觉呼之不应，膜拜之亦不答，又无水可饮，虽闻潺溪之声，而溪皆深邃，不得下，乃有退心，迎面一古衣冠道人来，先觉欢欣相接，牵其衣裾而拜，尊之曰“大仙”。其人笑曰：“予凡夫耳，焉得仙？仙人肯使汝见耶？”相与就石磴共坐，叩所从来，其人曰：“予世居越国，徐姓。秦皇时为役夫，见楚汉争雄，投楚为卒，乌江之败，逃入此山，不复出矣。”先觉告以渴甚，乃身解佩匙，即于石上穿之，得水与饮，清凉渗骨。连给二匙，收藏欲去。先觉曰：“弟子尚未足也。”其人曰：“不但汝渴已已，即不食，亦无碍矣。何必多饮！”先觉犹执裾不释。

    Explanation

    264

    On the fourth side, there was a sheer cliff with no path to pass through, and it was unknown from where one could ascend. Xianjue called out but received no response, and even when he prostrated himself, there was no reply. Moreover, there was no water to drink, although he could hear the sound of a stream, but the stream was too deep to reach. Thus, he began to feel discouraged. Suddenly, an old man dressed in ancient attire approached. Xianjue welcomed him joyfully, pulled at his sleeves and bowed, respectfully calling him "Great Immortal." The old man smiled and said, "I am just an ordinary person, how could I be an immortal? Would the immortals allow you to see me?" They sat together on a stone step, and Xianjue asked about his origins. The old man replied, "I have lived in the state of Yue all my life, and my surname is Xu. During the time of Emperor Qin, I was a laborer. I witnessed the struggle for supremacy between Chu and Han, joined the Chu forces as a soldier, and after the defeat at the Wu River, I fled into this mountain and have not come out since." Xianjue told him of his great thirst, and the old man then took off his pendant, pierced the stone with it, and obtained water to drink. The water was cool and refreshing to the bone. He gave Xianjue two more portions, then collected his things and was about to leave. Xianjue said, "I am not yet satisfied." The old man replied, "Not only are you thirsty, but even if you don't eat, it will not matter. Why drink so much?" Xianjue still held onto his sleeves and did not let go.

    265

    哀求超度。其人曰：“道不同，不相为谋，我非汝师，汝必欲度，不见前山又有人来耶？”先觉回首间，一阵清风，绝裾而去，手中仅留深蓝布一握，其人不知何往。乃以布裹所携之玉大士像，自饮石髓后，觉身体益轻健，不食不饥，日行四五百里，遂出终南。游山左，不复反。或曰在青莱之间，山深不知处耳。
    　　
    　　华疯子
    　　
    　　江右华三祝，幼名多官，其父母晚年得之，溺爱过甚，听其所为，读书不就，亦不事别业，终日游荡而已。见鸣钲开道者过，随之入衙，观其升堂理事，詈人挞人皆不敢违拗，心甚艳之。于是归家演习，亲友俱以疯子目之。逢人则问：官从何来？有诳之者曰：“官易事耳，但须北京去见皇帝，求之必得。”乃窃其父金数两，往北逃逸。至浦口，一无所有矣。遇官舫之赴楚者觅纤手，昧然应募，日行百里，惟问北京何时可到。众知其疯，皆玩戏之，惟官舫中一叟屡自顾之，周其衣食。

    Explanation

    265

    He pleaded for salvation. The other person said, "If our paths are different, we cannot plan together. I am not your teacher, but if you insist on being saved, have you not seen someone else coming from the front of the mountain?" The enlightened one turned around, and a gust of wind swept him away, his robe torn off, leaving behind only a handful of deep blue cloth. The other person did not know where he had gone. He then wrapped the cloth around the jade statue of the Great Master he had brought with him. After drinking the essence of the stone, he felt his body become lighter and stronger, not eating yet not feeling hungry, able to travel four or five hundred miles a day. He eventually left Mount Zhongnan and wandered to the east, never returning. Some said he was in the region between Qing and Lai, where the mountains are deep and his whereabouts are unknown.

    Hua the Madman

    Hua Sanzhu from Jiangxi, whose childhood name was Duoguan, was the child of his parents in their old age, and they doted on him excessively, allowing him to do as he pleased. He did not succeed in his studies and did not engage in other professions, wandering aimlessly all day. He would follow a procession of officials into the government building, watching them ascend the hall and handle affairs, scolding and beating people, all of whom dared not disobey. He greatly admired this. Returning home, he practiced this behavior, and his friends and relatives all called him a madman. He would ask people, "Where does the official come from?" Someone lied to him, saying, "Being an official is easy; you just need to go to Beijing to see the emperor, and you will surely get it." He then stole several taels of gold from his father and fled north. Upon reaching Pu Port, he had nothing left. He encountered a government boat going to Chu and, in a dazed state, volunteered as a boatman, traveling a hundred miles a day, only asking when he could reach Beijing. Everyone knew he was crazy and played tricks on him, but only an old man on the government boat repeatedly looked after him, providing him with food and clothing.

    266

    一日遇飓风，停泊闲地，华在小庙檐下坐卧，忽叟来邀之入庙，密语之曰：“舫内之官是吾子张某，其貌与汝无异。即至亲亦难辨认。以吏员铨得楚县尉，至楚赴任，不意昨得暴疾卒矣。我与眷属贫不能回家，依我计议，可将汝衣与我子互易，汝承我子职，官中所得割半均分，则两全其事矣。”华如其所愿，疯痰顿愈。是夜，潜相易换，明日，舟子唤疯子，则见其倒毙于庙矣。叟为买棺厝之，遂赴任。分衙署为两宅，与眷属各居。嫂事张妻，叟为经理出入，荏苒三年，各分得千金。

    Explanation

    266

    One day, Hu encountered a typhoon and anchored in a quiet place. He was sitting and lying under the eaves of a small temple when an old man suddenly came and invited him into the temple. The old man whispered to him, saying, "The official on the boat is my son Zhang, and his appearance is no different from yours. Even close relatives would find it hard to recognize him. He was appointed as the county magistrate of Chu through the official selection process and went there to take up his post. Unfortunately, he died suddenly yesterday. My family and I are poor and cannot return home. Follow my plan: you can exchange clothes with my son, and you will take over his position. Split the income from the office equally, and both of us will benefit." Hu followed his wishes, and his madness and phlegm suddenly improved. That night, they secretly exchanged clothes. The next day, the boatman called for the madman and found him lying dead in the temple. The old man bought a coffin and buried him, and then Hu took up his post. He divided the government office into two residences and lived separately with his family. His sister-in-law took care of Zhang's wife, and the old man managed the affairs in and out. Three years passed gradually, and each of them received a thousand gold coins.

    267

    忽闻新太守莅任，探知为龚姓，系华氏姑母之子，以科甲由部曹出守是邦者，难与相见，然在其属下，不敢不谒。是日，以张姓职名入见，太守睹其状貌，核其履历，心甚疑之。入见太夫人，言其故，太夫人泣曰：“尔舅父仅有一子，不知何往，吾亦欲观其貌，纵非真者，藉以解渴想耳。”太守唯唯，立命传见。华俯首攒眉，置身无地，惟有叩首而已。太夫人命仆扶之起，详加体察，曰：“是吾侄也。奈何勿认？”华益惶恐曰：“野鸟何敢冒凤，貌相似者，自古有之。孔子阳贷，圣狂不类耳。”即告退。

    Explanation

    267

    Suddenly word came that the new magistrate had arrived in office. It was learned that he was surnamed Gong, and that he was the son of the aunt of the Hu family — a man who had risen through the imperial examination system from a central government official to govern this region. Such a person was difficult to meet, yet as his subordinate, one dared not fail to pay respects. On that day, he presented himself under the surname Zhang and with his official title. The magistrate observed his appearance and examined his credentials, and was greatly suspicious. He then went to see the magistrate’s wife and explained the matter. The magistrate’s wife wept and said, “Your uncle had only one son, and we do not know where he has gone. I, too, wish to see his face. Even if it is not the real one, it will at least relieve my longing.” The magistrate nodded in agreement and immediately ordered him to be summoned. Hua bowed his head and furrowed his brow, feeling utterly at a loss, and could only kneel and bow in submission. The magistrate’s wife then ordered a servant to help him up, and she carefully observed him, saying, “This is my nephew. Why not recognize him?” Hua became even more frightened and said, “How dare a wild bird pretend to be a phoenix? There have been cases since ancient times of people resembling others. Confucius and Yangdai were not the same — a sage and a madman are not alike.” Then he withdrew.

    268

    太夫人未能挽留，目送之，将出门，忽呼其幼名曰：“多官儿安在耶？”华闻之，不觉回顾，太夫人急使仆婢群拥之入，作色视之曰：“汝是吾侄华三祝也，何得背父母瞒亲戚，好官自为耶？”华窘极，叩首曰：“非也。”太夫人曰：“吾自呼多官儿，何劳汝回首？再欲强辩，吾命汝表兄以官法治汝矣。”华至是无奈，直陈始末，太守骇曰：“此事性命相关，弟奈何为之？其速回乃任，与张叟交割，我以公事参革，弟即嘱张叟代报病故，潜回家乡。我为弟以真姓名别纳一官可也。”华从之，得以父子团聚，保其首领以殁。

    Explanation

    268

    The lady mother could not detain him, waving goodbye with her eyes. As he was about to leave, she suddenly called out his childhood name: "Where is my dear little official?" Hu heard her and unconsciously looked back. The lady mother quickly had the servants and maids crowd him in and glared at him, saying, "You are my nephew, Hu Sanzhu. How could you betray your parents and hide from your relatives, going off to be an official on your own?" Hu was extremely embarrassed and bowed, saying, "No, it's not like that." The lady mother said, "I called you my dear little official, why did you look back? If you try to argue further, I will have your cousin punish you according to official law." Hu, having no choice, told the whole story directly. The prefect exclaimed in horror, "This matter concerns life and death. Why did you do this? You should quickly return to your post and hand over to Old Zhang. I will dismiss you for official misconduct. You must instruct Old Zhang to report your death and return secretly to your hometown. I will help you take another official position under your real name." Hu followed his advice and was able to reunite with his father, preserving his head and dying peacefully.

    269

    骗子十二则
    　　
    　　京师某王，因公受罚，缘是致贫，众所知也。时值创建大寺，布施已成，惟乏殿材。匠人各处构求，乃有随官服色者，登大匠之门，告曰，“我为某王府四品护卫，今王当窘急之际，欲货其殿廷旧料，易以轻巧之木，冀得余资，以济急需。”匠知王为开国勋裔，其府第皆梓楠为之，欣然愿售。约日往观，其人以亲王名柬至王府，告门官曰：“我为某亲王护卫，今王欲新殿廷，幕府内规模宏大，谕我带同匠人观之，以便如式构造。”王许之，乃邀匠人入，指点其梁楹，筹度其丈尺，详细阅毕，偕至匠寓评价。

    Explanation

    269

    The Twelve Scams

    In the capital city, there was a prince who was punished for official duties, leading to his poverty, a fact known to all. At the time, a large temple was being constructed, and donations had been completed, but only the materials for the main hall were lacking. Craftsmen were searching everywhere for the necessary materials, and a person dressed as an official approached the master builder, saying, "I am a fourth-rank bodyguard of a certain prince. Now, the prince is in a time of urgent need and wishes to sell the old materials from his hall, replacing them with light and flexible wood in order to obtain additional funds to help in his urgent need." The craftsmen knew that the prince was a descendant of founding heroes, and that his palace was made of cypress and nanmu, so they were glad to purchase the materials. They agreed to visit on a certain day, and the person used a letter of introduction from a prince to gain access to the prince's palace, telling the gatekeeper, "I am a bodyguard of a certain prince. Now, the prince wishes to renovate his hall, and the scale inside his office is grand. He has ordered me to bring the craftsmen to see it, so that they can construct according to the model." The prince approved, and they invited the craftsmen in, pointing out the beams and pillars, measuring their dimensions, and after a thorough inspection, they went together to the craftsmen's residence for evaluation.

    270

    其人曰：“先王成此殿，费十万金，汝愿以若干售之，不妨明言。”匠曰：“材料己旧，大而无当，将必改为小用。不过万金而已。”其人故作不愿，往来数次，曰：“王今无奈，姑以售汝，当在某庄立券，汝先往俟之。”是日，朱转华毂，王果至庄。其人为前驱，带匠人入庄，王南面坐，匠跽请书券，王颔之，命从官书就，亲笔签押，先索定契银三千两，余俟拆换之日准算。其人偕匠入城交银，并索费银，亦先给三百，约日而散。至日，大匠带人往拆殿廷，门官拒之，匠告以故，门官入白王，王召匠人，仰观王貌，非前日之人矣。知为拐骗，无言而退。

    Explanation

    270

    The person said, "The former king built this palace, costing ten thousand taels of silver. How much would you like to sell it for? Feel free to say it openly." The craftsman replied, "The materials are already old, large but not suitable for its intended purpose. It will definitely be used for smaller purposes. It won't be more than ten thousand taels." The person pretended to be unwilling, after several rounds of negotiation, and said, "The king is now helpless, temporarily sell it to you. The contract will be signed at a certain estate. You go ahead and wait for it." On that day, the carriage was adorned with red and gold decorations, and the king indeed arrived at the estate. The person led the way, bringing the craftsmen into the estate. The king sat facing south, and the craftsman knelt to request the signing of the contract. The king nodded in agreement, ordered the officials to write it down, and signed it personally. First, he asked for a deposit of three thousand taels, and the rest would be settled on the day of demolition and replacement. The person accompanied the craftsmen to the city to pay the money, also requested additional fees, and gave three hundred taels upfront. They agreed on a date and then dispersed. On that day, the master craftsman led people to dismantle the palace hall. The gatekeeper refused them, and the craftsman explained the reason. The gatekeeper went inside to report to the king. The king summoned the craftsman, looked up and saw that the king's appearance was not the same as the previous day. Knowing it was a fraud, he silently withdrew.

    271

    有衣冠华丽者，乘车带仆至质库，脱金手镯二以质钱。掌柜人细阅之，黄赤无伪，秤各重五两，问需京钱五百贯，掌柜人还之，其人让至三百贯，北地尚钱帖，如数给之而去。旁一丐者，脱其破袄质二十贯，掌柜人叱之，丐笑曰：“假金镯当钱三百贯，我袄虽破烂，尚非膺物，何不值二十贯耶？”掌柜人心疑，复阅其镯，则已被易包金者。问丐何以知之，丐曰：“此有名骗子手，我知其寓处。”掌柜人愿给丐钱两贯，偕往寻之。

    Explanation

    271

    A man dressed in fine clothes arrived at the pawnshop with a servant, removing two gold bracelets to pawn for money. The pawnshop manager carefully examined them, finding them pure gold, each weighing five taels. He asked for 500 guan of money, but the man refused and reduced the price to 300 guan. In the north, paper money was still in use, so the manager gave him the full amount and he left. Nearby, a beggar removed his tattered coat to pawn for 20 guan, but the pawnshop manager scolded him. The beggar laughed and said, "The fake gold bracelets were worth 300 guan. My coat is tattered, but it’s not counterfeit. Why is it only worth 20 guan?" The pawnshop manager became suspicious and re-examined the bracelets, discovering they had already been replaced with gilt ones. He asked the beggar how he knew, and the beggar replied, "This is a famous trickster. I know where he lives." The pawnshop manager offered to give the beggar 2 guan to help find the trickster, and they went together to seek him out.

    272

    至寓，果见其车在外，丐遥指其人，得钱脱身去矣。掌柜人入寓，则见其与显者共饮，未敢喧哗，因寓主通其仆，唤之出，与之辩论，其人曰：“物既伪，何以质钱如此之多？明是汝换我也。”互相争执，显者闻声，邀二人入，笑谓其人曰：“我辈宁吃亏，毋占便宜。不可与市井之徒较量，有失官体。足下钱尚未用，何不还之。”其人似不得已，委屈听命，乃以原钱帖赎还二镯，掌柜人欣然领去，至晚往钱局取钱，则已取去。出其帖比对，后帖系好手描摹者。复至其寓，则去已久矣。丐亦不知所往。

    Explanation

    272

    Upon arriving at the inn, the shopkeeper indeed saw the person's carriage outside. The beggar pointed at the person from a distance, and the person received money and left. When the shopkeeper entered the inn, he saw the person drinking with a prominent individual. The shopkeeper did not dare to make a commotion and, through the innkeeper, communicated with the person's servant, calling the person out to debate. The prominent person heard the commotion, invited both parties inside, and smiled, saying to the person, "We would rather suffer a loss than gain an advantage. We cannot argue with commoners, as it would be a loss of dignity for an official. Your money has not yet been used; why not return it?" The person seemed unwillingly and reluctantly obeyed, returning the two bracelets using the original money note. The shopkeeper happily took them away and, late in the day, went to the money bureau to collect the money, but it had already been taken. The shopkeeper compared the note and discovered that the later note was copied by a skilled hand. Returning to the person's residence, he found that the person had already left for a long time, and the beggar also did not know where he went.

    273

    有京卿，恶其子之不肖而逐之者。其子不知所之，后京卿出为方伯，入庙行香，其时府县以下何应者数十员，士庶环观者数百人。突有衣冠破烂者至方伯前，长跪号哭，自称情愿改过，任凭父亲处治，再不敢稍有违犯，伏望收留，叩首无算。方伯细察之，非其子也，大怒叱曰：“何处匪徒，敢冒吾子，殊属胆大！”饬役加以累绁，面交首府问罪。首府带署讯之，其人供称，前因不好读书，不受训饬，偶有触犯，被逐在外。

    Explanation

    273

    There was a high-ranking official in the capital who, disliking his son's lack of virtue, expelled him. The son, not knowing where to go, wandered aimlessly. Later, the official was appointed as a regional governor and entered the temple to burn incense. At the time, there were dozens of officials who should have responded, and hundreds of commoners gathered to watch. Suddenly, there was a person with tattered clothes who approached the governor, stood up, and wept bitterly, humbly hoping to be taken in and pleading that he would accept any punishment, vowing never to offend again. The governor closely examined him and realized that he was not his son, and in anger, he shouted, "Where did you come from, you rascal, to dare to impersonate my son? Truly a bold act!" He ordered the guards to bind him with chains and personally handed him over to the governor for punishment. The governor took him to the office for interrogation, and the person confessed, saying, "Earlier, because I did not like to read and did not accept discipline, I occasionally offended and was expelled from home."

    274

    只求为之挽回，情愿奋勉用功，不敢稍惰，有负隆恩。首府诘其家世，言之凿凿，且察其神色悲惨，语言明爽，似非作伪者。试以学问，亦能成文，信其为方伯之逐子也。留之府署，易其衣冠。公余为方伯委婉道之，方伯曰：“实非我子，若讯无为匪情事，重责递籍可也。”方伯归，与众官议之，众皆请见其人，问其志向，则遍拜而泣曰：“父性严厉，己实不才，惟有回籍应试，或得科名，以赎前衍。但无旅资，奈何？”众怜其志而哀其遇，公为资助，集千金送之去，而以责逐复方伯也。后闻家人言，始知实非方伯子。

    Explanation

    274

    Only seeking to make amends, willing to work hard and diligently, not daring to be the slightest bit lazy, not wanting to辜负 the great favor. The governor questioned his family background, and he spoke with certainty. The governor also observed his tragic expression and clear and bright speech, and it seemed not to be feigning. He was tested with knowledge and was also able to write, leading the officials to believe him to be the banished son of the Prefect. He was kept in the prefectural office, and his clothes were changed. After official duties, the Prefect gently explained to him, and the Prefect said, "It is not my son. If there is no evidence of criminal activity, a severe reprimand and expulsion from the register would suffice." The Prefect returned and discussed the matter with the officials, and all requested to see the person. They asked about his aspirations, and he prostrated himself and wept, saying, "My father is stern, and I am indeed not talented. Only to return home to take the exams, perhaps to achieve a degree, to redeem past mistakes. But lacking travel funds, what can be done?" The officials sympathized with his aspirations and pitied his circumstances, and the official provided financial assistance, collecting a thousand gold pieces to send him away, and then held him accountable to the Prefect. Later, hearing from family members, it was learned that he was not actually the Prefect's son.

    275

    越人倪某，世习申韩，由幕而官，去官复幕，在保阳待聘，住旅寓有时矣。迫切钻营，无微不至。是年冬，寓中之旁宅有人来住，章服华焕，仆从趋跄，往来之客络绎不绝，似皆宪司之纪纲也。倪询其仆，知为大名县总司阍，奉本官命，来省置办衣饰为少君完姻，并延访善于刑名、钱谷之士，以易旧友者。倪探得其情，即具衣冠往拜。其人稍闲，即与盘桓，竟成莫逆。知其性情慷爽而目不识丁，一夜，漏已三下，突有役来传本官谕帖，催其人归去。

    Explanation

    275

    A man from Yue named Ni, whose family had long practiced the teachings of Shen Buhai and Han Fei, rose from the position of a consultant to become an official, then left office to return to consultancy, waiting in BaoYang for employment opportunities. He had been living in temporary accommodations for some time, making intense efforts to seek advancement with thorough and meticulous dedication. In that winter, a wealthy and influential person moved into a house adjacent to his lodging. The visitor wore richly decorated official clothing, and his servants moved in a respectful and orderly manner. Visitors came and went in a continuous flow, seeming to be officials or subordinates of the provincial administration. Ni inquired about the visitor from his servants and learned that he was the gatekeeper of the magistrate of Daming County, who had come on orders from the magistrate to purchase clothing for the magistrate's daughter's wedding and to seek out experts in legal matters and financial administration to replace old friends. Ni discovered the situation and dressed formally to pay respects. When the visitor had a bit of free time, they spent time together, eventually becoming close friends. Ni learned that the visitor was generous and straightforward but illiterate. One night, when the third strike of the night watch had sounded, a sudden messenger arrived with a message from the magistrate, urging the visitor to return.

    276

    其人得信，叩倪寝门而告之曰：“请先生起，烦为一读来书，有役守候，不能待旦矣。”倪起而诵之，不过因办公乏人，谕令迅将什物置妥即去，并旧友辞定。所访之新友，必须于开篆以前到署等语。其人踟蹰曰：“办物不难，访友非易，先生意中有信托之人乎？”倪遂自述其历就州县，助其东人升迁者不一。其人笑曰：“吾以先生为记室耳，畴知申韩妙手近在邻居，而由外求人耶？”即请代某禀复云：以千金订定倪先生，俟各物办齐即回，不敢逗留也。倪为书就，交役竟去。

    Explanation

    276

    The messenger received the message, knocked on Mr. Ni's bedroom door, and said, "Please get up, Mr. Ni. I ask you to read the incoming letter. There is a servant waiting, and we cannot wait until dawn." Mr. Ni got up and read it. It was just because there was a lack of personnel for office work, and he was commanded to quickly arrange the items and leave, and to finalize the farewell with old friends. The new friend to be visited must arrive at the office before the start of the term. The messenger hesitated and said, "Arranging the items is not difficult, but visiting friends is not easy. Do you have someone you trust in mind?" Mr. Ni then described how he had served in various prefectures and counties, helping many officials from the east to advance. The messenger smiled and said, "I thought you were just a secretary, who knew that a master of Shang and Han is right next door and not seeking someone from outside?" He immediately asked to write a reply on behalf of someone: "To engage Mr. Ni with a thousand gold, to return immediately after all items are arranged, and not to linger." Mr. Ni wrote the letter and handed it over to the servant, who then left.

    277

    次日，其人送关书聘仪十六金来，请安侍立，不敢抗礼。其仆从咸来叩喜。倪大悦，拉其人坐曰：“相好在前，既承不弃，断不可以俗情拘也。”其人称谢侧坐，告以首饰尚未造完，俟工竣先归，当遣车奉迓耳。又数日见倪，似有不豫之色。倪惶然叩故，其人曰：“贵乡亲某号缎局内看定货物，不过偶挂数百金耳，坚持不舍，何其吝也？”倪曰：“市侩恒情无足怪者，吾与素识，代为作保，虽千金亦不勒矣。”其人曰：“如此固好，恐先生来之信某耳。”倪日。

    Explanation

    277

    The following day, the man arrived with the marriage contract and a dowry of sixteen units of currency, bowed to greet Ni and stood respectfully, not daring to be on equal terms. His servants all came to congratulate. Ni was greatly pleased and invited the man to sit, saying, "We have been friends before, and now you accept me, so we must not be bound by convention." The man thanked him and sat down, informing him that the jewelry was not yet finished and that he would return after the work was completed, at which time a carriage would be sent to welcome him. A few days later, when Ni saw the man again, he seemed to have an unhappy expression. Ni was alarmed and asked for the reason. The man said, "Your respected relative, Mr. So-and-so, had agreed on goods at his silk shop, but he only occasionally spent a few hundred units of currency, yet he insisted on not giving them up. How stingy is that?" Ni replied, "It is not surprising that merchants are so stingy; I know him well and have acted as a guarantor for him, even for a thousand units of currency, I would not force him to give them up." The man said, "That is indeed good, but I fear that you only came because of Mr. So-and-so." Ni nodded.

    278

    “忝在同署，尚何可疑，是足下多心矣。”即偕赴缎局。缘所定之物实千余金，仅付百两，余皆挂欠，故未肯与也。然局主已访得大名，确有姻事，及倪就其幕，被倪至殷勤款接。其人笑曰：“尔铺主不我信耶？请以先生为质，明年来请先生时，其价原车带到，决无贻误。”倪亦言同事之故，愿力任之。局主欣然交倪货物，给其人满载而去。次年俟至春仲，无车来接，局主怀疑，邀倪同往大名，问司阍并无其人，亦无易幕事，倪归，货其行李以偿铺账，悔恨无及。

    Explanation

    278

    Since we are in the same office, how can there be any doubt? You are being too suspicious. We immediately went together to the silk bureau. The goods ordered were worth over a thousand taels, but only a hundred taels were paid, and the rest was on credit, so they were unwilling to provide the goods. However, the bureau head had already learned of your distinguished name and there is indeed a marriage affair. When Ni joined his entourage, he was treated with great hospitality by Ni. The person smiled and said, "Does your shop owner not trust me? Please take Mr. as a guarantee. When you come to invite Mr. next year, the price will be brought by carriage, and there will be no delay." Ni also explained the reason for being colleagues and was willing to take full responsibility. The bureau head gladly handed over the goods to Ni, giving the person a full load to take away. The following year, when spring arrived, no carriage came to pick up. The bureau head became suspicious and invited Ni to go to Da Ming together. When asked the gatekeeper, there was no such person, and there was also no change in the entourage. Ni returned, sold his belongings to pay off the shop's debt, and regretted it too late.

    279

    江右某宰相，保举其门人某为江南中丞者。某感恩戴德，思有以报之。适相之次公子以书来干谒，中丞见之，其人少年俶傥，应对如流，缘书中有告助意，问其所需，以二千金为请。中丞允之，留与盘桓，则以父命促迫为辞。及回拜，则见其巨舫悬旂，行李仆从莫不眩耀。中丞意师相素尚俭朴，而其子不类，心有所疑。其幕中有同门某孝廉，以疑质之，孝廉曰：“某公子予幼曾见之，今相隔有年矣。”出其所书扇与中丞观之，楷法挺秀，笑谓孝廉曰：“明日我宴公子，屈阁下作陪，面索其书，则真伪立辨矣。

    Explanation

    279

    During the reign of a certain chancellor from Jiangxi, he recommended one of his disciples to become the governor of Jiangnan. The disciple was deeply grateful and thought of a way to repay him. Just as the chancellor's younger son came to seek an audience with a letter, the governor met him. The young man was bold and unrestrained, responding fluently. Because the letter contained a hint of seeking assistance, the governor asked what he needed, and the younger son requested two thousand taels of silver. The governor agreed to it and kept him around, but the younger son used his father's urgent summons as an excuse. When he returned to pay respects, the governor saw a large boat with flags, and his attendants and servants were all splendid. The governor had some doubts because he knew the chancellor had always been frugal and simple, while his son did not seem to be like that. In the governor's entourage, there was a fellow student who had passed the imperial examination, and the governor asked him about his doubts. The fellow student said, "I met this young man when I was young, and we have been apart for many years." The fellow student then showed the governor a fan he had written on, and the handwriting was neat and elegant. The governor smiled and said to the fellow student, "I will host a banquet for the young man tomorrow. Please join me as a guest, and ask him to write something in person. Then the authenticity will be immediately clear."

    280

    ”乃设席请公子，公子来，见孝廉先呼曰：“兄台相隔多年，尚识鄙人否？”孝廉不能辨，唯唯而已。中丞出纸求书，公子欣然允诺，命仆研墨，其仆面赤手战，目视公子，公子吟哦联句，提笔欲书，忽掷笔叱仆曰：“曷去诸！彼慢我矣。乘我有所干求之际，故索我书，无乃以卖字之交丐视我耶？”扬长出外，登舆竟去。中丞追送之，亦不回顾。中丞自悔卤莽，具黄金二百，至其舟中谢罪，强之收受而去。未几，又一中堂之幼子来，与前人名柬相同，并无信札，见之则朴素暗淡，恂恂儒雅，使孝廉相晤，各不认识。留之入署，亦愿住，索其书法亦勿辞。中丞狐疑莫解，使捷足入都探之，始知前者入骗子手矣。

    Explanation

    280

    Then he set up a feast and invited the young master. When the young master arrived, he saw the scholar-official and called out, "Brother, after many years, do you still recognize me?" The scholar-official could not identify the young master and responded with a non-committal "yes" or "indeed." The high-ranking official took out paper and requested a calligraphy. The young master gladly agreed, ordered the servant to grind ink. The servant blushed and trembled, looking at the young master. The young master recited couplets, picked up the brush to write, but suddenly threw down the brush and scolded the servant, saying, "Why are you still here? He has been disrespectful to me. He takes advantage of my need to ask something and requests my calligraphy, is it not treating me like a beggar?" He strode out, climbed into the carriage, and left abruptly. The high-ranking official chased and sent him off, but the young master did not look back. The high-ranking official regretted his hasty action, prepared two hundred gold pieces, went to the boat, apologized, and forced him to accept it before leaving. Not long after, another young son of a high-ranking official came, with the same name card as the previous one, but without a letter. When seen, he appeared simple and dim, humble and refined. He made the scholar-official meet him, but they did not recognize each other. The high-ranking official kept him in the office, and he was willing to stay. He requested his calligraphy without refusal. The high-ranking official was doubtful and could not understand, so he sent a swift messenger to the capital to investigate. Only then did he know that the former one was a fraudster.

    281

    浙有洋商金姓者，送其子侄应童子试，寓学院之东辕外。有人叩门请见，金见之，衣冠华焕，仆从甚都，访问诸生陈某有无在是。金与陈系中表，知其向在江南游幕者，其时实未回籍，乃告以故，叩其来访之意。其人踌躇曰：“陈君不来事不谐矣，我施姓，江南某科乙榜，与陈君至好。我为学使所聘，来此阅卷者。”遂告别而出门，有肩舆，施登舆由东辕直入仪门去矣。金闻学使向有货取之名，疑来人与陈必有勾串事，然亦无从探问，已置之矣。一日，偕亲友遨游西子湖，于圣因寺前遇施公，偕两客，俱翩翩少年，从仆三四人，随有舆马。遥见金来，施出队趋迎，握手道故，指一衣秋香绉袄、带盘金便帽者曰：“此学使之大公子也。

    Explanation

    281

    A foreign merchant surnamed Jin sent his nephew to take the examination for the status of a scholar (童生) and stayed at an inn outside the eastern gate of the academy. Someone knocked on the door and requested to see him. Jin saw the visitor, who was dressed in splendid attire and accompanied by well-dressed attendants. The visitor asked if Chen was present. Jin, who was related to Chen by marriage (中表), knew that Chen had been traveling in the southern region (江南) and had not yet returned home. Jin explained the situation and inquired about the visitor's purpose. The visitor hesitated and said, "Chen's absence will make our plan difficult. My name is Shi, and I obtained a second-class degree (乙榜) in a certain year in the southern region. I am a good friend of Chen. I was invited by the provincial examination commissioner (学使) to come here to examine the papers."

    The visitor then bid farewell and left through the door, accompanied by a sedan chair (肩舆). Shi entered the main gate of the academy directly by riding in the sedan chair. Jin heard that the provincial examination commissioner had a reputation for corruption (货取), and he suspected that the visitor and Chen might have colluded (勾串) in some way. However, he had no way of confirming this and put it aside.

    One day, Jin traveled with friends and relatives to the West Lake (西子湖). At the front of Saint Yin Temple (圣因寺), he encountered Mr. Shi, who was accompanied by two young men in fine clothing and several servants. There were also sedan chairs and horses with them. From a distance, Jin saw Mr. Shi step out of his sedan chair and come to greet him warmly. He pointed to a young man wearing a light green robe (秋香绉袄) and a hat with embroidered gold (盘金便帽) and said, "This is the eldest son (大公子) of the provincial examination commissioner."

    282

    ”又指一表卵色纺衫者曰：“此某孝廉，我同年同事也。”又谓二人曰：“此金长者，系我至友。”遂问金同行之亲友姓名，互相揖让，施大笑曰：“难得英俊多人，不期而会，我合作东道主，畅叙一日矣。”邀入五柳居，登楼列坐，酒家以水版来，指点酒肴之精妙者，次第供应。施与孝廉纵谈古人，议论文墨，公子殷勤劝金浮大白。酒正酣，苍头飞马而来曰：“大人有命，请公子同归。”金乃起给资，酒家曰：“上坐之客，入门时先已付讫。”金蹐跼不安，施等皆下楼谓金曰：“三生有幸，始得订交，如蒙相思，只须告把门张老，我自来耳。”作别登舆乘马，纷纷而散。金归，与子侄言之，佥参赞作报琼之举，以探消息。遂于旅邸盛设，具柬交张老以邀三君子。次日施来赴宴，云公子与孝廉皆为公事所羁，不能共酌，心甚歉，然嘱某致谢。

    Explanation

    282

    Pointing to a person wearing a light-colored silk shirt, he said, "This is a certain scholar-official, my contemporary colleague." He then said to the two people, "This is Mr. Jin, my closest friend." He then asked Mr. Jin about the names of his fellow travelers and friends, and they bowed to each other. Shi laughed loudly and said, "It's rare to have so many handsome and talented people meet by chance. I will be the host and we can chat freely for the day." He invited them to Wuliu Pavilion, where they climbed the stairs and sat in order. The innkeeper brought a menu, pointing out the delicacies, and served them in order. Shi and the scholar-official discussed ancient people and literary works, while the young master warmly urged Mr. Jin to drink a large cup of wine. When the wine was flowing, a servant on horseback came and said, "The master has ordered, please return with the young master." Mr. Jin then stood up and gave the money. The innkeeper said, "The guests at the head table had already paid upon entering." Mr. Jin felt uneasy and restless. Shi and others went downstairs and said to Mr. Jin, "It's a great fortune to meet and become friends. If you miss me, just tell the gatekeeper Old Zhang, and I will come myself." They bade farewell, got into carriages and mounted horses, and scattered in different directions. Mr. Jin returned home and told his nephews and nieces about it. They all suggested a return gift, to explore the situation. They then set up a grand banquet at the inn and sent an invitation to Old Zhang to invite the three gentlemen. The next day, Shi came to the banquet and said the young master and the scholar-official were both occupied with official duties and could not join in the drinking. He was very apologetic, but asked me to express his thanks.

    283

    遂入席，请金之子侄皆出，索窗课阅之，曰：“佳则佳矣，但与学使风气尚不甚合，就文论之，即如某篇，某处应提，某处应顿，结以大尾，则投学使之好，无不命中矣。”咸服其论。金乃重伸陈君之说，究为何事，施曰：“尔我既成相好，不妨实告。学使之大公子好与人交接，嘱我辈为之介绍，陈君前约贵处之某姓，通邑富豪，欲与公子纳交，不意陈君逗遛不至，实为缺典。”金曰：“如我子侄，不识可以充数乎？”施曰：“无不可者，但缟紵之费，各需千金，能乎？否乎？”金曰：“得半之道，尚可勉力。”施难之，金再三恳切，既而曰：“幸有我在，或可商办。”言未已，有二役以提督学院大灯来迎，金送出门，见其由中道入，文武巡官皆拱立候进，金深信不疑。

    Explanation

    283

    He took his seat and invited Mr. Jin’s nephews and grandsons to come out, then reviewed their window assignments. He said, “It is good, indeed, but it does not quite match the style of the provincial examiner. Speaking of the text itself, in such a piece, such a point should be highlighted, such a place should be emphasized, and the conclusion should be strong. If you follow this advice, you will surely win the examiner’s favor.” All agreed with his argument. Mr. Jin then reiterated Mr. Chen’s statement, asking what it was exactly. Mr. Shi replied, “Since we have become good friends, there is no need to hide it. The eldest son of the provincial examiner enjoys socializing with people and asked us to introduce him. Mr. Chen previously arranged with someone from your place, a wealthy family in the town, who wanted to establish a friendship with the young man. Unbeknownst to us, Mr. Chen did not show up, and it was indeed a significant omission.” Mr. Jin said, “Do you think my nephews and grandsons could fill the quota?” Mr. Shi replied, “There is nothing that cannot be done, but the cost of fine clothing, each requires a thousand taels of silver. Can you afford it? Or can you not?” Mr. Jin said, “Half the amount, still can be managed with effort.” Mr. Shi hesitated, but Mr. Jin persisted, and then said, “Fortunately, I am here, and we can discuss it.” Before he finished speaking, two attendants came to welcome with a large lantern. Mr. Jin saw him enter through the main gate, where civil and military officials stood in a respectful posture waiting for him to enter. Mr. Jin believed it without doubt.

    284

    翌辰，施来曰：“关防在迩，迟恐不得出矣。大公子以我故，屈允所请，须面封礼物，榜发来取。”相与同赴钱局，如数兑银。公封而回，给以关节。未几试毕，金之子侄皆落孙山外，始疑之，赴局开兑，则原封不动而易以砖石矣。内有一纸书云，“大宗师如此清正，汝曹妄想功名，理应重罚。所封千金，权借济急，销汝罪衍。以佛法论之，或者来世有奉还之日，未可知也。不必冤屈好人，此嘱。”金大怒，纳交巡官以访其事，始知学使幕中实无施姓，即大公子亦年貌不符，细揣其故，系骗子先冒杂役，放水菜时入内，其时号舍尚空，藏匿其中，易衣冠而出，巡官见其华焕，且自内出，则拱候之，其入亦然。入则仍易破衣，由杂役中出矣。至把门张老及灯笼夫，皆其党投充者，榜后俱逸。所骗亦不仅金姓一处也。

    Explanation

    284

    The next day, Shi came and said, "The examination hall is near; if we delay, we might not be able to leave. The eldest son, due to my sake, reluctantly agreed to the request, and we must present the gifts in person, to be collected when the results are announced." They went together to the mint, exchanged the exact amount of silver. The official sealed it and returned it, giving some inside information. Soon after the exam was over, all of Jin's nephews and grandsons failed to be listed among the successful candidates. Jin began to suspect and went to the mint to open and check the sealed contents, only to find that it was still sealed but replaced with bricks and stones. Inside was a note that read, "The chief examiner is so upright and incorruptible. You all foolishly aspire to fame and fortune, and thus deserve severe punishment. The sealed thousand gold pieces are temporarily lent to help in an emergency, to cancel your sins. According to Buddhist teachings, perhaps there will be a day in the future life when you can repay it, who knows? Do not wrong good people, this is my advice." Jin was furious and turned to the patrol officer to investigate the matter. It was then discovered that there was no one named Shi in the examiner's entourage, and even the eldest son did not match in age or appearance. Upon closer examination, it turned out that the swindler had first pretended to be a menial worker, entered the examination hall when they were releasing water and vegetables, at which time the examination rooms were still empty. He hid inside, changed his clothes, and came out. The patrol officer saw him in splendid attire and, since he came from inside, greeted him respectfully. When he entered, he changed back into ragged clothes and left among the menial workers. As for the gatekeeper Zhang and the lantern bearer, they were all accomplices who had joined the swindle, and they all fled after the results were announced. The scam was not limited to Jin's family alone.

    285

    有贵公子挟重资游姑苏以买妾者，官媒唤来数十人，皆不合意。一老妪随舆而来，曰：“郎君法眼过高，此等人皆不中选，非我姨家瑶仙大姑不能如愿，惜身价过高耳。”公子闻之曰：“如果真正佳人，何妨重价？第恐有名无实耳。汝姑带来一观。”妪笑曰：“我知郎君只好看瘦马家婢耳。清白人家，即穷至不吃饭，何肯将娇女送与人看耶？”公子谢过，愿同往访之。妪曰：“我试言耳，知人家愿不耶？”公子许重给媒资，妪请姑探之。隔五六日，公子望眼几穿，妪始欣欣然来曰：“凭我一片舌煞费苦心，肯与郎君一面矣。”遂往观之。其女一拜而退，娉婷之态，秀丽之容，公子已神魂欲堕。

    Explanation

    285

    There was a wealthy young nobleman who traveled to Suzhou with a large sum of money to purchase a concubine. The official matchmakers brought several candidates, but none met his standards. An elderly woman followed the carriage and said, "Your honor has high standards, and none of these women are suitable. Only my aunt's daughter, the beautiful and divine aunt, can fulfill your wishes, but her price is high." The nobleman replied, "If she is truly a beautiful woman, why not pay a high price? I only fear that she might be just a name without substance. Please bring her for me to see." The woman laughed and said, "I know your honor only looks at courtesans. Even if respectable families are poor enough to go without food, they would never send their cherished daughters to be viewed by others." The nobleman apologized and agreed to visit the woman. The woman asked, "I just suggested it, do you know if her family would agree?" The nobleman promised to pay a generous fee, and the woman asked her aunt to consider it. After several days, the nobleman waited anxiously, and the woman returned with good news, saying, "With my efforts, she has agreed to meet your honor." They went to see her. The woman bowed and left, but her graceful and beautiful appearance left the nobleman deeply moved.

    286

    旋闻琴韵铿然，和以燕语莺声，长吟度曲，公子几入痴魔，亟问价值，妪忙掩其口拉之出曰：“郎君几自误，此女不可唐突，当云聘作亚妻，则其父贪而好名，或可动之，既至尔家，则任分嫡庶矣。”又言聘只千金，女须衣饰，尚需彩舆迎之，缺一不能成也。公子已心醉，无不从命。乃立婚书，纳聘，约吉迎娶。公子雇巨舫作洞房，欲仿范蠡载西子游五湖故事。先买二婢以候，至日彩舆迎来，妪与婢扶新人入舱坐，妪乘闹逸去，公子揭去新人面巾，神色焕然，惟不言不动。爰设席遣婢，公子亲手扶之，新人失跌倒地，其声秃然。以火照之，乃庙中之术偶耳。急带人追至女家，则大门锁闭，访诸邻右，曰：“是家偶赁此宅以嫁女，兹已送女去，不知所之。”问诸官媒，无识妪者。公子费千余金，仅载二粗婢索然而归。

    Explanation

    286

    Soon the young nobleman heard the clear and resonant sound of a lute, accompanied by the chirping of swallows and the singing of orioles, and the prolonged singing and composing of music. He was almost entranced, and he immediately asked about the value. The old woman hurriedly covered her mouth and pulled her out, saying, "Young master, you nearly made a mistake. This girl cannot be approached lightly. You should say you will marry her as a secondary wife, then her father, who is greedy and fond of reputation, might be persuaded. Once she arrives at your home, then it will be decided whether she is the primary or secondary wife." She also mentioned that the dowry was only a thousand gold pieces, the woman needed clothing and ornaments, and still required a decorated sedan chair to welcome her. If any one of these was missing, the marriage could not be completed. The young nobleman was already deeply charmed and followed all her instructions without fail. They then established a marriage contract, accepted the dowry, and agreed on an auspicious day to marry her. The young nobleman hired a large boat as a bridal chamber, intending to imitate the story of Fan Li carrying Xi Shi to travel the Five Lakes. He first bought two maids to wait, and on the day, the decorated sedan chair welcomed her. The old woman and the maids supported the new bride into the cabin and sat her down. The old woman took advantage of the commotion and fled away. The young nobleman removed the new bride's veil, and she had a radiant expression, but she neither spoke nor moved. He then set up a banquet and dismissed the maids, and he personally supported her. The new bride fell and hit the ground, making a dull sound. When he lit a fire to examine her, he found that she was merely a magic figure from a temple. He hurriedly took people to chase her to her family's home, but the main gate was locked. He visited the neighbors, who said, "This family temporarily rented this house to marry off their daughter, and now they have already sent the daughter away, but we do not know where she went." He asked the official matchmakers, but none of them knew the old woman. The young nobleman spent over a thousand gold pieces and returned disappointedly with only two rough maids.

    287

    姑苏阊门外，通衢大道，百货交集之区，而人参行尤盛。间有空宅，亦甚宽广，时有服四品衣冠者，迁于空宅。门悬候补府陈姓封条，其司阍、司账、司厨及侍从之仆数十人，出入乘四人舆，张红盖。现任之府厅州县佥鸣钲开道来拜。时亦宴客，舆马纷纭，参行中人觅之屡矣。一日，有二仆衣履鲜华，相率至各参行阅货问价。行家叩其主，则曰：“西人也，为陈天官之长公子，以萌生加纳太守，分发江苏候补者，家资亿万，举宅皆嗜人参，以代茶饮。是以寓此就便也。主人命我等选择公平之家，以便长相交易。

    Explanation

    287

    Outside the Chuan Gate of Suzhou, on the main thoroughfare, is a bustling commercial area where a variety of goods converge, and the ginseng shops are particularly prominent. Occasionally, there are large, vacant houses nearby, and sometimes officials of the fourth rank, wearing their official attire, move into these houses. The door of such a house is adorned with a placard from the waiting official Chen, and there are dozens of servants, including the gatekeeper, accountant, cook, and attendants, who enter and exit in a sedan chair carried by four people, with a red canopy. Current officials from the county and prefecture also come to pay their respects, clearing the way with bells or drums. The official sometimes hosts banquets, with a flurry of carriages and horses, and people from the ginseng trade frequently visit. One day, two well-dressed servants went together to various ginseng shops to inspect goods and inquire about prices. When the shop owners asked about their master, they replied, "He is a Westerner, the eldest son of the high-ranking official Chen, who has been appointed as a magistrate and is waiting for a position in Jiangsu province. His family has immense wealth, and the entire household is fond of ginseng, using it as a substitute for tea. He resides here for convenience. The master has instructed us to select a fair and reliable shop for long-term transactions."

    288

    ”于是行主争趋其仆，而仆游十余家，皆不恰意。是时参业中专有陪宾之伙，已遍传各行，故二仆远至一里之外，行主皆知之，情愿许二仆重扣。仆喜，行主遣伙持参同去，其主先秤一两试尝之，价值三百余，换与宝银七提回。其伙侈张公馆内之华靡，且探知其太夫人每月须服参三钱，一年有十数万金交易。其眷属不日到矣。行主甚悦。未几，其主便服乘舆而来，谓行主曰：“尔家贷真价实，我太夫人将到，为所嗜好，须至佳者。”行主奉以顶毫，择定四十两，命二伙携参偕往兑价，曰兑齐后遣工人抬送银封来也。二伙相执至馆台，登堂入室，旋达后楼。

    Explanation

    288

    The innkeepers were eager to accommodate the servants, who visited over ten households but were not satisfied with any of them. At this time, the ginseng business had specialized staff to serve guests, and this information was spread throughout various businesses. Therefore, the two servants went as far as a mile away, and all the innkeepers knew about it, so they were willing to offer a high price. The servants were delighted, and the innkeepers sent their staff to take ginseng with them. The customer weighed one ounce to taste it first, finding it worth more than three hundred, and exchanged it for seven pieces of silver. The staff displayed the luxurious decorations inside the public hall and discovered that the lady needed to take three ounces of ginseng per month, resulting in tens of thousands of gold coins in transactions. Her family would arrive soon. The innkeeper was very pleased. Soon after, the customer arrived in plain clothes by carriage and said to the innkeeper, "Your ginseng is of true value and quality. My lady is coming, and she has specific preferences, so we need the best."
