Suddenly, a strict edict was issued, arriving just as the Governor was hosting a banquet. All guests retreated, and as the Governor read the edict, he became speechless and collapsed in despair. His servants, in a state of panic, rushed to inform the prince. The prince arrived, first presenting the imperial edict to his father, and then read it with the advisor, Mr. Yao. Shocked, he said, 'We are in trouble now! The emperor severely criticizes our delay, demanding that the rebellion be quelled within ten days. For now, we must immediately advance our troops to align with the imperial policies.' The prince replied, 'Father is on the brink of death; how can he lead the campaign?' Mr. Yao said, 'I have been in close service with your father for a long time, and today, I cannot remain silent. I will take your father to the main hall and issue the command on his behalf.' Reluctantly, the prince agreed. He then ordered all civil and military officials to gather at the gate to receive orders."

    332

    鸣炮升堂，连坐抬制军于暖阁，垂其帏幕若避风然，姚服从者衣冠，以令箭出入传谕。先命水师立备战舰，命水军总戎为先行，五鼓放洋，直取鹿耳门。左军从左，右军从右，张两翼以助先行。中军擐甲执械，齐集海门，以候本督部征进。命藩司速运军需，并拣选文职之可以参赞军谋者，随后军听刖。移请中丞督率臬、道、府、县保守城垣，以备非常。是时，文自制军以下畏葸不前，武自千把总以上争欲先战，以致内外惶惑。兹闻制军忽然振作，且号令井井有条，军弁莫不踊跃从事。令毕，抬制军入内，以安神丸与参汤灌之，渐苏，见妻子环伺，失声悲泣，曰：“吾命休矣。

    Explanation

    332

    Cannons were fired to signal the start of the session, and the commander was moved to the warm hall, with the curtains lowered as if to shield from the wind. Yao and his attendants were dressed in their formal attire, and orders were conveyed using command tokens. First, the naval forces were ordered to prepare the warships, and the naval commander was appointed as the vanguard. At the fifth drumbeat, the fleet set sail, directly targeting the Lu'er Gate. The left and right armies flanked from their respective sides, extending their wings to support the vanguard. The central army was armed and ready, assembling at the sea gate to await the main force. The provincial administration was ordered to quickly transport military supplies, and civilian officials who could advise on military strategy were selected to follow the rear army. The provincial governor was requested to oversee the defense of the city walls, preparing for unexpected events. At that time, civilian officials below the commander were hesitant to act, while military officers above the rank of hundred-man commander were eager to lead the charge, causing confusion both inside and outside the ranks. Now, it is heard that the commander suddenly became active, and the commands were well-organized, with all military officers enthusiastic and ready to act. After the orders were given, the commander was taken inside, administered a calming pill and ginseng soup, gradually regaining consciousness, seeing his wife and children around him, and breaking down in tears, saying, "My fate is sealed."

    333

    ”公子以姚先生所为告之，益骇曰：“如此则尸骨不得存矣。”请姚先生商之。姚曰：“此事无可再议者，不进则圣怒莫测，难保身家。进则虽死犹荣，况生还可必乎？”制军思之，跃然而兴曰：“先生之言，胜于良药，吾无病矣。惟有求先生偕渡重洋，始终其事。”姚曰：“诺，兵贵神速，请即启行。”挽制军手而出，文武官弁群拥登舆，至海门，前军已发，两翼犹留，讯其故，缘艨艟不足。姚传制军令于海口曰：“有能以商舟挤我师者，予五品官。”有舟子来试投之，立给水师守备礼，于是群舟争集，五军毕登。

    Explanation

    333

    The young lord informed him of what Mr. Yao had done, and he became even more frightened, saying, "In this case, the body will not be preserved." He then asked Mr. Yao to consider the matter. Mr. Yao replied, "There is no further discussion to be had. If we do not proceed, the anger of the emperor will be unpredictable, and it will be difficult to protect oneself and one's family. If we proceed, even death will be honorable, how much more so life, which can still be ensured?" The commander thought about it, jumped up in excitement, and said, "Mr. Yao's words are better than good medicine; I am now cured. Only by asking Mr. Yao to cross the vast ocean with me and see this matter through to the end." Mr. Yao said, "Yes, speed is essential in war. Please start immediately." He pulled the commander by the hand and they went out, with civil and military officials gathering around and escorting them into the carriage. They arrived at the sea gate, where the vanguard had already departed, but the flanks were still waiting. They inquired about the reason, and it was because the warships were insufficient. Mr. Yao conveyed the commander's order at the mouth of the sea, saying, "Whoever is able to provide commercial ships to support our troops will be given the rank of fifth-class official." A boatman came to test the offer, and he was immediately given the rank of water army commander. Thus, many boats gathered, and all five armies boarded.

    334

    扬帆冲浪而前，海神呵护，一昼夜直抵中门。攻其无备，前军己入，两翼从之，制军统全师进围台城，出逆意外，贼党掳掠未回，皆散漫村落间，守御单弱。姚与制军巡督，见城系竹木为之，伏两翼于后道，夜使前军纵火声喊，缺后道以逸之。贼出遇伏，一鼓成擒，招降余党，分派内地，不旬日而全台俱平。飞骑报捷，帝大悦，召制军入觐，曰：“朕视尔猥葸犹昔，此举何其奋勇？必有能手赞之者。”制军不敢隐，直陈姚幕友之事，特旨召见，欲予一官，姚顿首曰：“草莽之臣，敢因圣训而自居功？且赞襄助顺，即所以报效朝廷，与有官等耳。敢辞。”帝嘉其刚直，锡以四品卿衔，命永镇闽督幕府。
    　　
    　　芗厈曰：才如姚君，方不愧为入幕之宾。不然庸庸者流，奚啻书吏之头目，臧获之首领耶？乌足道哉。

    Explanation

    334

    Sailing bravely against the waves, protected by the sea god, they reached the central gate in a single night. Attacking when the enemy was unprepared, the vanguard had already entered, followed by the flanks, and the general in charge led the entire army to besiege Tai City. This was an unexpected move, and the bandit group, who had not yet returned from looting, were scattered and disorganized in the villages, with weak and isolated defenses. Yao and the general conducted patrols and inspections, finding that the city was made of bamboo and wood. They ambushed the flanks along the rear path, and at night, the vanguard set fire and made loud shouts, cutting off the rear path to allow the bandits to escape. When the bandits emerged, they encountered the ambush and were captured at the first attack. The remaining bandits were recruited, and they were deployed in the interior. Within ten days, the entire island was pacified. Swift riders reported the victory, and the emperor was greatly pleased, summoning the general to court and saying, "I see you as timid as before, yet this action was how brave! There must be a capable person assisting you." The general did not hide it, directly mentioning Yao's assistant, and the emperor specially summoned him to meet, intending to grant him an official position. Yao knelt and said, "A humble subject from the countryside, dare I attribute success to my own merit because of the emperor's teachings? To assist in promoting order is to serve the court, which is the same as having an official position. I dare not refuse." The emperor praised his integrity and bestowed a rank of fourth-class noble, ordering him to permanently serve in the governor's military council.

    Xiang Yan said: Talent like Yao's, he would not be ashamed to be an assistant in the military council. Otherwise, those of average ability, what is he more than a supervisor of clerks or a leader of servants? How worthy of mention is that?

    335

    周封翁
    　　
    　　蜀之周老人，八旬外樵于山而得宝藏。娶卖浆者女，生二子，此见于记载久矣。更有异者，其长子目有真光，察人无错。生子煌，少年入词林，故其父皆以封翁尊之。有楚人某进士，煌同年也，人本俶傥，貌亦魁梧，与煌友善。因归班候选，急欲自效，谋捐分发，素稔周氏多财，与煌借银两千。煌曰：“我祖年届百有二十，我将告假上寿，兄其偕我回籍，与父商之，谅无不慨允也。”归告乃翁，翁出见客，款接殷勤，入室谓煌曰：“如数与银，还否听之，但此后务宜疏远，以防后患。”某得银，援例分发江南，旋补巨邑。专丁备礼赍银付还，致书感谢，并达思慕之忱。翁使记室答之，以司总列名，厚给使者盘费，嘱告乃主，以翁父子俱未能作书，因病故也。不久某以赃败身戳，是时甫定寄顿者罪例，富室被诬往往有之，是以究其家人，词连周氏。煌时在馆上，以与某虽系同年，向无音信往来为辩，上命检查无据，宥释不问。

    Explanation

    335

    The Elder Zhou, Enfeoffed by the Zhou Dynasty

    　　An elderly man from the Zhou family in Shu, beyond eighty years old, was chopping wood in the mountains when he discovered a treasure. He married the daughter of a water seller and had two sons, a fact well-documented for a long time. What is even more unusual is that his eldest son had a penetrating gaze, never making a mistake in observing people. This son, named Huang, entered the scholarly ranks at a young age, and his father honored him as the Elder Zhou. There was a certain scholar-official from Chu, who was Huang's contemporary. This man was naturally bold and had a commanding presence, and he became friends with Huang. Because he was waiting for an official appointment, he urgently wanted to prove himself, so he sought to secure a specific posting through a donation. Knowing that the Zhou family was wealthy, he borrowed two thousand taels of silver from Huang. Huang said, "My grandfather is now a hundred and twenty years old. I will take leave to celebrate his longevity. Please accompany me back to our hometown, and we will discuss it with my father. I am sure he will generously approve." Upon returning, Huang informed his father, who warmly received the guest and entertained him with great hospitality. Entering the room, the father told Huang, "Give him the silver as requested. Whether he will repay it or not is up to him, but from now on, you should maintain a distance to avoid any future troubles." The scholar-official received the silver, followed precedent to secure a posting in Jiangnan, and soon was appointed to a large and important city. He sent a special messenger with gifts and silver to repay the loan, expressing his gratitude and conveying his admiration. The Elder Zhou instructed his secretary to respond, listing the official in charge and giving the messenger generous travel expenses, instructing him to inform the official that both he and his father were unable to write due to illness. Soon after, the scholar-official was disgraced and executed for corruption. At that time, a new law had just been established for those who harbored or concealed criminals, and it was common for wealthy individuals to be falsely accused. As a result, his family was investigated, and the Zhou family was implicated. Huang, who was then in the capital, defended himself by stating that he and the scholar-official, though contemporaries, had never had any correspondence. The emperor ordered an investigation, which found no evidence, and Huang was pardoned and released without further inquiry.

    336

    又程老实者，质库中之立柜伙也。其库主以浮荡败，欲举十万金之货本，愿以八折速售。程每对人嗟叹曰：“焉得八万金，则先发加二财矣。”或诒之曰：“周氏以数百万举债，汝往贷八万金有何难哉？”程信以为然，乃假伙伴衣冠，往周宅请见封翁。翁见之，陈其来意，翁笑曰：“八乃金似非容易，姑缓图之。”程自觉卤莽，跼跽告退。翁送之出，值阴沉欲雨，客去。翁回而雨渐大，翁思山路难行，程已年高，恐其失跌，使家人追之去。

    Explanation

    336

    A Clerk Named Cheng

    Cheng Laozhu was a clerk who stood behind the counter in a pawnshop. The pawnshop owner had fallen into ruin due to reckless behavior and wished to sell his inventory of ten million coins at a discount of eighty percent. Cheng often sighed to others, wishing he could get eight million coins to double his wealth. Someone told him, "The Zhou family lends money in the millions; why should it be difficult to borrow eight million coins?" Cheng believed this and dressed in his partner's clothes to visit the Zhou family and meet the old man. The old man saw him and heard his intention, and smiled, saying, "Eight million coins seem not easy to obtain; let's consider it later." Cheng realized he was rash and bowed and knelt to leave. The old man saw him out, and it was overcast and about to rain. When the guest left, the old man returned and the rain started to fall heavily. He worried that the mountain path would be difficult to travel, especially since Cheng was elderly, and sent family members to follow and send him off.

    337

    逾时而客始还，翁密询家人，云客出门遇雨，在树林下去其衣履包裹而行。小人追回，复在大门内重着衣冠方入，是以迟迟。翁乃问程曰：“足下衣冠自有之乎？抑假于人乎？请以实告。”程忸怩曰：“借诸同伙数人凑合者。”翁曰：“善，今天雨泥泞，勿损人之物，姑止一宿，明日我与足下偕往城中检点质库，再商行止可耳。”程唯唯。次日，翁果与程车马仆从而去库中。人见之深讶其事，翁历内外，捡阅一过，谓程曰：“足下之言不谬。”竟与八万金接续，且平分余资，使之司总。程感激图报，克俭克勤，是以岁入之利，较他处为优。

    Explanation

    337

    After some time, the guest finally returned. The old man privately inquired of his family, who reported that the guest had encountered rain and, in a forest, removed his clothes and shoes, wrapping them around himself before proceeding. A servant had chased him back, and he had re-dressed in formal attire at the main gate before entering, hence the delay. The old man then asked Cheng, "Did you have your own formal attire, or did you borrow it from others?" Cheng, feeling embarrassed, replied, "I borrowed from several friends and put it together." The old man said, "Good, since it is raining and the ground is muddy, do not damage others' belongings. Let us stay overnight, and tomorrow I will accompany you to inspect the pawnshop in the city and discuss our next steps." Cheng agreed. The next day, the old man indeed accompanied Cheng to the pawnshop with carriages, servants, and attendants. People were deeply surprised by this. The old man inspected the inside and outside of the pawnshop thoroughly, then told Cheng, "Your words were not false." He then took an additional 80,000 coins and shared the remaining funds equally, appointing Cheng to manage the business. Cheng was deeply grateful and worked diligently and frugally, resulting in annual profits that were superior to those of other places.

    338

    或问于翁曰：“某进士，品格貌相人人所愿交者，而翁必欲绝之，后果败，几被其累。程掌柜贸然而来，人人所讥诮者，而翁毅然从之，至今深得其力，是何道也？”翁曰：“道在目前，人自不察耳。某身为进士，入仕有途，何必欲速？其贪可知矣。贪以败官，古之训也。程掌柜假人衣履，尚知保惜爱护，矧巨万财物乎？故知其受托不苟也。”
    　　
    　　陬邑官亲
    　　
    　　西域之变，大将军福公奉命进剿。统领巴图鲁及吉林健旅，声势赫奕，所过州县，以办差不善登白简者不一。传檄至陬邑，缺既清苦，官亦疲惫，闻前途才干之员每有失误，心切恐惶，日惟涕泣而已。其官亲某向司征比，默默无闻，今见其戚官将败矣，不忍坐视，乃谓之曰：“库中有二三百金乎？”令曰：“有之，不敷所用。”曰：“既有之，尚可为也。尽以予我，不问出入，或者无碍前程乎？”大令无可如何之际，姑以听之。时值盛署炎炎，几至流金烁石。

    Explanation

    338

    Or someone asked the old man, "A certain jinshi, a person of high moral character and appearance, whom everyone wishes to befriend, but you insist on severing relations with him. Later, he fails, and you almost suffer because of him. Cheng, the shopkeeper, suddenly arrives, a person who is mocked by everyone, but you decisively associate with him, and you have greatly benefited from him until now. What is the reason for this?" The old man said, "The reason is right before your eyes, but people fail to see it. He is a jinshi, and there are established paths to becoming an official; why rush? His greed can be inferred. Greed leads to the downfall of officials, as ancient teachings have warned. Cheng, the shopkeeper, knows to take care and show respect when borrowing someone's clothes and shoes; how much more so for large sums of money? Therefore, I know that he does not act carelessly when entrusted with something."

    As for the relative of the local official, during the Western Regions' upheaval, General Fu was ordered to advance and suppress the rebellion. He led the elite soldiers and troops from Jilin, with great prestige and influence. As he passed through the states and counties, there were many officials who were criticized for their poor performance. An official order was sent to the local area, which was poor and underdeveloped, and the officials were exhausted. Hearing that capable officials often made mistakes, he was extremely anxious and could only cry every day. One of his relatives, who was previously unknown, now sees that his relative is about to fail and cannot stand by and do nothing. He said to him, "Is there two or three hundred gold coins in the treasury?" The official replied, "There is, but it is not enough for the purpose." He said, "If there is, it can still be done. Give it all to me, and I will not ask about the details of how it is used. Perhaps it won't harm your future?" The official had no choice but to listen to him. At that time, the intense summer heat was so severe that it could melt metal and burn stone.

    339

    官亲乃与工房相度馆舍，极其宽厂，染古色纸以表糊之，字画皆用旧物，其持桌以油核桃仁熏作乌木色，以人家用旧之藤竹席片蒸洗一色。按其大小形象制为引枕靠垫，蓝缎为边，以玫瑰杂杨花代絮实之。其帘幕用深绿色虾须竹为之。其天棚目村口直接至上房，长有里许，因借买多席，仅敷一层，恐透暑气，加以盐包杂松毛盖之。其陈设则山中购小松柏，栽数百盆，夹道而列。所有茉莉花、夜来香之属，反在其后，只觉芳馥而不见形影。公馆后及左右墙外，三面环列水桶，以竹截作喷筒，伏民夫数百人，各持一筒吸水向上，细细喷之，俾屋上棚间不干不湿，泽润而已。

    Explanation

    339

    The high officials, in consultation with the construction department, measured and assessed the residential quarters, which were extremely spacious. They dyed ancient-colored paper to cover the walls, and used old items for calligraphy and paintings. The tables were treated with walnut oil to achieve an ebony color, and the bamboo and rattan mats were steamed and washed to achieve a uniform color. Pillows and cushions were made according to their size and shape, using blue satin for the borders and rose and willow flowers mixed for the stuffing. The curtains were made from deep green bamboo with shrimp-like nodes. The ceiling extended directly from the village entrance to the main house, about a li long. Because only one layer of many mats covered it, they worried about the heat penetrating, so they added salt bags and pine needles to keep things dry. For the furnishings, they purchased small pine and cypress trees from the mountains, planted in hundreds of pots, and lined them up along the path. All jasmine and night-blooming jasmine, and similar flowers, were placed behind, so only the fragrance was felt but not the shape or shadow. Behind the public hall and outside the walls on both sides, water buckets were arranged in three sides. Bamboo was cut to make spray nozzles, and hundreds of laborers were employed, each holding a nozzle to suck water upward and spray it carefully, keeping the roof and ceiling neither dry nor wet, moist and lustrous.

    340

    西瓜为汁，以绢沥之，稍加冰糖籍荷水相和，其凉沁腹。茶用兰芽雪瑞，本系北产，气香味厚，色亦清烈，用沙瓯烹熟，坐于水筒，以取温和。铺陈甫毕，大将军至矣，令出远迓，将军由皎日之下而来，舆入村口，已觉阴凉。至公馆内，两旁松柏阴森，更觉沉静古穆，并无结彩悬灯、耀目增光之物，心地一爽。坐其铺垫，皆软滑清香，不觉大乐曰：“天仙界水晶宫不是过矣，不意僻陋小邑竟有是耶？可见人以才能为贵也。

    Explanation

    340

    The watermelon is juiced through a fine cloth, slightly mixed with rock sugar and lotus water, resulting in a cooling and refreshing drink. The tea is made from orchid shoots and snowy dew, originally produced in the north, with a fragrant and rich aroma and a clear and intense color. It is cooked thoroughly in a sand pot and served while sitting near a water container to achieve a moderate temperature. The preparations are just finished when the high-ranking military official arrives. An order is issued to go far to meet the general, who comes from under the bright sun and enters the village entrance in a carriage or sedan chair, already feeling the shade and coolness. Upon arriving at the public hall or mansion, the pine and cypress trees on both sides create a gloomy and solemn atmosphere, making the place even more deeply calm and anciently dignified. There are no decorations or hanging lights that would blind and brighten the surroundings, refreshing and relieving the heart and mind. Sitting on the soft, smooth, and fragrant cushions, one unconsciously exclaims, "This is not exaggerated to be a heavenly fairyland or a crystal palace; how could there be such a thing in a remote and humble town? It can be seen that people value talent."

    341

    ”及进西瓜汤，饮兰雪茶，莫名其妙，唤从官入曰：“此系尖站，并非住宿之所，然我自出都以来，日夜不得偃息，讵肯舍此清凉地而就火焰山耶？汝等弹压兵役前往宿站，只须留数人伺应，我五鼓启程来也。”传令入曰：“我食不甘昧，睡不贴席者久矣。贤大令造此福地，我今夜宿此巳分外挠搅，毋多备酒席，毋给赏从人银钱，有强索者，即告我处置。汝以长才屈于下邑，上司之过也，我当保奏。”令叩首谢出，及进酒席，则雪雁水参虾鸭糟鸡之类，只觉香鲜配合，无一毫肥腻气味，亦大醉饱乐甚，以二千金与令，以偿其费。及起马，令来道谢叩送，将军赞叹不绝而去。此令果膺保荐，仕至宪司，皆一官亲力也。

    Explanation

    341

    When the general was served with watermelon soup and lansnow tea, he was at a loss as to why they were being served, and called in his attendants, saying, "This is a resting station, not a place for lodging. However, since I left the capital, I have not been able to rest day or night. How could I abandon this cool place for a place like Mount Flame? You all should control the troops and go to the lodging station, just leave a few people to serve me. I will set out at the fifth watch." He then issued orders, saying, "For a long time, I have not been able to enjoy my food or sleep soundly. The honorable magistrate has created this blessed place, and I am staying here tonight, which is already quite a disturbance. Do not prepare too much food or give silver as a reward to the attendants. If anyone forcefully demands something, inform me and I will handle it. You are using your superior talent in a minor post, which is the fault of your superior. I will recommend you for promotion." The magistrate bowed and thanked before leaving. When the general was served with a banquet of snow goose, water chestnut, shrimp, duck, and fermented chicken, he found it to be perfectly seasoned and free of any greasy or fatty taste. He was extremely drunk and well-fed, very happy, and gave two thousand taels of silver to the magistrate to cover the expenses. When the general set out, the magistrate came to thank and see him off. The general praised continuously and then left. This magistrate was indeed recommended and rose to the position of vice-provincial governor, all thanks to the general's efforts.

    342

    或日；“才能之士湮没无闻，惜乎！”芗厈曰：“此其小焉者耳。古来救君主于垂危，转覆败于俄顷，而史不列其姓名者，如赵之厮养卒，金之两书生，功高不赏，是以名没不彰，其怨恸于九原耶？抑或不屑不洁，如遗逸辈之特隐其名耶？东坡云：但愿我儿愚且鲁，无灾无害到公卿。由此观之，是卒与书生固不屑为公卿也。”

    Explanation

    342

    Some say, "Talented individuals are buried without fame, what a pity!" Xiang Yan replied, "This is just a minor aspect of it. From ancient times, those who saved their sovereigns in dire straits and turned defeat into victory in a moment, but whose names are not listed in history, such as the servant of Zhao and the two scholars of Jin, though they achieved great deeds, were not rewarded, thus their names are not made known. Are they deeply resentful in the netherworld? Or perhaps they disdain impurity, like the recluses who particularly hide their names? Su Dongpo said, 'I only wish my son to be stupid and dull, without disaster or harm, reaching the position of a high official.' From this perspective, the servants and scholars indeed disdain to become high officials."

    343

    同胞三鼎甲
    　　
    　　明季之乱，盗贼纵横，宇内几无完邑。有某翁者，乡居而巨富，好行善事，平日造桥修路，舍药施棺，无不踊跃从事。及饥馑之年，助赈外独赡其乡邻者非一次，故遐迩称善人，莫不感激，虽流贼之暴，戕官劫库，亦不忍犯翁家。时有草冠中之伪大将军，掳掠妇女千余人，肆其淫乐。忽奉伪王调取进兵，伪将不能携带妇女，贼伙商令寄存翁家，因其闲房宽大而有养赡也。翁不敢辞，受而舍之，给与饮食。贼去后，夜闻妇女悲恸声，翁不忍，欲纵之。入问妇女，有所归否？众皆曰：“愿死于道路，不愿生为贼妻也。”翁各给银米，夜使远飏。已，遣仆广收牲畜骨殖，散布各屋内，纵火焚之，嘱邻里勿泄。未几贼归，翁垂涕而告以失火故，贼见瓦砾场上焦骼残骸无算，信之。不久，圣主即真，戡定大难，人民复业，翁家三子，皆读书入泮。

    Explanation

    343

    Three Siblings, All Achieving the Highest Ranks

    During the Turmoil of the Late Ming Dynasty, bandits roamed freely, and the entire realm was nearly devoid of safe places. There was an old man who lived in the countryside and was extremely wealthy, known for his benevolence. He was always eager to do good deeds, such as building bridges, repairing roads, distributing medicine, and providing coffins, all of which he undertook with enthusiasm. In times of famine, he not only helped with relief efforts but also repeatedly supported his neighbors. Thus, people far and near called him a good person, and even the Wandering Bandits, who killed officials and looted treasuries, did not dare to attack the old man's household.

    At that time, there was a Pretender General who had captured over a thousand women and was indulging in their company. Suddenly, he received an order from a False King to advance his troops. The Pretender General could not take the women with him and suggested that they be entrusted to the old man's household, as his home had spacious, unused rooms suitable for their housing. The old man, though hesitant, agreed and took them in, providing them with food and drink. After the Pretender General left, the old man heard the women weeping at night and could not bear it. He asked them if they had anywhere to go, and they all replied, "We would rather die on the road than live as the wives of bandits." The old man gave each of them silver and rice, and at night, he helped them escape. Afterward, he sent his servants to collect the bones of the animals that had been killed and scattered them throughout the house, then set it on fire, instructing his neighbors not to reveal what had happened.

    Not long after, the True Sovereign ascended the throne, quelling the great calamity, and people resumed their former occupations. The old man's three sons all studied and entered the school of learning, achieving high ranks in the imperial examination system.

    344

    其大郎娶王氏女，旧族也，得时文真传，能决科第，于归后索其夫窗课阅之，曰：“郎君笔下超超玄箸，惜法脉未清，词华杂凑，师之过也。妾为郎君一点窜间当入彀矣。”大郎心悦诚服，录示法家，佥曰：“数日不见，学业骤长至此，其入生花之梦耶？”于是二郎三郎闻嫂氏能，各以文呈教，王夫人曰：“两叔皆天才也，虽不及乃兄元气混沦，然偏师先济，皆科甲中人耳。”亦尽心指导，三郎甚颖悟，数月间卓然入妙，旋中式，连捷南宫，探花及第。欲于中门悬匾额，王夫人阻之曰：“是不能越分，应俟两兄。”命悬于门右。众皆窃笑之，然不敢不从。二郎奋志苦功，逾年亦发，榜眼及第，王夫人命悬匾于门左。

    Explanation

    344

    The eldest son married a daughter of the Wang family, a woman from an old and respected lineage. She had mastered the true essence of contemporary literature and was capable of passing the imperial examinations. After the wedding, she reviewed her husband's studies and said, "Your writing is extraordinarily profound and elegant, but unfortunately, the method is not yet clear, and the words are mixed and unrefined. It is the fault of the teacher. I will make a few corrections, and you will surely pass the exams." The eldest son was heartily convinced and fully satisfied, and he showed her work to other scholars, who all said, "In just a few days, his learning has improved so much. Is he entering the dream of the blooming flower?" Thus, the second and third sons heard that their sister-in-law was skilled and each presented their writings for her guidance. Madame Wang said, "Both uncles are geniuses, though not as good as their elder brother, who is fully developed. But they will still succeed in their own ways, and they will all pass the imperial examinations." She also gave them full guidance. The third son was very quick-witted, and in a few months, he became exceptionally skilled. He quickly passed the exams, advanced through the palace exams, and became the third-place winner. He wanted to hang a plaque on the main gate, but Madame Wang stopped him and said, "This cannot go beyond your proper place. You should wait for your two elder brothers." She ordered it to be hung on the right side of the gate. Everyone secretly laughed at him, but they dared not disobey. The second son worked hard with determination and, after a year, he also passed, becoming the second-place winner. Madame Wang ordered a plaque to be hung on the left side of the gate.

    345

    是时大郎犹诸生也，对其妻泣曰：“两弟皆飞腾而去，我恐投世无闻矣。”王夫人笑曰：“偏师先济，妾预言之，郎君毋灰厥志，龙头属老成，自古云然。俟元气充沛，自当超两弟而上之。”是科仅登榜尾，又越三年，果状元及第。泥金报至，戚里无不佩服夫人者。翁大悦，谓之曰：“是皆儿妇之力也，今尚有小姑未婚，我欲得传胪婿，则尽善尽美矣。”王夫人曰：“文笔之发与不发，妇能决之，两叔鼎甲，亦偶然耳，何能预定传胪耶？虽然，姑请试之。”议开文会，凡邑中之生童未婚者皆延之，勿论贫富。三复而得一生，甚寒苦，翁赘之于家，使王夫人督课之，果传胪及第。翁自为门联曰：“一婿传胪今世有，三儿鼎甲古来无。”

    Explanation

    345

    At that time, Da Lang was still a scholar, and he wept to his wife, saying, "My two younger brothers have all risen to prominence and left, and I fear I will remain unknown when I enter the world." Lady Wang smiled and said, "A smaller force succeeded first, as I predicted. Do not lose heart, my young master. The lead belongs to the experienced, as has been the case since ancient times. Wait until your energy is fully restored, and you will naturally surpass your two younger brothers." In that examination, he barely made the list, and after three more years, he indeed became the top scholar. When the gold-embossed notice arrived, the relatives and neighbors all admired Lady Wang. The old man was delighted and said to her, "This is all due to your efforts, my daughter-in-law. We still have a younger daughter who is unmarried. If I can find a son-in-law who achieves second place, then everything will be perfect." Lady Wang replied, "The flourishing or lack of flourishing of one's writing is within my power to decide. The two uncles who achieved the highest ranks did so by chance. How can one predict the runner-up? Nevertheless, let us try it." They decided to hold a literary gathering and invited all unmarried young scholars in the county, regardless of their wealth. After multiple rounds, one person was chosen, who was very poor and miserable. The old man married him into the family and had Lady Wang oversee his studies. Indeed, he passed as the runner-up. The old man composed a couplet for the door: "One son-in-law achieves second place in this era, three sons achieving the highest ranks is unprecedented in history."

    346

    芗厈曰：幼时闻我父我兄言之凿凿，久而忘其姓氏，姑缺，俟补。《书》曰：“作善降祥”，天之定理。第以一少妇而决科如神，远逾男子，似言者过矣。殊不知翁之所活妇女千余，其精华萃于王氏一身，英灵敏妙，如握宝鉴，如持玉尺，自然大胜凡庸。且冥冥中示果报之巧，为善人劝，其何疑耶？

    Explanation

    346

    芗厈 said: When I was young, my father and elder brother spoke about it with great certainty, but over time, I have forgotten their names. For now, I will leave this blank, to be filled in later. The Book of Documents says, "Doing good brings blessings," which is a fixed law of heaven. However, to say that a young woman could decide matters like a god, surpassing men, seems to be an exaggeration. What is not known is that the old man had saved thousands of women, and their essence was concentrated in one woman named Wang, who was exquisite and wondrous, like holding a precious mirror or a jade ruler, naturally far superior to the average. Moreover, the subtle display of divine retribution serves to encourage good people, so what reason is there to doubt it?

    347

    续客窗闲话
    　　
    　　卷一
    　　
    　　吴封翁
    　　
    　　霅溪吴封翁，富而好德。生二子先后举孝廉，亦佐乃翁济急扶危，以成善事。乾隆丁未岁饥，二子皆出助赈。翁无所事，止息于园庭以养静。因山林空旷，以防宵小。
    　　
    　　除夕，请翁入宴。二婢执烛前导，翁仰见树端伏人，即止不进。嘱二婢曰：“汝等留烛于亭，归告太夫人，我守静已久，不愿赴热闹场，可移樽独酌，以适我意。”太夫人知翁性情，不敢拂，命仆移筵就之。翁屏退家人，仰树呼曰：“树上君子，此间已无外人，曷来一叙耶？”树上人闻之，战栗几坠。翁曰：“老夫岂执汝哉？毋恐。”其人下，叩首称死罪。翁视之，邻人某也。邀入亭中，曰：“现备酒肴，先酌三杯以御严寒。姑告所需几何，助汝可也。

    Explanation

    347

    Continuation of Idle Talks in the Guest Room

    　　Volume One

    　　Mr. Wu the Elder

    　　Mr. Wu the Elder of Zhe Xi was wealthy and virtuous. He had two sons who passed the local examination and became scholar-officials, and they also assisted their father in helping those in need and supporting those in danger, thus accomplishing good deeds. In the year of Ding Wei during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, there was a famine, and both sons went out to assist in relief efforts. The elder had nothing to do, so he rested in the garden to maintain a peaceful and calm state of mind. Because the mountains and forests were vast and could harbor mischievous people, he remained vigilant.

    　　On New Year's Eve, the family invited the elder to a feast. Two maids held candles in front of him as they led the way. As the elder looked up, he saw someone crouching at the top of a tree and immediately stopped. He instructed the maids, "You two, leave the candles in the pavilion and go back to tell Lady Mother that I have been keeping a peaceful and calm state of mind for a long time and do not wish to attend a noisy gathering. You can move the wine and drink alone to suit my wishes." Lady Mother knew the elder's temperament and dared not disobey, so she ordered the servants to move the feast to where he was. The elder dismissed the family members and called out to the tree, "Gentleman on the tree, there are no outsiders here. Why not come down and have a conversation?" The person on the tree heard this and trembled almost falling down. The elder said, "I would not arrest you. Do not be afraid." The person came down, knelt and confessed their wrongdoing. The elder recognized him as a neighbor. He invited him into the pavilion and said, "I have prepared food and wine. Let us drink three cups first to withstand the cold. Tell me how much you need, and I will help you."

    348

    ”邻人泣对曰：“小人有母，不幸遇荒年，无以卒岁。素稔翁家富有，故行此不肖事耳。”翁曰：“不能周济邻居，以至为非，老夫之过也。汝其饱餐，当以二十金畀汝，卒岁之余，小作贸易，足以度日矣。切勿再为此事，他人不汝恕也。一成盗名，则为捕役鱼肉，没齿不能掩盖。且陷老母于不义。其奈之何？”邻人益感泣，叩首无算。翁乃予银，并布裹食物送之墙下，曰：“归遗尔母。汝仍出此，勿使我家人知，以留汝颜面。”邻人缘墙而去。方唤家人，见杯盘狼藉，翁忽有兼人之量，且疑且喜。

    Explanation

    348

    The neighbor wept and replied, "I have a mother, and unfortunately, we have encountered a famine, unable to make it through the year. I always knew that your family was wealthy, so I did this unseemly thing." The master said, "It is my fault that I could not help my neighbor, leading to this wrongdoing. You should eat well, and I will give you twenty taels of silver. After making it through the year, engage in small trade, which will be enough to live on. Do not do this again, for others will not forgive you. Once you become a thief, you will be preyed upon by the authorities, and you will never be able to hide it. You will also disgrace your mother. What can be done about it?" The neighbor was even more moved to tears and bowed his head many times. The master then gave him silver, along with food wrapped in cloth, and sent it down the wall, saying, "Take it back to your mother. Climb the wall and leave, do not let my family know, to preserve your dignity." The neighbor climbed the wall and left. Just as he called his family, he saw the plates and bowls in disarray. The master suddenly showed an extraordinary capacity, feeling both surprised and delighted.

    349

    后十余年，长公子发甲，官至大中丞。次公子亦入词林，提督学院。翁受二品封而卒。会葬之日，戚友以千计。忽有灵隐寺方丈大和尚来，侍者云从，祭品丰洁。俯伏柩前，哭之恸甚。中丞慰谢之，而不知是何交情。和尚造膝密告曰：“僧本邻居，前曾为盗，蒙老大人不责其罪，反以廿金为助，藉以奉母。母卒，弃去为僧，苦志虔修。今入方丈称大禅师，皆老大人之德有以成之。僧不敢忘，谨以实告。”吴氏乃知其事云。

    Explanation

    349

    More than ten years later, the eldest son, having passed the imperial examinations, reached the rank of Grand Censor. The second son also entered the imperial examination system and became the overseer of the imperial academy. The father received a second-rank noble title and died. On the day of the funeral, there were hundreds of relatives and friends in attendance. Suddenly, the abbot of Lingyin Temple arrived, accompanied by his attendants, bringing abundant and pure offerings. He knelt before the coffin and wept bitterly. The Grand Censor consoled and thanked him, but did not know the nature of their relationship. The abbot whispered close to his ear, saying, "I was once a neighbor, and before that, I was a thief. The old gentleman pardoned me without punishment and instead gave me twenty gold pieces as assistance to support my mother. After my mother's death, I renounced the world and became a monk, diligently practicing my vows. Now, I have entered the abbot's quarters and am called a great master, all due to the old gentleman's virtue. I dare not forget, and respectfully report the truth." The Wu family then learned of this matter.

    350

    芗厈曰：此不难于济助，难于惟恐人知。谆谆告诫，俾改行从善，是为阴德。
    　　
    　　难女
    　　
    　　余舅金氏，以上海之洋行为业。自置洋船五，在东西两洋贸易。每船必有标客以御盗贼。甲子春，船将开行，大宴标客。招优演剧，甚盛设也。标客自然首座，傲睨一切。余舅命其子侄陪宴，皆少年好事之辈。见客倨甚，切切私议，欲试其能。半酣小歇，肃客入园散步，坚请试其技。客左右顾，见道旁有卧柳，曰：“此碍步，请为公子去之。”迅以掌劈柳本，截然中断，如斧劈者。众皆咋舌。

    Explanation

    350

    Xiang'an said: It is not difficult to help someone, but it is difficult to fear being known. Repeatedly advising and earnestly warning, making someone change their ways and follow goodness, is considered a hidden virtue.

    A Difficult Woman

    My uncle Jin, who is engaged in the maritime trade business in Shanghai, owns five foreign-style ships that trade in the East and West Oceans. Each ship must have guards to protect against pirates. In the spring of the year Jiǎzǐ, the ships were about to set sail, and a grand banquet was held for the guards. Performers were hired to stage plays, and the banquet was very elaborate. The guards naturally took the first seats, looking down on everything. My uncle ordered his nephews and nieces to accompany them, and they were all young people who liked to show off. Seeing the guards being so arrogant, they whispered among themselves, wanting to test their abilities. After drinking a little and taking a short break, they invited the guards into the garden for a walk, insisting that they demonstrate their skills. The guards looked around and saw a leaning willow by the path. One of them said, "This willow obstructs the way, please allow me, sir, to remove it." Swiftly, he struck the base of the willow with his hand, and it suddenly broke in two, as if it had been cut with an axe. Everyone gasped in amazement.

    351

    当其时，有淮阳难民过境，沿肆乞钱。内有处女，矫矫不群，亦随众募化。至洋行，轻薄之伙以一钱投之，女怒叱曰：“视汝姑为何如人，而以一钱为戏耶？今日罚汝千钱，不然吾不行矣。”随坐大门槛以阻人出入。时脚夫运糖包至，每包约重百七八十觔，皆壮而多力者，肩之疾趋。至大门，见女碍路，喝之起。女故张其肱阻之。脚夫怒，作失手势，以糖包压之。女接而投掷，不甚费力。群夫大哗，佥以糖包共压女。女无惧色，左抵右抛如弄丸然，纷纷飞出市头，反将群夫击退。

    Explanation

    351

    At that time, refugees from Huaiyang passed through, begging for money along the streets. Among them was a young maiden, outstanding and unique, who also joined in the alms-soliciting. Upon reaching a foreign trading house, a group of disrespectful people threw a single coin at her. The maiden angrily rebuked them, saying, "What sort of person do you think your aunt is, to treat her with such disdain? Today, I will punish you with a thousand coins, or I will not proceed." She then sat at the threshold of the main gate, blocking people's entry and exit. Just then, porters arrived, carrying heavy bags of sugar, each weighing about one hundred and seventy to one hundred and eighty pounds. They were strong and powerful, shouldering the loads and moving quickly. When they reached the main gate, they saw the maiden obstructing the path and shouted for her to move aside. The maiden deliberately extended her arms to block them. The porters became angry, made a gesture of losing control, and tried to press her down with the sugar bags. The maiden caught and threw the bags with ease, causing them to fly out of the market in a scattered manner. Instead, she drove the group of porters back.

    352

    女大怒曰：“汝曹欺压孤女，使之内伤。＿罪在不赦，非多给钱养伤，事不能已矣。”时吆喝之声达于内，主人止戏，客亦出观。少年共议曰：可以观客之长矣。随激客曰：“我等观此女之力，恐无敌于世。客能退之否？”客视女弱甚，曰：“吾以二指提之出矣。”攘臂而前，女以一掌拍客胸，跌去数丈，入柜内如菩萨座。内外哗然，老主人出，命仆扶客入。以千钱赠女，好言劝之去。方叱少年滋事。入视标客，已从后户遁矣。少年兄弟密议曰：若得此女保标，谅海洋无敌手。其兄欲买以为妾。

    Explanation

    352

    The girl was very angry and said, "You all have bullied this lonely daughter, causing her internal injuries. The crime is unforgivable, and without giving more money to heal the injury, the matter cannot be stopped." At that moment, the shouting reached inside, and the owner stopped the game, and the guests also came out to see what was happening. The young men discussed among themselves, saying, "Now we can see the guest's strength." They then provoked the guest, saying, "We can see how powerful this girl is. Can you defeat her?" The guest looked at the girl and thought she was weak, saying, "I can lift her out with just two fingers." He thrust his arm forward, and the girl struck him with one palm on the chest, throwing him back several feet, and he fell into the cabinet like a statue of a Buddha. There was a commotion inside and out, and the old master came out, ordering the servant to help the guest inside. He gave the girl a thousand coins and kindly persuaded her to leave, then scolded the young men for causing trouble. When he entered, he saw that the guest had already fled through the back door. The young men secretly discussed, saying, "If we could have this girl as a guarantor, surely there would be no match in the world." His brother wanted to buy her as a concubine.

    353

    次日闻官以舟与资，将护送难民出境。少年兄弟访至马头，挨舟觅女，见舱中坐一叟，衣冠虽破，冠蓝顶冠。女侍其侧，方絮絮教训，女俯首垂泪。少年登舟拜之，叟喝女退。出迎，肃客入坐。少年曰：“叟居何职，因何窘迫至是？”叟曰：“老夫淮之山阳人，忝为都阃，以老致仕。不意今夏雨甚河决，田庐皆没，不能不随众觅食。老夫无子，只有一女，年方及笄。昨因乞钱，用泰山压顶势伤一标客。女子何可逞强，擅动煞手，败人衣食。老夫正训斥之。”少年极誉女能，问将焉往。叟曰：“老夫亲家为渐军水师提督，婿亦开府矣，将送女完姻。而老夫依以终身也。”少年诺诺而退。

    Explanation

    353

    The next day, they heard that the official had provided boats and funds to escort refugees out of the country. The young men went to the dock and searched through the boats to find the girl. They saw an elderly man sitting in the cabin, his clothes tattered but wearing a blue-topped hat. The girl was standing by his side, being scolded by him, with her head bowed and tears falling. The young man boarded the boat and bowed to the elderly man, who then ordered the girl to step back. The elderly man came out to welcome the young man and seated him. The young man asked, "What position do you hold, and why have you fallen into such dire straits?" The elderly man replied, "I am from Shan Yang in Huai Prefecture, and I humbly served as a general, retiring due to old age. Unexpectedly, this summer the rain was so heavy that the river broke, and all my fields and houses were destroyed. I had no choice but to join the crowd in seeking food. I have no sons, only one daughter, who is just coming of age. Yesterday, she injured a traveler with a powerful move while begging for money. How can a girl act so recklessly, using lethal force, and ruin someone's livelihood? I am scolding her now." The young man highly praised the girl's abilities and asked where they were going. The elderly man replied, "My relative is the commander of the naval forces of the Yangtze River, and his son has also established a government office. I am taking my daughter to complete her marriage to them. And I will rely on them for the rest of my life." The young man nodded in agreement and then left.

    354

    芗厈曰：女子之强者，功胜于男子，何也？其心专也。昔闻献县来一绳伎，有姿首。方开场作剧，有武举能开十四石弓者，以元恶霸一方，纵淫，无敢与较。见此女投所好，强欲留宿。班主曰：“我等卖艺不卖身。客何犯我规耶？”武举怒，拳击班主伏地。众皆曰：“此武举官人也，良家妇女尚不敢抗。尔等既卖伎，何敢拂之，自取苦恼耶？”女子乃笑，迎武举而慰解之曰：“官人果与妾有情，请以夜持五十金来。否则，不能承也。”武举哂曰：“五十金非难事，果处子亦不为费。”

    Explanation

    354

    芗厈曰：女子之强者，功胜于男子，何也？其心专也。昔闻献县来一绳伎，有姿首。方开场作剧，有武举能开十四石弓者，以元恶霸一方，纵淫，无敢与较。见此女投所好，强欲留宿。班主曰：“我等卖艺不卖身。客何犯我规耶？”武举怒，拳击班主伏地。众皆曰：“此武举官人也，良家妇女尚不敢抗。尔等既卖伎，何敢拂之，自取苦恼耶？”女子乃笑，迎武举而慰解之曰：“官人果与妾有情，请以夜持五十金来。否则，不能承也。”武举哂曰：“五十金非难事，果处子亦不为费。”

    355

    入夜至女室，置五十金案头曰：“可以共卧矣。”女曰：“妾请先睡，官人能犯妾，任意为之。如其不能，请留金而送客。”武举曰：“汝不过欲盖羞耳。何有于是？”女子乃闭门，去衣俯伏炕上。武举腾身上，以两手翻其躯，竟如铁铸，莫动分毫。随作开弓势，尽平生之力劈分两股。力尽而股不稍移。武举怒击其臀，坚如石。遍击首背，皆然，拳反作疼，乃伏其背，以柔情动之，闻女子酣呼睡熟。播弄终夕，无可如何。迟明，女子跃起曰：“官人既不伤妾，妾亦无伤于官人，请留金而退可也。”武举从此力脱而死。嗟乎，色与力不可并用，并用必内伤自毙。彼武举何坠女子之术中而不悟，此为恶之报也。吁，可戒矣！

    Explanation

    355

    As night fell, the man arrived at the woman's room and placed fifty pieces of gold on the table, saying, "We can sleep together now." The woman replied, "I will sleep first. If the gentleman can violate me, do as you please. If you cannot, please leave the gold and send the guest away." The martial arts champion said, "You are just trying to cover your shame. What is there in this?" The woman then closed the door, removed her clothes, and lay face down on the kang. The man leaped onto her, using both hands to try to turn her body, but it was as if she were cast in iron, not moving a hair. He then assumed the bow-drawing posture, using all his life force to split her thighs. Exhausted in strength, yet her thighs did not move slightly. The man angrily struck her buttocks, as hard as stone. Striking her head and back all over, all the same, his fist instead became painful. He then lay on her back, trying to move her with gentle feelings, hearing the woman's loud snoring as she slept deeply. He played with her all night, unable to do anything. Until dawn, the woman leaped up and said, "The gentleman has not hurt me, and I have not hurt the gentleman. Please leave the gold and depart." The martial arts champion died of exhaustion from that point on. Alas, desire and strength cannot be used together; using them together will inevitably cause internal injury and self-destruction. Why did that martial arts champion fall into the woman's trap without realizing it? This is the retribution for evil. Alas, this is a lesson to be learned.

    356

    语怪七则
    　　
    　　怪者，圣人所不语也。而商羊萍实载在《家语》，何也？有理可明，虽怪犹常；若无情理，徒骇人听闻，斯圣人不语。然同一怪事，有理无理，庸愚所不能明。姑志之以俟达者。
    　　
    　　粤东臬署二堂后院有榕树一株，其本三人合抱，其末高七八丈，扶苏广荫。树有神甚灵，故建庙立碑。凡臬使必以礼虔祀，朔望演剧，则安然无事。若稍有懈怠，神即显形，绯袍乌帽，据坐公案，必有殃咎。是以无敢渎者。道光壬寅，乔廉访在任，有僮溺树侧，立即疯狂，操刀飞舞，砍尽书院芭蕉数十本。主人缚僮谢罪而后已。有仆不信其事，故溺之。神情顿迷，入房抽剑插脚，饮刃透背。人见之惊喊，仆无伤也，自拔出剑，而血不流，惟前后红痕一线而已。不言痛亦不变色，饮食如常。主人虑其有变。资遣回籍。

    Explanation

    356

    Strange Tales VII

    　　Strange phenomena are not discussed by sages. Yet, stories of Shang Yang and Ping Shi are recorded in the Analects of Confucius, why is this? If there is a rational explanation, even strange phenomena are considered normal; if there is no rational explanation, they are merely shocking and thus not discussed by sages. However, the same strange event may or may not have a rational explanation, which ordinary people cannot discern. Therefore, I record these tales for the consideration of those who are more knowledgeable.

    　　In the rear courtyard of the Yuedong Judicial Office, there stands a banyan tree. Its trunk is so large that three people cannot encircle it, and its height reaches seven or eight zhang (about 20-24 meters). The tree provides extensive shade. The tree is believed to be inhabited by a very powerful spirit, hence a temple and a stele were erected. All officials must perform respectful rituals to the tree during the new and full moons, and when theatrical performances are held, everything proceeds smoothly. If there is any negligence, the spirit will manifest itself, appearing in a red robe and black hat, sitting on a public desk, and there will be negative consequences. Therefore, no one dares to be disrespectful to the tree. During the year 1822 of the Daoguang era, Qiao, the Censor-in-Inspector, was in office. A servant urinated near the tree and immediately went mad, dancing with a knife and cutting down dozens of banana trees in the academy. The owner tied up the servant and apologized to the tree before the incident was resolved. Another servant did not believe the story and urinated near the tree as well. He went mad again, entered the room, drew a sword and thrust it into his foot, piercing through his back. People saw him and shouted in alarm. The servant was unharmed, pulled out the sword, and there was no blood flow, only a thin red line on his body. He did not show pain or distress and ate normally. The owner was concerned and sent the servant back home.

    357

    又前任公子当署宴客，爱树阴清凉，移席其下。无云而雨，着菜上皆臭秽不可食，宾主败兴而散。
    　　
    　　又前使族人昼寝于榕树堂侧室，忽睹云雾中一蟒奔床前，惊骇逸出。觅得鸟枪，实贮火药将往击之。或问其故，其人以前事告，或往觇之，毫无形迹。其人指蟒卧案下，遂携枪执火，潜入帐内。觉又奔之，即燃枪轰击，响震远近，其人昏迷伏地矣。众皆聚观，见窗纸尽裂，鸟枪断折。其人尚右手执把，左手如截，连腕脱去。遍寻之，不见枪筒与手所在。乃救其人醒问之，曰：“枪鸣时即震惊而毙，不觉手之脱与枪之折也。”噫，此更异矣。凡鸟枪贮药过多则裂，尚有情理可言。然不能销熔铁筒与人之手也。或曰其人曾亵树神，故有此怪事。

    Explanation

    357

    A previous nobleman hosted a banquet for guests, loving the cool shade of the trees, and moved the seating area under them. Suddenly, it rained without clouds, and the food on the dishes became foul and inedible, causing the guests and hosts to leave disappointed.

    Another nobleman's family member took a nap in a side room of a hall with banyan trees. Suddenly, they saw a snake in the clouds rushing towards the bed, and were startled and fled. They found a bird gun, loaded it with gunpowder, and went to shoot it. Someone asked why, and the person told them about the previous events. Someone went to look, but found no trace. The person pointed to the snake lying under the table, and carried the gun and held the fire, secretly entering the tent. They felt it rushing again, and immediately lit the gun and shot, the sound shaking the far and near, and the person fainted and fell to the ground. Everyone gathered to watch, seeing the window paper all torn, the bird gun broken. The person still held the right hand with the handle, but the left hand was as if cut off, with the wrist detached. They searched everywhere, but did not find the gun barrel and hand. They saved the person and woke them up to ask, and the person said, "When the gun went off, I was immediately shocked and killed, not realizing that my hand had fallen off and the gun had broken." Ah, this is even more strange. A bird gun will break if too much gunpowder is stored, there is still some reason to explain. However, it cannot melt the iron barrel and the person's hand. Someone says the person had dishonored the tree god, therefore, there is this strange event.

    358

    吾乡有朱氏翁，年周甲，为米市伙。其为人也，严以正己，和以接物，故人皆亲之。每出行，遇有碍足之物，必去净而后已。见棺椁之暴露者，必为掩盖。一日索逋至野，见破冢内有巨瓮，白镪满中。翁恐且迷，方捡阅间，冢旁农人觏之奔而前曰：“此我祖父墓也，方因雨破。汝徘徊其间，得无盗我墓中物耶？”翁谢过曰：“原物归君，我未动毫厘也。”农人识翁，故挥之去。而呼其兄弟子侄来曰：“冢中不知谁氏物露我目中，天其富我乎，盍共取之。”众皆合力起出，视瓮中盘旋蠕动皆毒蛇也。农人恚曰：“翁先见此，而曰原物，戏我实甚，我其还戏之。”众曰诺。共舁瓮至翁宅后，俟其寝息，升屋拨瓦而倾之。翁夫妇跃起曰：“天雨金矣，姑趋避之。”候雨定而后捡较，得数千金，家以是富。彼农人倾毕，负空瓮归，方自以为得计也。

    Explanation

    358

    In my hometown, there was an old man surnamed Zhu, who was sixty years old and worked as a partner in the rice market. He was strict in self-discipline and harmonious in dealing with others, so everyone loved him. Whenever he went out, if he encountered anything that obstructed his path, he would remove it completely before stopping. If he saw exposed coffins, he would cover them. One day, while collecting debts in the countryside, he saw a large jar inside a broken tomb, filled with silver. The old man was afraid and confused, and while examining it, a farmer nearby saw him and rushed forward, saying, "This is my grandfather's tomb, which just broke due to the rain. Are you not stealing from my tomb?" The old man apologized, saying, "The original items belong to you; I did not move anything at all." The farmer recognized the old man and dismissed him. Then he called his brothers and nephews, saying, "The contents of the tomb, whose property it is, have been revealed to me. Is heaven making me rich? Let's take it together." Everyone worked together to lift it out, and they saw snakes coiling and moving inside the jar. The farmer angrily said, "The old man saw this first and said it was the original items, really playing a trick on me. I will play a trick on him in return." They agreed and carried the jar to the old man's house, waiting for him to sleep. They climbed onto the roof, removed tiles, and tipped it over. The old man and his wife jumped up, saying, "Heaven is raining gold! Let's temporarily avoid it." After the rain stopped, they checked it and found several thousand gold coins, making their family wealthy. The farmer finished tipping it, carried the empty jar back, and thought he had succeeded.

    359

    或曰：金银之气，上属青龙。蛇，龙类也，故变幻及之。凡南方人家蛇多者必富，殊不然也。昔某家有婢，闻空室丁当之声，趋视之，梁上一翠色四足蛇，方吐钱着地。婢奔告主母，偕往观之，蛇去钱存，仅十余枚耳。异日又闻房中铮铮然，婢探之。见翠蛇据床顶，吐钱盈席矣。又报主妇走观，则蛇不见，捡得时钱千余文。妇以告主。其家本小康，意谓神欲益其富，盛设祭祀。自此蛇不复来，而家随中落。

    Explanation

    359

    Someone once said: The energy of gold and silver ascends to the Azure Dragon. Snakes, being part of the dragon family, can change and affect it. It is commonly believed that families in the south with many snakes are destined to be wealthy, but this is not so. Once, in a certain family, a maid heard a knocking sound in an empty room and hurried to investigate. On the beam above, she saw a green snake with four legs, spitting out money onto the ground. The maid ran to tell the head of the household, who went with her to see. When they arrived, the snake was gone, leaving only about ten or so coins. Later, she heard a clinking sound in the room and went to check. She saw a green snake perched on the bed canopy, spitting out money that filled the mat. She then reported this to the wife, who went to see. When she arrived, the snake was gone, but she picked up over a thousand coins. The wife told the head of the household. The family was originally moderately well-off and thought that the god wanted to increase their wealth, so they held elaborate sacrifices. From then on, the snake did not return, and the family soon fell into decline.

    360

    浙有诸生某，名宿也。久困场屋，在闱中艺毕诗成，吟诵间四鼓人静，忽见青面撩牙凹胸凸肚一怪当面，生胆素壮，不甚惧。徐问曰：“汝来何为？”怪曰：“吾得一佳破，欲助有福者抡元，遍觅闱中，惟汝可。”生曰：“试诵之。”怪曰：“香油煎鲞香，豆油炒千张。二语不甚佳乎？”生曰：“此孩童急口令也，若以为文，笑死万人，且玷我声名实甚。”举砚欲击之。怪出朱笔点其额，不觉首肯，竟录其词作破，怪大笑而没。以下皆己作也，录毕缴卷，若忘其事者。二三场悉尽心为之，受卷所不贴。誊录照缮而入。是科座主系名公卿，卷落一同考官处，乃以翰林散馆出为令者，亦自命不凡，阅此卷破题，不觉大笑，致颔颏振脱，张口不能言。仆扶入室，卧不起。座主与考官有年谊，往候其疾。

    Explanation

    360

    A Scholar from Zhejiang

    There was a scholar from Zhejiang, a renowned figure. He had long been unsuccessful in the imperial examinations, and during one exam, he had completed his required literary works and was reciting them when it was the fourth drumbeat of the night and everyone was asleep. Suddenly, he saw a monstrous figure with a green face, sharp teeth, a concave chest, and a protruding belly standing before him. The scholar was known for his courage and was not particularly afraid. He calmly asked, "What brings you here?" The monster replied, "I have a good opening section and want to help someone with good fortune to win first place. I have searched the entire examination hall and found only you." The scholar said, "Let me hear it." The monster recited, "Sesame oil frying dried fish, soybean oil frying thousand sheets of tofu. Are these two lines not very good?" The scholar responded, "These are just children's tongue twisters. If taken as literature, they would make ten thousand people laugh to death and greatly tarnish my reputation." He picked up his inkstone to hit the monster. The monster took out a red pen and pointed at his forehead, and the scholar unconsciously nodded in agreement. He then wrote down the words as the opening section, and the monster laughed and disappeared. The rest of the essay was written by the scholar himself. After finishing and submitting his exam papers, he seemed to forget the entire incident. In the subsequent exams, he put in his best efforts, but the examiners did not favor his work. He submitted his final copy, and the chief examiner, a prominent official, received the exam papers. The examiner, who had graduated from the Hanlin Academy and been appointed as a magistrate, was also a self-important person. When he read the opening section of the essay, he couldn't help but laugh, so intensely that his jawbone shook loose, and he could not speak. His servant had to carry him into the room, and he lay there unable to get up. The chief examiner, who had a close relationship with the subordinate through shared academic experiences, went to visit his illness.

    361

    曰：“老年兄素称康强，何忽惠此恙？”房官以手指案上卷，笑容可掬。座主检阅数过，不觉钦佩，曰：“老年兄得此佳卷，何虞不入彀耶？我将与副总裁共赏之。”遂携卷去。两主司皆朗诵健羡，谓无出其右者，竟定解元。榜放，同考官之疾自愈，闻此卷发解，忍笑往见座主曰：“大人与职声名从此扫地矣。是何言也，而可作解首耶？”座主曰：“文实佳甚，岂出老年兄门下，过作谦词乎？”房官曰：“无论下文如何，观其一破，概可知矣。”主司共读之，不觉狂笑曰：“我等皆自开讲阅起，未及详观承破，至有此失，奈何？”考官方全诵之，曰：“文实高超，大人所取允当。无已，请召此生问明其故，易卷可也。”监临速召生来共鞠。生始悟场中之事，以实对。考官曰：“必魁星欲为是科光，故作此戏。不然走马看花之际，恐遗珠耳。”众皆曰然。

    Explanation

    361

    The senior colleague said, "Older Brother, you were always praised for being healthy and strong; why did you suddenly suffer from this illness?" The examiner pointed at the scroll on the table with a smile so warm it seemed to hold itself. The chief examiner reviewed it several times and was unconsciously impressed, saying, "Older Brother, with such an excellent scroll, how could you not be selected? I will share it with the deputy chief examiner for appreciation." He then took the scroll with him. Both the chief and deputy examiners recited it with admiration, agreeing that there was none better, and ultimately decided on the top scorer. When the results were announced, the co-examiner's illness mysteriously healed. Upon hearing that this scroll had been selected as the top scorer, he went to see the chief examiner with a reluctant smile, saying, "Sir, your and my reputation is now completely ruined. What are you talking about, and how could this be the top scorer?" The chief examiner replied, "The writing is truly excellent; could it be from Older Brother's hand? Perhaps you are being overly modest?" The examiner said, "Regardless of the rest of the text, just by looking at the first part, we can generally understand it." The chief and deputy examiners read it together and could not help but burst into laughter, saying, "We all started reading from the beginning, without carefully examining the continuation, and thus made this mistake. What can we do?" The chief examiner then read it in full, saying, "The writing is truly outstanding; the selection made by Sir is appropriate. There is no other choice but to call the candidate to explain the situation and change the scroll." The supervisor quickly summoned the candidate to come and explain. The candidate finally realized what had happened in the examination hall and answered truthfully. The chief examiner said, "It must be that the 魁星 wanted to bring glory to this year's examination, so he played this trick. Otherwise, during the cursory examination, we might have overlooked a gem." Everyone agreed.

    362

    豫西沈孝廉，名士也。以文会友，卓卓一时。忽患时症，头疼身热。医以生军下之，所下皆白膏。病痊而愚甚。至一丁不识。向之朋友来，议论纵横，孝廉殊愦愦，自亦不知其故。或曰：人能记忆一切皆在脑，脑脂也。脑减则忘其所有，信如斯言。药补其脑，当能复原矣。何以沈孝廉终身不复也？
    　　
    　　某官保
    　　
    　　某宫保未达时，寒士也，卖卜浙之吴山。有鹾商某，武林巨富，生平喜风鉴，书无不览。遇能者，俯首降心，专诚求教，故其术甚精。生一女，自视之合封一品夫人，凡议婚者，必面相攸曰：“非吾女匹。”延至廿余年，无当意者，戚友皆非笑之。时商行香，至庙遇雨，舆不得行，在庙闲玩，过卖卜处，见公骨格非凡，又睹其旋焉而返，足迹印地者皆中满，商大悦，确信为大贵人也。询姓名年庚籍贯，知为旧族，已游泮而未婚，齿又与女相若，曰：“先生步君平后尘，亦属韵事，但不如教学有相长之益。

    Explanation

    362

    The Western Henan scholar-official, Shen, was a renowned gentleman. He gathered friends through his writings and was outstanding at the time. Suddenly, he contracted a seasonal illness with symptoms of headache and fever. The doctor treated him with "sheng jun," and the purgation resulted in the excretion of a white, paste-like substance. Although the illness was cured, he became extremely dull-witted, to the point of being unable to recognize even a single character. When his former friends came to visit, they engaged in lively discussions, but Shen was thoroughly confused and did not understand the reason for his condition. Someone said, "The ability to remember everything lies in the brain, which is like brain fat. When the brain diminishes, one forgets everything." If this is true, then using medicine to replenish the brain should restore him to his former state. Why did Shen never recover?

    The official, whose title was "guan bao," was once a poor scholar, selling fortune-telling services on Wu Mountain in Zhejiang. A wealthy salt merchant from Hangzhou, who had a deep interest in divination and had read extensively, met a man who appeared to be exceptionally gifted. The merchant's daughter was considered to be a match for a first-rank wife, and he insisted that any suitor must be of equal status. Despite this, he had not found a suitable match for over twenty years, and his relatives and friends ridiculed him. One day, while the merchant was burning incense at a temple and encountered rain, his carriage could not proceed. He stopped at the temple to pass the time and passed by the fortune-teller's place. He saw the official, who had an extraordinary bone structure, and noticed that he returned with his feet firmly planted in the ground. The merchant was very pleased and firmly believed that the official was destined for great success. He inquired about the official's name, birth year, and place of origin, and learned that he came from a distinguished family, had passed the imperial examination, was unmarried, and was of similar age to his daughter. He said, "Mr. Shen, following in the footsteps of Zeng Shen, is also a beautiful and meaningful event, but it would be better if you could benefit from mutual learning and growth."

    363

    ”公曰：“某异乡人，此间无人相识，谁作曹邱者？”商曰：“先生果有意乎？我即主人，请以训我子何如？”公欣诺。商返，具衣冠什物延公，使二幼子受业。阅半载，见其性情通达，学问书法俱佳，遂遣媒纳为赘婿。未几商卒，长子袭父业，渺视公而戏弄之，且不使诸弟入学，曰：“何学丐为？”公读闺中，其夫人讥之曰：“丈夫不能身自振发，而依亲戚为事，妾亦何颜？”公怒欲去，夫人曰：“妾奉严命，相随终身，岂有他志？然不得不劝夫子自立也。今夫子舍妾而去，将焉往？”公曰：“寒苦，我命也，不能仰人鼻息。还我故人，任我所之。”夫人苦留之，不可，竞脱华服，衣故衣，不名一钱，携来时笔砚而去。

    Explanation

    363

    The stranger replied, "I am a stranger from a foreign land, and no one here knows me. Who will act as a matchmaker?" The merchant said, "Do you truly have the intention? I am the host. How about you teach my children?" The stranger happily agreed. The merchant returned, prepared clothes and other items to welcome the stranger, and had his two young sons learn from him. After half a year, seeing that his nature was open and adaptable, and that his learning and calligraphy were excellent, the merchant sent a matchmaker to take him as a son-in-law. Not long after, the merchant died, and the eldest son inherited his father's business. He looked down on the stranger and teased him, and did not allow his younger brothers to attend school, saying, "Why study to be a beggar?" The stranger lived in seclusion, and his wife criticized him, saying, "A man cannot rise up on his own, but relies on relatives for livelihood. How can I be ashamed?" The stranger was angry and wanted to leave. His wife said, "I have followed your strict orders all my life, and I have no other intentions. However, I cannot help but urge you to stand on your own. Now that you are leaving me, where will you go?" The stranger said, "Poverty is my fate, and I cannot live at the mercy of others. Return me to my old self, and let me go where I will." His wife tried to persuade him to stay, but he refused, finally removing his fine clothes, wearing old clothes, not having a single coin, and taking his writing brush and inkstone with him as he left.

    364

    行至嘉禾，无腰缠，不能前，止于三塔寺，仍举旧业。卜案之左，有星士为人谈命有验，业甚盛，日进制钱数千。因江湖同道，与公往来，觉异于流辈，使书八字俾决之曰：“阁下当得一品职，若往北行，不久上达，吾推命多矣，决无错谬。”公曰：“承君美意，奈无赀何？”星士曰：“是不难，吾自来此，积十余金，尽以助阁下。十年后，无忘今日，则吾食报多多矣。”公曰：“诚如是，何敢忘？”遂藉其赀，附粮船，达津门，费用将尽。闻保阳有族人业茶者相识，谋往依之，至则其人已歇业南旋矣。皇皇失所，访得同乡数人，皆无力赀助，荐入藩署科房帖写，日得数十钱，仅敷糊口而已。数月，瘧疾作，北方以此症无救，谋弃之。主吏悯公，以数百钱纳怀中，乘其昏迷，使工人负出，置古庙庑下。

    Explanation

    364

    Traveling to Jiahe, he found himself without funds and unable to proceed further, so he stopped at San Ta Temple and resumed his old profession. To the left of the divination table, there was a star diviner who could predict fate accurately, and his business was very prosperous, earning thousands of cash coins daily. Due to fellow travelers in the world of itinerant performers, he interacted with him and found him different from his peers. He asked the diviner to write down eight characters to decide his fate, saying, "You will attain a first-rank position. If you go north, you will soon rise to prominence. I have predicted many fates, and my predictions are never wrong." The man replied, "I appreciate your kind intentions, but what can I do without money?" The diviner said, "It is not difficult. Since I came here, I have accumulated over ten gold pieces and will give all of it to you. Ten years later, do not forget today, and then I will receive much reward." The man said, "If it is truly so, how could I forget?" So he borrowed the money, boarded a grain ship, and reached Jinxian, but his expenses were almost depleted. He heard that there was a relative in Baoyang who was involved in the tea trade and knew him, and planned to go and rely on him. When he arrived, the person had already stopped the business and returned south. Feeling lost and disoriented, he found several fellow townspeople, but all of them were unable to provide financial assistance. He was recommended to work as a clerk in the provincial office, earning dozens of coins daily, just enough to make ends meet. After several months, he contracted malaria, a disease in the north that was considered incurable, and planned to abandon him. The official felt pity for him and put several hundred coins in his pocket. When he was unconscious, the official had workers carry him out and left him in the eaves of an ancient temple.

    365

    时大雪压公身，热气得雪而解，醒知其故，忿忿北行。至漕河，雪益大，不辨道路，扑趺入河水，僵不能起。河上有庙，老僧主之，方围炉假寐，梦伽蓝神告曰：“贵人有难，速往救之。”老僧开目，见河内伏一白虎，惊觉出观，则窭人卧雪中，不知死生，不敢救，入室复卧，见伽蓝神怒叱曰：“出家人以慈悲为本，见死不救，祸且及汝。况救之，将兴尔庙。”老僧遂决救之，其徒与火工力阻之，不听，喝往扶公入，去湿衣，温以棉被，进姜汤而苏。僧问行踪，知为儒者，益敬之，为易衣供奉。卒岁，至春暖时，僧谓公曰：“先生功名中人也，此地无可发迹，我师弟主都中隆福寺方丈，王公大人皆与往来，请以一函书及赀两缗，送先生往投之。”公欣然附车往，见大和尚志高气扬，非大众比，谓公曰既师兄书来，命侍者引入客舍，姑住此以候机缘。公得住且住，虽饔飧无虑，而衣赀无所出。

    Explanation

    365

    At that time, heavy snow fell on the gentleman's body. The warmth of his breath melted the snow, and he woke up knowing why, angrily heading north. Upon reaching the canal, the snow became even heavier, and he could not see the path. He tripped and fell into the river, freezing and unable to move. There was a temple on the riverbank, and an old monk was in charge of it. The monk was warming himself by a fire and dozing off when he dreamed that the temple guardian spirit told him, "A noble person is in trouble, hurry to save him." The monk opened his eyes and saw a white tiger crouching in the river. He woke up in shock and went out to see, and there he found a poor man lying in the snow, unsure if he was alive or dead. The monk was afraid to help, so he went back inside and lay down again. He saw the temple guardian spirit angrily scold him, "As a monk, compassion is the foundation; if you see someone die and do not help, disaster will befall you. Moreover, if you save him, your temple will flourish." The monk then decided to help. His disciples and the temple workers tried to stop him, but he did not listen. He shouted for someone to carry the man inside, removed his wet clothes, covered him with a warm blanket, gave him ginger soup, and he revived. The monk asked about his journey and learned he was a scholar, treating him with greater respect, changing his clothes, and providing him with food and shelter. By the end of the year, when spring warmed up, the monk said to him, "You are a person of ambition, but there is no opportunity for advancement here. My master's brother is the abbot of Longfu Temple in the capital, and many high officials visit him. Please give me a letter and two strings of cash to send you to him." The scholar agreed and happily went with him. He saw the senior monk, who was ambitious and proud, unlike the common monks. The senior monk said, "Since my elder brother sent a letter, I have ordered a servant to bring you to a guesthouse. Stay here and wait for opportunities." The scholar stayed and stayed, though he did not worry about meals, he had no money for clothes.

    366

    众僧皆喜公和婉，暇即聚谈，日习见先生书法甚佳，曰：“曷不于庙外售联，则所需有着。”公听之。买者源源而来，日得数百钱，春服藉以告成。时皇太后许书妙法莲华经百部，施舍天下名山，作大功德。上命词林书式呈览，不合太后意。有旨，旋命诸王在外访善书者。王识大和尚，命书记僧作字进，愈不合旨，王无以应命，惟有责重大和尚而已。乃与众僧谋，一僧以公对，大和尚晒曰：“此不过卖春联之字法耳，何足以当大任？”僧曰：“舍此无人，盍姑试之？”不得已，使公书字，因王进呈。太后大悦，命延入王府，虔诚书经，功成之日，厚酬之。王自来延请，大和尚皇然失措，盛备行装，送公入府。书经告竣。上请问合旨之意，太后曰：“在廷诸臣，非无书法出其右者，第福分届满，即无功德。此人将来必为正直大臣，有功于国，故其书浑厚多力，可与一官酬之。”上奉懿旨，召公见，钦赐举人，以部曹用。遇缺即补。

    Explanation

    366

    The monks all admired the gentleman’s amiable and gentle nature. Whenever they had leisure, they gathered to converse, and having become accustomed to seeing his excellent calligraphy daily, they said, “Why not sell couplets outside the temple, so that the required funds will be available?” He listened to their suggestion. Buyers came in a steady stream, and each day he earned hundreds of coins, with which he was able to complete his spring clothes. At that time, the Empress Dowager permitted the copying of one hundred copies of the Lotus Sutra, to be distributed to famous mountains across the land as a great act of merit. The Emperor ordered the Hanlin scholars to submit samples of their writing for review, but these did not meet the Empress Dowager’s approval. An imperial decree was then issued, and soon the princes were ordered to search outside the capital for skilled calligraphers. One prince recognized the great monk and ordered the monk in charge of scriptural matters to write and present it. But this too did not meet the imperial will, and the prince had no way to fulfill his orders but to blame the great monk. Then, together with the monks, they consulted, and one monk suggested the gentleman. The great monk scoffed and said, “This is merely the style of writing used for selling spring couplets—how could it be sufficient to bear such a great responsibility?” The monk replied, “There is no one else, why not try him for a while?” With no alternative, they had him write, and through the prince, the writing was presented to the Empress Dowager. She was greatly pleased and ordered him to be invited into the prince’s residence, where he was to write the sutras with sincerity and devotion. On the day the work was completed, he was richly rewarded. The prince himself came to invite him, and the great monk was greatly startled and at a loss, preparing elaborate travel arrangements and sending him into the prince’s residence. When the copying of the sutras was completed, the Emperor asked for the meaning behind the Empress Dowager’s approval. She said, “Among the officials in the court, there are none whose calligraphy surpasses his, but their fortune is complete, so they have no merit. This man will certainly become an upright minister and serve the state with merit. Hence, his calligraphy is thick, solid, and full of strength—it is fitting to bestow an official position upon him.” The Emperor followed the imperial decree, summoned the gentleman, and granted him the title of juren by imperial decree, appointing him as a bugao official. Whenever a vacancy occurred, he was immediately appointed.

    367

    时公以书召夫人，夫人复命曰：“妾自夫子去后，心向空门，今已习静，自维不能相夫子。如念结发情，置媵可也。”公思妇翁识拔恩，竟虚内阃。上信公之为人忠诚朴实，一岁三迁，不及十年，官至直隶方伯。未几，总督畿内，星士来见，为之延誉，获重赀去。漕河老僧时年八十，因公往祝之，文武员弁以公故，争献寿仪，又为倡修庙宇，规模宏大，且在彼立给孤独园。隆冬，收养流民，通省咸置留养局，无饥寒之民。

    Explanation

    367

    At that time, the official summoned his wife with a letter. She responded, saying, "Since my husband left, my heart has turned towards the Buddhist path. I have now practiced meditation and contemplation, and I believe I cannot assist him. If you still cherish the bond of our marriage, it would be acceptable to have a concubine." The official remembered the favor of his father-in-law who had recognized and promoted him, and decided to remain unmarried. The emperor trusted the official for his loyalty, honesty, and simplicity, and within ten years, he rose to the position of governor of Zhili. Shortly after, he became the governor-general of the capital region. Astrologers came to visit and spread his good name, receiving a large sum of money. An old monk from the canal, who was eighty years old, came to wish him well. Civil and military officials competed to present birthday gifts because of the official. They also initiated the renovation of temples on a grand scale and established a garden for the Buddha's disciples. During the severe winter, he took in homeless people, and throughout the province, shelters were set up to care for the homeless, ensuring no one suffered from hunger or cold.

    368

    在其境又开水田，通河渠，设桥梁，初北人不习种棉，公聘南方男妇导之，作耕织图以进，上为题咏。当公作帖写时，知吏之利弊，故宫文书皆刻颁格式，使吏不能高下其手。又修复义仓，作义仓图，均贮四乡，以便赈济。于是上下肃清，闾阊丰足，上益重之。入觐，询公尚未有子之故，公以实情奏。上为降旨召夫人，不得不来，以命服迎入署。夫人见公再拜曰：“妾来应君命，然马齿长矣，无生育理，仅能主内政而已。请纳如姬。”公不听，上知之，以江南织造所进宫人赐之，生一子。上贤夫人之不妒，加公宫保衔，命正奏为一品夫人，妾赐宜人，其子后亦为大中丞。
    　　
    　　芗厈曰：公之善政，所以结主知者，当不止此，不过依客所能言者记之。或曰：“此方宫保讳观承事。”然核公行状及袁简斋先生《随园文集》所载不合，意或鄙俗之事，节去以成体裁。或传述失实，或未可定。惟小说不嫌荒唐，且历言穷达，亦鼓励寒儒之一道也。

    Explanation

    368

    In his territory, he opened up paddy fields, connected rivers and canals, and built bridges. Initially, northerners were unfamiliar with cotton cultivation, so the official hired men and women from the south to guide them. He created illustrations of farming and weaving to present to the emperor, who composed verses in response. When the official was drafting documents, he understood the advantages and disadvantages of the officials, so all official documents were engraved and distributed in a standardized format, ensuring that officials could not manipulate or abuse their power. He also repaired the public granaries, created illustrations of the public granaries, and distributed them evenly across the four villages to facilitate relief efforts. As a result, the hierarchy was clear and orderly, and the people in the villages were prosperous. The emperor valued him even more.

    When the official presented himself to the emperor, he asked why the official had no sons yet. The official reported the truth. The emperor issued an edict to summon the official's wife, who had no choice but to come. She was welcomed into the official's residence in imperial attire. The wife bowed twice to the official and said, "I came in response to your command, but I am past childbearing age and can only manage the household affairs. Please take in a concubine like Ruhui." The official did not listen, but the emperor knew about it. The emperor granted him a palace woman from the Jiangnan textile factory, and she gave birth to a son. The emperor praised the wife for not being jealous, granted the official the title of Palace Guard, ordered that she be officially designated as a First-Rank Lady, and granted her concubines the title of Yiren. His son later became a Grand Censor.

    Xiang Yan said: "The official's good governance, which won the emperor's favor, was certainly not limited to these things, but it is recorded here based on what the guest could say." Some say, "This official's title of Palace Guard was originally given to Guan Chengshi." However, when cross-referenced with the official's biography and Mr. Yuan Jianzhi's Sui Yuan Wen Ji, it does not match. Perhaps it was a matter of low taste or vulgarity, omitted to maintain the proper form. Perhaps the transmission was inaccurate, or it is uncertain. Novels do not shy away from absurdity, and stories of fortune and misfortune also serve as encouragement for poor scholars.

    369

    黄大王
    　　
    　　大王，豫人，前明诸生，生农家，性喜水。幼孤，母抚之。三岁时，母为人浣衣，从井汲水，儿在其侧，嬉戏井旁，照见己影，跃入从之。母瞥见呼救，邻人咸集，见儿坐水上，两手拍弄己影，人见其不沈也，共异之。以绳悬入下，抱之起。至七八岁，母夫人又殁，其姑渔家妇也，无子，养为己子，儿随姑父母舟中，更以弄水为乐。其姑夫屡次教戒，顽梗如故，厌之。一日，姑夫闲卧舟首，几激水湿其衣，怒甚。以足蹬儿，坠河中，随波逐流而去。姑见之，急喊：“何杀我儿？不但绝黄氏后，我老何依？”号泣不已。其姑夫曰：“顽童无用，将贻后患，不如早绝之。”夫妇口角时，下游一舟来，问故，曰：“无伤也。离此十余里，有儿戏水中，乐甚，无乃是耶？速往救之，狁可及。”夫妇放舟下，果见儿在水中，抱大鱼出入波间。其姑如获至宝，扶之登舟而回，知此儿之不能容于其夫也，使受雇为人牧儿。

    Explanation

    369

    King Huang

    King Huang was a man from Yü, a former scholar-official of the Ming dynasty, born into a farming family, and by nature he loved water. As a child, he was orphaned, and his mother raised him. At the age of three, while his mother was washing clothes for others and drawing water from the well, the child played by the wellside, saw his reflection in the water, and leaped in after it. His mother glimpsed this and cried for help; the neighbors all gathered and saw the child sitting on the water, using both hands to play with his reflection. Seeing that he did not sink, they were all astonished. They lowered him with a rope into the well and pulled him up.

    By the time he was seven or eight years old, his mother had died. His grandmother was a woman from a fishing family, childless, and raised him as her own son. The child lived on the boat with his grandmother and grandfather, finding even greater joy in playing with water. His grandfather repeatedly scolded him, but the child remained stubborn and unyielding, and the grandfather grew tired of him. One day, while the grandfather was idly lying at the front of the boat, the child splashed water and nearly wet his clothes, which greatly angered him. In a fit of rage, he kicked the child, who fell into the river and was carried away by the current. His grandmother saw this and cried out in anguish: “Why have you killed my son? Not only will this end the Huang lineage, but what will become of me in my old age?” She wept without ceasing. His grandfather said, “This mischievous child is useless and will bring future trouble; it would be better to get rid of him early.” While the couple were quarreling, a boat came from downstream. The man on board asked what had happened, and said, “No harm has been done. About ten miles from here, there is a child playing in the water, having great fun—could it be this one? Hurry to save him; it is still in time.” The couple set off in their boat and indeed saw the child in the water, holding a large fish as he moved through the waves. His grandmother, as if finding a treasure, helped him onto the boat and returned. Knowing that this child could not be tolerated by her husband, she sent him to work as a hired child caregiver.

    370

    放牛之际，见村童读书，悦之，日往潜听。师出入屡见之，异其勤也，问儿亦解此耶？儿背诵如流，且讲大旨无误。师异之，告居停，使为学。儿受教，益奋，不数年，入庠，居停以女妻之，遂家焉。其姑夫妇相继亡，生无以为业，因设帐焉。秉资颖悟，无书不解，能剖晰至理，为人所服，故从游者众。名大振，为豫藩周王所闻，招为记室。在府数载，值闯贼肆乱，围豫，生知时不可救，去之。购大小舟数十，沿河而上。贼决黄河灌城，将没，生急救周王亲丁及从者数百人载去，贼追之，作五里雾，迷不知所往。或曰在嵩岳深处，人迹罕到，为王作第隐其间。生仍回家教读。时土寇虽多，皆遥望生村甲兵隐现，莫敢犯者，避难者佥往依之。恒见侍卫辈，夜以马邀生，周王召之也。

    Explanation

    370

    While herding cattle, he saw village children reading and was delighted by it. He went there every day to listen secretly. The teacher often saw him and found his diligence unusual, asking if he understood it as well. The child recited fluently and explained the main points without error. The teacher was surprised by him, told the host, and had him taught. The child received education and became even more diligent. Within a few years, he entered school, and the host gave his daughter to him in marriage, and he settled down. His aunt and her husband died one after another, leaving him with nothing to do for a living, so he set up a school. Possessed natural intelligence, he understood all books, could analyze profound truths, was respected by people, and many people followed him. His fame spread widely and was heard by the Prince of Yu, Zhou, who invited him to serve as a secretary. He served in the prince's office for several years when the rebel bandits caused chaos, surrounded the state of Yu. The scholar knew the situation could not be saved and left. He bought dozens of large and small boats and sailed upstream along the river. The rebels breached the Yellow River to flood the city, the city was about to be submerged, and the scholar rescued hundreds of the prince's relatives and attendants. The rebels pursued them, creating a five-mile fog, and lost track of them. Some say he is in the deep mountains of Song and Yu, where few people go, and built a residence for the prince to hide there. The scholar returned home to teach. At that time, there were many bandits who all saw the scholar's village with hidden troops and dared not attack, and people seeking refuge all came to rely on him. He often saw guards coming at night on horseback to invite him, it was the prince calling him.

    371

    时我大清兵已定豫，使大臣来，河决不能塞，募能建策者，生出应募，指挥筑堤，无不如意。将合龙之际，选四健卒，使抱椿，随扫下，卒惧死不从。生见之，洒泪而谓之曰：“汝四人数合建此大功，享千年奉祀，若不从，受违令之诛，等死耳。与其死无名，何如神长在耶？”四人乐，尽醉持椿入水，血随波泛。椿定扫进，功以成。大臣欲奏官之，生曰：“我明诸生也，既不能死，又从而官之，圣朝安用无耻之徒耶？我之来，为万民恤难，岂为功名？”遂去之。后水退沙涨，运粮河没，千万人不能开，民不堪命，共荐黄生。河督召之不来，使民往请命，始至，相度旧河形势已定，曰：“以某月日兴工，颁示居民远避，吾将独力为之。”至日，风雨雷电交作，云雾中见黑龙下垂，天地震动，喧腾之声，如黄流倒泻，如劲敌鏖兵。远近惊惧，如是督三昼夜而定。

    Explanation

    371

    At that time, the Great Qing army had pacified Henan, and a high-ranking official was sent to address the situation. The breach in the Yellow River could not be stopped, and those who could devise strategies were sought. The scholar stepped forward to respond to the call, directing the construction of the embankment, and everything went smoothly. At the moment when the breach was about to be sealed, four strong soldiers were selected to carry the wooden stakes and place them in the water. The soldiers, fearing death, refused to comply. The scholar saw this, shed tears, and said to them, "You four will collectively achieve this great feat, and your names will be honored for a thousand years. If you do not comply, you will face punishment for disobeying orders, but it is all the same; you will die anyway. Would you rather die without a name, or live on in legend?" The four soldiers were moved and, drinking until they were drunk, carried the stakes into the water, their blood floating with the waves. The stakes were firmly placed in the water, and the project was completed. The high-ranking official wanted to recommend him for an official post, but the scholar said, "I am a scholar from the Ming dynasty. Since I could not die, I would not accept an official post. Why would the holy dynasty use a shameless person? I came here to alleviate the suffering of the people, not for the sake of fame and fortune." He then left. Later, the water receded, and the sand rose, submerging the canal for transporting grain. A million people could not open it, and the people could not bear the hardship. They collectively recommended the scholar. The river commissioner summoned him, but he did not come. He sent the people to plead for his help. When he finally arrived, he had already assessed the old river's situation and said, "I will start work on a certain day, instruct the residents to stay far away, and I will do it alone." On that day, wind, rain, lightning, and thunder occurred together.
