A black dragon was seen descending in the clouds and mist, the earth and sky shook, and the roaring sound was like yellow water pouring down, like fierce enemies fighting. People near and far were terrified. He supervised for three days and nights until the project was completed.

    372

    河督往视之，运河已成，黄生不知所之矣。访之，闻已劳瘁卒。为奏封河神，立庙河干。后凡黄河有事，则神冕旒见波涛间，前驱四将，即抱椿四卒也。所至有功，进封为王，至今河滨有小黄蚘见，河工官弁即以舆迎之入庙。设享演剧，或留一二日，或三四日，即不见，人咸谓黄大王寄迹也。

    Explanation

    372

    The Governor-General of the Yellow River went to inspect the canal and found that it was already completed, but Huang, the scholar, had disappeared without a trace. Upon inquiry, it was learned that he had died from exhaustion and overwork. A proposal was made to enshrine the Yellow River as a deity, and a temple was built by the riverbank. Afterward, whenever there was an event concerning the Yellow River, the deity would appear in the waves with a crown and jade beads, accompanied by four generals leading the way, who were the same four soldiers who had held the stakes. Wherever the deity went, there was success, and it was promoted to the rank of king. Until today, small yellow worms are seen by the riverbank, and the officials of the Yellow River project welcomed him into the temple with a carriage, setting up offerings and performing plays. Sometimes he stayed for one or two days, sometimes for three or four days, and then he would disappear. People all say that the Great King Huang is leaving traces.

    373

    芗厈曰：古语有云，生而为英，死而为灵。王，英豪也，至今游中州，过大王镇者，犹觉凛凛然有生气。王之全传，事迹尚多，此其大略。
    　　
    　　祝由科
    　　
    　　祝由一科，其术甚神，凡金枪及跌打死者，顷刻能生之，系楚南破头老祖所传。其祖师北宋时人，太祖闻其名，召入禁内。时有小臣，不合上意而斩，非其罪者，祖师为续其首而生。太祖怒，使武士以大斧劈祖师，去其脑而弃之。其徒潜移尸回，以术生之，遁深山穷谷间，从此不履尘世矣。逮今千余年，尚存其传。术分两派：一派非重聘不行，一派不受谢礼，见死必救。受业时，各发誓愿，如违教者，身亡家灭，故敬谨奉持不失。楚中惟米客习是术，多不受谢者，故其术甚神。

    Explanation

    373

    Xiang Yan said: As ancient saying goes, "Born as a hero, died as a spirit." The King, a hero, still roams the Central Plains, and those who pass through Great King Town still feel his chilling, lifelike presence. The complete biography of the King, with many more deeds, is summarized here.

    The Zhubi School:
    The Zhubi School, its techniques are very powerful. It can bring back to life in a moment those who have died from spear wounds or falls. This art is passed down from the Southern Chu's Head-Breaking Ancestor. His ancestor was a person of the Northern Song Dynasty, and Emperor Taizu heard of his name and summoned him to the inner palace. At that time, there was a minor official who displeased the emperor and was beheaded, but he was not guilty of the crime. The ancestor revived him by reattaching his head. Emperor Taizu was furious and ordered a warrior to chop off the ancestor's head with an axe, remove his brain, and discard him. His disciples secretly moved his body back, used the art to revive him, and hid in deep mountains and remote valleys, never setting foot in the secular world again. To this day, more than a thousand years later, the tradition still exists. The art is divided into two schools: one requires a substantial fee, and the other does not accept thanks or gifts. They will save the dying. When they take on apprentices, they each make vows, swearing that if they violate the teachings, they will suffer the death of their family and the destruction of their household, so they hold the teachings with utmost respect and care. In the Chu region, only the Mike practitioners learn this art, and many of them do not accept thanks, hence their art is very powerful.

    374

    有冯、陈二客，运米至吾乡，投行主出粜，即寓其家。二人同出剃发，坐肆中。凡发匠，吾乡呼曰待诏。有小待诏者，龙阳君也，方与一客用小刀取耳，有棍徒以手挖小待诏之臀，出其不意，待诏惊耸失手，刀尖直刺客脑，客倒地而卧，肆人皆惊惶无措，肆主执棍徒缚之，将以鸣官。冯客谓陈客曰：“我等不能见死不救，曷以术生之？”陈客曰：“诺。”肆主闻之，欲跪求，冯急止之曰：“如此则不能救矣。诚出吾心，由吾所为则验。汝速沽烧酒一斤，白纸一束来，付陈客行法。”陈客曰：“曷披我发。”戟手持诀，向死者之耳，噀酒令湿，以一纸糊之，喃喃诵咒画符。又噀酒，又盖纸，如是者数十重，酒完纸尽而止，曰：“辫我发，使多人助我拔刀出。”则扶死者出门，喝之走，其人狂奔回家，妻子问之，不答，僵卧于其床。

    Explanation

    374

    There were two visitors, Feng and Chen, who transported rice to my village, deposited it with the shopkeeper to sell, and then stayed at his house. The two went out together to get their hair cut and sat in the shop. All hair-cutters in our village are called daizhao. There was a young daizhao named Longyang Jun, who was in the process of trimming the ear of one of the guests, when a thug suddenly grabbed the young daizhao by the buttocks, startling him and causing him to drop the knife, which immediately pierced the guest’s brain. The guest collapsed to the ground, and all the people in the shop were frightened and at a loss. The shop owner seized the thug and bound him, intending to report him to the authorities.

    Feng said to Chen, “We cannot stand by and watch someone die; how can we use a technique to save him?” Chen replied, “Agreed.” Upon hearing this, the shop owner wanted to kneel and beg for their help, but Feng quickly stopped him, saying, “If we do that, we will not be able to save him. It must come from my heart, and if I do it, it will work. You must quickly buy a pound of burning wine and a bundle of white paper, and give them to Chen to perform the ritual.”

    Chen said, “Then let me braid your hair.” Holding a spear-like object in his hand and making a ritual gesture, he approached the dead man’s ear, spat wine to moisten it, and covered it with a sheet of paper, muttering incantations and drawing a talisman. He repeated this process—spitting wine, covering the paper—layer upon layer, until the wine was finished and the paper was used up. Then he said, “Braid my hair, so that many people will help me pull the knife out.” They then carried the dead man outside and shouted at him to run. The man ran wildly home, and when his wife and children asked him what had happened, he did not answer, but lay stiffly on his bed.

    375

    家人莫知其故，环守之，经夜而醒，曰：“我忆昨日为小待诏以刀刺耳，疼极而昏，何今日无恙，其梦耶？则发已剃矣。”正转辗自疑问，肆主偕棍徒来问候，始知其故，曰：“我受米客再生恩，当有以报之。”棍徒曰：“是我之过，客脱我罪，已备盛礼来，邀君往谢耳。”其人欣然偕至行家，主人见以礼物来，知谢救生之事者，急阻之曰：“不可，我知客必不受谢。尚高卧后楼，勿扰之。”众皆曰：“岂有受恩不谢哉？客纵不受，亦明我等心耳。且问此后伤复发否。”主人不得已，领入楼下，唤二客，冯客启楼窗见之，摇手曰：“切不可谢，谢则恩将仇报矣。”棍徒与受伤人感极不觉跪地，闻临河后窗开，似有人跃入河中声，冯客顿足曰：“尔等逼死陈君矣。”随下楼同往后河，问众舟子，见一客跳河，打捞无获。冯客亦不甚追究，为脱货易银携装而去。

    Explanation

    375

    The family did not know the reason, so they kept a close watch on him. After a night, he woke up and said, "I remember yesterday being a low-ranking official who stabbed my ear with a knife. The pain was so intense that I fainted. Why am I unharmed today? Is it a dream? Then my hair has been shaved." He was turning over and questioning himself when the shop owner, accompanied by the ruffians, came to greet him. Only then did he know the reason. He said, "I received the life-saving grace of the rice guest. I should have something to repay him." The ruffians said, "It was my fault. The guest freed me from my crime. He has already prepared a grand ceremony and is coming to invite you to thank him." The person happily accompanied them to the shop. The shop owner saw the gifts coming and knew that this was to thank for saving a life. He urgently stopped and said, "Not possible. I know the guest will certainly not accept the thanks. He is still sleeping in the back building. Do not disturb him." Everyone said, "How can there be someone who receives grace but does not thank? Even if the guest does not accept, it also makes our intentions clear. Also, ask if the injury will recur in the future." The shop owner had no choice but to lead them down to the lower floor and called the two guests. Mr. Feng opened the window of the upper building and saw them. He shook his hand and said, "Absolutely do not thank. If you thank, the grace will turn into resentment." The ruffians and the injured person were so moved that they knelt down without realizing it. Hearing the back window by the river open, it seemed like someone jumped into the river. Mr. Feng stamped his foot and said, "You have forced Mr. Chen to die." He followed down the stairs and went to the back river, asking the boatmen. They saw one guest jump into the river, but the search was unsuccessful. Mr. Feng also did not pursue it much. To unload the goods, exchange for silver, and leave with the belongings.

    376

    芗厈曰：客之术神矣，不受礼物则可，何至避一跪而水遁？可谓玄之又玄，众妙之门。
    　　
    　　李蒙师
    　　
    　　吾乡李生应童子试，年至不惑，不得入泮，遂以训蒙为业。乡农延之，岁入修廿余缗。及门者十数顽童，甘厌其烦，然舍此更无别业可为，不得不下气降心以就之，非其本心也。居停十余家，轮流供膳，有余则以施丐。有一老人，日向先生求乞，不以为厌，求必饱而后去。一日先生病目，肿甚，伏几卧，老人来视，曰：“先生之疾，易为也。我往觅草药，煮而洗之，不日愈矣。”未几，果以药草来，嗅之香甚。如法熬煎洗，果就痊。

    Explanation

    376

    Xiang Yan said: The guest's skill is indeed divine, and it is acceptable not to accept gifts. But why go so far as to avoid a bow and escape by water? This can be called the most profound and mysterious, the gateway to all wonders.

    Mr. Li, my fellow townsman, took the examination for young scholars. At the age of forty, he did not enter the school, so he then took up teaching as a profession. The villagers hired him, and he earned more than twenty strings of cash annually. A dozen or so unruly children came to his door, and though he found them tiresome, he had no other profession to fall back on, so he had to humble himself and do it, though it was not his true desire. More than ten families took turns providing him with meals, and any surplus was given to beggars. An old man came to the teacher every day to beg, and the teacher did not find him annoying; he would give until the old man was full before leaving. One day, the teacher's eyes became swollen, and he lay down on his desk. The old man came to visit and said, "The teacher's illness is easy to cure. I will go and gather some herbs, boil them, and wash them on you, and you will recover soon." In a short while, he indeed brought some herbs, which smelled very fragrant. Following the method to boil and wash, he indeed recovered.

    377

    遂以制钱二百具肉食谢。老人来，食讫曰：“先生之目，有以异于平日乎？”先生曰：“无以异也，觉清爽而已。”老人曰：“不然。吾以书置案下，先生试隔木视之。”书似在玻璃中，字字朗澈，先生不胜诧异。老人曰：“先生有此目力，何必教读？请日以百文往博场中压宝，探囊而得，不胜于求童蒙耶？然人生福德有限，不得过贪，日得教百文，一家饱暖，无异仙人。若贪多则犯鬼神之忌，必致祸，戒之！戒之！”先生乐甚，不暇细问其由，老人亦大笑去。

    Explanation

    377

    Thus, he offered two hundred government-issued coins to prepare a meat meal as a token of gratitude. The old man arrived, and after finishing the meal, said, "Is there anything different about your eyesight today compared to usual?" The scholar replied, "There is no difference—only that I feel particularly clear and refreshed." The old man said, "That is not correct. I have placed a book under the table; please try to look at it through the wood." The book seemed as if it were in glass, each character clear and distinct, and the scholar could not help but be astonished. The old man said, "With such eyesight, why bother teaching and reading? Go daily to the gambling house with a hundred coins to place bets, and you will be able to take the prize from your pocket—would that not be better than teaching children? However, human life has limited blessings and virtue; one must not be overly greedy. If you teach a hundred students a day, your family will be well-fed and warm, no different from an immortal. But if you greedily take more, you will anger the ghosts and gods, and disaster will surely come. Be warned! Be warned!" The scholar was greatly pleased and had no time to inquire further into the reason, and the old man also laughed heartily and departed.

    378

    次日，先生辞馆，日游博场，家人皆丰衣足食，甚自得也。然谨守老人戒，不敢多取，故人亦不觉。当时有好压宝者富室子张姓，万金事业将尽，不胜忿恨，思求师以佐之。访诸乡人，其弟子以先生目力告，张子欣然执贽往拜。先生初不承，张子跪求无已，先生知机已泄，情不能却，曰：“吾术只可日得数百钱耳，何济于君？”张子曰：“先生守法持廉，不敢逾分，我随先生后，任我所为，无碍先生。且我得恢复旧物，将重酬，先生且无须为此矣。”先生心动，携往场中，先生下百文，其人即满注。

    Explanation

    378

    The next day, the teacher resigned from his position and began visiting the gambling house daily. His family was well-fed and clothed, and he was very content. However, he carefully followed the old man's advice and dared not take more, so others did not notice. At that time, there was a wealthy young man named Zhang who enjoyed gambling. His fortune was nearly depleted, and he could not bear the anger and resentment, thinking to seek a teacher to help him. He inquired among the villagers, and his disciples informed him of the teacher's keen eyesight. Zhang happily presented gifts and went to pay respects. The teacher initially did not agree, but Zhang knelt and pleaded persistently. The teacher knew the secret was out and could not refuse, saying, "My skill can only earn a few hundred coins a day; how can it help you?" Zhang replied, "The teacher adheres to the law and maintains integrity, daring not to exceed the bounds. I will follow you, and I can do as I please, it will not hinder you. And if I can recover my lost wealth, I will give you a generous reward; the teacher need not worry about this." The teacher was moved and took him to the gambling house. The teacher bet a hundred coins, and the other person immediately bet the full amount.

    379

    不数日，宝场半为张子败业。赌者聚谋曰：“异哉！张子昔来必败，何近日所向无敌？不求其故，我等皆不能为业矣。”访之，得李先生确耗，众曰：“此人不除，何以营生？”遂共谋以五百金贿丐，告之曰：“某村李先生，吾仇也。与汝等五百金，只须挖其双目，为首者不过军流，以此金至配所营运，富可致矣。且在外乐业，何如在乡贫苦？”一壮者诺之，偕数丐伏林中，见先生过，突出与斗，共擒之，抠其目睛。投官，以忿争误伤自首，得减等，拟城旦。先生失目，悔之晚矣。幸张子复己业，闻先生为所累，改过迁善，以千金报先生，得以考终焉。
    　　
    　　芗厈曰：仙人何播弄下愚，益之适以损之。使我遇之，当求性命双修之道，则超凡入圣可期矣，何仅以温饱自得哉？或曰：“君所以不得遇仙人，贪故也。”竟无词以对。

    Explanation

    379

    Within a few days, half of the treasure field was ruined by Zhang Zi. The gamblers gathered and plotted, saying, "Strange! Zhang Zi used to be sure to lose, but why is he now invincible in all directions? If we don't find out the reason, we won't be able to continue our business." They investigated and found out the accurate news about Mr. Li. The group said, "If this person is not eliminated, how can we make a living?" They then devised a plan to bribe beggars with five hundred gold coins and told them, "Mr. Li from a certain village is my enemy. I will give you five hundred gold coins. Just dig out his eyes. The leader will not receive more than military exile. With this money, operate at the place of exile, and one can become rich. Moreover, to enjoy work outside is better than being poor in the village." One strong man agreed, and along with several beggars, they hid in the forest. Seeing Mr. Li pass by, they suddenly attacked him, fought, and captured him, plucking out his eyes. They reported to the authorities, confessing to unintentional injury due to anger. They received a reduced sentence and were sentenced to forced labor. Mr. Li lost his sight and realized it was too late to regret. Fortunately, Zhang Zi restored his own business and heard that Mr. Li was involved. He corrected his mistakes and improved, repaying Mr. Li with a thousand gold coins, allowing Mr. Li to die a natural death.

    Xiang Yan said, "Why do immortals toy with the foolish? To benefit them is to harm them. If I were to meet them, I would seek the way of cultivating both life and spirit, and one can hope to transcend the ordinary and enter the realm of the sage. Why settle for just warmth and food?" Someone said, "You have not met immortals because of greed." He had no words to respond.

    380

    唐词林
    　　
    　　吾乡唐生业儒，应童子试，年三十不得游庠。其父市侩也，谓生曰：“吾家世以负贩为业，汝欲读书，不得志，今吾老而汝壮，不得赡养乃翁，固无论矣，但汝何以终身？无已，汝叔在京师，闻事业兴隆，曷往依之？亦可博升斗以养家，胜于坐食多多矣。”生诺诺，自惭不能博一衿，亦图改业，乃随粮艘北上。其叔在前门外，立南货肆，见生来，甚悦，曰：“我肆中正乏人料理，汝二弟皆在学读书，不能助我。一人能有几许精神，而独掌营运耶？汝其为我司帐。岁得数十金，亦可寄家，不无小补。果勤能也，再益汝俸。”生安之。但生喜读书，虽在肆，手不释卷，兴至则拍案朗诵，肆伙及买物者见之，皆大笑。叔愠甚，屡诤不改，谓生曰：“汝既愿读书，我送汝入学，伴两弟读亦可。”

    Explanation

    380

    Tang’s Career in Commerce

    My fellow townsman, Tang Sheng, pursued the Confucian path and took the tongzi examination, but at thirty, he had still not entered the school. His father was a merchant, and he said to his son, “Our family has always been engaged in trade. You wish to study, but have not succeeded. Now that I am old and you are strong, I cannot be supported by you, which is of no concern. But how will you support yourself for the rest of your life? If there is no other way, your uncle is in the capital, and his business is thriving. Why not go and rely on him? You could at least earn a modest income to support the family, which is far better than idling and eating without working.”

    The son hesitated, ashamed that he could not obtain even the scholar’s robe, and decided to change his career. Thus, he traveled north by grain ship. His uncle was stationed outside the front gate, having set up a shop selling southern goods. When he saw his nephew arrive, he was very pleased and said, “My shop is in need of someone to manage it. Your two younger brothers are both studying at school and cannot help me. How much energy can one person have, and yet I must manage the business alone? You should be in charge of the accounts. If you earn tens of gold coins each year, you can send some home, which will be a small help. If you prove to be diligent and capable, I will increase your salary.”

    Tang accepted this arrangement. However, he still loved reading. Though he worked in the shop, he never let go of his books. When inspiration struck, he would bang the table and recite aloud. The shop assistants and customers who saw this all laughed. His uncle became very angry and repeatedly admonished him, but Tang did not change his ways. The uncle said to him, “If you truly wish to read, I will send you to school, where you can study alongside your two younger brothers.”

    381

    生益喜，随送入垫。其师，都中廪膳生也，年逾半百，岸然道貌，收生为徒，曰：“汝齿已长矣，能学文乎？”生曰：“固所愿也，不敢请耳。”乃命题试之，文成，师阅之，击节赞叹曰：“汝在南方，造诣如是，不能入泮，屈死真才矣。”生见师所改两弟文，甚平庸，心轻之。每遇课期，潜往观剧，晚归，一挥而就，师又善其词也。两弟密告父，含怒至塾，训生曰：“汝在肆，则以读书至高；在学，又嬉戏无度，何怪一事无成，真废物也。”师闻之曰：“乃侄平日勤学，虽课期出外，亦不误作文，其聪明才力，胜乃郎百倍，若以应试，高发可期。”叔曰：“试期在迩，果如先生言，得游庠序，亦可光以门闾，恐未必耳。”师曰：“无他法传，生惟小试场中，断不可自作佳文，当尽心为两弟成之，以余力毕乃事，附名榜尾足矣。盖两弟寄迹久，高标无碍。汝初冒北籍，名列前茅，恐招人忌耳。汝切志之。”

    Explanation

    381

    The protagonist became increasingly pleased and was taken to the academy. His teacher, a stipend-funded student in the capital, was over fifty years old, with a dignified and solemn appearance. He took the protagonist as a disciple and said, "You have reached adulthood. Can you learn literature?" The protagonist replied, "It is indeed what I wish, but I dare not ask." The teacher then gave him a test. After completing the test, the teacher read the protagonist's work and clapped in admiration, saying, "You have achieved a high level of learning in the south, yet you cannot enter the academy, it is a pity that your talent is not recognized." The protagonist felt that the other students' work was inferior. Whenever there was a class, he would secretly go to watch a play, return late, and complete his assignments quickly and effortlessly. The teacher praised his work. The other two students secretly told their father, who angrily went to the academy and scolded the protagonist, saying, "You are praised for studying hard in the academy, but in school, you play too much, it is no wonder you achieve nothing, you are truly useless." The teacher heard this and said, "Your nephew studies diligently every day. Even though he leaves during class, he still manages to complete his assignments. His intelligence and ability surpass those of the other students by a hundredfold. If he were to take an exam, success would be expected." The uncle said, "The exam is approaching soon. If it is as you say, and he succeeds in the exam, it would bring honor to our family, but it is doubtful." The teacher said, "There is no other way. In the small exam, he must not write a good essay himself. He should focus on helping the other two students, and use the remaining effort to complete his own task, being listed at the end of the list would be sufficient. The other two students have been around for a long time and are not at risk of being overshadowed. You are new and have a northern background, being at the top might attract jealousy. Take this advice seriously."

    382

    生诺。县府两试，谨如师命，助两弟在前十名，自附而已。是年以案首入学，又带一弟，名列第二。从此连捷，中式，登进士，入词垣矣。迎养乃翁，重酬其师，且貤封叔父，所以报本也。

    Explanation

    382

    He agreed. He passed the county and prefectural examinations, strictly following his teacher's instructions, helping his two younger brothers to rank among the top ten, and passing himself. That year, he was the top scorer and entered the school, bringing his younger brother along, who ranked second. From then on, he continued to succeed, passing the examination, becoming a jinshi, and being appointed to the Hanlin Academy. He welcomed and supported his father, generously rewarded his teacher, and posthumously bestowed an official title on his uncle, all to repay the source.

    383

    芗厈曰：唐君在南，一衿难博，在北则隆隆直上，置身青云，岂北人果不如南人耶？曰：非也，有命存焉。
    　　
    　　卷二
    　　
    　　南宋高宗遗事
    　　
    　　康王构自金营遁归，创业后，为汪黄所误，复为金人大破，王易服南奔。自泥马渡江后，到处有土神保护。
    　　
    　　道经盐官州，追兵将及，乱窜至蛇墩。王见田间二人并耕，呼救，农夫视王貌异常，知为贵人，迎谒马前，请王下马释服，与其弟相易。农弟服王衣，骑马南驰，使王以泥污而戴笠荷蓑，立田沟中，执耜工作，农夫候于道左。追兵至，问农夫，告以状貌，曰：“见如此人否？”农夫曰：“适有骑黄马官人，南去不远。”追兵驰及，执之，非王也，怒戮之。时王已去蓑笠裋褐，从别道逸。金兵回斩农夫，四路追寻。

    Explanation

    383

    Xiang Yan said: "When Tang Jun was in the south, it was difficult to gain recognition; when he was in the north, he rose rapidly to a high position, standing among the clouds. Is it really that northerners are inferior to southerners? The answer is no, it is due to fate."

    Volume II

    Anecdotes of Emperor Gaozong of the Southern Song Dynasty

    After Emperor Kang of the Southern Song Dynasty fled from the Jin camp, he founded the state. Later, he was misled by Wang Huang and was again defeated by the Jin forces. The king changed his clothes and fled south. After the legend of the mud horse helping him cross the river, he was protected by local deities everywhere.

    He passed through Yanguan State, and the pursuing troops were about to catch up, so he fled chaotically to Snake Hill. The king saw two men plowing in the fields and called for help. The farmer noticed the king's unusual appearance and recognized him as a noble person. He bowed before the king's horse, asked the king to dismount and change his clothes, and switched places with his brother. The farmer's brother wore the king's clothes and rode south, while the king was made to look like a common laborer, wearing a straw hat, carrying a raincoat, and standing in a field, holding a hoe and working. The farmer waited on the side of the road. When the pursuing troops arrived, they asked the farmer, who described the king's appearance. They asked, "Have you seen such a person?" The farmer replied, "Just now, there was an official riding a yellow horse who went south not far from here." The pursuing troops chased after him, captured the wrong person, and killed him in anger. At that time, the king had already removed his straw hat, raincoat, and coarse clothing and escaped through another path. The Jin troops returned and killed the farmer, pursuing him in four directions.

    384

    王至州城郭北有古庙，见茅篷内一僧席地诵经，王潜入求庇，僧以衲衣盖之，嘱勿动。追兵主将骑白马者遥见大汉入篷，遂下马执刀往，将搜觅。僧见来将喝曰：“降人欲杀故主，良心安在？即得封侯，亦留万代骂名。况有总领，气谊不投，将冒汝功而戳汝。宋朝气数未尽，不如依老僧言，退去为善。”来将悟，再拜曰：“予实南人，被金人掳去，降非本意。谨遵师命释王。但不能掩北兵耳目，请就死以明心。”遂自刎。兵见主将死，皆散去。王出，僧请无负来将，因不知姓名，封为白马将军、土地尊神。僧指示王速上临安，尚有一难。过此即投张帅，能退金人，仍登九五之尊，作南朝创业主也。王谢去。

    Explanation

    384

    The king arrived at an ancient temple in the northern part of the city wall. He saw a monk inside a thatched hut, sitting on the ground and reciting scriptures. The king secretly entered the hut to seek shelter, and the monk covered him with his robe, instructing him not to move. The commander of the pursuing army, riding a white horse, saw a tall man enter the hut from afar. He dismounted, drew his sword, and went forward, intending to search. The monk, seeing the approaching commander, shouted and said, "A defector wants to kill his former master. Where is his conscience? Even if he gets a marquis title, he will still leave a name of disgrace for all time. Besides, there is a general whose feelings and intentions do not align. He will claim your merit and kill you. The mandate of the Song Dynasty is not yet exhausted. It would be better to follow the old monk's advice and retreat for the good." The commander understood, kneeled twice, and said, "I am actually a southerner, captured by the Jin people. Defection was not my intention. I obediently followed the master's command and released the king. But I could not hide from the northern soldiers. I requested to die to clarify my heart." Then he committed suicide. The soldiers saw the commander die and all dispersed. The king emerged, and the monk requested not to dishonor the commander, since his name was unknown, and enshrined him as the White Horse General and the Land Deity. The monk instructed the king to quickly go to Lin'an, noting there was still one difficulty. After this, he should go to General Zhang, who could repel the Jin people, ascend to the imperial throne, and become the founder of the Southern Dynasty. The king thanked him and departed.

    385

    行至半山。追兵又至，王避入桑林，见树上有女采桑，呼救。女下树，出巨筐，使王跃入，以桑叶盖之。甫定，金将入林，问女子见裋褐高大之男子否？女曰：“过去已半日矣。”诈来将退。女归家，唤父兄舁筐归，倾之，见少年丈夫，父兄皆詈女无耻。王不暇辩，即望山后奔走，天已暮，不知去路，依树憩息。夜将阑，见一灯望树而来，王惊骇，藏树后，一女携灯跪告曰：“此非吉地，请王随妾灯速行。西去即抵张帅营，可平安矣。”王不暇细问，随灯行甚急，转瞬间鸣钲呵号之声，王止不敢前。女子跪伏万岁曰：“妾采桑人也，因父母不谅，痛加呵责，无以自明，遂缢。此心不昧，故携灯送王，请乞阴封。”王曰：“寡人登极，当敕封汝为半山神君，永享祭祀。”女叩首谢曰：“前途即张帅营，忠臣也。王速投之，妾不能追随矣。”倏不见，天亦向晨。

    Explanation

    385

    The king walked to the middle of the mountain. The pursuing soldiers arrived again, and the king hid in the mulberry forest. He saw a woman picking mulberries on a tree and called for help. The woman came down from the tree, brought out a large basket, made the king jump into it, and covered it with mulberry leaves. Just as they had settled, the golden general entered the forest and asked the woman if she had seen a tall, large man wearing coarse clothes. The woman replied, "He has already passed by half a day ago." The false general withdrew. The woman returned home, called her father and brothers to carry the basket back. When they emptied it, they saw a young man. The father and brothers all cursed the woman for being shameless. The king had no time to argue and immediately ran towards the back of the mountain. It was already dark, and he did not know the way, so he leaned against a tree to rest. As the night was almost over, he saw a lamp coming towards the tree. The king was terrified and hid behind the tree. A woman carried a lamp and knelt to say, "This is not a good place. Please follow my lamp quickly. Going west will reach the camp of General Zhang, and you will be safe." The king did not have time to ask in detail and followed the lamp quickly. In an instant, the sound of gongs and shouts was heard, and the king stopped and dared not go forward. The woman knelt and said, "Long live," and said, "I am a woman who picks mulberries. Because my parents do not understand, they severely scold me, and I cannot prove my innocence, so I hanged myself. My heart is not dark, so I carry the lamp to guide the king. Please grant me an underground title." The king said, "When I ascend the throne, I will order you to be enshrined as the Half-Mountain God, forever honored with sacrifices." The woman knelt and thanked, saying, "The future is the camp of General Zhang, a loyal minister. Quickly seek refuge there; I cannot follow you anymore." Suddenly she disappeared, and the sky also turned to morning.

    386

    王先闻僧与神言，放胆闯入军营，被获，送帅营。张帅见王来，欢呼迎拜，遂号召韩岳张刘四帅，合击破金军，定鼎于武林。帝不忘救驾功，封二农夫为蛇墩双土地、崇善明王，征僧为护国大禅师，不至。为女神立庙山巅，至今香火不绝，春间佥朝拜半山娘娘云。
    　　
    　　王土地
    　　
    　　吾乡王生，邑中正士也。奉君子之九思，遵圣人之四勿，朴诚恭敬，为通邑名师。时年逾花甲，在多塾教读，忽谓门人曰：“我将赴任，告归理家事。”门人知先生向无妄言，计出贡未久，焉得官？然不敢诘，谨问先生之任何所？生曰：“玉环耳。限期紧迫，速为尔父兄言之，即此告别。”门人入告，父兄皆来贺，且请文凭阅视。生匣中郑重出之，实无一纸，空指向居停曰：“限仅十日，是以不敢逗留耳。”居停谓先生思为官，入痰迷矣，即具舟备物送归。

    Explanation

    386

    The protagonist first heard the monk speaking with a deity and boldly entered the military camp without hesitation, where he was captured and sent to the general's camp. The general, upon seeing the protagonist, greeted him joyfully and paid him homage. He then summoned the four generals—Han, Yue, Zhang, and Liu—to launch a coordinated attack that broke through the Jin army, establishing a capital in Wulin. The emperor did not forget the protagonist's heroic deed of saving his life and bestowed the title of "Snake Dune Twin Earth Gods" and "Chongshan Bright King" upon the two farmers. He also offered the monk the high-ranking title of "Guardian of the Nation, Great Zen Master," but the monk declined. A temple was built on a mountain peak to honor the goddess, and to this day, her worship continues, especially during the spring season when people collectively pay homage to the Half Mountain Lady.

    Wang Land God

    In my hometown, there was a man named Wang, a virtuous scholar in the county. He followed the nine reflections of a gentleman and adhered to the four prohibitions of the sage, demonstrating simplicity, honesty, and reverence. He was a renowned teacher in the region. At the age of sixty, he was teaching in many schools when he suddenly told his disciples, "I will be taking up a new position, so I must return home to settle family matters." The disciples knew that Mr. Wang never spoke without reason, and though he had only recently passed the imperial examination, it was unlikely that he had already been appointed to an official post. However, they dared not question him and respectfully asked, "To what position are you being appointed, sir?" Wang replied, "The position of Yu Huan. The deadline is very tight, so I must quickly inform your fathers and brothers, and I will bid you farewell now." The disciples went to inform the parents, who all came to congratulate him and asked to see his official credentials. Wang solemnly took out a box and presented it, but there was no document inside. He pointed to his host and said, "The deadline is only ten days, so I dare not linger." The host thought that Mr. Wang had gone mad with the desire for an official post and immediately prepared a boat and provisions to send him back home.

    387

    至家，欣然为妻子述之，咸疑无此事，乃遍拜戚友，约日话别，戚友皆惑，互相商订于所约之日，移樽候饯。至日，生早起，具衣冠候门，戚友皆集。生以家贫子幼，谆嘱照拂，戚友唯唯，家人无不惶恐者。若谓有疾，则精神健旺，实无死法。若谓疯疾，则言行清楚，毫无错谬。至夜在厅事内大开筵宴，生兴勃然，历叙平生旧情，欢呼畅饮。交三鼓，忽闻远来音乐声，异香满堂。生整衣冠，命子开大门曰：“吏役来迎者至矣。”乃居中坐，问来者几人，似以手接物，向空指点，分付开道赴任。转向亲朋一揖告别，作登舆势，倒地不动，已卒矣。举哀盛殓毕，夫人欲验其实，使其子往玉环访之。至则土地庙之道人迎于境曰：“王公子来耶？”问何以知之，曰：“某日阖境梦王公莅任，且告道人以公子至期。”随入庙泣拜，居民闻之，来观者百千人。玉环司马恐其惑众，资送公子回籍。

    Explanation

    387

    Upon arriving home, he happily recounted his experience to his wife and children, but they all doubted that such a thing had happened. He then went to visit all his relatives and friends, setting a date for a farewell gathering. His relatives and friends were all perplexed, and on the agreed-upon day, they prepared wine to wait for the farewell feast. On the day, he got up early, dressed in formal attire, and waited at the door. His relatives and friends all gathered. He earnestly asked them to take care of his poor family and young children. They all nodded, and the family members were all filled with anxiety. If one said he was ill, his spirit was vigorous, and there was no way he should die. If one said he was insane, his words and actions were clear, with no mistakes. At night, he held a grand banquet in the hall. He was full of enthusiasm, recounting old stories from his life, and they drank merrily. As the third drumbeat struck, suddenly distant music and a strange fragrance filled the hall. He straightened his clothes and ordered his son to open the main gate, saying, "The officials come to welcome me." He sat in the center and asked how many people were coming, as if he were receiving something and pointing to the air, instructing them to clear the way to his new post. He turned to his relatives and friends, bowed, and made a gesture of mounting a carriage, then fell to the ground motionless, having died. After mourning and the burial, his wife wanted to verify the truth. She sent her son to visit Yuhan to inquire about it. Upon arriving, the Taoist priest at the land temple greeted him, saying, "Is it Prince Wang coming?" He asked how the priest knew, and the priest replied, "On a certain day, the whole region dreamed that Prince Wang was taking office, and he told me about your arrival date." They entered the temple and wept in worship, and when the residents heard about it, thousands came to watch. The military governor of Yuhan was afraid of causing a disturbance and provided funds to send the prince back to his hometown.

    388

    芗厈曰：王公聪明正直，宜其为神，然子孙无达者何也？或曰：“近代如岳武穆、于忠肃，皆赫赫神灵，后裔尚无显达，想必祖宗发泄过甚耳。”余曰：“否。天之报施正人也，香火万年，胜于子孙千亿。”
    　　
    　　许湛然
    　　
    　　湛然名澄清，直隶唐山县人。幼失怙，嗣其姑为子。姑后有子皆成立，分与湛然薄地十余亩，使携妻自食。湛然力农朴诚，立志凡与交者，成人之善，格人之非，尤好排难解纷，故邑人莫不倾心焉。中年家业成就，纳粟得从九品，不愿登仕版，虽不甚读书，而精求歧黄之术，为人医治立痊，不索谢，曰：“此圣贤遗留济世之道，岂可利己乎？”是以求之者益众，亦无稍厌倦。

    Explanation

    388

    Xiang Yan said: The Duke Wang was intelligent and upright, fitting to become a deity, yet why are his descendants not successful? Someone said: "In recent times, figures like Yue Fei and Yu Song, both renowned deities, still have descendants who lack prominence, likely because their ancestors' virtue was so great that it has been depleted." I replied: "No. How the heavens reward virtuous people is to ensure their veneration lasts for a thousand years, which is more valuable than billions of descendants."

    Xu Zhanran

    Zhanran's name is Qingcheng, from Tangshan County, Zhili Province. He lost his father at a young age and was adopted by his aunt as her son. After his aunt had other children who grew up, she gave him a small plot of land, about ten acres, to support himself and his wife. Zhanran worked hard and was simple and sincere, determined to help anyone he met, encouraging good deeds and correcting wrongdoings, especially excelling in resolving disputes and difficulties. Therefore, no one in the town did not admire him. In middle age, he achieved prosperity and obtained the rank of ninth grade by donating grain to the government, but he did not want to serve as an official. Although he did not read much, he studied medicine diligently and treated people with immediate recovery, without asking for payment. He said: "This is the way of the sages to save the world; how could it be for personal gain?" Therefore, those seeking his help increased, and he did not grow tired of it.

    389

    一日自乡间治病归，过城外之留养局，局隆冬有人栖止，至春则人散，僻静所也。见局前有少妇坐地而泣，湛然问故，妇曰：“我柏乡某村人，不堪姑虐，出外暂避，不觉行远至此。天将暮矣，既无宿处，又无饮食，是以悲也。”湛然曰：“汝在此不可动，我将归取饭食汝。”遂去至家，携胡饼来，已不见妇，觅之，闻局内有嚷嚷哭泣声。入见两男子一老媪劝妇随伊去，妇号泣不从。湛然知为匪人，叱曰：“我在此，鼠辈敢尔！”皆张皇遁去。乃唤妇出，使之食讫，曰：“我送汝暂寄尼庵可乎？”妇允诺，遂送交大士庵老尼收之。湛然于路已问悉妇人居址，距唐邑三十余里。

    Explanation

    389

    One day, after returning from treating a patient in the countryside, Zhanran passed by the orphanage outside the city. During the harsh winter, the place was occupied by those in need, but by spring it was deserted, making it a quiet and secluded spot. Seeing a young woman sitting on the ground weeping before the orphanage, Zhanran asked her why she was crying. The woman replied, “I am from a certain village in Bai Xiang. I could no longer endure the cruelty of my mother-in-law, so I left home to take temporary refuge, but I did not realize I had wandered so far. The day is nearly over, and I have neither a place to stay nor food, and so I am filled with sorrow.” Zhanran said, “You must not move from here. I will go home and bring you some food.” He then went home, took a trapezium bread with him, but by the time he returned, the woman was no longer there. He searched for her and heard loud crying coming from inside the orphanage. Entering, he saw two men and an old woman persuading the woman to go with them. She wept and refused to go. Zhanran realized they were bandits and shouted, “I am here! How dare you, you rats!” All of them fled in panic. He then called the woman out, had her eat, and said, “Shall I send you temporarily to a nunnery?” The woman agreed, and so he delivered her to an old nun at the Dashi Temple to take in. On the way, Zhanran had already inquired and learned the woman’s residence, which was thirty or more li from Tang Yi.

    390

    次日往寻其家人，路遇二人访收留少妇者。湛然问明妇之年貌服色相符，询知二人为妇之夫叔及兄，偕二人归至尼庵，劝妇回家。妇惧不敢归，湛然曰：“我送汝去，能劝汝姑不加责，而生欢喜心可乎？”妇曰：“诚如是，一家聚顺有何不愿？”遂行。湛然先驱至妇家，则妇之父母翁姑方勃谿，湛然进而排解之曰：“汝等能相安也，还汝妇。否则终必为人掠卖，奈何？”姑曰：“我方悔督责过严，致有此失。使妇无恙而归，有不相安者乎？”湛然始告之故，少妇偕二人亦至。众皆感谢。湛然后他出过其门，妇之翁姑及夫邀遮入室，见其一庭和睦，妇已生儿，佥再拜感激不已。

    Explanation

    390

    The next day, he went to search for the woman’s family and on the way met two men visiting the person who had taken in the young woman. Zhanran inquired and confirmed that the woman’s age, appearance, and attire matched, and learned that the two men were her husband’s uncle and her brother. Accompanied by them, he returned to the nunnery and urged the woman to go home. The woman was afraid and dared not return, so Zhanran said, “I will take you there. Can I persuade your mother-in-law not to scold you and make her happy?” The woman replied, “If that is truly possible, what would I not wish for if my whole family could be reunited in harmony?” So they set off. Zhanran went ahead to the woman’s home, where her parents and in-laws were just quarreling. Zhanran stepped forward and mediated, saying, “If you can live in peace with one another, return your daughter-in-law to you. Otherwise, she will eventually be kidnapped and sold—how can you bear that?” The mother-in-law said, “I am now regretting that I was too harsh in my discipline, which led to this situation. If the woman returns unharmed, how could there be any disharmony?” Zhanran then told them the reason, and the young woman, accompanied by the two men, also arrived. All of them expressed their gratitude. Afterward, as Zhanran was leaving, the woman’s father-in-law, mother-in-law, and husband stopped him and invited him into their home. They saw that the whole household was now in harmony, and the woman had already given birth to a child. All of them bowed twice in deep gratitude and were moved beyond words.

    391

    又一日，湛然自外来，见坟林内隐隐一男子就树自缢，抚之犹温，急解释之，撅而苏。年约弱冠，衣虽槛楼，貌殊清俊，不似乞丐中人。问其故，泣曰：“小人任县某村人，父母伯叔只小人一子。在学读书，被匪徒诱使博，大败不敢归。无资以应，匪徒尽留小人衣物，尚不足抵，易与破烂衣裤，使推小车送客觅钱偿债。今送至唐邑而回，思之不胜愤苦，故求死耳。”湛然慰留至家，易其衣服饮食而教诲之。往访其诸父，因失儿数日，正皇急，湛然告之，同往见少年，皆大悦，如获奇珍。湛然以劝儿改悔之言向述，皆感激涕零，再拜而去。少年归，果绝游戏之事，奋勉为学，次年入泮。

    Explanation

    391

    Another day, Zhanran came from outside and saw a man faintly visible in the cemetery, who had hanged himself on a tree. His body was still warm when touched, so Zhanran quickly revived him. The man was about twenty years old, his clothes though tattered, his appearance unusually handsome, not like a beggar. When asked why he had done this, he wept and said, "I am from a certain village in Ren County. My parents and uncles have only me. While studying in school, I was lured by thieves into gambling, and after losing heavily, I dared not return home. With no money to pay, the thieves took all my clothes, and it was still not enough to cover the debt, so they gave me some old, tattered clothes and made me push a cart to serve guests and earn money to repay the debt. Now, after being sent back to Tang County, thinking about it, I could not bear the bitterness and thus sought death." Zhanran comforted him and brought him home, changed his clothes and fed him, then taught him. He went to visit the man's uncles, who were very anxious because they had lost their son for several days. Zhanran told them, and they went together to see the young man, all of them very happy, as if they had found a rare treasure. Zhanran explained to them the words he had used to encourage the boy to repent, and they were all moved to tears and bowed twice before leaving. The young man returned home and indeed gave up all play, diligently studying. The following year, he entered the school.

    392

    自此湛然名益噪，不仅扶危济困而已。邑中修庙宇，建书院，劝赈济，立义冢诸大事，凡所首倡，无不立成。人信其诚故也。
    　　
    　　芗厈曰：湛然以大有为之才，尽力向善，光明磊落，可对天人，实今世所罕见。恒与余言，自恨未曾饱学。余笑慰之曰：“仲尼祖述尧舜，尧舜曾读何书，不过认真十六字耳。彼子衿中或迂腐固执，或诪张为幻。穷则为闾里之毒蛇，达则为朝廷之大蠹。夫如是，何必读书？”

    Explanation

    392

    From this point on, Zhan Ran's reputation grew increasingly famous, not just for helping those in danger and relieving their distress, but for much more. In the town, he initiated the construction of temples, the establishment of academies, encouraged relief efforts, and founded public graves for the poor—all major undertakings that he led and which were accomplished immediately. People trusted his sincerity, hence his success.

    Xiang Yan said: "Zhan Ran, with his great talent for action, strives to do good, is honest and upright, and can face heaven and humanity. He is truly a rare figure in modern times. He often speaks with me, regretting not having studied enough. I comforted him with a smile and said: 'Confucius followed the examples of Yao and Shun. What books did Yao and Shun read? It was just about being serious about the sixteen characters. Some scholars are rigid and stubborn, or deceptive and false. When poor, they become a scourge to their neighborhood; when successful, they become a great pest to the court. If that is the case, why bother reading?'"

    393

    王理堂
    　　
    　　理堂名敷正，河南光山县人，随其叔之任丹徒。值英夷犯顺，崇明危急，中丞檄属员之能解围者。其叔招募乡勇，将以应命，使理堂择得千余人，告之曰：“是行也，有死之心，无生之路，方能奋发有为。汝曹归与父兄谋，不愿者退，无相强也。”翌日，皆来覆命曰：“父兄有训，我等所以应募者，急父母之急，非仅崇明也。官能视我等如子，焉有子而敢背亲命者乎？”皆愿死斗，复查其孤子及兄弟同役者，退一人，得敢死之士八百。欢呼听令，遂给粮饷器械而操演之，与其叔航海而前。时军门所发之救兵千人亦到，见乡勇皆踊跃争先，兵气亦作，二十艘乘风破浪前进。

    Explanation

    393

    Wang Litang

    Wang Litang, whose name was Fu Zheng, was from Guangshan County in Henan Province. He followed his uncle to his post in Dantu. When the English barbarians violated the right order, Chongming was in dire peril, and the provincial governor issued an edict summoning officials capable of relieving the siege. His uncle raised a force of local volunteers to respond to the call, and sent Wang Litang to select over a thousand men. He addressed them, saying: "In this mission, one must have the resolve to die, not the path to survival, in order to be truly vigorous and effective. You all should go and consult your fathers and brothers; those who do not wish to go should withdraw, do not force them." The next day, they all returned to report: "Our fathers and brothers have given us instructions; the reason we have responded to the recruitment is to relieve the urgency of our parents, not merely for Chongming. If officials can regard us as their children, how could any child dare to disobey the command of their parents?" All were willing to fight to the death. After reviewing those who were orphans or whose brothers were also serving, one person was withdrawn, and eight hundred brave warriors ready to die were obtained. They cheered and listened to the orders, then were given provisions, weapons, and trained, and set sail with their uncle at the head. At that time, a thousand soldiers dispatched by the commander also arrived, seeing the local volunteers all rushing forward eagerly, and the soldiers' morale also rose. Twenty ships advanced, riding the wind and waves.

    394

    英夷望见，佥掉舟向来船发炮。夫夷舟高大，较我舟超越数倍，是炮皆上飞，烟焰迷空，不睹我舟，夷人意谓已击沈矣，殊不知理堂奋勇当先，炮子落身边，烧其衣襟不顾，冒烟直入，竟抵夷人之火轮船。其将白鬼子所主，理堂以大挠钩钩其船舷，持刀跃登，乡勇官兵相继，顷刻满船相杀。夷自犯顺以来，从未见勇敢如此者，皆战栗而伏。遂生擒夷将，戮尽丑虏。余舟皆遁，遂报崇明之捷。中丞受俘保奏，给理堂赏戴六品衔。中丞赴浙，援救台州之急，短给口粮，乡勇鼓噪。理堂蠲八百，以安士心，然知其必无功也，虑为所累。适奉母命归省，遂呈其家书，以母老告退，就例报捐九品，分发来燕。

    Explanation

    394

    The British spotted us and turned all their ships to fire at our approaching vessels. Their ships were much larger than ours, and their cannons soared into the air, creating a thick smoke and flame that obscured our ships. The British thought they had sunk us, but they did not know that Li Tang bravely led the way. Cannonballs landed near him, burning his clothes, but he paid no heed. He charged through the smoke and reached the enemy's steamship. The commander of the white devil was there, and Li Tang used a large grappling hook to hook the ship's side, leaping aboard with a knife. Local militia and soldiers followed, and soon the entire ship was engaged in battle. The British had never seen such bravery before, and all trembled and surrendered. Thus, Li Tang captured the enemy commander alive and killed all the enemy troops. The remaining ships fled, and the victory at Chongming was reported. The governor-general received the captured enemy and recommended a promotion for Li Tang, awarding him the rank of sixth grade official. The governor-general went to Zhejiang to rescue the urgent situation in Taizhou, but there was a shortage of food rations, causing the local militia to become restless and clamorous. Li Tang waived 800 taels to calm the soldiers' minds, but he knew that there would be no success and feared being implicated. Just then, he received an order from his mother to return home. Thus, he presented his family letter, retiring on the grounds of his mother's old age. He volunteered to donate to the ninth grade official position and was assigned to Yan (Beijing).

    395

    方伯知其能，癸卯冬，使往赵州缉捕，驻大桥之元帝庙。理堂不惜辛勤，每夜必出，逡巡各村庄，往往黎明而返，风雪寒冷皆不顾。于是数里无警，几至夜不闭户，民已深感之。时大桥之南村将起庙会，凡会，娼赌在所不免，且无此不足以聚众，会必消索，故主会之衿耆，虑委员执法，欲贿之，遂以制钱十余贯，因庙祝而通于理堂。僧为婉达其情，理堂即延衿耆入坐而问曰：“公等恐我执娼赌，此有地方官在，非吾分内事也，我何干预？但娼赌之场混留盗贼，而盗贼之迹难瞒土著，公等能为我寻之，胜于纳贿多多矣。”坚辞不受，衿耆曰：“官顾清廉，仆宜酬志。请以予从者。”理堂曰：“此纳贿方便法门也，我岂为之？公等既悯我仆劳，我宜酬之。”随以衣命僧立质钱三贯，呼仆而给之曰：“此诸公之赐，嗣后不许名一钱也。”衿耆皆感激称叹而退。于是理堂日夜在会，巡而不扰，衿民为获贼以献，是以会毕无失事。商民感戴，公悬额于庙门上。

    Explanation

    395

    The Regional Governor, recognizing his abilities, dispatched him in the winter of the year Gui Mao to Zhaozhou to apprehend criminals, where he stationed himself at the Yuan Emperor Temple on the Big Bridge. The commissioner spared no effort, venturing out every night to patrol the various villages, often returning at dawn, regardless of the harsh winds and cold. As a result, there were no disturbances within a few miles, and the people were nearly at the point of leaving their doors unlocked at night, deeply appreciating his work. At that time, a temple festival was about to be held in the southern village of the Big Bridge. Such festivals, where prostitution and gambling were inevitable, were necessary to attract crowds; otherwise, the festival would dwindle. The organizers, fearing that the commissioner would enforce the law, attempted to bribe him, offering more than ten strings of minted coins through the temple priest to the commissioner. The monk gently conveyed their intentions, and the commissioner immediately invited the elders to sit down and asked, "Are you concerned that I will crack down on prostitution and gambling? There are local officials in charge of such matters; it is not within my purview. Why should I interfere? However, such places often harbor thieves, and the traces of thieves are hard to hide from the locals. If you can help me find them, it would be far better than accepting a bribe." The commissioner firmly declined, and the elders said, "You are known for your integrity; I should reward your efforts. Please give me your followers." The commissioner replied, "This is a convenient method of accepting bribes; how could I do such a thing? You already pity my servant's hard work; I should reward him." He then ordered the monk to pawn his clothes for three strings of coins and called for his servant, giving him the money and saying, "This is a gift from all of you; from now on, I do not allow you to mention even a single coin." The elders, deeply grateful, praised him and left. The commissioner then patrolled the festival day and night, without causing any disturbance, and the respected citizens helped capture thieves and presented them. Thus, the festival ended without any mishaps. The merchants and civilians were grateful, and the official publicly hung a plaque on the temple door.

    396

    当是时，赵州营应给兵饷，例以文员领而偕武弁发。刺史已领得饷，因事阻，逾月犹未放，一军大哗，刻期欲围衙署而劫刺史。流言传布，刺史大惧，素稔理堂能，迎之署而谋焉。理堂曰：“光日之下，何敢叛逆？此虚声恐吓耳。然已违时，应速给之，军心即安矣。”刺史曰：“畏而给之，其如后患何？”理堂曰：“无难也，使其主将伏辜而后与之。”刺史曰：“能如是乎？何敢勒也？”理堂往拜主阃而说之曰：“公等知有灭门之祸乎？”主阃愕然曰：“何谓也？”曰：“我闻属下欲劫兵饷，有诸？”主阃曰：“此小人之言，不足道也。

    Explanation

    396

    At that time, the military camp in Zhaozhou was supposed to be supplied with soldiers' rations. By custom, a civil officer would lead and a military officer would distribute. The governor had already received the rations, but due to some obstruction, they were still not distributed after a month. The entire army became very agitated and set a deadline to surround the government office and seize the governor. Rumors spread, and the governor was greatly afraid. He was well acquainted with the ability of Li Tang and invited him to the office to discuss the matter. Li Tang said, "Under the bright sun, how dare they rebel? This is just empty threats. But since it has already been delayed, it should be given quickly. Then the soldiers' hearts will be at ease." The governor said, "If we give it out of fear, what about the future troubles?" Li Tang said, "It is not difficult, have the main general be punished and then give it." The governor said, "Can you do this? How dare I order you?" Li Tang went to greet the main general and said to him, "Do you know there is a disaster that could destroy your family?" The main general was startled and said, "What do you mean?" Li Tang said, "I heard that your subordinates want to seize the soldiers' rations, is this true?" The main general said, "This is just the talk of petty people, it's not worth mentioning."

    397

    然冬饷例应上季首给，今逾月不发，刺史无乃侵蚀乎？何独怪我兵之不戢乎？”理堂仲天大笑曰：“公等真不谙国事而血气自用者也。当今有扣一两平之说，始于今冬，宪司所发兵饷，扣与不扣尚无明文，是以刺史请示而后敢给，至今尚未见批回，因此迟迟。公以侵蚀诬之，能不激成大祸乎？”主阃曰：“是吾曹之过也，兵无御冬计，奈何？”理堂曰：“无难也，公即谕令兵目往刺史署长跪请罪，吾将劝刺史自垫，立时给散，何如？”主阃曰：“能如是，与君偕往。”随赴刺史署负荆，文武和好如初，顷刻发饷讫，一军皆悦曰：“我曹非委员公恩，父母妻子皆将冻馁，不仅我曹脱罪也。此恩此德可弗图报？”随公制匾额，主阃率队伍戎装，鼓乐卤簿，送至元帝庙，群拜而悬之。甲辰仲春，理堂事毕，奉调旋省。閤州之绅士兵民饯行路侧，以万民朱盖导进出境，至今两匾悬元帝庙门，人皆仰之。

    Explanation

    397

    In accordance with tradition, winter rations should be distributed at the beginning of the previous season. However, it has now been over a month since they were supposed to be given out. Could it be that the governor is embezzling the funds? Why is it only my soldiers who are not disciplined?" Governor Zhongtian of the prefecture laughed and said, "You all truly do not understand state affairs and act on impulse. Nowadays, there is a practice of deducting one liang per level, which began this winter. The military rations issued by the censorate, whether to deduct or not, still lack clear regulations. Therefore, the governor requests approval before daring to distribute them. Until now, no approval has been seen, which is why the distribution has been delayed. If you accuse the governor of embezzlement, how could this not provoke a great disaster?" The commander replied, "It is our fault. We have no plan for winter provisions. What should we do?" Governor Zhongtian said, "There is no difficulty. You should instruct the soldiers to go to the governor's office and kneel to ask for forgiveness. I will persuade the governor to advance the funds and distribute them immediately. How does that sound?" The commander said, "If that is possible, I will go with you." They then went to the governor's office with their heads bowed in apology, and the relationship between the civil and military officials returned to harmony. In an instant, the rations were distributed, and the entire army was pleased and said, "If not for the commissioner's grace, our parents, wives, and children would all freeze and starve. Not only have we been freed from blame, but we also owe this grace and virtue. How can we not repay it?" The commissioner then made a plaque, and the commander led the troops in full military attire, with music and a grand procession, to the Temple of Emperor Yuan. They all bowed and hung the plaque. In the second month of the year Jia Chen, after completing his duties, the governor was summoned back to the capital. The officials, soldiers, and civilians of the entire state saw him off on the side of the road, with red umbrellas held by the people guiding him in and out of the city. To this day, the two plaques hang at the entrance of the Temple of Emperor Yuan, and people all look up to them.

    398

    芗厈曰：古今来颂委员之德而争悬匾额，实罕闻见。盖微员末职，不过借上游之力，博数金而已。理堂展其才力，竟能如是，非知仁勇三者兼全不能。”

    Explanation

    398

    Xiang Yan said: "Throughout history, people have praised the virtues of commissioners and competed to hang plaques in their honor, but such behavior is rarely heard or seen. Minor officials, in their low-ranking positions, merely borrow the power of their superiors to gain a few coins. However, the commissioner demonstrates his talents and abilities, and achieves such feats only because he possesses all three virtues—wisdom, benevolence, and courage."

    399

    权阎罗王
    　　
    　　吾乡有延陵生者，甫入泮，夜卧书斋，梦役持红柬来请曰：“有公事，宜速行。”生意谓学师召之耳，未阅其柬，即易衣冠而出，见乘舆候之，生曰：“近在咫尺，何用舆为？”遂登舆急行，见黄沙茫茫，天色似明似暗，所由之路，向所未经。旋入一大城，三街六市往来者咸避道。至一大署，体统比督抚犹壮。舆入中门，鼓乐鸣炮。至甬道，有红袍纱帽者十六员迎舆而揖，生欲下，役已当舆唱免，生意学师演剧，使优迎之耳。舆至殿陛始停，出舆见大殿九楹，甚宏敞，殿中以白石筑台，台上金龙黄幕下垂。由左入内，两旁回廊甚深邃，直接后殿。入殿之左室，帘帏之内，陈设精雅，役引至此，请生入坐而退。有八童子年约十一二者进，或捧金幞头，或捧黄龙袍，或捧云履，或捧假面，或捧玉带，或镜或巾或炉，无赤手者，请生易服，生曰：“此何为也？”童曰：“此处规矩应如是耳。

    Explanation

    399

    The King of Hell

    　　In my hometown, there was a scholar named Yanling who had just entered school. One night, while sleeping in his study, he dreamed of a spirit holding a red summons coming to invite him, saying, "There is official business; you should act quickly." Yanling thought it was his teacher summoning him, so he did not read the summons and immediately changed into his clothes and went out. He saw a carriage waiting for him and said, "It is very close by; why use a carriage?" He then got into the carriage and hurried along. He saw a vast expanse of yellow sand, with the sky appearing both bright and dim, and the road was one he had never traveled before. Soon, he entered a large city where people in the bustling marketplaces all avoided the path. He arrived at a grand office building, which was even more impressive than those of high-ranking officials. The carriage entered the main gate, and there was music and the sound of cannons. In the hall, sixteen officials in red robes and black hats bowed to welcome the carriage. Yanling wanted to get out, but the spirit stopped him and said, "No need to get out; your teacher is performing a play and making actors welcome you." The carriage finally stopped at the steps of the hall. Yanling got out and saw a large hall with nine rooms, very spacious and grand. In the hall, a platform was built of white stone, with a golden dragon flag hanging down from a yellow curtain. He entered from the left, and the two sides of the corridor were very deep and long, leading directly to the back hall. In the left room of the hall, inside the curtains, the furnishings were elegant and refined. The spirit led him there and asked him to sit down, then left. Eight children, aged about eleven or twelve, came forward, carrying various items: a golden headdress, a yellow dragon robe, cloud shoes, a mask, a jade belt, a mirror, a handkerchief, and an incense burner. No one was empty-handed. They asked Yanling to change clothes, and he said, "What is this for?" The children replied, "This is the rule here; it should be like this."

    400

    ”生不得已，易服冠带履，一童请戴假面，生曰：“何必如是？”童亦以规矩对，生思既强作古装，恐为识者所笑，蒙假面亦得，遂戴之。着面如生成者，引镜自照，失其本来面目，方面长髯，俨然王者像矣。八童齐唱升殿，外似有接唱者，不觉随童行至前殿，自中间自石台之后户，历阶而升，内设公案，签筒印箱朱墨砚俱备。黄幕已启，两青衣更拥生升座，前十六官入殿恭揖，吏唱免参。阶下吏役不知其数，内有夜叉一部，牛头马面一部，佥跪谒，吏唱起，扬声齐诺，旁列甚肃。十六官退分两庑，各有公位。

    Explanation

    400

    Out of necessity, the man changed his clothes, hat, and shoes. A child suggested wearing a mask, and the man asked, "Why bother with that?" The child replied according to propriety. Reflecting on the situation, the man thought that since he was already trying to adopt ancient attire, he might be ridiculed by those who knew better, so wearing a mask would do. He decided to wear it. The mask fit him perfectly, and when he looked in the mirror, he saw that he had lost his original appearance, with a square face and a long beard, looking remarkably like a king. Eight children sang in unison to ascend the throne, and it seemed there were others singing in response. He followed the children to the front hall, ascending the steps from behind the stone platform, where inside was a public desk, and all the necessary items such as a seal case, inkstone, and red and black ink were ready. The yellow curtain had been opened, and two attendants helped him ascend to the throne. Sixteen officials entered the hall and bowed respectfully, and the official announced the exemption from participation. The number of officials and servants below was unknown, but there were groups of night demons and ox-headed horse-faced beings, all of whom knelt and paid homage. The official then announced to stand up, and they all responded loudly, with the sides being very solemn. The sixteen officials withdrew and divided into two wings, each taking their public positions.

    401

    吏役送卷，自外带囚就讯，刑罚甚重，有剥脸破腹抽肠拔舌者，哀嚎甚惨。生坐远，不知所审何词，第见一起毕，则卷案送呈，青衣吏接展案上，仅露年月，请生以朱笔某日书行，即持去，不使见狱词也。未几，外报节孝妇到，吏请生下阶迎，官役咸跪。见天际红云下垂，光华灿灿中，有金童玉女擎幡盖，侍一黄瘦中年妇人，将至地，忽一阵香风，吹往西方去矣。吏请生归座，以签票一卷送案上，仅露其姓名，请生过朱判行，皆男犯也。又呈女犯签票亦如前。生点至某氏，心忽动，疑似其姑母，时方有疾。欲再阅，吏即持去。

    Explanation

    401

    The clerks and officials brought scrolls into the courtroom, accompanied by prisoners for interrogation. The punishments were extremely severe, including facial mutilation, abdominal incision, evisceration, and tongue extraction, with cries of pain so pitiful. The magistrate sat far away, not knowing what cases were being reviewed, only seeing each case completed before the scroll was presented. A blue-robed clerk received and unfolded it on the desk, only showing the date, and asked the magistrate to write with red ink on a certain date, then took it away without allowing the magistrate to see the details of the cases. Shortly after, there was a report that a woman of filial piety and chastity had arrived. The clerk asked the magistrate to descend the steps to greet her, and all the officials knelt. They saw a red cloud hanging down from the sky, shining brilliantly, with golden boys and jade girls holding banners and umbrellas, serving a thin middle-aged woman. As she approached the ground, suddenly a fragrant wind blew her away to the west. The clerk then asked the magistrate to return to his seat and presented a scroll of tickets on the desk, only showing the names, asking the magistrate to sign and approve. All the offenders were male. Another scroll of tickets for female offenders was also presented as before. The magistrate pointed to a certain surname, and his heart suddenly moved, suspecting it might be his aunt, who was currently ill. He wanted to read again, but the clerk took it away.

    402

    唱公事毕，纷纷告退时，生亦下座，仍入内殿，去假面，易故衣，童送登舆，十六官仍在甬道揖送，惟舆前较来时多大灯一对，上首墨书第七殿，下首书阎罗王。直送生至家，释服仍卧，有送信者在床前唤觉，时甫黎明。生问来人曰：“自大门至此五六层，汝由何入？”来人曰：“并未闭门。”生曰：“异哉！此我夜间亲自关闭者，何人开放？”呼家人，皆未起，问来人，知姑母疾重，使速往。生即趋候，姑母已奄奄一息。张目执生手曰：“汝来甚好，床前鬼使皆趋避矣。汝坐此勿离。”生问疾，慰之曰：“偶患时症，即日就痊，勿戚戚也。”姑哂曰：“汝既不欲我死，何点我名？然天命也，我不能责汝忘亲情。”生悚然莫对。未几，报医来，家人请生出迎之，至再至三，姑始释手曰：“噫！止此一刻，竟不延矣。汝去。”生出而其姑遂逝。

    Explanation

    402

    When the official rites were concluded and the attendants began to disperse, the protagonist also rose from his seat and entered the inner hall. He removed his mask and changed back into his old clothes, and a child servant carried him onto the palanquin. The sixteen officials still stood in the corridor to bow and send him off, though this time, in front of the palanquin, there were two large lanterns—more than before. The top one was inscribed in black ink with "Seventh Hall," and the bottom one with "Yama King." They carried him directly home, where he removed his ceremonial robes and lay down again. A messenger then appeared at his bedside and called him awake. It was just at dawn. The protagonist asked the messenger, "From the main gate to here, there are five or six layers—how did you enter?" The messenger replied, "The gate was not closed." The protagonist said, "Strange! This was closed by me personally last night—so who opened it?" He called for the servants, but none had risen yet. He asked the messenger again and learned that his aunt was gravely ill and had sent for him urgently. The protagonist immediately went to her side, but his aunt was already on the verge of death. She opened her eyes, took his hand, and said, "You came just in time—the ghost attendants have all fled. Sit here and do not leave." The protagonist asked about her illness and comforted her, saying, "It is only a seasonal illness; you will recover today. Do not be worried." His aunt smiled faintly and said, "If you did not wish for my death, why did you call my name? But it is fate—I cannot blame you for forgetting kinship." The protagonist was startled and had no reply. Shortly afterward, word came that the physician had arrived. The family asked the protagonist to go out and meet him, and he did so repeatedly. At last, his aunt released his hand and said, "Ah! This is the final moment—it cannot be extended. You may go." As the protagonist stepped out, his aunt passed away.

    403

    芗厈曰：异哉！夫何以生人署阎王之职，而又不使之主政，则十六官皆可代也，何用生为？且聪明正直之为神，此生与余一人交，未能成其为正人君子，岂阴曹之鬼犹不如生耶？不可解矣。或曰：“阳间显官，岂尽正人君子为之，君何泥也？”余笑曰：“诺。”
    　　
    　　补讼师二则
    　　
    　　有楚生某，娶妻美而艳。邻有寡妇，与外村少年狎，少年过寡妇家，偶见生妇，极为垂涎，以重赀贻寡妇，谋通之。寡妇曰：“彼宴尔新婚，夫妻和睦，且良家妇不可以势逼，亦不能以利诱，虽其夫毕姻后出外教读，但有老媪作伴，亦难以游词进，实无法可施也。”少年情不自禁，以重价觅得积贼闷香来，曰：“我已得计，第不能穿穴逾墙。若诱至汝家，事必偕矣。”寡妇曰：“是不难。”使少年入市，市食物之精者，往候生妇，以甘言结好，后此屡往，密甚。生妇知寡妇家无男子，且近邻，以礼往答。寡妇留生妇入卧室，饭之，而遣媪妇守门户。

    Explanation

    403

    Xiang Yan said: How strange! Why would a human be appointed to the position of Yama, the king of hell, and yet not be allowed to rule? Then, any of the sixteen officials could replace him. What is the point of living? Moreover, being intelligent and upright is what makes one a god. This person and I have interacted, and he has not become a virtuous and honorable person. Are the ghosts in the underworld even less virtuous than him? This is incomprehensible. Someone said, "Prominent officials in the mortal world are not all virtuous and honorable. Why are you so rigid?" I laughed and said, "Yes."

    Additional Case of a Lawyer

    There was a man from Chu named [Name], who married a beautiful and charming wife. A neighboring widow was intimate with a young man from another village. The young man passed by the widow's house and accidentally saw [Name]'s wife, and he was extremely attracted to her. He gave the widow a large sum of money and plotted to seduce her. The widow said, "He is newly married, and his marriage is harmonious. Moreover, a virtuous woman cannot be coerced by force, nor can she be tempted by money. Even though her husband left to teach elsewhere after their wedding, and only an elderly woman accompanies her, it is still difficult to use flattery to win her over. There is truly no way to do it." The young man could not control his feelings and bought a large amount of a powerful sleeping drug from a notorious thief. He said, "I have a plan, but I cannot break through walls or dig tunnels. If I can lure her to your house, the deed will surely be done." The widow said, "That is not difficult." She sent the young man to the market to buy the finest food, and he went to visit [Name]'s wife, flattering her and making friends with her. After that, he visited her frequently, and their relationship became very close. [Name]'s wife knew that the widow's house had no men and was also a nearby neighbor, so she went to visit her in return. The widow invited [Name]'s wife into her bedroom, fed her, and sent the elderly women to guard the doors and windows.

    404

    时少年预伏室中，室焚闷香，妇觉香甚软，伏于座，少年突出，拥妇登床，寡妇避去。妇手足麻木，口不能言，而心甚了了，任其淫狎，饮泣而已。少年思得其欢心，许与衣饰，妇益忿怒，泪如泉涌。少年知难久留，草草讫事而逸。妇醒，索寡妇不见，破其室中什物而归，郁郁成疾。其父，名讼师也，往问之，女欲求计划，以实情告。父责其不应轻出，然彼以阴谋来，我当以诡计报之。随作一函，纳女枕畔曰：“能从之，则使媪来，我命两兄助之。”父去，妇阅其书，大喜，病寻愈，益自修饰，使媪强寡妇来谢过，与亲昵之，留寡妇宿，密告之曰：“前日之事，曷不先与我谋，是所恨也。

    Explanation

    404

    At that time, a young man had secretly hidden in the room. The room was filled with a smoky incense, and the woman felt the incense was very soft. She fell asleep on the seat. Suddenly, the young man appeared and took the woman to the bed. The widow fled. The woman's limbs were numb, and she could not speak, but her mind was very clear. She allowed him to act indecently and could only weep silently. The young man wanted to please her and promised to give her clothes and decorations. The woman became even more angry, and tears flowed like a spring. The young man knew he couldn't stay long and hurriedly finished and escaped. When the woman woke up, she couldn't find the widow and smashed the things in the room and returned home. She became ill with depression. Her father was a famous lawyer. He went to ask her, and the daughter wanted a plan and told him the truth. The father scolded her for acting recklessly, but since he came with deceit, he would repay him with cunning. He then wrote a letter and placed it beside her pillow, saying, "If you agree, I will send the old woman, and I will have my two brothers help her." After the father left, the woman read the letter, was very happy, and her illness soon improved. She began to dress herself more carefully and sent the old woman to force the widow to come and apologize. She became close to her and kept the widow overnight. She secretly told her, saying, "Why didn't you consult me first about what happened yesterday? That is what I regret."

    405

    第事巳如此，纵决西江之水，难洗清白。况良人久客不归，晨昏孤宿，乃子实获我心，但面许我衣饰，何食言也？”寡妇知其心动，曰：“是人富而多情，实慕娘子，无路可通，非负所许也，我为导引何如？”妇曰：“须防外人耳目，来必夜深人静，我逐媪候门，方无他虑。”因与订期而去。归告少年，自诩有功，少年大悦，急购鲜衣美珠，使寡妇先致之。如期留寡妇家，漏四下，潜以指叩门，与妇携手入室，即欲乱之，妇曰：“即来此，何急急？请同酌三杯以助兴。”案上酒食具奋，妇酌酒以进，少年目视而不饮。妇举杯坐怀中，口含以哺，少年迷乱，接而吞之。

    Explanation

    405

    But matters have already come to this — even if we were to release the waters of the Western River, it would be hard to wash away one's innocence. Moreover, my husband has been away for a long time and has not returned; I live alone in solitude, morning and evening. Indeed, you have truly captured my heart, yet you only promised me clothing and ornaments — why have you broken your word?" The widow sensed his heart was moved and said, "This man is rich and full of emotion; he truly admires you. He has no way to reach you, so it is not that he has broken his promise. What do you think of me acting as a guide?" The woman replied, "We must be careful of outsiders' eyes and ears. He must come when it is deep into the night and quiet. I will wait by the door for you, my lady, so there will be no other concerns." So they made an appointment and departed. When she returned, she told the young man, who boasted of his own merit. The youth was greatly pleased and hurriedly bought fine clothes and beautiful pearls, sending the widow to deliver them first. On the appointed day, he stayed at the widow's home. When the fourth watch struck, he secretly tapped on the door, took the woman's hand, and entered the room, immediately intending to take advantage of her. The woman said, "You've come here — why are you so hasty? Please drink three cups with me to raise our spirits." The table was set with food and drink. The woman poured the wine and offered it to him, but the young man only looked at it and did not drink. The woman raised the cup, sat in his lap, and fed him with her mouth. The young man was confused and accepted it, swallowing it.

    406

    徒见两壮士操刀直入，少年狂奔出户，遇绳绊足而跌，妇以烛来，壮士缚少年手足，割其辫发，妇释缚纵之逸。壮士携辫操刀，轻叩寡妇门，妇误谓少年归来，俏语曰：“夜已深，正好安睡，何又草草回？想必败阵而逃也。”门甫辟，壮士即刺其胸，饮刃而倒，以辫发缚妇右手数匝，归告生妇曰：“大事已毕，可以复父命矣。”此盖妇两兄预伏房外而安绊绳者，所哺少年之酒，哑药也。少年逃归，哑不能言。次日亭长见寡妇被杀于门，鸣官，验得凶刀插心，妇手握发辫，系强奸不从登时杀死者。密访失辫之人，获少年，无言置辩，遂按律斩之。至今楚俗，凡执奸者，必去其辫发云。

    Explanation

    406

    Only see two strong men enter directly with swords, the youth runs wildly out the door, trips over a rope and falls. The woman brings a candle, the strong men tie the youth's hands and feet, cut off his queue, and the woman releases the bindings and lets him escape. The strong men carry the queue and sword, lightly knock on the widow's door. The woman mistakenly thinks the youth has returned and says in a flirtatious manner, "It's already late at night, perfect for sleeping. Why did you return so hastily? You must have fled after losing the battle." The door just opens, the strong man immediately stabs her chest, and she falls dead. He ties her right hand with the queue several times, returns and tells the living woman, "The great matter is finished, and we can fulfill the father's command." It was because the woman's two brothers had secretly set up a rope outside the room. The wine they gave the youth was drugged to make him mute. The youth escaped back home, mute and unable to speak. The next day, the village headman saw the widow killed at the door, reported it to the authorities, who found the murder weapon stuck in her heart. The woman's hand was tied with the queue, indicating that she was killed immediately for refusing rape. They secretly searched for the person who lost the queue and caught the youth. He had no words to defend himself and was executed according to the law. To this day, in Chu customs, anyone who catches an adulterer must cut off their queue.

    407

    有潘生者，党讼师，与庵之少尼昵甚，有必正妙常之约。一日，潘生入庵，见州署官亲幕友开宴密室，少尼傅坐。潘生怒叱曰：“汝曹皆关防衙署中人，挟尼饮酒，知法犯法。何以佐尔主理民事乎？”众唯唯谢过而退。回署，传各役之首，谕令能获生奸者有赏。不能，必革役。皆领命，日使散役伏庵左右伺之。生恋尼，晨入庵，役拥而入，脱生与尼衣，合缚之，舁出。将鸣官，遇生党怒目拦阻，或许以重贿，纷纷嬲入班馆，不使速报。一生捷入潘生家，告其母妻，速邀亲戚入庵，嘱老尼用残香烛，在大殿开无量寿经，旁供潘生之父神主。安置毕，知会诸生，各易素衣冠，入州廨鸣冤。官升堂问故，诸生以潘生之父百年阴寿，同眷属戚友在尼庵礼忏，州役讹诈不遂，强以奸论，请官往验。役亦以获奸报。官难辨，同往验之，见有妇女及佛前香烛将尽，曰：“此非仓卒所能办者，其为蠢役无疑。”遂杖役而释生尼。

    Explanation

    407

    There was a man named Pan, who was a partisan of a lawyer. He had a very close relationship with a young nun in a temple, and they had a promise or agreement, possibly alluding to a specific historical or literary context. One day, Pan entered the temple and saw that officials from the state office and their close associates were holding a secret banquet in a private room, with the young nun serving them. Pan angrily shouted, "You all are officials in the government office, taking the nun to drink, knowing the law and breaking it. How can you assist your master in handling civil cases?" They all humbly apologized and left. Upon returning to the office, Pan summoned the leaders of the various officers and ordered that whoever could catch the adulterer would be rewarded. If they failed, they would be dismissed. They all accepted the order and sent scattered officers to hide around the temple to watch. Pan was infatuated with the nun, and one morning, he entered the temple. The officers rushed in and grabbed him, stripped him and the nun of their clothes, bound them together, and carried them out. They were about to report to the authorities, but they encountered Pan's party, who glared and blocked them, possibly offering a large bribe, and repeatedly forced them into a private room, preventing them from reporting quickly. One person quickly entered Pan's house, told his mother and wife, and they quickly invited relatives to the temple. They instructed the old nun to use leftover incense and candles, opened the Infinite Life Sutra in the main hall, and placed the spirit tablet of Pan's father beside it. After everything was set up, they informed the other students, who all changed into plain clothes and hats, and went to the state office to lodge a complaint. The official ascended the hall to inquire about the reason. The students said that Pan's father was celebrating his hundredth year of the afterlife, and along with family, relatives, and friends, they were at the temple to repent. The state officers tried to extort money but failed and forced the accusation of adultery, asking the official to come and verify. The officers also reported catching the adulterer. The official found it difficult to distinguish, so he went with them to verify. He saw women and the incense and candles before the Buddha were almost finished and said, "This could not have been prepared hastily; there is no doubt that they were foolish officers." He beat the officers and released the man and the nun.

    408

    粤东狱
    　　
    　　粤东某生，娶某氏女，国色也。偶出观剧，被为富不仁者所见，重贿女母，私之，往来甚密。恐旁人执奸，乃于女卧榻下，穿一地道，通后院密室中。倘有恶耗，为潜避计。未几，某生入泮，使媒来订婚期。富室与母女谋，使生入赘而毙之。母女皆诺，告媒曰：“婿家无父母，老妇亦无夫无子，两无依倚。如肯入赘，两得其便，否则姑缓，待我卒也而后于归。”媒覆之生，欣然愿赘，期于清和之吉完姻。时男女亲朋集贺者数十人，同观花烛，无不啧啧羡新妇美者。生喜甚，送客入席，即归新房与妇对酌。时无一女客，生得畅意为欢，新妇不作恒常羞涩，竟执爵相酬饮，生入醉乡。

    Explanation

    408

    Yue East Prison

    In the region of Guangdong, there was a scholar who married a woman of unparalleled beauty. One day, he went out to watch a play and was seen by a wealthy but unkind individual. This man bribed the woman's mother and began a secret affair with her, meeting frequently in secret. Fearing that others might discover their relationship, he dug a secret passage under the woman's bed, leading to a hidden room in the back courtyard. If any bad news came, they could use this passage to hide.

    Not long after, the scholar passed an examination and entered the ranks of the literati. A matchmaker was sent to set the wedding date. The wealthy man, along with the woman and her mother, plotted to have the scholar marry into their family and then eliminate him. The mother and daughter agreed, and the mother told the matchmaker, "The groom's family has no parents, and I, the old woman, have no husband or children. Both of us are without support. If he agrees to marry into our family, it will be convenient for both of us. Otherwise, we will delay it, and I will wait until I die before I marry off my daughter." The matchmaker relayed this to the scholar, who gladly agreed to marry into the family and planned the wedding for a favorable date in the Qinghe period. At the time, dozens of male and female relatives gathered to celebrate, and together they watched the ceremonial candles, marveling at the beauty of the new bride. The scholar was very happy, and after sending the guests into the banquet hall, he returned to the bridal chamber to drink with his wife. There were no female guests present, and the scholar enjoyed himself fully. The new bride did not act with her usual shyness and even took up the cup to toast him, and the scholar fell into a state of drunkenness.

    409

    对外客闻内宅惨呼一声，共骇愕间，见新郎衣履如故，散发覆面，狂跃而出，群欲询之，已疾奔出外，客皆追行。里许，遇大河，即跃入水而没。客呼渔舟捞救，经日夜不知尸所在，客叹息而返。新妇与母皆惶急，候于堂，见客来，即问新郎所在，客告之故，并叩其由。妇曰：“婿方在房中筵宴，忽发狂冲门出，我辈不知所以，谅出外亲友必阻之使归，何任其投河而没耶？是客杀我婿也。”遂鸣诸官，官讯客，皆曰：“我等猝不及防，追之无及，事出意外，岂有至亲好友见死不救哉？”讯诸新妇及母，则哀求还尸而已。官至河涘验勘，荡荡大河，流长源远，无从求尸，遂为疑狱。

    Explanation

    409

    A sudden cry of terror echoed from within the inner quarters, and all the guests were startled. They saw the groom, still dressed as he was before, with his hair loose and covering his face, leap out in a frenzied manner. The guests wanted to ask him what had happened, but by the time they reached the door, he had already run out quickly. The guests followed him, and after about a mile, they encountered a large river. Without hesitation, the groom jumped into the water and drowned. The guests called for fishing boats to rescue him, but after a day and night of searching, they could not find his body. The guests sighed and returned.

    The bride and her mother were in a state of panic, waiting in the hall. When they saw the guests return, they immediately asked where the groom was. The guests explained what had happened and asked why he had done such a thing. The bride said, "My husband was just in the room enjoying a feast when he suddenly went mad and rushed out of the door. We didn't know what had happened. Surely his close friends and relatives would have stopped him and brought him back, so why did they let him jump into the river and drown? It must be that the guests killed my husband!" She then reported the matter to the authorities. The officials questioned the guests, who all said, "We were caught off guard and couldn't catch up to him. It was an unexpected event. How could close relatives and friends not come to the rescue when someone is dying?" The officials then questioned the bride and her mother, who simply begged for the return of the body. The officials went to the riverbank to investigate, but the vast river, with its long and deep flow, made it impossible to find the body. The case remained unresolved.

    410

    未几，易一令，有明察声，见前官交有是案，反复推求，恍然曰：“婿投河而反诬客，非诬客也，欲客证新郎之死以实之也，是必有故。”变服为星卜流，访诸其邻，邻人曰：“有某富室，素与妇女无亲故，忽往来甚密，我侪亦疑有故。但是日新郎投河，众目共睹，岂非怪事？”令曰：“汝见之否？”对曰：“我亦在坐。”令曰：“汝视新郎貌作何色变？”对曰：“披发覆面不及见。”令曰：“道在是也。富室安在？”对曰：“今日犹见其入新妇家也。”令辞去。易服，率健役百余，突至妇家，围其前后户而搜之，仅有母女在，叩官欲何为？令无辞以对。

    Explanation

    410

    Not long after, a new magistrate was appointed, known for his clarity and discernment. Upon reviewing the case handled by his predecessor, he pondered deeply and suddenly realized, "The groom threw himself into the river but then accused the guest, not out of malice, but to have the guest confirm the death of the new husband to substantiate his claim. There must be a reason behind this." Disguising himself as a fortune-teller, he inquired among the neighbors, who said, "There is a certain wealthy man who has recently been visiting the woman closely, despite having no prior connection with her. We also suspect there is something amiss. However, on that day, the new husband was seen throwing himself into the river by everyone present. Isn't that strange?" The magistrate asked, "Did you see it?" The neighbor replied, "I was also there." The magistrate then asked, "What did the new husband's face look like?" The neighbor answered, "He was covering his face with his hair, and I couldn't see." The magistrate said, "The truth lies here. Where is the wealthy man?" The neighbor replied, "I saw him enter the new wife's house today." The magistrate left and changed into different clothes, leading more than a hundred strong men to suddenly arrive at the new wife's home, surrounding and searching the front and back of the house. Only the mother and daughter were found inside. They asked the magistrate, "What do you want from us?" The magistrate had no words to respond.

    411

    举步将入闺中，老妇横身阻曰：“此嫠女室，三尺童子不许入门，况为民之父母而不知礼乎？”令微哂曰：“欲为汝婿明冤耳。”老妇曰：“倘入室而无冤可明，将何如？”令曰：“我偿汝婿命。”乃呼役掖老妇出。令入房，见铺陈精洁，皆是常用什物，无可疑者。正踌躇间，俯视床下，见一男子履，回顾新妇，骇然失色。令呼众役人移床而观，则地板有新垫者，命役举之，地道见。令带役入，穿出，至一密室。室隅，一鲜衣少年伏焉。执之。推门至院落，见地有新挖状，命役启之，生尸在，经年不变，喉间扼痕显然。遂出，聚案内人证，一讯服辜，论如律。乃知生醉后，妇女与富室共扼其喉而毙，从地道舁入后院埋之。投水之人，系富室以重价觅善泅者为之也。

    Explanation

    411

    The magistrate, about to step into the room, was blocked by an old woman who said, "This is the room of a widow; not even a three-foot-tall child is allowed to enter, let alone a person in a position of authority who does not know propriety?" The magistrate smiled faintly and replied, "I wish to clear your son-in-law's name." The old woman said, "If I enter the room and there is no wrong to clear, what then?" The magistrate said, "I will compensate for your son-in-law's life." He then called the officials to escort the old woman out. The magistrate entered the room and saw that it was meticulously arranged, with only common household items, nothing suspicious. As he hesitated, he looked down under the bed and saw a man's shoe. Turning to the widow, he was startled and pale. The magistrate called the officials to move the bed and examine, and they discovered that the floor had been newly padded. He ordered the officials to lift it, revealing a secret passage. The magistrate led the officials through the passage and into a secret room. In the corner of the room, a young man in fine clothes was crouching. They arrested him. Pushing open the door to the courtyard, they saw that the ground had been newly dug up. The magistrate ordered the officials to dig it up, and there was a body, unchanged for years, with clear marks of strangulation around the neck. He then stepped out and gathered the evidence and witnesses, and after one interrogation, the young man confessed. The verdict was in accordance with the law. It was then known that the man was killed by strangulation after he was drunk, by a woman and a wealthy man, who then threw his body into the water via the secret passage.
