Only his great-grandchildren and great-great-grandchildren, numbering over ten, were left. Two students were chosen to support him, and he was summoned to the capital for an audience. Qi Shinan responded in accordance with the emperor's wishes and was granted the title of Vice President of the Imperial Academy. He was instructed to take office for three days and serve as a permanent appointment. On the day of the banquet, he wore official robes and a cap, was given a seat in the imperial court, and led the group of thousand elders. With long, white eyebrows and a white head, he danced in the red hall, raised the cup to toast the emperor, and the emperor was greatly pleased. After the ceremony concluded, he respectfully declined further honors and was given imperial poems, articles, a cane, gold, and silk, with varying degrees of generosity. He was also instructed to return to his hometown slowly by official carriage. Truly, he was a rare treasure of the prosperous era.

    656

    芗厈曰：《礼?外传》称寿者百二十，过此为祅怪，不及为短折。然稽之史册，盘古及天皇兄弟各一万八千岁。此或上古年月未定，以月为岁，亦未可知。至女娲氏百三十岁，苍颉氏二百一十五岁，伊耆氏百七十二岁，伏羲百九十四岁，炎帝百五十五岁，神农百六十八岁，黄帝二百四十岁，皆古圣人也，岂得谓袄怪耶？以是而论，唐尧百一十八岁，虞舜百有十岁，周文王九十七，武王九十三，穆王百有五岁，尚可称短折耶？要之，古人守道，冲和恬淡，是以多寿。今人为物欲所蔽，以致夭亡。试观《路史》，称伊尹百三十岁，召公百八十岁。三代以下，秦穆公时王安百八十岁，魏文侯乐人窦公，亦百八十岁。汉霍光典衣奴还东，六百岁，范明友鲜卑奴，二百四十岁，东方朔父张夷，二百岁，魏道人蒯京，百七十八岁。罗吉一百七岁时为都官，精爽不倦，百十岁归老，百二十九岁卒。

    Explanation

    656

    Xiang Yan said: The Li Ji (Book of Rites) and its Wai Chuan (External Transmission) state that a lifespan of one hundred and twenty years is the norm, beyond which it is considered an omen or supernatural, and below which it is considered a short life or premature death. However, when examining historical records, it is recorded that Pangu and the Heavenly Emperors each lived for eighteen thousand years. This may be because the ancient years and months were not fixed, and a month was counted as a year, so it is not entirely clear. As for Nüwa, she lived for one hundred and thirty years; Cangjie, two hundred and fifteen years; Yiqi, one hundred and seventy-two years; Fuxi, one hundred and ninety-four years; Yan Emperor, one hundred and fifty-five years; Shennong, one hundred and sixty-eight years; and Huangdi, two hundred and forty years. All of these were ancient sages. How can their long lives be considered supernatural? By this standard, Yao lived for one hundred and eighteen years, Shun for one hundred and ten years, King Wen of Zhou for ninety-seven years, King Wu of Zhou for ninety-three years, and King Mu of Zhou for one hundred and five years. Can these lifespans still be considered short? In essence, ancient people followed the Dao, maintaining a calm and simple lifestyle, which is why they lived long. Modern people, however, are blinded by material desires, leading to early death. If we look at Lu Shi (History of Lu), it records that Yi Yin lived for one hundred and thirty years and Shao Gong for one hundred and eighty years. After the Three Dynasties, during the reign of Duke Mu of Qin, Wang An lived for one hundred and eighty years, and during the reign of Duke Wen of Wei, the musician Dou Gong also lived for one hundred and eighty years. During the Han Dynasty, the servant of Huo Guang named Huadong lived for six hundred years; Fan Mingyou's Xianbei servant lived for two hundred and forty years; Zhang Yi, the father of Dongfang Shuo, lived for two hundred years; and during the Wei Dynasty, the Daoist Priest Kuai Jing lived for one hundred and seventy-eight years. Lu Ji, at one hundred and seven years old, served as a county official, remaining sharp and energetic; at one hundred and ten years old, he retired, and at one hundred and twenty-nine years old, he died.

    657

    梁普通中穰成人，二百四十岁，钟离人顾思远百十二岁，为散骑侍郎，百二十岁而卒，见《南史》。张元始九十六岁，生子无影，百六岁始卒，见《粱书》。唐洛阳九老中，李元爽百三十六岁，太原于百龙百二十八岁，隋仁寿年生，开元东封诣阙，赐以紫袍牙笏，孙思邈百余岁应诏，俱见《唐史》。琼州杨遐举父叔连，百二十岁，祖宗卿，百九十五岁，九世祖居鸡窠中，形如小儿，不知其年，见《洞微志》。

    Explanation

    657

    During the year 520 of the Liang dynasty, a person named Rangcheng lived to be 240 years old. In Zhongli, there was a man named Gu Siyuan who lived to be 112 years old and served as a Scattered Rider Attendant, dying at the age of 120, as recorded in the Southern History. Zhang Yuan started at the age of 96, had a son named Wuying, and died at the age of 160, as recorded in the Liang History. Among the Nine Elders of Luoyang during the Tang dynasty, Li Yuanshuang lived to be 136 years old, Yu Bailong from Taiyuan lived to be 128 years old, was born during the Ren Shou year of the Sui dynasty, and visited the imperial palace during the Eastern Expedition of Emperor Xuanzong, receiving a purple robe and a jade tablet, while Sun Simiao responded to the imperial summons in his hundreds of years, all as recorded in the Tang History. In Qiongzhou, Yang Xiaju's father, Shu Lian, lived to be 120 years old, his grandfather, Zongqing, lived to be 195 years old, and his ninth-generation ancestor lived in a chicken coop, resembling a child and whose age is unknown, as recorded in the Dongwei Records.

    658

    宋政和中，福清林雄百有七岁，绍兴中林洞百十七岁，尤时泰举博学宏诃科，年百二十岁，见《宋书》。逮前明昆山周寿谊，百六十岁，太祖召见，赐酒馔。茹文忠百有十岁，英皇辟之，予冠服。成化间，济宁民王士能百二十三岁，顺天孔无似四百岁，福州致仕知府林志泽，百有四岁，苏州毛弼百岁时，其孙澄状元及第，朝廷为建人瑞状元坊，千户朱政曾祖信，百有六岁，祖全，百有二岁。洛阳刘太师健，百有七岁，义乌陈世恭，万历戊寅百有六岁，俱见《明史》。此皆历历可据，代不乏人，予学殖荒落，多所挂旃耳。

    Explanation

    658

    During the Zhenghe period of the Song Dynasty (1111), Fuqing's Lin Xiong was 107 years old. During the Shaoxing period of the Song Dynasty (1131), Lin Dong was 117 years old. You Shitai was selected for the "博学宏词科" (Boxue Hongci Ke) and was 120 years old; all of these are recorded in the "History of Song." Up to the Ming Dynasty, there were also many such cases. In the Ming Dynasty, Zhou Shouyi from Kunshan was 160 years old, and the Taizu (Hongwu Emperor) summoned him for an audience and bestowed wine and food upon him. Ru Wenzhong, who was 110 years old, was summoned by the Yingzong Emperor and bestowed official robes. During the Chenghua period (1465–1487), Wang Shinen from Jining was 123 years old, Kong Wusi from Shuntian was 400 years old, Lin Zhize, a retired prefect from Fuzhou, was 104 years old, and when Mao Bi from Suzhou was 100 years old, his grandson Mao Cheng became the top scholar in the imperial examination, and the court built a "Human Auspice" Zhuangyuan Archway in his honor. Zhu Zheng, a thousand-household officer, had an ancestor named Zhu Xin who was 106 years old, and his grandfather Zhu Quan was 102 years old. Liu Taishi Jian from Luoyang was 107 years old, and Chen Shigong from Yiwu was 106 years old during the Wuyin year of the Wanli period (1608); all of these are recorded in the "History of Ming." These are all well-documented cases, and throughout the ages, there have been no shortage of such people. My own learning is barren and neglected, and there are many uncertainties.

    659

    彭祖云，人之受气，养之得宜，常至百二十岁。小小晓道，可得二百四十岁。加之，可得四百八十岁。此言修养者然也。稽诸《列仙传》，东海黄翁三千年一反骨洗髓，二千年一刻骨伐毛，已三洗髓，五伐毛。黄安坐神龟二千年一出头，已五见其出，是皆万余岁矣。则洪崖三千岁，白石先生二千岁，河上公千七百岁。广成子千二百岁，安期生一千岁，尚不得与二黄公比寿。至李阿、彭祖俱八百岁，黄初平兄初起，五百岁，老子西入流沙，化胡成佛，寿四百四十岁，更不足言矣。释家慧可百有七岁，达摩百五十岁，佛图澄百十七，赵州从谂百二十岁，此信而有征者。至西竺长耳一千岁，西天宝掌二千七十二岁，虽见诸《大藏》，恐为僧人夸诞之词，抑异气所钟，亦不诬耶？以平常论，人寿至八十以外，藉人扶持，凡百不能自主，纵不死亦味同嚼蜡。或曰，修道则强，千百岁尚如童子，其信然耶？其传之非其真耶？

    Explanation

    659

    Peng Zu said that if a person is nurtured properly, they can live to be one hundred and twenty years old. With a basic understanding of the Dao, one can live to be two hundred and forty years old. With additional effort or knowledge, one can live to be four hundred and eighty years old. This is what is meant by the practice of cultivation. Consulting the "Biographies of Immortals," we find that Huang Wang from the East Sea rejuvenates his bones and muscles every three thousand years and regenerates his flesh every two thousand years, having done so three times for bone regeneration and five times for flesh regeneration. Huang Wang sits on a divine turtle, which emerges every two thousand years, and he has seen this happen five times, indicating that he is over ten thousand years old. Therefore, Hong Yai is three thousand years old, Bai Shi Master is two thousand years old, and He Shang Gong is one thousand seven hundred years old. Guang Chengzi is one thousand two hundred years old, and Anqi Sheng is one thousand years old, yet they still cannot compare in age to the two Huang Wangs. As for Li A and Peng Zu, both are eight hundred years old. Huang Chuping and his brother, Huang Chuchi, are five hundred years old. Laozi traveled west into the desert, transformed into a Buddha, and lived to be four hundred and forty years old, which is even less noteworthy. In Buddhism, Huike is one hundred and seven years old, Bodhidharma is one hundred and fifty years old, Fotucheng is one hundred and seventeen years old, and Zhaozhou Congshen is one hundred and twenty years old—these are all figures whose ages are well-documented. As for Chang'er from the West and Baopan from the Western Heaven, who are one thousand and two thousand seven hundred and twenty years old respectively, although they are mentioned in the Buddhist canon, it is likely that these are exaggerated claims by monks. Alternatively, perhaps they were blessed with special qi, and this is not entirely false. In ordinary terms, when a person reaches eighty years of age, they need assistance from others and cannot manage their affairs independently. Even if they do not die, life becomes as tasteless as chewing wax. Some say that practicing Daoism can maintain strength, and one can remain as a child even at a thousand years old. Is this true? Or are these claims not genuine?

    660

    陈君
    　　
    　　浙有陈君，言者忘其名字。世业骨董，至君家小康中止。祖传有大鼎如瓮，三足两耳，周身古篆，斑驳奇丽，无能辨者。饰以紫檀盖垫，上植玉顶，宝藏有年。是乡崇奉三官神，春秋设坛，诵经惟虔，陈设玩器，彼此争胜。陈君之鼎，戚友恒假之。是年事毕，友以供神佛手二枚置鼎内，持还之人，忘为转述。次年借鼎者又来，陈妇以奉神故，必须浩涤，启盖，则佛手根盘满鼎，绿叶扶疏，异香扑鼻。陈君异之，诡谓鼎为鼠尿所污，不可供神，给以他物。因思枯者尚可得生，夏月藏肉食必不败。试之，经旬如初烹者。意必至宝也，惜数十年未逢识者。挟以游通都大邑，博求知遇。历教省，虽不乏博古人，均未能辨其款识。

    Explanation

    660

    Mr. Chen

    　　In the region of Zhejiang, there was a Mr. Chen, whose name is forgotten by those who speak of him. His family had long been engaged in the trade of antiques, and their wealth had reached a moderate level. His ancestor had passed down a large tripod, shaped like a jar, with three legs and two ears. The entire body of the tripod was inscribed with ancient characters, which were mottled yet strangely beautiful, and no one could decipher them. It was adorned with a purple sandalwood lid and padded with jade at the top, and it had been treasured for many years. In this village, the Three Officials were greatly revered, and altars were set up in the spring and autumn seasons for the recitation of scriptures with great reverence. Decorations and playthings were displayed, and people competed to outdo each other. Mr. Chen's tripod was often borrowed by relatives and friends. At the end of that year, a friend placed two Buddha's hand fruits inside the tripod for the worship of the gods, and the person who returned it forgot to mention it. The next year, another person who borrowed the tripod came, and Mr. Chen's wife, because it was used for worshipping the gods, insisted on thoroughly cleaning it. When the lid was opened, the roots of the Buddha's hand fruits filled the tripod, with green leaves spreading out and a strange fragrance filling the air. Mr. Chen was astonished and falsely claimed that the tripod had been soiled by mouse urine and could not be used for worship, giving it to someone else instead. He then thought that even something that had withered could still be revived, and if meat were stored in it during the summer, it would not spoil. He tested this, and after ten days, the meat was as fresh as if it had just been cooked. He concluded that it must be a treasure, but it was a pity that he had not met someone knowledgeable about it in the past few decades. He took it with him to travel through the major cities and towns, seeking out knowledgeable people for recognition. He traveled through various provinces, though there were no shortage of learned people, none could identify its inscription or style.

    661

    乃入燕京，有某亲王出游，见而重之，召物主询价，陈曰：“小人有鼎，未知为何代所造。若能识其文，知其异者，方愿售之。为斯鼎得主幸，价所勿计。如识之不真，虽万金不易也。”王曰：“汝可谓爱古之士。”乃出册藉，宋刻锦装，上绘古器无数，王指一页，命陈自对，图象宛然，款识皆以楷书旁注，始知为周初虢叔鼎也。王曰：“鼎历三千余年。铜性灵活，能生一切物。”陈曰：“然。既遇恩王，鼎得其所。小人愿献殿下，赐以资斧回乡足矣。”王曰：“鼎果无价，然汝亦雅士，白金五百，姑偿来京之费。惜汝老矣，有子能读书乎？可命之来，我将拔之，以报鼎值。”陈九顿首称谢而还，使其子诸生某入都就王，出入府第，俾游国学，因得科举，为一时名孝廉。皆鼎力也。

    Explanation

    661

    He then entered Yanjing, where a certain prince was on an outing. Upon seeing the tripod, he greatly valued it and summoned its owner to inquire about the price. Chen said, "I have a tripod, but I do not know of which dynasty it was made. If one can recognize its inscriptions and understand its uniqueness, only then would I be willing to sell it. For the sake of finding a worthy owner for this tripod, the price is not worth considering. If it is not recognized correctly, even if it were worth ten thousand gold pieces, it would not be exchanged." The prince said, "You can be called a lover of antiquities." He then took out a catalog, beautifully bound and printed in Song dynasty style, which depicted countless ancient artifacts. The prince pointed to a page and ordered Chen to compare it himself. The image was vividly similar, and the inscriptions were all in regular script with annotations. Only then did he realize that it was the Zhou Dynasty early period Guo Shu tripod. The prince said, "The tripod has endured for over three thousand years. The copper has a flexible nature and can produce everything." Chen said, "Indeed. Having met the benevolent prince, the tripod has found its rightful place. I am willing to offer it to your highness, and if you grant me provisions, returning home will be sufficient." The prince said, "The tripod is indeed priceless, but you are also a cultured person. I will give you five hundred taels of silver to compensate for your expenses of coming to the capital. Alas, you are old. Do you have a son who can read and study? You can summon him, and I will promote him to repay the value of the tripod." Chen Kui prostrated himself, expressed thanks, and returned. He sent his son, a scholar named Chen, to the capital to serve the prince, entering and exiting the palace, studying at the national academy, and obtaining success in the imperial examinations, becoming a renowned filial and upright official for a time. All of this was due to the power of the tripod.

    662

    芗厈曰：藉一物起家，世恒有之。即如周姓以钱四百，得颜鲁公初拓多宝塔碑，完善无缺。识者曰：“此碑在陕西，碑洞适当门镮，仅拓八纸，而文中‘凿井见泥’，‘凿’宇已伤，故谚有之曰，凿字全，值一千。今此拓在八纸之列。”以四百金售去，周得资作米业而富。有张姓者，以百钱得紫石如碗大。识者曰，此中蕴红宝石，以万金售去。张纳贷为郎。市有陈铁仙鹤大立台二，遇识者至鹤所，察其四日，旋以数千钱抉目去。有黄姓者访之，曰：“此目系黄宝石所为，名猫儿眼。予日观其瞳，应时而转，价值数万。”黄闻之，即以贱值买其铁鹤。镕之，赤金也。此大吏之侈者，以金铸鹤而漆之，防籍没也。黄意以至宝饰目，岂有铁胎者？果获黄金百六之数。余不胜枚举，可见藉物获资，不足为异。而陈君所遇，贤王留心古学，且能好士，惜陈子傲慢不恭，仅以孝廉终，亦不寿。不然，既经推挽，岂功名仅止于是哉？

    Explanation

    662

    Xiang Yan said: It is always the case in the world that one can become wealthy through a single item. For example, a Zhou family member bought an early rubbing of Yan Zhenqing's "Multitower Pagoda" stele for 400 coins, and it was complete and flawless. An expert said, "This stele is located in Shaanxi, and the stele hole is appropriately positioned for the door ring. Only eight rubbings were made, but the character '凿井见泥' ('drilling a well to see mud') in the text already shows damage to the character '凿.' Therefore, there is a saying that if the '凿' character is intact, its value is a thousand coins. Now, this rubbing is among the eight." The Zhou family member sold it for 400 coins and used the money to start a rice business and became wealthy. There was also a Zhang family member who bought a purple stone as large as a bowl for 100 coins. An expert said that it contained a red gemstone, and he sold it for ten thousand coins. Zhang borrowed money and became an official. In the market, there were two large standing platforms shaped like iron cranes. When an expert visited the cranes, he examined them for four days and then removed the eyes with several thousand coins. A Huang family member visited and said, "These eyes are made of yellow gemstones, known as 'cat's eye.' I observe its pupils daily, and they turn with the time, valued at tens of thousands." Huang heard this and bought the iron cranes at a low price. When melted, they turned out to be pure gold. This was done by a wealthy official who cast golden cranes and painted them to avoid confiscation. Huang thought that the eyes were decorated with precious stones, but could there be iron inside? Indeed, he obtained a hundred and six pieces of gold. There are many more examples like this, showing that gaining wealth through objects is not unusual. As for Mr. Chen's encounter, the noble prince was attentive to ancient learning and appreciated scholars. Unfortunately, Mr. Chen was arrogant and disrespectful, and he ended his career as a孝廉, also dying young. Otherwise, having been recommended, would his achievements have been limited to this?

    663

    耳神
    　　
    　　幽闲空洞之日，无可奈何之天，予闷昏而卧，见锦衣使者执简耜召。予随至一宫，重堂丹赤，玉籥金铺，两扉将閟。内坐紫衣罗裳王者，侍卫森严。予顿首请命，王者曰：“吾乃耳神，名空闲，字幽田。职守泥丸宫之左右，闻子执业大费心神，以致聩败。子不安义命而生怨尤，故召予劝戒，子呕心挖肾，徒自伤耳。”予曰：“否，否，一事不知，儒者之耻，不能奋兴，命也。而可自堕其学乎？”王者曰：“子既为学，能赋聋乎？”对曰：“某虽不敏，亦常从事于斯，请限官韵，知非宿构。”王者曰：“以‘听而不闻，无耳者也’为韵。”左右进文房四宝，予一挥而就。

    Explanation

    663

    A Spirit of the Ears

    On a day of quiet and emptiness, a day of helplessness, I lay down in a drowsy, confused state and saw a messenger dressed in fine robes holding a simple farming tool. I followed him to a grand hall with red walls, adorned with jade locks and golden door fittings, where the two doors were about to close. Inside, a king or ruler dressed in purple robes and silk garments sat, surrounded by strictly guarded attendants. I bowed and requested an audience, and the king said, "I am the Spirit of the Ears, named Kōngxīan, with the courtesy name Yōutian. I am responsible for the left and right sides of the Níwán Palace. I heard that you are greatly expending your spirit and energy in your duties, leading to deafness and failure. You are unhappy with your fate and harbor resentment, so I have summoned you to advise and warn. You are exhausting yourself mentally and physically, only harming yourself." I replied, "No, no, not knowing one thing is a shame for a scholar; it is not possible to rise up and strive, it is fate. How can I fall into my own learning?" The king said, "Since you are a scholar, can you write a poem on deafness?" I answered, "Although I am not very intelligent, I often engage in such matters. Please limit it to official rhymes, knowing it is not premeditated." The king said, "Use 'to hear but not listen, that is the deaf' as the rhyme." The attendants brought the four treasures of the study: brush, ink, paper, and inkstone, and I finished it in one stroke.

    664

    其词曰：“有聩聩翁者，年届七旬，未归三径。因兹肾脏水亏，耳官火胜，不闻车毂飞驰，有似瞿昙入定。第乏骅骝之德，愧号聋虫；若运黄老之功，击须仙磬。一唱三叹，但见口之翕张；六律五音，惟有心为响应。纵八面争传号令，尚觉无言；虽九霄迅走雷霆，未惊清听。爰有性成浮躁，情益离奇。乍逢喜笑颜开，误猜嫌其鹘突。偶遇正言厉色，以怒骂为狐疑。甚至妄肆咆哮，见绝于良朋执友；莫从讲解，术穷于顺子贤姬。

    Explanation

    664

    There was an old man named Kui, in his seventies, who had not yet retired to his secluded garden. Due to his kidney deficiency and the imbalance of fire in his ears, he could not hear the wheels of a carriage flying by, much like a meditating monk (Qutan). However, he lacked the virtue of a fine horse (Huailiu) and was ashamed to be called a deaf insect. If he practiced Daoist methods (Huanglao) and struck the divine bell (Xian Qing), his heart would still respond, even though his body was deaf. Even if commands were shouted from all directions, he still felt silent; even if thunder roared swiftly in the heavens, it did not startle his clear hearing. By nature, he was restless and impulsive, and his feelings became increasingly strange and unpredictable. When encountering joy, his face would light up, but he would mistakenly suspect that it was confusing or unclear. When encountering stern words and a serious expression, he would take anger and insults as suspicion. Even going so far as to wildly roar, he would be rejected by good friends and companions; no longer able to follow explanations, the methods of the wise and virtuous wife were exhausted.

    665

    我状其形，如是如是；谁与为伴，已而已而。亦有气本和平，心无抑郁。雅正宜人，喜同古佛。时或遭夫狺詈，仅觉耾耾；时或追乎欢欣，只摹仿佛。彼笑则笑，何如乐与参同；人云亦云，奚必知其委屈。纵使辟开琴腹，蛀取鞠通；无从觅到仙乡，丹尝押不。更有多忧多惧，难舍难分。怵胆披肝，惟恐失仪于上座；镂心刻骨，时虞责抚乎前军。瞷称秦晋之间，堪与瘖痖为侣；耸续江淮之谓，耻偕孩稚同群。惟耵聍实杜其孔窍，岂辟咡能启其声闻。乃竟有不然者。或志癖烟霞，佯以拒聘；或畏登台阁，托以全躯。

    Explanation

    665

    I describe its form, just as it is; who can be its companion, it is already enough. There are also those whose nature is inherently peaceful and whose hearts are free from depression. Elegant and proper, they are pleasing to others, happy to be like an ancient Buddha. Sometimes they encounter harsh and abusive language, feeling only a deep rumbling; sometimes they pursue joy, only vaguely approximating it. When others laugh, they laugh too, how much better to be in harmony; when others speak, why must one know the grievances? Even if one opens the belly of a lute and passes through a ball, there is no way to find a heavenly abode, and the elixir cannot be sealed. There are also many fears and worries, difficult to part with. Terrified and exposed, they fear to lose decorum in a high position; deeply engraved in heart and bones, they often worry about being criticized by the leading troops. They are situated in the border region between Qin and Jin, able to be companions with the mute; they stand in the region of the Yangtze and Huai rivers, ashamed to be in the same group as children. Only earwax blocks their earholes, how can opening the mouth reveal their voice? Yet there are those who are not like this. Some are obsessed with the mist and clouds, feigning refusal of appointments; others fear to ascend the platform, relying on it to preserve their bodies.

    666

    或作家翁，故昧问闻于绣阘；或垂礼范，籍需充塞以绵繻。是皆超群之贤俊，讵同混世于庸奴。我知其外占坎窞，内协巽孚。故蜀汉元勋，进杜微以大夫之秩；颖川太守，留许丞为廉吏之模。不加退黜，藉使匡扶。此古时所有，而近日渐无。于是访和缓之遗书，求轩歧之医理。角因能听，借灵角于斑龙；脑可益聪，剖脂脑于赤鲤。茫茫苍海，畴登符隅而采芝；霭霭云坛，仅就春社以饮酏。是虽欲集夫众长，恐难图痊于薄技。惟能力判万言，庶冀神酬三耳。嗟乎！方木鲜征，名医才寡。天降之灾，人何能舍。

    Explanation

    666

    Some become old men, and thus lack knowledge in a refined setting; some establish models of propriety, relying on filling in gaps with fine silk. These are all outstanding and talented individuals, how can they be compared to ordinary slaves in the world? I know that outwardly they occupy the position of the Hexagram of Water (Kǎn), while inwardly they harmonize with the Hexagram of Wind (Xùn). Therefore, the founding heroes of the Shu Han state promoted Du Wei to the rank of high official; the governor of Yingchuan, Xu Cheng, was left as a model of an upright official. Without being demoted or dismissed, they were relied upon to support and stabilize. This was common in ancient times but is gradually disappearing in recent times. Thus, we seek out the lost writings of He Huan and study the medical theories of Xuan and Qi. The horn can be used to listen, borrowed from the mythical dragon; the brain can enhance intelligence, extracted from the red carp. The vast ocean, who can reach its edges to gather herbs? The misty cloud altar, only at the spring sacrifice to drink the offering. Even if one wishes to gather the strengths of many, it is difficult to cure with such limited skills. Only by exerting great effort can one hope for divine recompense for the three ears. Alas! Square wood is rare, and skilled doctors are few. When disaster falls from heaven, how can humans avoid it?

    667

    呜呼！绮筵开处，坐默默兮无情；嘉客来时，词谆谆兮欠雅。虽非损五官而四存，然已数六根而一假。惜哉清士，难与击筑弹琴；髦矣老夫，只好悬车束马。果能绝利乎，愿学忘机者。巳矣乎，幽田去兮莫强把，歌楚些兮招不下。其鸣也，瓮中声兮起叿隆，海上涛兮急湍泻。大块怒兮鼓狂飙，凯军旋兮奏肆夏。万马归兮齐奔槽，千炉铸兮忽跃冶。仪秦之舌不能形，班马之笔不能写。从此一年甚一年，依我终身如是也。”赋毕。左右取呈，王者披览，首肯曰：“吾为转达天庭，虽不能复聪，俾子不加重可也。”予再拜谢而觉。

    Explanation

    667

    Alas! At the splendid banquet, the guests sit silently, indifferent; when distinguished guests arrive, they speak earnestly but lack elegance. Though it does not harm the five senses, it already involves the six sense organs in a deceptive manner. Alas for the pure scholar, who finds it hard to engage in music or other refined activities; the elderly man can only retire and stop riding horses. If one can truly renounce profit, I wish to learn from those who forget their intentions. Alas, it is time to leave this secluded place and not force myself to stay, singing songs of farewell that cannot bring anyone back. The sound rises like that of a pot, and the sea roars with rushing waves. The earth's fury stirs up fierce winds, and the victorious army returns, playing triumphant music. Thousands of horses return to their stables, and metal suddenly moves in the furnaces. Even the eloquence of Ouyang Xiu and the writing of Ban Gu cannot fully capture this scene. From now on, each year will be worse than the last, and I will remain the same for the rest of my life." The poem was completed. The attendants took it and presented it to the king, who read it and nodded in approval, saying, "I will convey this to heaven. Though I cannot regain my hearing, I will ensure that you are not burdened further." I bowed twice in gratitude and returned to consciousness.

    668

    滇南丁菊溪太守曰：先生自谓聋也，藉题自赋。予谓先生不聋，殆今之司旷也。何言之？世之聩聩者流，迩言不察，大道未闻。或倾耳听，探人阴私；或附耳低言，示人叵测。虽宫商悉辨，灵府早失指归；虽丝竹能分，天君已迷响往。妄听招愆，多闻何益。如先生者，敛神于静，恍同入定之僧；行气若空，卓尔归真之士。莠言不入于耳，恶声不动于心。人自雌黄，我安履素。无须塞纩，岂畏多言；不必属垣，何从误听。而心醉经畲，早已六通四辟；才兼史管，尤能一本万殊。天以聋养先生之德，予以聋卜先生之寿也。不然，予固不聋也。抚躬自问，察言能如先生之明乎？见理能如先生之慧乎？听断能如先生之敏乎？辨论能如先生之精乎？不聋于耳而聋于心，何如先生不聋于心而聋于耳乎？聋何病？聋然后见先生。予谓先生为今之司旷，夫复何疑？

    Explanation

    668

    The governor of Dian Nan, Ding Juxi, said: The gentleman calls himself deaf, and uses this as a pretext to compose a poem. I say the gentleman is not deaf; he is perhaps the modern-day Kuang. Why do I say this? Those who are deaf in the world do not understand nearby words, nor have they heard the great principles. Some listen intently to probe others' private affairs; others whisper to show themselves inscrutable. Even if they can distinguish all the tones, their inner spirit has long lost its direction; even if they can distinguish all the musical instruments, their mind has already lost its way in sound. Listening carelessly invites faults, and much hearing is of no benefit. As for the gentleman, he concentrates his spirit in stillness, as if he were a monk in meditation; his breath flows as if in the void, a distinguished man who returns to the true. Words of evil do not enter his ears, and evil sounds do not move his heart. Others may slander, but I remain in my natural state. He does not need to plug his ears with cotton, for he is not afraid of much speech; he does not need to listen at the wall, for how could he be misled? His heart is intoxicated by the classics, and he has long been able to understand all aspects of the world; his talent encompasses both history and governance, and he can see the unity in diversity. Heaven has made the gentleman's deafness a virtue, and I use it to predict his longevity. Otherwise, I am not deaf. I reflect on myself and ask: Can I understand speech as clearly as the gentleman? Can I see principles as wisely as the gentleman? Can I judge and decide as quickly as the gentleman? Can I argue and discuss as precisely as the gentleman? Not deaf in the ear but deaf in the heart, how is this compared to the gentleman who is not deaf in the heart but deaf in the ear? Deafness is no illness; only through deafness can we see the gentleman. I say the gentleman is the modern-day Kuang, and there is no doubt about it.
